questions.title: Ureteric bud development and CAKUT
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old boy is found to have a left multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK) on ultrasound performed for a urinary tract infection. His right kidney is normal. His mother asks how this happened and whether future children are at risk.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the embryological origin of the ureteric bud and metanephric mesenchyme, with reference to the chronologies of pronephros, mesonephros and metanephros. [6 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the role of GDNF-RET signalling in ureteric bud induction and branching morphogenesis, and how disruption of this pathway leads to renal agenesis or MCDK. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the Meyer-Weigart law and its relevance to the position of the MCDK in this child. [4 marks]
(d) [EVALUATE] the genetic counselling considerations for CAKUT, including inheritance patterns and recurrence risk in siblings. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (6 marks): Pronephros appears day 22-28, non-functional, regresses; Mesonephros day 24-32, functions as interim kidney until week 10; Metanephros day 28-32, definitive kidney. Ureteric bud arises from mesonephric duct at week 5, invades metanephric mesenchyme. (2 marks for each stage). Common error: confusing mesonephros with metanephros timing.

b) (6 marks): GDNF secreted by metanephric mesenchyme, binds RET receptor on ureteric bud tip. Signalling via MAPK/PI3K pathways. Essential for bud outgrowth, branching, nephron induction. Disruption → failed induction → renal agenesis; partial disruption → MCDK. (2 marks each for mechanism, ligand-receptor detail, clinical correlate). Examiner trap: candidates forget RET is on the bud, not mesenchyme.

c) (4 marks): Meyer-Weigart law states that when one kidney is absent, the ipsilateral ureteric orifice is located more medially and caudally on the trigone. In MCDK, the ureter is atretic. Explains ectopic ureter position. (2 marks for law statement, 2 for relevance).

d) (4 marks): CAKUT is genetically heterogeneous. Most cases sporadic. Familial forms (~10%) show autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance. Genes: PAX2, HNF1B, RET, GDNF, SIX1, SIX5. Recurrence risk ~5-10% for siblings if parent affected. HNF1B mutation associated with renal cysts + diabetes. (2 marks for pattern, 2 for gene examples and recurrence data).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The human kidney develops through three successive stages. The pronephros appears at day 22-28 in the cervical region, is non-functional and regresses completely. The mesonephros develops day 24-32 in the thoracolumbar region and functions as an interim excretory organ until week 10. The metanephros, the definitive kidney, appears at day 28-32 from the interaction between the ureteric bud (mesonephric duct outgrowth) and metanephric mesenchyme. The ureteric bud invades the metanephric mesenchyme at week 5, inducing nephrogenesis.

b) GDNF (Glial cell line-Derived Neurotrophic Factor) is secreted by the metanephric mesenchyme and binds to the RET receptor tyrosine kinase on the ureteric bud tip. This activates MAPK and PI3K signalling pathways, promoting ureteric bud outgrowth, elongation, and iterative branching. Each branch induces nephron formation in the surrounding mesenchyme. Disruption of GDNF-RET signalling through mutation or reduced expression leads to failed ureteric bud induction, resulting in renal agenesis. Hypomorphic mutations produce insufficient signalling, leading to MCDK through abnormal branching and cystic dilatation of collecting ducts.

c) The Meyer-Weigart law states that when a kidney is absent, its corresponding ureteric orifice is situated more medially and caudally on the trigone than normal. In this child's left MCDK, the atretic ureter may terminate blindly or insert ectopically. The law explains that the trigone develops from the common nephric duct (mesonephric duct insertion), so absence of the ipsilateral kidney alters trigonal configuration.

d) CAKUT is genetically heterogeneous. Most cases are sporadic, but ~10% are familial, typically autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance. Key genes include PAX2 (renal-coloboma syndrome), HNF1B (MODY5 + renal cysts), RET, GDNF, SIX1, and SIX5. Recurrence risk for siblings is 5-10% when a parent carries a pathogenic variant. HNF1B mutations may present de novo. Prenatal ultrasound in future pregnancies is advisable. Genetic testing should be offered if syndromic features are present.
discriminators: Integrates developmental chronology of all three kidney stages
Demonstrates understanding of molecular signalling (GDNF-RET) not just anatomy
Applies Meyer-Weigart law to a specific clinical scenario
Incorporates up-to-date genetic counselling information for CAKUT
commonErrors: a) (6 marks): Pronephros appears day 22-28, non-functional, regresses; Mesonephros day 24-32, functions as interim kidney until week 10; Metanephros day 28-32, definitive kidney. Ureteric bud arises from mesonephric duct at week 5, invades metanephric mesenchyme. (2 marks for each stage). Common error: confusing mesonephros with metanephros timing.

b) (6 marks): GDNF secreted by metanephric mesenchyme, binds RET receptor on ureteric bud tip. Signalling via MAPK/PI3K pathways. Essential for bud outgrowth, branching, nephron induction. Disruption → failed induction → renal agenesis; partial disruption → MCDK. (2 marks each for mechanism, ligand-receptor detail, clinical correlate). Examiner trap: candidates forget RET is on the bud, not mesenchyme.

questions.title: Cloacal division, sexual differentiation and testicular descent
questions.questionText:
A newborn with ambiguous genitalia is found to have a single perineal opening. Investigations reveal a persistent cloaca with a common channel of 3 cm. The karyotype is 46,XY. You are asked to advise on the embryological basis of the findings.

(a) [OUTLINE] the embryological division of the cloaca into the urogenital sinus and anorectum during weeks 4-7, including the role of the urorectal septum. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the roles of SRY, SOX9 and DAX1 in testicular determination and how imbalance in these pathways contributes to disorders of sexual development. [5 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the mechanism of Müllerian duct regression in the male fetus, including AMH signalling and the significance of persistent Müllerian duct syndrome. [5 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the two-stage model of testicular descent, differentiating the roles of AMH (stage 1) and testosterone (stage 2), and the role of the gubernaculum in each phase. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): Cloaca is endodermal hindgut dilation; urorectal septum (Tourneux fold + Rathke plicae) grows caudally from week 4-7; fuses with cloacal membrane at week 7; divides cloaca into urogenital sinus (anterior) and anorectum (posterior). Cloacal membrane ruptures week 8 forming urogenital sinus opening and anal opening. (1 mark for each key step). Common error: timing of septation (not complete until week 7).

b) (5 marks): SRY on Y chromosome (SOX3 region) upregulates SOX9 → testis determination. DAX1 on X chromosome antagonises SRY; duplication of DAX1 → XY DSD (dosage-sensitive sex reversal). SF1/NR5A1 cooperates with SRY to activate SOX9. (2 marks for SRY-SOX9 cascade, 1 mark for DAX1 antagonism, 2 marks for DSD consequences).

c) (5 marks): AMH secreted by Sertoli cells from week 7; binds AMHR2 on mesenchyme of Müllerian ducts → regression. Paracrine effect (ipsilateral). Persistent Müllerian duct syndrome (PMDS): AMH mutation or AMHR2 mutation → uterus and Fallopian tubes in 46,XY male. Cryptorchidism common due to gubernacular abnormality. (2 marks for AMH signalling, 3 marks for PMDS).

d) (5 marks): Stage 1 (transabdominal, 8-15 weeks): AMH-dependent swelling of gubernaculum holding testis near inguinal ring; regression of cranial suspensory ligament. Stage 2 (inguinoscrotal, 25-35 weeks): Testosterone (via INSL3) mediates gubernacular contraction and migration to scrotum. Androgen receptor required. Common error: thinking testosterone alone drives stage 1. (2 marks for stage 1, 2 marks for stage 2, 1 mark for INSL3).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The cloaca is the endodermal dilation of the hindgut that receives the mesonephric ducts and the allantois. Between weeks 4 and 7, the urorectal septum (composed of the cranial Tourneux fold and the lateral Rathke plicae) grows caudally toward the cloacal membrane. Fusion is complete by week 7, dividing the cloaca into the urogenital sinus anteriorly and the anorectum posteriorly. The cloacal membrane then ruptures at week 8, creating the urogenital and anal openings. Failure of septation results in persistent cloaca.

b) SRY, located on the Y chromosome, is the master regulator of testicular determination. It upregulates SOX9, which drives Sertoli cell differentiation and downstream testis development. DAX1 (NR0B1) on Xp21 normally represses testicular pathways; duplication of DAX1 causes XY sex reversal (dosage-sensitive sex reversal) by overriding SRY action. SF1 (NR5A1) cooperates with SRY to activate SOX9. In this case, the 46,XY karyotype with persistent cloaca and ambiguous genitalia suggests a complex DSD possibly involving DAX1 duplication or WT1 mutation.

c) AMH is secreted by fetal Sertoli cells from week 7. It binds to the AMH type 2 receptor (AMHR2) on mesenchymal cells surrounding the Müllerian ducts, inducing apoptosis and regression of the fallopian tubes, uterus, and upper vagina. This is a paracrine effect acting ipsilaterally. PMDS results from inactivating mutations in AMH or AMHR2; affected 46,XY individuals have a uterus and tubes with normal male external genitalia, often presenting with cryptorchidism or inguinal hernia containing Müllerian structures.

d) Testicular descent occurs in two stages. Stage 1 (transabdominal, 8-15 weeks): AMH-mediated swelling of the gubernaculum holds the testis near the internal inguinal ring while the cranial suspensory ligament regresses. Stage 2 (inguinoscrotal, 25-35 weeks): Testosterone and INSL3 stimulate gubernacular contraction and migration through the inguinal canal into the scrotum. The gubernaculum is essential in both stages — anchoring the testis in stage 1 and guiding descent in stage 2.
discriminators: Integrates cloacal septation with sexual differentiation in a complex clinical scenario
Demonstrates understanding of both genetic (SRY/DAX1/SOX9) and hormonal (AMH/testosterone) pathways
Detailed knowledge of PMDS as a distinct clinical entity
Two-stage model of testicular descent with correct assignment of signalling molecules
commonErrors: a) (5 marks): Cloaca is endodermal hindgut dilation; urorectal septum (Tourneux fold + Rathke plicae) grows caudally from week 4-7; fuses with cloacal membrane at week 7; divides cloaca into urogenital sinus (anterior) and anorectum (posterior). Cloacal membrane ruptures week 8 forming urogenital sinus opening and anal opening. (1 mark for each key step). Common error: timing of septation (not complete until week 7).

c) (5 marks): AMH secreted by Sertoli cells from week 7; binds AMHR2 on mesenchyme of Müllerian ducts → regression. Paracrine effect (ipsilateral). Persistent Müllerian duct syndrome (PMDS): AMH mutation or AMHR2 mutation → uterus and Fallopian tubes in 46,XY male. Cryptorchidism common due to gubernacular abnormality. (2 marks for AMH signalling, 3 marks for PMDS).

d) (5 marks): Stage 1 (transabdominal, 8-15 weeks): AMH-dependent swelling of gubernaculum holding testis near inguinal ring; regression of cranial suspensory ligament. Stage 2 (inguinoscrotal, 25-35 weeks): Testosterone (via INSL3) mediates gubernacular contraction and migration to scrotum. Androgen receptor required. Common error: thinking testosterone alone drives stage 1. (2 marks for stage 1, 2 marks for stage 2, 1 mark for INSL3).

questions.title: Renal developmental chronology
questions.questionText:
(a) [LIST] the three successive renal developmental stages in order, stating the gestational week of appearance for each. [3 marks]
(b) [STATE] which of these stages is functional, and for how long. [2 marks]
(c) [DEFINE] the term 'metanephros' and describe the tissue interaction that gives rise to the definitive kidney. [3 marks]
(d) [CALCULATE] the approximate gestational week by which nephrogenesis is complete in a term infant. [2 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (3 marks): Pronephros week 3 (day 22); Mesonephros week 4 (day 24-32); Metanephros week 5 (day 28-32). 1 mark each, must give correct week.

b) (2 marks): Mesonephros functions as interim kidney from week 6 to week 10. 1 mark for mesonephros, 1 mark for timing. Common error: candidates say metanephros is first functional — it is not functional until much later.

c) (3 marks): Metanephros = definitive kidney. Develops from interaction between ureteric bud (mesonephric duct outgrowth) and metanephric mesenchyme (intermediate mesoderm). 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for tissue interaction details.

d) (2 marks): Nephrogenesis is complete by week 34-36 of gestation. No new nephrons form after birth. 1 mark for week, 1 mark for fact that it ceases at birth.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) Pronephros — week 3 (day 22); Mesonephros — week 4 (day 24-32); Metanephros — week 5 (day 28-32).

b) The mesonephros is functional as an interim kidney from week 6 to week 10 of gestation.

c) The metanephros is the definitive adult kidney. It develops through reciprocal induction between the ureteric bud (an outgrowth of the mesonephric duct) and the metanephric mesenchyme (derived from intermediate mesoderm). The ureteric bud branches to form the collecting system, while the mesenchyme forms nephrons.

d) Nephrogenesis is complete by 34-36 weeks of gestation. No new nephrons are formed after birth, and final nephron number (~300,000 to 1.2 million per kidney) is determined at this point.
discriminators: Pure recall of embryological chronology
Clear distinction between functional stages
Understanding of tissue interaction in metanephric development
Knowledge of nephrogenesis completion timing
commonErrors: b) (2 marks): Mesonephros functions as interim kidney from week 6 to week 10. 1 mark for mesonephros, 1 mark for timing. Common error: candidates say metanephros is first functional — it is not functional until much later.

questions.title: Testicular descent and the gubernaculum
questions.questionText:
A 9-month-old boy is referred with bilateral undescended testes. He was born at term. On examination, both testes are non-palpable. You suspect a disorder of testicular descent.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the two anatomical phases of testicular descent with their corresponding gestational age ranges. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the role of the gubernaculum in each phase. [3 marks]
(c) [DIFFERENTIATE] between the hormonal control of stage 1 (transabdominal) and stage 2 (inguinoscrotal) descent. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): Stage 1 (transabdominal): 8-15 weeks. Testis moves from urogenital ridge to internal inguinal ring. Stage 2 (inguinoscrotal): 25-35 weeks. Testis traverses inguinal canal to scrotum. 2 marks per stage with correct weeks.

b) (3 marks): Stage 1: Gubernaculum swells (reaction to AMH/INSL3) anchoring testis near inguinal ring; cranial suspensory ligament regresses. Stage 2: Gubernaculum contracts and shortens (under INSL3), guiding testis through inguinal canal. Common error: saying gubernaculum pulls the testis down actively — it provides a guiding structure, not traction.

c) (3 marks): Stage 1 controlled by AMH (Sertoli cells) and INSL3 (Leydig cells) → gubernacular swelling. Stage 2 controlled by testosterone and INSL3 → gubernacular contraction and inguinoscrotal migration. 1.5 marks per stage with correct hormones. Examiner trap: many candidates omit INSL3.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) Stage 1 (transabdominal phase) occurs from 8 to 15 weeks gestation — the testis moves from the urogenital ridge to the internal inguinal ring. Stage 2 (inguinoscrotal phase) occurs from 25 to 35 weeks — the testis passes through the inguinal canal and descends into the scrotum.

b) In stage 1, the gubernaculum swells under the influence of AMH and INSL3, anchoring the testis near the inguinal ring, while the cranial suspensory ligament regresses. In stage 2, the gubernaculum contracts and shortens under INSL3 stimulation, guiding the testis through the inguinal canal into the scrotum.

c) Stage 1 is primarily controlled by AMH (from Sertoli cells) and INSL3 (from Leydig cells), which promote gubernacular swelling. Stage 2 is driven by testosterone (from Leydig cells, via androgen receptor) and INSL3, which mediate gubernacular contraction and migration. The two stages are hormonally distinct.
discriminators: Clear two-stage framework with correct timing
Differentiation of gubernacular roles between phases
Knowledge of INSL3 as a key hormone
Understanding that hormonal control differs between stages
commonErrors: b) (3 marks): Stage 1: Gubernaculum swells (reaction to AMH/INSL3) anchoring testis near inguinal ring; cranial suspensory ligament regresses. Stage 2: Gubernaculum contracts and shortens (under INSL3), guiding testis through inguinal canal. Common error: saying gubernaculum pulls the testis down actively — it provides a guiding structure, not traction.

c) (3 marks): Stage 1 controlled by AMH (Sertoli cells) and INSL3 (Leydig cells) → gubernacular swelling. Stage 2 controlled by testosterone and INSL3 → gubernacular contraction and inguinoscrotal migration. 1.5 marks per stage with correct hormones. Examiner trap: many candidates omit INSL3.

questions.title: 46,XY DSD with ambiguous genitalia
questions.questionText:
A newborn is noted at delivery to have ambiguous genitalia with a phallic structure 1.5 cm, fused labioscrotal folds, and a single perineal orifice. The karyotype is 46,XY. Serum 17-hydroxyprogesterone is normal. Ultrasound shows a uterus and no gonads in the scrotum. Testosterone and AMH levels are low.

(a) [PROPOSE] an embryological diagnosis based on the findings, with justification. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the role of SRY, SOX9, DAX1, and SF1 in testicular determination and how their disruption could explain this presentation. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the embryological basis of Müllerian duct regression and why the presence of a uterus indicates failure of AMH signalling. [5 marks]
(d) [OUTLINE] the additional investigations you would perform to reach a definitive genetic diagnosis. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): Likely complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer syndrome) or partial gonadal dysgenesis given 46,XY with uterus, low AMH → absent or dysgenetic testes. Must justify: low T and AMH suggest non-functioning testicular tissue; uterus indicates failure of Müllerian regression; ambiguous genitalia reflect inadequate T/DHT. (3 marks for diagnosis, 2 marks for justification).

b) (6 marks): SRY activates SOX9 → Sertoli differentiation; SF1 cooperates. DAX1 represses male pathway. Duplication of DAX1 → XY sex reversal. WT1 (Denys-Drash/Frasier) also important. Mutations in SRY (~15% of complete gonadal dysgenesis), SF1/NR5A1, SOX9, WT1, DHH, MAP3K1 can cause. (2 marks for cascade, 2 marks for DAX1, 2 marks for gene list).

c) (5 marks): AMH from fetal Sertoli cells week 7-8; binds AMHR2 → Müllerian regression. Fallopian tubes and uterus develop if AMH absent or receptor defective. Paracrine effect (each testis regresses ipsilateral duct). Presence of uterus = absent/insufficient AMH, confirming testicular dysgenesis. (2 marks for AMH mechanism, 2 marks for reasoning, 1 mark for paracrine nature).

d) (4 marks): 1. Array CGH for copy number variants (including DAX1 duplication). 2. Targeted gene panel for DSD genes (SRY, SF1, SOX9, WT1, DHH, MAP3K1, NR5A1). 3. Whole exome/genome sequencing if panel negative. 4. Gonadal biopsy? Usually performed later at time of gonadectomy due to malignancy risk. (1 mark each).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The most likely diagnosis is 46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer syndrome) or partial gonadal dysgenesis. The low testosterone and low AMH indicate absent or severely dysgenetic testicular tissue. The presence of a uterus confirms failure of Müllerian duct regression, which normally requires AMH from fetal testes. The ambiguous genitalia reflect insufficient testosterone and DHT production during the critical window of masculinisation (weeks 8-14).

b) SRY on the Y chromosome is the master switch for testicular determination. It upregulates SOX9, which together with SF1 drives Sertoli cell differentiation and subsequent testis formation. DAX1 on the X chromosome normally antagonises this pathway; overexpression (due to duplication) can override SRY and cause XY DSD. SF1/NR5A1 mutations cause a spectrum from hypospadias to complete gonadal dysgenesis. WT1 mutations cause Denys-Drash or Frasier syndromes. In this case, the pattern suggests a defect early in the testicular determination pathway.

c) AMH is produced by fetal Sertoli cells from week 7 of gestation. It binds to the AMHR2 receptor on mesenchymal cells surrounding the Müllerian ducts, inducing apoptotic regression. This is a paracrine signal acting ipsilaterally. The presence of a uterus in this 46,XY infant indicates that AMH was either absent or the receptor was non-functional. Since testosterone is also low, the underlying problem is testicular dysgenesis rather than an isolated AMH defect.

d) Further investigations include: array comparative genomic hybridisation (aCGH) to detect copy number variants such as DAX1 duplication; a targeted DSD gene panel sequencing SRY, SF1/NR5A1, SOX9, WT1, DHH, and MAP3K1; whole exome sequencing if the panel is negative; and laparoscopic examination with gonadal biopsy at the appropriate time given the risk of gonadoblastoma in dysgenetic gonads.
discriminators: Synthesises hormonal, genetic, and anatomical findings into a coherent embryological diagnosis
Demonstrates understanding of the SRY-SOX9-DAX1-SF1 genetic network
Applies AMH paracrine signalling to clinical findings
Includes practical molecular diagnostic approach
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Embryological basis of congenital renal anomalies

Options:
A. Failure of ureteric bud induction
B. Supernumerary ureteric bud from mesonephric duct
C. Incomplete ascent of metanephros
D. Fusion of metanephric masses across midline
E. Premature branching of ureteric bud
F. Abnormal division of ureteric bud before metanephric invasion
G. Persistent urogenital sinus septum
H. Failure of cloacal septation
I. Defective GDNF-RET signalling
J. Abnormal apoptosis of metanephric mesenchyme

Scenarios:
1. A 4-year-old boy has an ultrasound showing the right kidney located in the pelvis with a short ureter. The left kidney is normally positioned. What is the most likely embryological mechanism?
2. A newborn with Potter sequence has bilateral renal agenesis. The parents are consanguineous. What is the most likely embryological mechanism?
3. A 6-year-old girl has recurrent UTIs. MCU reveals a complete duplicated collecting system on the left side with two separate ureteric orifices. What is the most likely embryological mechanism?
4. A 5-year-old boy is found to have a horseshoe kidney on ultrasound performed for enuresis. What is the most likely embryological mechanism?
5. An antenatal ultrasound at 20 weeks shows a multicystic dysplastic kidney on the right. The left kidney is normal. What is the most likely embryological mechanism?
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: CAKUT spectrum and clinical presentation
questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old girl is referred with recurrent febrile UTIs. Ultrasound shows a left multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK) and right hydronephrosis with a dilated ureter. Serum creatinine is 75 micromol/L.

(a) [DEFINE] the CAKUT spectrum and list the key structural anomalies it encompasses. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the embryological basis of the association between MCDK and contralateral vesicoureteric reflux. [5 marks]
(c) [CALCULATE] this child's eGFR using the Schwartz formula (assume height 90 cm, kappa 0.55). Interpret the result and discuss the significance of a solitary functioning kidney in childhood. [6 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the long-term surveillance strategy for a child with a solitary functioning kidney, including blood pressure, proteinuria, and renal function monitoring. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): CAKUT = Congenital Anomalies of the Kidney and Urinary Tract. Encompasses: renal agenesis, MCDK, renal hypoplasia/dysplasia, horseshoe kidney, ectopic kidney, ureteropelvic junction obstruction, ureterovesical junction obstruction, primary VUR, ureteric duplication, posterior urethral valves. 1 mark for definition, 3 marks for listing at least 6 anomalies.

b) (5 marks): Common nephric duct hypothesis — abnormal insertion of the ureteric bud into the mesonephric duct results in abnormal metanephric induction (MCDK) on one side and abnormal trigonal development with deficient anti-reflux mechanism (VUR) on the contralateral side. Both anomalies arise from the same developmental field defect at week 5. (2 marks for embryology, 2 marks for MCCD hypothesis, 1 mark for link).

c) (6 marks): eGFR = (k × height) / creatinine = (0.55 × 90) / 75 = 49.5 / 75 = 0.66 mL/min/1.73 m²? No — Schwartz: eGFR = (k × height in cm) / creatinine in mg/dL. Convert creatinine 75 micromol/L = 75/88.4 = 0.85 mg/dL. eGFR = (0.55 × 90) / 0.85 = 49.5/0.85 = 58.2 mL/min/1.73m². This is CKD stage 3. (2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for correct conversion, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for significance). Common error: failing to convert creatinine units.

d) (5 marks): Annual BP measurement; annual first-morning urine for proteinuria (PCR or ACR); annual eGFR by Schwartz; renal ultrasound; dietary advice (avoid nephrotoxins, maintain hydration); ACEi if proteinuria or hypertension develops. Long-term risk: CKD progression, hypertension, proteinuria, ESKD ~10-20% by adulthood. (1 mark each for 5 valid points).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) CAKUT refers to a spectrum of congenital structural anomalies of the kidneys and urinary tract resulting from abnormal embryonic development. It includes renal agenesis, multicystic dysplastic kidney, renal hypoplasia/dysplasia, horseshoe kidney, ectopic kidney, ureteropelvic junction obstruction, ureterovesical junction obstruction, primary vesicoureteric reflux, ureteric duplication, posterior urethral valves, and other anomalies.

b) Both the left MCDK and contralateral VUR likely arise from a single embryological defect involving the common nephric duct. Abnormal insertion of the ureteric bud into the mesonephric duct at week 5 leads to failed metanephric induction (MCDK) on one side and abnormal trigonal formation (predisposing to VUR) on the contralateral side. This is known as the common nephric duct malformation hypothesis.

c) Serum creatinine 75 micromol/L = 0.85 mg/dL (divide by 88.4). Height 90 cm. Schwartz formula: eGFR = (k × height)/creatinine = (0.55 × 90)/0.85 = 49.5/0.85 = 58.2 mL/min/1.73 m². This corresponds to CKD stage 3 (moderate reduction in GFR). A solitary functioning kidney must compensate through hyperfiltration, which carries long-term risks of glomerular injury, proteinuria, and declining function.

d) Surveillance includes: annual blood pressure measurement; annual first-morning urine for protein-to-creatinine ratio; annual Schwartz eGFR; renal ultrasound every 2-3 years to assess compensatory hypertrophy; avoidance of nephrotoxic medications; maintaining good hydration; and initiation of ACE inhibitor if hypertension or proteinuria develops. Long-term nephrology follow-up is recommended into adulthood.
discriminators: Integrates embryological and clinical aspects of CAKUT
Demonstrates ability to calculate and interpret paediatric eGFR with unit conversion
Knowledge of common nephric duct hypothesis linking MCDK and VUR
Practical long-term surveillance strategy for solitary kidney
commonErrors: b) (5 marks): Common nephric duct hypothesis — abnormal insertion of the ureteric bud into the mesonephric duct results in abnormal metanephric induction (MCDK) on one side and abnormal trigonal development with deficient anti-reflux mechanism (VUR) on the contralateral side. Both anomalies arise from the same developmental field defect at week 5. (2 marks for embryology, 2 marks for MCCD hypothesis, 1 mark for link).

c) (6 marks): eGFR = (k × height) / creatinine = (0.55 × 90) / 75 = 49.5 / 75 = 0.66 mL/min/1.73 m²? No — Schwartz: eGFR = (k × height in cm) / creatinine in mg/dL. Convert creatinine 75 micromol/L = 75/88.4 = 0.85 mg/dL. eGFR = (0.55 × 90) / 0.85 = 49.5/0.85 = 58.2 mL/min/1.73m². This is CKD stage 3. (2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for correct conversion, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for significance). Common error: failing to convert creatinine units.

questions.title: GFR physiology and CKD staging in children
questions.questionText:
A 6-month-old infant with bilateral renal hypodysplasia has a plasma creatinine of 55 micromol/L. You are asked to assess her renal function and stage her chronic kidney disease. Her weight is 7 kg, length 66 cm.

(a) [EXPLAIN] why neonatal GFR is physiologically low when not corrected for body surface area, and discuss the concept of 'renal reserve' in the developing kidney. [5 marks]
(b) [CALCULATE] this infant's eGFR using an appropriate formula for age < 1 year. Discuss how the paediatric K/DOQI CKD staging system differs from the adult system. [6 marks]
(c) [CRITICALLY APPRAISE] the limitations of using creatinine-based equations for estimating GFR in infants under 1 year of age. [5 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] how the concept of 'nephron endowment' at birth influences long-term cardiovascular and renal outcomes in children with CAKUT. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): Neonatal GFR is ~20 mL/min/1.73m² at birth, rising to adult levels by 2 years. Reasons: low renal blood flow, high renal vascular resistance, immature glomeruli (superficial cortex nephrons last to mature), low mean arterial pressure, small filtering surface area. Renal reserve: the capacity to increase GFR under stress (e.g., high-protein meal, pregnancy). Children with CAKUT have reduced renal reserve, limiting compensatory hyperfiltration. (3 marks for factors, 2 marks for renal reserve concept).

b) (6 marks): Recommended formula: Schwartz bedside formula eGFR = (0.413 × height in cm)/creatinine in mg/dL (for >1 year) — but for <1 year, use revised Schwartz or Counahan-Barratt. Counahan-Barratt: eGFR = (0.43 × height in cm)/creatinine in mg/dL. Creatinine 55 micromol/L = 55/88.4 = 0.62 mg/dL. eGFR = (0.43 × 66)/0.62 = 28.4/0.62 = 45.8 mL/min/1.73m². K/DOQI differences in children: normals differ by age (no single normal); thresholds for CKD stages same GFR values but applied relative to age-corrected norms; CKD Stage 3 subdivided (3a 45-59, 3b 30-44); emphasis on growth + nutrition. (2 marks for calculation, 2 marks for formula choice, 2 marks for K/DOQI differences).

c) (5 marks): Limitations: 1) Creatinine reflects maternal levels for first 48-72 hours. 2) Low muscle mass in infants → low creatinine → overestimation of GFR at low values. 3) Tubular secretion of creatinine and extrarenal clearance proportionally more significant in infants. 4) Rapidly changing body composition in first year. 5) Schwartz formula validated for >1 year, less accurate in infants. Cystatin C may be better in this age group. (1 mark per point).

d) (4 marks): Nephron endowment is determined by birth (nephrogenesis ceases ~36 weeks). Low endowment → hyperfiltration in remaining nephrons → glomerulosclerosis → progressive CKD. Barker hypothesis: low nephron number links to hypertension, cardiovascular disease in adulthood. In CAKUT, nephron endowment is typically reduced, conferring increased risk. (2 marks for mechanism, 2 marks for long-term implications).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) At birth, GFR is ~20 mL/min/1.73 m² due to low renal blood flow, high renal vascular resistance, immature superficial cortical nephrons, low systemic blood pressure, and small glomerular filtration surface area. GFR increases to adult levels (~120 mL/min/1.73 m²) by age 2 years. Renal reserve is the capacity of the kidneys to increase GFR above baseline in response to physiological stress. Children with CAKUT have reduced nephron mass and correspondingly reduced renal reserve, limiting their ability to compensate.

b) For infants <1 year, the Counahan-Barratt formula is more appropriate: eGFR = (0.43 × height)/creatinine. Creatinine 55 micromol/L = 0.62 mg/dL. eGFR = (0.43 × 66)/0.62 = 45.8 mL/min/1.73 m² (CKD stage 3). K/DOQI paediatric modifications: normal GFR thresholds are age-dependent, not a fixed value; Stage 3 is subdivided into 3a (45-59) and 3b (30-44); staging must account for somatic growth; albuminuria categories are age-adjusted.

c) Creatinine-based equations in infants have significant limitations: maternal creatinine contributes in the first 48-72 hours; low muscle mass produces low creatinine levels, leading to overestimation of GFR; tubular secretion of creatinine is proportionally greater and more variable in infants; the rapid changes in body composition during infancy make single formulas inaccurate; and the Schwartz equation was validated for children 1-16 years, not neonates. Cystatin C, which is less dependent on muscle mass, may provide more accurate GFR estimation in this age group.

d) Nephron endowment is fixed by 36 weeks gestation; no new nephrons form after birth. Low endowment leads to hyperfiltration in the remaining nephrons, driving glomerular hypertrophy and eventual sclerosis. This creates a cycle of progressive CKD. The Barker hypothesis suggests that low nephron number also predisposes to hypertension and cardiovascular disease in adulthood. Children with CAKUT typically have reduced nephron mass, conferring elevated lifelong risk of CKD progression, proteinuria, and hypertension.
discriminators: Detailed understanding of age-specific GFR physiology beyond simple recall
Correct formula selection for infants under 1 year with unit conversion
Critical appraisal of creatinine limitations in infancy
Barker hypothesis linkage between nephron endowment and adult disease
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Oligohydramnios and Potter sequence
questions.questionText:
A newborn is delivered at 36 weeks with severe respiratory distress. There is facial dysmorphism, limb contractures, and the abdomen is distended with a palpable bladder. Amniotic fluid volume was severely reduced on prenatal ultrasound.

(a) [LIST] the four classic features of Potter sequence. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiological link between fetal renal dysfunction and oligohydramnios. [3 marks]
(c) [STATE] three potential underlying urological causes of oligohydramnios in a male infant with a palpable bladder. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): 1) Pulmonary hypoplasia (leading cause of death). 2) Facial dysmorphism (flattened nose, recessed chin, low-set ears, epicanthal folds). 3) Limb contractures/positional deformities (clubfeet, arthrogryposis). 4) Growth restriction/oligohydramnios sequence. 1 mark each.

b) (3 marks): After 16-18 weeks, fetal urine is the main contributor to amniotic fluid volume. Fetal renal dysfunction → reduced/absent urine output → oligohydramnios. The oligohydramnios then causes mechanical compression of the fetus leading to the Potter phenotype. 1 mark for fetal urine as source, 1 mark for renal dysfunction link, 1 mark for compression effects.

c) (3 marks): 1) Posterior urethral valves (most common cause in male infants). 2) Urethral atresia. 3) Prune belly syndrome. 1 mark each. Other acceptable: bilateral severe VUR, megacystis-microcolon-hypoperistalsis syndrome.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The four classic features of Potter sequence are: pulmonary hypoplasia, characteristic facies (flattened nose, recessed chin, low-set ears), limb deformities (clubfeet, contractures), and growth restriction. Pulmonary hypoplasia is the most critical determinant of survival.

b) From approximately 16-18 weeks gestation, amniotic fluid is predominantly derived from fetal urine. Bilateral renal agenesis or severe obstructive uropathy reduces fetal urine output, leading to oligohydramnios. The reduced amniotic fluid volume causes extrinsic compression of the fetus, impairing lung development (pulmonary hypoplasia), and causing positional limb deformities and characteristic facies.

c) In a male infant with a palpable bladder, the most likely causes of oligohydramnios are: posterior urethral valves (most common congenital cause of lower urinary tract obstruction in males), urethral atresia, and prune belly syndrome.
discriminators: Concise recall of Potter sequence stigmata
Clear pathophysiological understanding of oligohydramnios
Ability to differentiate urological causes in a male infant
commonErrors: c) (3 marks): 1) Posterior urethral valves (most common cause in male infants). 2) Urethral atresia. 3) Prune belly syndrome. 1 mark each. Other acceptable: bilateral severe VUR, megacystis-microcolon-hypoperistalsis syndrome.

questions.title: K/DOQI CKD staging modified for children
questions.questionText:
A 10-year-old boy with posterior urethral valves has a serum creatinine of 150 micromol/L. His height is 135 cm.

(a) [CALCULATE] the eGFR and state the CKD stage using the paediatric K/DOQI classification. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] how the K/DOQI classification is modified for children compared to adults, with specific reference to age-related normal values. [3 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the additional parameters that must be monitored in children with CKD beyond those monitored in adults. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): Schwartz formula: eGFR = (0.413 × height in cm)/creatinine in mg/dL. 150 micromol/L = 150/88.4 = 1.70 mg/dL. eGFR = (0.413 × 135)/1.70 = 55.8/1.70 = 32.8 mL/min/1.73 m². This is CKD stage 3b (30-44 mL/min/1.73 m²). 2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for correct unit conversion, 1 mark for stage.

b) (3 marks): Paediatric modifications: 1) GFR thresholds for each stage are the same numerical values as adults, but normal GFR is age-dependent (not reached until 2 years). 2) Stage 3 is subdivided into 3a (45-59) and 3b (30-44) to capture progression. 3) Growth and nutrition are key additional domains. 1 mark each.

c) (3 marks): 1) Growth parameters (height and weight centiles, height velocity). 2) Nutritional status (BMI, dietary intake, need for supplemental feeds or gastrostomy). 3) Neurodevelopment and school performance. Also: bone metabolism (Ca/PTH/vitamin D), anaemia management with age-appropriate targets, psychosocial support for transition. 1 mark each.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) eGFR by Schwartz formula: creatinine = 150/88.4 = 1.70 mg/dL. eGFR = (0.413 × 135)/1.70 = 32.8 mL/min/1.73 m². This corresponds to CKD stage 3b (moderate-to-severe reduction in GFR, 30-44 mL/min/1.73 m²).

b) The K/DOQI paediatric modifications include: using the same numerical GFR thresholds for staging, but recognising that normal GFR is age-dependent (it reaches adult levels at ~2 years, with term neonates ~20, increasing progressively); subdivision of stage 3 into 3a and 3b to better track progression; and addition of growth and nutritional status as core components of staging assessment.

c) Beyond standard adult CKD monitoring (BP, proteinuria, eGFR, electrolytes), children require: regular growth assessment (height velocity, weight, head circumference), nutritional monitoring with dietary intervention as needed, neurodevelopmental surveillance due to the impact of uraemia on developing brain, and preparation for transition to adult services.
discriminators: Accurate Schwartz calculation with correct unit conversion
Knowledge of paediatric-specific CKD staging nuances
Awareness of additional monitoring domains unique to children
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: CAKUT presenting with renal impairment
questions.questionText:
An 8-year-old boy presents with poor growth (height 3rd centile), polydipsia, and enuresis. His BP is 130/85 mmHg (>95th centile). Creatinine is 200 micromol/L. He was diagnosed with bilateral hydronephrosis and posterior urethral valves at birth, for which he underwent valve ablation at day 5 of life. He has been lost to follow-up since age 3.

(a) [CALCULATE] his current eGFR and CKD stage using the Schwartz formula (height 120 cm). Interpret the significance of his hypertension and growth failure. [5 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] the pathophysiological mechanisms driving CKD progression in boys with posterior urethral valves after valve ablation. [5 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the acute management plan for this child including pharmacological and dietary interventions. [5 marks]
(d) [DESCRIBE] the long-term surveillance required, including timing of referral for renal transplantation and the specific challenges of transplantation in boys with PUV. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): Creatinine 200 micromol/L = 200/88.4 = 2.26 mg/dL. eGFR = (0.413 × 120)/2.26 = 49.6/2.26 = 21.9 mL/min/1.73 m². This is CKD stage 4. Hypertension >95th centile for age → must be treated. Growth failure indicates long-standing uraemia, metabolic acidosis, and possibly renal rickets. 2 marks for calculation, 1 mark for stage, 2 marks for interpretation.

b) (5 marks): Mechanisms: 1) Valve bladder syndrome — high-pressure/high-volume bladder → upper tract deterioration. 2) Hyperfiltration injury due to reduced nephron mass. 3) Recurrent UTIs and pyelonephritis causing additional renal scarring. 4) Tubular dysfunction (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, salt wasting, acidosis). 5) Ongoing obstruction despite ablation (secondary bladder neck hypertrophy, urethral stricture). (1 mark each).

c) (5 marks): 1) ACE inhibitor/ARB for hypertension and renoprotection. 2) Sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis. 3) Vitamin D/calcium for renal bone disease. 4) Erythropoietin for anaemia (Hb target 11-12 g/dL). 5) Dietary: age-appropriate protein restriction (avoid excess), salt restriction, adequate calories for growth, phosphate binders if hyperphosphataemic. +/- recombinant growth hormone if growth velocity poor. (1 mark each).

d) (5 marks): Long-term: 3-monthly review with eGFR, electrolytes, BP, growth, urine PCR. Pre-emptive transplantation when eGFR <15 mL/min/1.73 m² or earlier if symptomatic. Challenges: valve bladder (poor compliance, high pressure) must be optimised pre-transplant to protect graft — may need augmentation cystoplasty or CIC; recurrent UTIs in native kidneys; occasional need for native nephrectomy if recurrent pyelonephritis; transition planning to adult services. (1 mark each for 5 valid points).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) eGFR = (0.413 × 120)/(200/88.4) = 49.6/2.26 = 21.9 mL/min/1.73 m² (CKD stage 4). His hypertension (>95th centile for age) is both a consequence of and contributor to CKD progression, mediated by volume overload and renin-angiotensin activation. Growth failure reflects cumulative effects of metabolic acidosis, anaemia, renal osteodystrophy, and uraemic toxins on the growth hormone-IGF-1 axis.

b) CKD progression after valve ablation is driven by: valve bladder syndrome where detrusor hypertrophy and poor compliance create high storage pressures transmitted to the upper tracts; hyperfiltration injury in remnant nephrons leading to proteinuria and glomerulosclerosis; recurrent pyelonephritis from persistent VUR causing parenchymal scarring; tubular dysfunction producing salt wasting, acidosis, and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; and potential for recurrent obstruction at the bladder neck or valve remnants.

c) Acute management includes: ACE inhibitor (enalapril) for hypertension and to reduce proteinuria; sodium bicarbonate to correct metabolic acidosis (target HCO3 >22); activated vitamin D (calcitriol) for secondary hyperparathyroidism; erythropoietin stimulating agent for anaemia (target Hb 11-12 g/dL); and dietary optimisation including adequate caloric intake for catch-up growth, controlled protein intake (100% DRI, not excess), salt restriction, and phosphate binders if serum phosphate elevated.

d) Surveillance: 3-monthly eGFR, electrolytes, albumin, urine PCR, BP, and growth measurements. Pre-emptive transplantation is indicated when eGFR <15 or earlier with symptomatic uraemia. Renal transplantation in PUV patients is challenging due to the valve bladder: poor compliance and high-pressure storage can damage the graft. Pre-transplant urodynamics are essential; patients with hostile bladders may require augmentation cystoplasty or clean intermittent catheterisation. Native kidneys may need removal if they cause recurrent infections. Coordinated transition to adult nephrology services is critical.
discriminators: Integrated management of a complex post-PUV patient with advanced CKD
Understanding of valve bladder pathophysiology as a driver of CKD
Practical acute and long-term management planning
Knowledge of transplant challenges specific to PUV patients
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Causes and classification of paediatric CKD

Options:
A. Posterior urethral valves
B. Renal hypodysplasia
C. Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
D. Congenital nephrotic syndrome
E. Haemolytic uraemic syndrome
F. Lupus nephritis
G. Cystinosis
H. Primary hyperoxaluria type 1
I. Autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease
J. Bilateral Wilms tumour with nephron-sparing surgery

Scenarios:
1. A 2-year-old boy has nephromegaly, hypertension, and a serum creatinine of 90 micromol/L. Ultrasound shows bilaterally enlarged kidneys with a 'salt and pepper' appearance. His parents are consanguineous. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis?
2. A 6-year-old girl with recurrent UTIs and daytime incontinence has a serum creatinine of 120 micromol/L. MCU shows left grade IV VUR. Ultrasound shows bilaterally small kidneys with poor corticomedullary differentiation. What is the most likely underlying cause of her CKD?
3. A 4-year-old boy presents with failure to thrive, polyuria, polydipsia, and rickets. Creatinine is 80 micromol/L. Urine dipstick shows proteinuria and glycosuria. Slit lamp examination reveals corneal crystals. What is the most likely diagnosis?
4. A newborn male has a palpable bladder, poor urinary stream, and bilateral hydronephrosis on antenatal ultrasound. Creatinine at day 1 is 120 micromol/L. What is the most likely cause of his CKD?
5. A 10-year-old girl develops oliguric acute kidney injury following a diarrhoeal illness. She is thrombocytopenic and has microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia. What is the most likely cause of her renal impairment?
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Hydronephrosis grading and renographic evaluation
questions.questionText:
A 4-week-old boy with antenatally detected right hydronephrosis (AP diameter 14 mm at 32 weeks) undergoes postnatal ultrasound which shows a right renal pelvis diameter of 18 mm, calyceal dilatation, and parenchymal thickness 3.5 mm. The left kidney is normal.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the SFU and ESPU grading systems for hydronephrosis and assign the likely grade for this infant. [5 marks]
(b) [DIFFERENTIATE] between MAG3 and DTPA renography in terms of tracer, radiation dose, physiological basis, and clinical preference in infants. [6 marks]
(c) [INTERPRET] the potential diuretic renography curve patterns (normal, obstructive, dilated non-obstructive, equivocal) and explain the role of furosemide timing (F-15, F-0, F+20 protocols). [5 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the role of the Whitaker test in the modern era of paediatric urological imaging and when it remains indicated. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): SFU: Grade 1 — renal pelvis only; Grade 2 — pelvis + few calyces; Grade 3 — pelvis + all calyces dilated, normal parenchyma; Grade 4 — Grade 3 + parenchymal thinning. ESPU (UTD): combines AP diameter, calyceal, ureteric, parenchymal features. This child: AP 18 mm (>10 mm), calyceal dilatation, parenchymal thinning 3.5 mm (<4 mm) → SFU Grade 4/UTD P3. (2 marks for classification, 1 mark for assignment, 2 marks for justifying).

b) (6 marks): MAG3 (mercaptoacetyltriglycine): mainly tubular secretion (80%), higher extraction fraction → superior cortical imaging, lower radiation dose (0.02 mSv/MBq vs DTPA 0.05), preferred in infants. DTPA (diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid): glomerular filtration only, lower extraction, higher background, inferior in low GFR. MAG3 preferred in neonates and infants with immature renal function. (3 marks for MAG3, 2 marks for DTPA, 1 mark for comparison).

c) (5 marks): Curve patterns: 1) Normal — rapid rise, prompt washout after furosemide. 2) Obstructive — rising curve, no washout. 3) Dilated non-obstructive — rising then plateau, complete washout with diuretic. 4) Equivocal — incomplete washout. F-15 (furosemide 15 min before tracer) reduces false positives. F-20 (furosemide at 20 min after tracer) — standard Well-Tempered Renogram. F0 — furosemide with tracer. (2 marks for curve types, 2 marks for protocols, 1 mark for preference in infants).

d) (4 marks): Whitaker test: pressure-perfusion study under fluoroscopy. Measure renal pelvic pressure during antegrade perfusion (10 mL/min). Normal <12 cm H2O, obstructed >22. Indications: persistent diagnostic uncertainty after renography, suspected occult obstruction, equivocal diuretic renogram in high-grade hydronephrosis, post-operative assessment. Largely replaced by improved renography and MR urography but remains useful in selected complex cases. (2 marks for technique, 2 marks for indications).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The SFU system grades hydronephrosis from 1 to 4 based on pelvic and calyceal dilatation and parenchymal thickness: Grade 1 — pelvis only; Grade 2 — pelvis and a few calyces; Grade 3 — pelvis and all calyces dilated, normal parenchyma; Grade 4 — parenchymal thinning. The ESPU UTD classification integrates AP diameter, calyceal dilatation, ureteric dilation, and parenchymal thickness. This infant has AP 18 mm, calyceal dilatation, and parenchymal thinning to 3.5 mm — consistent with SFU Grade 4 or UTD P3.

b) MAG3 is a tubular agent (80% extracted in a single pass) providing superior image quality even at low GFR, with a lower radiation dose than DTPA. DTPA is filtered solely by glomeruli with lower extraction (20%), higher background activity, and higher radiation burden. In neonates with immature renal function and low GFR, DTPA images are poor due to high background. Therefore, MAG3 is the preferred renographic agent in this age group.

c) Diuretic renography curve patterns: (1) normal — rapid tracer uptake followed by prompt washout after furosemide; (2) obstructive — progressive tracer accumulation without washout; (3) dilated non-obstructive — tracer accumulation then plateau with complete washout after furosemide; (4) equivocal — delayed but partial washout. Furosemide timing: F-15 (furosemide 15 minutes before tracer) reduces false-positive obstruction in dilated systems; F+20 (Well-Tempered Renogram) gives standardised timing; F-0 (simultaneous) is less used. F-15 is preferred in infants with significant hydronephrosis.

d) The Whitaker test is a pressure-perfusion study measuring renal pelvic pressure during antegrade perfusion via nephrostomy at 10 mL/min. Normal pressure <12 cm H2O; obstruction >22 cm H2O. Its role has diminished with improvements in diuretic renography and MR urography, but it remains indicated when non-invasive studies are equivocal, particularly in complex cases of suspected occult obstruction, after failed pyeloplasty, or when there is discrepancy between ultrasound and renography.
discriminators: Detailed knowledge of both SFU and ESPU grading systems
Clear differentiation between MAG3 and DTPA with age-specific recommendations
Accurate description of diuretic renography protocols and curve interpretation
Appreciation of the Whitaker test's evolving role
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: DMSA, MCU and radiation safety in paediatric imaging
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old girl presents with her second episode of febrile UTI (temperature 39.5°C, rigors). She was treated 4 months ago for a first febrile UTI and an MCU at that time showed left grade III VUR. Her parents are concerned about further imaging and radiation exposure.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the indications, timing, and interpretation of a DMSA scan, and differentiate between acute pyelonephritis and established renal scarring. [6 marks]
(b) [CRITICALLY APPRAISE] the technique, radiation dose, and diagnostic value of MCU versus contrast-enhanced voiding urosonography (ceVUS) in the follow-up of known VUR. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principles in paediatric uroradiology and how they apply to this child's imaging journey. [4 marks]
(d) [OUTLINE] an evidence-based imaging algorithm for this child with recurrent febrile UTIs and known VUR, justifying each investigation. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (6 marks): DMSA (dimercaptosuccinic acid): binds renal tubules, images cortical parenchyma. Indications: assess split renal function, detect pyelonephritis or scarring. Timing: acute (within 2 weeks of febrile UTI) — photopenic areas (usually wedge-shaped, loss of cortical contour) indicate acute pyelonephritis; delayed (4-6 months later) — persistent photopenic defects with cortical thinning indicate scar. DMSA is gold standard for scarring. (2 marks for mechanism, 2 marks for timing, 2 marks for interpretation).

b) (6 marks): MCU: catheterise bladder, fill with contrast, intermittent fluoroscopy during voiding. Grades VUR I-V. Radiation: typically 0.1-0.5 mSv (varies with fluoroscopy time). ceVUS: contrast microbubbles, US detection, no ionising radiation, higher sensitivity for VUR (especially low-grade). Limitations of ceVUS: operator dependent, no urethral imaging in voiding phase (male), no standardised grading. MCU remains standard for initial workup and for male infants (to exclude PUV). ceVUS preferred in follow-up for girls. (2 marks for MCU, 2 marks for ceVUS, 2 marks for comparison).

c) (4 marks): ALARA principles: 1) Justification — every study must have clear clinical indication (avoid repeat studies unnecessarily). 2) Optimisation — use paediatric-specific protocols (lower fluoroscopy pulse rates, last-image-hold, grid removal, gonadal shielding). 3) Limitation — use lowest dose for diagnostic quality; consider non-ionising alternatives (ceVUS, MRI). In this child: MCU already done; consider ceVUS for follow-up to minimise cumulative dose. (1 mark per principle + application).

d) (4 marks): Evidence-based algorithm: 1) Renal ultrasound (baseline anatomy, assess for changes). 2) DMSA after this acute episode (to document new scarring). 3) Follow-up MCU or ceVUS after 12-24 months to reassess VUR (spontaneous resolution expected in ~50% of grade III). 4) If scar progression or breakthrough UTIs despite prophylaxis → multidisciplinary discussion ± endoscopic injection. (1 mark each step).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) DMSA is taken up by proximal renal tubules, providing direct cortical imaging. In acute pyelonephritis (within 2 weeks of infection), photopenic defects are present with preserved renal contour — these may be reversible. After 4-6 months, persistent defects with loss of cortical contour and volume represent established scarring. DMSA is the gold standard for detecting renal scars. Split renal function is also calculated. In this child, a DMSA after 4-6 months would determine whether the first UTI caused permanent scarring and establish a baseline for future comparison.

b) MCU involves bladder catheterisation, retrograde filling with iodinated contrast, and intermittent fluoroscopy during filling and voiding (including urethral imaging during voiding). Radiation dose is 0.1-0.5 mSv. ceVUS uses ultrasound contrast agents (microbubbles) and is radiation-free, with sensitivity >90% for VUR detection. However, ceVUS does not adequately image the male urethra during voiding (essential to exclude PUV) and has a non-standardised grading system. For follow-up in this girl with known VUR, ceVUS is preferred to reduce cumulative radiation.

c) ALARA principles in paediatric uroradiology require: justification — only perform imaging when it will change management (this child has a clear indication); optimisation — using paediatric protocols with low pulse-rate fluoroscopy (3-7.5 pps), last-image-hold instead of continuous imaging, gonadal shielding, and removing the anti-scatter grid; modality selection — choosing non-ionising alternatives (ceVUS, MRI) where possible. Cumulative radiation exposure from multiple MCUs is a genuine concern.

d) Recommended imaging algorithm: (1) Renal ultrasound to assess hydronephrosis and parenchymal thickness. (2) DMSA scan at 4-6 months post-UTI to evaluate for new scarring. (3) Follow-up MCU or ceVUS at 12-24 months — grade III VUR resolves spontaneously in ~50% of children. (4) If breakthrough UTIs occur despite antibiotic prophylaxis, or if new scarring is demonstrated, consider endoscopic injection (Deflux). Repeated DMSA should be reserved for cases with recurrent febrile UTIs or deteriorating renal function.
discriminators: Clear distinction between acute pyelonephritis and scarring on DMSA with correct timing
Balanced comparison of MCU vs ceVUS with specific paediatric considerations
Practical application of ALARA principles to a clinical case
Evidence-based imaging algorithm with procedural justification
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: DMSA indications and interpretation
questions.questionText:
(a) [STATE] the four main clinical indications for performing a DMSA renal scan in a child. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the difference between an acute pyelonephritis defect and a renal scar on DMSA, with reference to timing of imaging. [4 marks]
(c) [CALCULATE] the relative function of the left kidney if the right kidney counts are 180,000 and the left kidney counts are 120,000 on DMSA. [2 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): 1) Detection of renal scarring following febrile UTI. 2) Assessment of split (differential) renal function. 3) Diagnosis of acute pyelonephritis in select cases. 4) Evaluation of congenital anomalies (e.g., ectopic kidney, MCDK, horseshoe kidney). 1 mark each. Also accepted: follow-up of known scarring, assessment of function in obstructed system.

b) (4 marks): Acute pyelonephritis: photopenic defect on DMSA performed within 2 weeks of febrile UTI; renal contour preserved; defect may be reversible with treatment (up to 60% resolve). Scar: persistent photopenic defect on delayed DMSA (4-6 months post-UTI), cortical thinning and volume loss; irreversible. 2 marks for each with timing.

c) (2 marks): Total counts = 180,000 + 120,000 = 300,000. Left function = (120,000/300,000) × 100 = 40%. Normal split is 45-55%. 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for stating normality range.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) Indications for DMSA: detection of renal scarring after febrile UTI; assessment of differential renal function; diagnosis of acute pyelonephritis when imaging is needed; and evaluation of congenital renal anomalies.

b) An acute pyelonephritis defect appears as a photopenic area with preserved renal contour on DMSA performed within 2 weeks of infection — this may be reversible. A renal scar is a persistent photopenic defect with cortical thinning and volume loss on delayed DMSA at 4-6 months — this is permanent.

c) Left kidney relative function = 120,000/(180,000+120,000) × 100 = 120,000/300,000 × 100 = 40%. Normal differential function is 45-55%.
discriminators: Clear differentiation of acute vs chronic DMSA findings with correct timing
Practical split function calculation
Concise recall of clinical indications
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: MCU technique and radiation considerations
questions.questionText:
A 6-week-old boy with antenatal hydronephrosis (AP 12 mm) and a normal postnatal ultrasound is referred for an MCU to rule out VUR and posterior urethral valves.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the key technical steps of performing an MCU in a male infant. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] how fluoroscopy time and radiation exposure can be minimised during MCU in children. [3 marks]
(c) [DIFFERENTIATE] the radiographic signs of posterior urethral valves versus primary VUR on MCU. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): 1) Aseptic catheterisation (5 Fr feeding tube or 6 Fr paediatric Foley). 2) Empty bladder. 3) Contrast (iodinated, ~30%) infused by gravity at low pressure (height <70 cm above table). 4) Intermittent fluoroscopy: during filling (early filling image of bladder, detect VUR), during voiding (urethral imaging in oblique/lateral), post-void (residual volume). Must ensure urethral imaging during voiding to exclude PUV. 1 mark each.

b) (3 marks): Strategies: 1) Use pulsed fluoroscopy (3-7.5 pulses per second) rather than continuous. 2) Use last-image-hold technique (store last frame, avoid re-screening). 3) Collimate X-ray beam to area of interest. 4) Remove anti-scatter grid. 5) Use lowest acceptable frame rate. 6) Gonadal shielding. 7) Minimise screening time — aim for <30 seconds total. 1 mark each for 3.

c) (3 marks): PUV: dilated posterior urethra proximal to valve leaflet (membranous urethra), with abrupt calibre change; thickened trabeculated bladder; 'spinning top' urethra; bilateral VUR may be present. Primary VUR: contrast refluxes into ureter(s) during filling/voiding; grading I-V depends on degree of ureteral and pelvicalyceal filling; normal urethral calibre. Key differentiator: urethral appearance during voiding. (1.5 marks each).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) MCU technique: aseptic catheterisation with a 5 Fr feeding tube; bladder emptied; iodinated contrast instilled by gravity (height <70 cm above table); intermittent fluoroscopy during filling to detect VUR; voiding phase with oblique imaging of urethra to exclude PUV; post-void image for residual volume.

b) Radiation minimisation: pulsed fluoroscopy at 3-7.5 pps instead of continuous; last-image-hold to avoid repeat screening; tight collimation; removal of anti-scatter grid; gonadal shielding; total fluoroscopy time target <30 seconds.

c) Posterior urethral valves show a dilated posterior urethra with abrupt narrowing at the valve (membranous urethra level), thick-walled trabeculated bladder, and often bilateral VUR. Primary VUR shows contrast reflux into one or both ureters of variable grade with a normal-calibre urethra. The urethral appearance during voiding is the key differentiating feature.
discriminators: Detailed technical knowledge of MCU performance
Practical radiation safety strategies
Clear differentiation between PUV and primary VUR on MCU
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Imaging workup for recurrent UTI with hydronephrosis
questions.questionText:
An 18-month-old girl presents with her third febrile UTI. She had a normal renal ultrasound at 6 months after a first UTI. Current ultrasound shows right hydronephrosis (AP 12 mm, SFU grade 2) with a distal ureter visible. The left kidney is normal. She is febrile (39°C) with rigors.

(a) [JUSTIFY] your choice of imaging investigations in the acute and follow-up phases, with specific reference to NICE CG54 guidelines. [6 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] the diagnostic yield and clinical utility of MAG3 renography versus MCU in this specific case. [5 marks]
(c) [INTERPRET] the potential DMSA findings if performed in the acute phase versus 6 months later, and how this affects clinical decision-making. [5 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the role of MR urography in the evaluation of complex paediatric hydronephrosis and its advantages and limitations compared to conventional imaging. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (6 marks): Acute phase: renal USS (already shows hydronephrosis) + DMSA (if available, to document acute pyelonephritis). MCU is indicated in children <2 years with atypical/complex UTI and abnormal USS (NICE). Follow-up: DMSA at 4-6 months to assess scarring; MCU if not done acutely; MAG3 if hydronephrosis persists to assess obstruction. NICE suggests USS within 6 weeks and DMSA at 4-6 months for recurrent febrile UTI. 2 marks for acute, 2 marks for follow-up, 2 marks for NICE reference.

b) (5 marks): MAG3: assesses split renal function and drainage — identifies obstruction (UPJO, UVJO). MCU: detects VUR — this child has distal ureter visible on USS suggesting possible VUR or UVJO. MAG3 cannot diagnose VUR; MCU cannot diagnose obstruction. Both are complementary. Given a visible distal ureter + hydronephrosis, both are indicated. MCU should be done first if VUR suspected (higher prevalence in girls <2 years). (2 marks for each modality, 1 mark for complementary role).

c) (5 marks): Acute DMSA: photopenic defects (wedge-shaped, preserved contour) indicating acute pyelonephritis — these are partially reversible. Delayed DMSA (6 months): persistent photopenic defects with cortical thinning = permanent scarring. Acute DMSA supports diagnosis of pyelonephritis and justifies aggressive management; delayed DMSA provides definitive scar assessment and prognostic information for long-term renal outcome. (2 marks for acute, 2 marks for delayed, 1 mark for clinical impact).

d) (4 marks): MR urography: combines anatomy (static T2) and function (dynamic contrast-enhanced). Advantages: no ionising radiation, excellent anatomical detail (especially complex anomalies), simultaneous functional information. Limitations: requires sedation/anaesthesia in young children, long acquisition time, expensive, limited availability, less validated for drainage assessment than MAG3. Role: problem-solving in complex cases (ectopic ureters, complex duplex systems, equivocal renography). 2 marks for advantages, 2 marks for limitations.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) According to NICE CG54 guidelines, children under 2 years with recurrent or atypical UTI should undergo renal USS and a DMSA scan. MCU is advised if USS is abnormal. In this child: acute USS shows new hydronephrosis with visible distal ureter, so MCU is indicated to assess for VUR. A DMSA should be performed at 4-6 months to evaluate for scarring. If the MCU shows no VUR, a MAG3 renogram would be appropriate to rule out obstruction at the ureterovesical junction.

b) MAG3 provides dynamic functional assessment: split renal function, drainage half-time, and can distinguish obstructed from dilated non-obstructed systems. MCU directly visualises the lower urinary tract and detects VUR. In this child with distal ureter visible on USS, both VUR and UVJO could explain the findings. MCU is more appropriate first, as VUR is more common and has different management (prophylaxis, endoscopic injection). MAG3 should follow if MCU is negative.

c) An acute DMSA (within 2 weeks) would show photopenic defects in the right kidney consistent with acute pyelonephritis — this confirms the renal parenchyma is involved and justifies aggressive treatment. A delayed DMSA at 6 months distinguishes reversible defects from permanent scars. If scarring is present, this child would require long-term follow-up for hypertension and renal function, with stronger consideration for VUR correction. Absence of scarring would be reassuring and allow less aggressive management.

d) MR urography provides high-resolution anatomical detail without ionising radiation, with functional information from contrast-enhanced sequences. Its advantages include superior soft tissue resolution for complex anomalies (duplex kidneys, ectopic ureteroceles) and simultaneous assessment of anatomy and function. Limitations include the need for sedation/general anaesthesia in young children, prolonged acquisition time, limited availability, and less validated semi-quantitative drainage assessment compared to MAG3. It is reserved for problem-solving in complex cases.
discriminators: NICE guideline-informed imaging algorithm
Clear understanding of complementary roles of MAG3 and MCU
Timing-dependent interpretation of DMSA findings
Balanced assessment of MR urography's role
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Diuretic renography curve interpretation

Options:
A. Normal — rapid uptake, prompt washout after diuretic
B. Obstructed — progressive accumulation, no washout
C. Dilated non-obstructed — plateau, complete washout with diuretic
D. Equivocal — delayed but partial washout
E. Poor renal function — flat trace, no response to diuretic
F. Extravasation — tracer outside collecting system
G. Acute pyelonephritis — focal photopenia on parenchymal phase
H. Artefact due to patient movement — tracing irregular

Scenarios:
1. A 6-month-old with SFU grade 3 hydronephrosis. MAG3 renogram with F-15 protocol shows tracer accumulation in the right renal pelvis with the curve rising progressively over 30 minutes. After furosemide there is no significant washout. The split function is 45% on the right. What is the most likely curve type?
2. A 4-year-old with previous left pyeloplasty. On follow-up MAG3, the left kidney shows good uptake with a rising curve that reaches a plateau at 15 minutes. After furosemide at 20 minutes, there is rapid and nearly complete clearance within 10 minutes. What is the most likely curve type?
3. A 3-week-old neonate with antenatal bilateral hydronephrosis. MAG3 shows bilateral poor tracer uptake with flat time-activity curves and no response to furosemide. Serum creatinine is 100 micromol/L. What is the most likely curve type?
4. A 10-year-old with intermittent flank pain. MAG3 shows normal uptake on the right. The left kidney shows an initial rise, then a plateau; after furosemide the curve falls by only 40% over 20 minutes. What is the most likely curve type?
5. A 2-year-old with antenatal right hydronephrosis (AP 8 mm). MAG3 shows rapid tracer uptake in both kidneys with prompt washout from the pelvis after furosemide. What is the most likely curve type?
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Antenatal hydronephrosis: grading, counselling and postnatal protocol
questions.questionText:
A 28-year-old primigravida is referred at 32 weeks gestation following a routine anomaly scan showing fetal right renal pelvis AP diameter of 15 mm with calyceal dilatation. The left kidney is normal (AP 4 mm). The bladder appears normal and liquor volume is adequate.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the antenatal hydronephrosis grading systems (SFU and UTD) and assign the appropriate grade for this fetus. [5 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the key counselling points you would discuss with the parents, including differential diagnosis, prognosis, and postnatal implications. [6 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] the NICE and ESPU-recommended postnatal surveillance protocol for this infant, including timing of imaging and criteria for surgical referral. [5 marks]
(d) the evidence for and against routine antibiotic prophylaxis in infants with antenatal hydronephrosis awaiting postnatal investigation. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): SFU: Grade 1 — pelvis only; 2 — + calyces; 3 — all calyces + normal parenchyma; 4 — + parenchymal thinning. UTD (urinary tract dilation): combines AP diameter (A1: 10-15mm, A2: >15mm), calyceal (C1: central, C2: peripheral), parenchymal (P1: normal, P2: thinning <4mm), ureteric (U0: normal, U1: dilated), bladder (B0: normal, B1: abnormal). This fetus: AP 15 mm, calyceal dilation (peripheral? → C2), normal parenchyma → UTD A2-C2-P0-U0-B0 or SFU Grade 3. (2 marks for each system, 1 mark for assignment).

b) (6 marks): Differential diagnosis: transient hydronephrosis, UPJO (most common), VUR, UVJO, megacalycosis, duplex system. Prognosis: majority resolve spontaneously (60-80% of mild-moderate). Risk of significant pathology <10% for moderate hydronephrosis. Postnatal: requires antibiotic prophylaxis until investigation; will need USS at day 5-7 and 4-6 weeks; possible MCU and MAG3. Rarely requires prenatal intervention. Reassure that liquor volume adequate and left kidney normal. (2 marks for differential, 2 marks for prognosis, 2 marks for postnatal planning).

c) (5 marks): NICE: USS within 5 days of birth (if bilateral/abnormal liquor/single kidney) or at 4-6 weeks (if isolated unilateral). Repeat USS at 4-6 weeks. If abnormal → MCU + MAG3. ESPU: USS at day 4-7 (after first 48h to avoid false negative from relative oliguria). If AP >10mm → MRI or MAG3 at 4-6 weeks. Prophylaxis until VUR excluded. Surgical referral: split renal function <40%, increasing hydronephrosis, symptomatic, obstructive renogram curve. (2 marks for NICE, 2 marks for ESPU, 1 mark for surgical criteria).

d) (4 marks): Evidence: RIVUR and PREDICT trials show modest reduction in febrile UTI with prophylaxis in VUR patients, but no benefit in non-VUR. In antenatal hydronephrosis awaiting investigation, prophylaxis is controversial. ESPU recommends prophylaxis for moderate-severe hydronephrosis until VUR excluded. No high-level evidence supports universal prophylaxis. Risks: bacterial resistance, side effects, cost. Pragmatic approach: consider in high-grade (SFU 3-4) or bilateral cases. (2 marks for evidence, 1 mark for recommendations, 1 mark for balanced discussion).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The SFU system grades hydronephrosis 1-4 based on pelvic and calyceal dilatation with parenchymal thinning indicating grade 4. The UTD system assigns risk based on AP diameter (A1/A2), calyceal involvement (C1/C2), parenchymal thickness (P1/P2), ureter (U0/U1), and bladder (B0/B1). This fetus has AP 15 mm (>15 mm = A2), calyceal dilatation, normal parenchyma, no ureteric dilation, normal bladder — UTD P3 A2-C2-P0-U0-B0, corresponding to SFU grade 3.

b) Counselling should include: the finding is moderate-severe hydronephrosis. The most likely cause is ureteropelvic junction obstruction (~40%), followed by transient hydronephrosis or VUR. Prognosis is generally good — most cases resolve or stabilise postnatally. The left kidney is normal and liquor volume is adequate, both favourable prognostic indicators. Postnatally, the baby will need antibiotic prophylaxis, a renal ultrasound at day 5-7 and 4-6 weeks, and possibly an MCU and MAG3 renogram. Surgical intervention is only needed in ~20% of cases. Reassurance that the risk of long-term renal impairment is low with appropriate follow-up.

c) NICE guidelines recommend postnatal USS within 5 days for bilateral cases, single kidney, or abnormal liquor; otherwise at 4-6 weeks. Repeat USS at 4-6 weeks. If abnormal, proceed to MCU and MAG3. ESPU recommends USS at day 4-7 (after the initial 48-hour oliguric period). If AP >10 mm, proceed to functional imaging (MAG3) at 4-6 weeks. Prophylaxis is recommended until VUR is excluded. Surgical referral is indicated for split renal function <40%, worsening hydronephrosis, breakthrough UTIs, or obstructive drainage curve on renography.

d) The evidence for prophylaxis is extrapolated from VUR studies. RIVUR trial showed prophylaxis reduced febrile UTI recurrence in VUR patients but at cost of increased resistant organisms. PREDICT trial showed no benefit in preventing renal scarring. For antenatal hydronephrosis, ESPU recommends prophylaxis for SFU grade 3-4 until evaluation is complete, while NICE does not mandate it. A pragmatic approach is to use prophylaxis in high-grade or bilateral hydronephrosis and withhold in mild cases, with careful parental counselling about UTI symptoms.
discriminators: Comprehensive knowledge of both SFU and UTD grading applied to a specific case
Structured antenatal counselling with appropriate reassurance
Detailed knowledge of NICE vs ESPU postnatal protocols
Nuanced discussion of prophylactic antibiotic evidence
commonErrors: b) (6 marks): Differential diagnosis: transient hydronephrosis, UPJO (most common), VUR, UVJO, megacalycosis, duplex system. Prognosis: majority resolve spontaneously (60-80% of mild-moderate). Risk of significant pathology <10% for moderate hydronephrosis. Postnatal: requires antibiotic prophylaxis until investigation; will need USS at day 5-7 and 4-6 weeks; possible MCU and MAG3. Rarely requires prenatal intervention. Reassure that liquor volume adequate and left kidney normal. (2 marks for differential, 2 marks for prognosis, 2 marks for postnatal planning).

questions.title: Congenital lower urinary tract obstruction and vesicoamniotic shunting
questions.questionText:
A 20-week anomaly scan in a male fetus shows bilateral severe hydronephrosis (AP 20 mm on each side), a thickened bladder with keyhole sign, and oligohydramnios. You are asked to advise on management options including vesicoamniotic shunting.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the sonographic features of congenital lower urinary tract obstruction and differentiate posterior urethral valves from urethral atresia and prune belly syndrome. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the selection criteria for fetal intervention, including the role of serial fetal urine analysis in predicting postnatal renal function. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the evidence for vesicoamniotic shunting in LUTO, including the PLUTO trial findings and ongoing controversies. [5 marks]
(d) [OUTLINE] the multidisciplinary postnatal management plan for a male infant with PUV and prenatally detected severe obstructive uropathy. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): LUTO features: bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, thick-walled bladder, dilated posterior urethra (keyhole sign), oligohydramnios. PUV (most common): eccentric keyhole, variable obstruction, male sex. Urethral atresia: severe, no flow, early onset oligohydramnios, always fatal if untreated. Prune belly: deficient abdominal wall musculature, dilated non-obstructed ureters, cryptorchidism; bladder not thick-walled; not truly obstructed. 2 marks for LUTO features, 1 mark each for differentiation.

b) (6 marks): Fetal intervention considered if: 1) Isolated LUTO (no major extra-renal anomalies). 2) Normal karyotype. 3) Good prognostic fetal urine biochemistry: sodium <100, chloride <90, osmolarity <200, beta-2-microglobulin <6 (repeat sampling after vesicocentesis). Poor values → irreversible renal damage. Fetal bladder drainage for 48-72 hours can predict postnatal renal function. (2 marks for selection criteria, 2 marks for urine biochemistry, 2 marks for interpretation).

c) (5 marks): PLUTO RCT: vesicoamniotic shunting vs conservative. Trend toward improved perinatal survival with shunting but not statistically significant (primary outcome: survival to 2 years). High crossover rate. Shunted fetuses had better survival but survivors had high rate of CKD. Complications: shunt blockage/dislodgement, premature rupture of membranes, chorioamnionitis, procedure-related fetal loss. Controversy: shunting improves survival but does not improve long-term renal function — may convert perinatal death to childhood ESKD. (2 marks for PLUTO, 2 marks for complications, 1 mark for controversy).

d) (4 marks): Immediate: respiratory support (pulmonary hypoplasia), catheterisation (5 Fr feeding tube) to decompress bladder, renal USS, creatinine monitoring, antibiotic prophylaxis, first 72-hour urine output assessment. Within week 1: MCU for definitive diagnosis of PUV, valve ablation. Long-term: monitor renal function, VUDS for valve bladder, manage incontinence, reflux, UTIs.
