Continuous follow-up for CKD progression, hypertension, growth. Transition to adult services. (1 mark each for 4 stages).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) LUTO is characterised by bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, thick-walled bladder, dilated posterior urethra (keyhole sign), and oligohydramnios. Posterior urethral valves show the classic keyhole sign with a distended bladder and variable hydronephrosis — the obstruction is intermittent and incomplete. Urethral atresia is complete obstruction presenting very early (16 weeks) with severe oligohydramnios and is almost universally fatal. Prune belly syndrome has deficient abdominal wall, dilated tortuous ureters (not truly obstructed), cryptorchidism, and the bladder is large but not typically thick-walled — the keyhole sign is absent.

b) Selection for fetal intervention includes: isolated LUTO (no other major anomalies), normal karyotype, good fetal urine biochemistry. Fetal urine is obtained by serial vesicocenteses (2-3 samplings 48-72 hours apart) after bladder drainage. Favourable predictors: sodium <100 mmol/L, chloride <90 mmol/L, osmolarity <200 mOsm/L, beta-2-microglobulin <6 mg/L. These values indicate preserved fetal renal function with the potential for recovery after decompression. Poor biochemistry indicates established renal dysplasia and intervention is unlikely to benefit.

c) The PLUTO (Percutaneous shunting for Lower Urinary Tract Obstruction) trial remains the only RCT. It showed a trend toward improved perinatal survival with shunting (relative risk 1.84, 95% CI 0.91-3.70, not statistically significant due to small numbers and high crossover). However, survivors in the shunt group had high rates of CKD — shunting converts fetal death to childhood CKD rather than restoring normal renal function. Complications include shunt migration, blockage, preterm rupture of membranes, and procedure-related loss. The controversy centres on whether improved survival without improved renal function is a meaningful outcome.

d) Postnatal management: at delivery, immediate respiratory assessment for pulmonary hypoplasia; catheterisation with a 5 Fr feeding tube to decompress the bladder; renal USS and serum creatinine (interpreted with maternal levels in first 48 hours); prophylactic antibiotics. MCU within the first week confirms PUV and allows valve ablation. Following ablation, close monitoring of voiding, serum creatinine nadir, and UTIs. Long-term surveillance for valve bladder syndrome, CKD, hypertension, and growth. Consider urodynamics at 1 year. Lifelong nephro-urological follow-up.
discriminators: Detailed differentiation of LUTO aetiologies on prenatal imaging
In-depth knowledge of fetal urine biochemistry interpretation
Critical appraisal of PLUTO trial evidence with balanced discussion
Comprehensive multidisciplinary postnatal plan
commonErrors: a) (5 marks): LUTO features: bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, thick-walled bladder, dilated posterior urethra (keyhole sign), oligohydramnios. PUV (most common): eccentric keyhole, variable obstruction, male sex. Urethral atresia: severe, no flow, early onset oligohydramnios, always fatal if untreated. Prune belly: deficient abdominal wall musculature, dilated non-obstructed ureters, cryptorchidism; bladder not thick-walled; not truly obstructed. 2 marks for LUTO features, 1 mark each for differentiation.

questions.title: Antenatal hydronephrosis grading on ultrasound
questions.questionText:
(a) [DEFINE] the AP diameter thresholds for mild, moderate, and severe antenatal hydronephrosis in the second and third trimesters. [3 marks]
(b) [STATE] the UTD classification system parameters (A, C, P, U, B) and what each represents. [5 marks]
(c) [LIST] three features on antenatal USS that would classify hydronephrosis as 'high risk'. [2 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (3 marks): Second trimester (<28 weeks): mild 4-7 mm, moderate 7-10 mm, severe >10 mm. Third trimester (≥28 weeks): mild 7-9 mm, moderate 9-15 mm, severe >15 mm. 1 mark per severity.

b) (5 marks): A = Anterior-posterior renal pelvis diameter (A1: 10-15mm, A2: >15mm). C = Calyceal dilation (C0: none, C1: central calyces, C2: peripheral calyces). P = Parenchymal thickness (P0: normal, P1: thin, P2: <4mm). U = Ureter dilation (U0: normal, U1: dilated). B = Bladder (B0: normal, B1: abnormal). 1 mark each.

c) (2 marks): High-risk features: 1) Peripheral calyceal dilation (C2). 2) Parenchymal thinning (<4 mm). 3) Ureteric dilation (U1). 4) Abnormal bladder (B1, e.g., thick-walled, keyhole sign). 5) Oligohydramnios. 6) Bilateral involvement. Any 3 = 2 marks.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) Second trimester: mild 4-7 mm, moderate 7-10 mm, severe >10 mm. Third trimester: mild 7-9 mm, moderate 9-15 mm, severe >15 mm.

b) UTD parameters: A = AP diameter (A1 10-15 mm, A2 >15 mm); C = calyceal dilation (C0 none, C1 central, C2 peripheral); P = parenchymal thickness (P0 normal, P1 thin, P2 <4 mm); U = ureter (U0 normal, U1 dilated); B = bladder (B0 normal, B1 abnormal).

c) High-risk features include peripheral calyceal dilation (C2), parenchymal thinning <4 mm, and ureteric dilation (U1). Other features: abnormal bladder, oligohydramnios, bilateral involvement.
discriminators: Accurate trimester-specific AP diameter thresholds
Complete recall of all UTD classification components
Recognition of high-risk sonographic features
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: NICE/ESPU postnatal surveillance protocol
questions.questionText:
A term male infant is born with prenatally detected right SFU grade 3 hydronephrosis (AP 14 mm at 36 weeks). The left kidney is normal. Liquor volume was normal.

(a) [OUTLINE] the NICE-recommended postnatal imaging protocol for this infant, including timing of ultrasound and indications for further imaging. [4 marks]
(b) [STATE] the ESPU-recommended differences in timing of initial postnatal USS compared to NICE. [2 marks]
(c) [LIST] the indications for surgical intervention in an infant with persistent prenatal hydronephrosis. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): NICE: USS within 5 days if bilateral, solitary kidney, oligohydramnios; else 4-6 weeks after birth. This infant (unilateral, normal liquor) → USS at 4-6 weeks. Repeat USS at 4-6 weeks later. If abnormal → MCU + MAG3. Prophylaxis not mandated. 2 marks for timing, 1 mark for repeat, 1 mark for triggers for further imaging.

b) (2 marks): ESPU recommends initial USS at day 4-7 of life (not first 48 hours because of transient neonatal oliguria causing false-negative). NICE recommends within 5 days for high-risk or 4-6 weeks for low-risk. ESPU also recommends starting antibiotic prophylaxis until evaluation complete if hydronephrosis is moderate-severe. 1 mark for timing, 1 mark for rationale.

c) (4 marks): Indications: 1) Split renal function <40% on MAG3. 2) Worsening hydronephrosis on serial USS. 3) Obstructive drainage curve on renogram (T1/2 >20 min after furosemide). 4) Breakthrough febrile UTI on prophylaxis. 5) Symptomatic (pain, vomiting). 1 mark each. Also acceptable: bilateral high-grade obstruction, deteriorating function on follow-up, parental preference.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) For this infant with unilateral hydronephrosis and normal liquor, NICE recommends initial USS at 4-6 weeks of life (not earlier), with a repeat USS at 4-6 weeks after that. If the USS shows persistent or worsening hydronephrosis, further imaging with MCU and MAG3 renogram is indicated.

b) ESPU recommends the first postnatal USS at day 4-7 of life, specifically avoiding the first 48 hours when transient neonatal oliguria may produce a false-negative result. ESPU also recommends prophylactic antibiotics for moderate-severe hydronephrosis until the evaluation is complete.

c) Surgical intervention is indicated for: split renal function <40% on the affected side; worsening hydronephrosis on serial ultrasound; an obstructive drainage curve on MAG3 renogram (half-time >20 minutes after furosemide); or breakthrough febrile UTIs despite antibiotic prophylaxis.
discriminators: Clear distinction between NICE and ESPU protocols
Understanding of the physiological rationale for timing of USS
Concise list of surgical indications
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Counselling for antenatally detected duplex system with ureterocele
questions.questionText:
A 30-year-old woman is referred at 24 weeks because the anomaly scan shows a right-sided duplex kidney with a dilated upper pole moiety and a cystic structure in the bladder consistent with a ureterocele. The left kidney is normal. Liquor volume is normal. She asks what this means for her baby.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the embryological basis of a duplex system with an upper pole ureterocele, including the Meyer-Weigart law. [5 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the potential clinical consequences for the baby after birth (UTI, obstruction, incontinence). [5 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the postnatal imaging pathway and key decision points for management of an obstructed upper pole moiety with ureterocele. [5 marks]
(d) [PROPOSE] the surgical management options (endoscopic puncture, upper pole nephrectomy, ureteric reimplantation) and factors influencing the choice. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): Duplex kidney arises when two ureteric buds arise from the mesonephric duct (or one bud bifurcates early). The upper pole ureter inserts ectopically (Meyer-Weigart law: upper pole ureter inserts medially and caudally in the bladder or ectopic). Ureterocele forms at the stenotic upper pole ureteric orifice. The lower pole ureter inserts laterally and cranially → associated with VUR. (2 marks for embryology, 2 marks for law, 1 mark for ureterocele formation).

b) (5 marks): Upper moiety: obstruction → hydroureteronephrosis, possible loss of function. Infection: recurrent UTIs, pyelonephritis in the obstructed moiety. Incontinence: if ureter inserts outside bladder (ectopic, e.g., vagina) → continuous dribbling incontinence (presentation after toilet training). VUR into lower pole may cause reflux nephropathy. (2 marks for obstruction, 1 mark each for infection, incontinence, VUR).

c) (5 marks): Imaging: postnatal USS at day 4-7 (delayed to avoid false negative); MCU for VUR assessment + to assess ureterocele; MAG3 (or DMSA for function) to assess upper pole function; MR urography for complex anatomy. Decision points: function of upper pole (>10% → reconstructive options; <10% → upper pole nephrectomy); presence of VUR (lower pole or contralateral); ureterocele type (intravesical vs ectopic); UTI breakthrough. (2 marks for imaging, 3 marks for decision points).

d) (5 marks): Options: 1) Endoscopic puncture of ureterocele — minimally invasive, risk of de novo VUR (10-50%). 2) Upper pole nephrectomy — if minimal function, definitive. 3) Ureteric reimplantation + ureterocele excision — definitive reconstruction, more invasive. 4) Ureterocele excision + common sheath reimplantation. Factors: upper pole function; ureterocele type (intravesical better outcomes with puncture); presence/grade of VUR; age; surgeon preference. (2 marks for listing options, 3 marks for factors).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) A duplex kidney results from two separate ureteric buds arising from the mesonephric duct. According to the Meyer-Weigart law, the upper pole ureter inserts medially and caudally (Wolffian duct origin), often ectopically, and is prone to stenosis — leading to obstruction and ureterocele formation. The lower pole ureter inserts laterally and cranially with a short intramural tunnel, predisposing to VUR.

b) After birth, potential consequences include: upper moiety obstruction causing hydroureteronephrosis and potential loss of renal function; recurrent UTIs, especially if obstruction causes urinary stasis; pyelonephritis in the obstructed upper pole; continuous dribbling incontinence if the ureter inserts ectopically (below the external sphincter); and VUR into the lower pole causing reflux nephropathy. Fortunately, with normal liquor volume, the contralateral kidney and overall renal prognosis are likely good.

c) Postnatal imaging: USS at day 4-7 (not first 48 hours due to neonatal oliguria); MCU to assess for VUR and visualise the ureterocele; MAG3 renogram or DMSA at 4-6 weeks to assess upper pole function. MR urography may help define complex anatomy. Key decision points: function of the upper pole (>10% favours reconstruction, <10% favours upper pole nephrectomy); presence and grade of VUR; ureterocele type (intravesical vs ectopic); and UTI occurrence.

d) Surgical options: endoscopic puncture of the ureterocele is first-line for intravesical ureteroceles — simple, effective, but risks de novo VUR (10-50%). Upper pole nephrectomy is indicated if the moiety is non-functioning. Ureterocele excision with common sheath reimplantation is definitive but more invasive. Factors guiding choice: upper pole function is the most important determinant; intravesical ureteroceles respond well to puncture while ectopic ureteroceles are more complex; presence of high-grade VUR favours open reconstruction; and the risk of postoperative VUR must be considered.
discriminators: Embryological basis of duplex system applied to clinical case
Comprehensive antenatal counselling covering all potential outcomes
Structured postnatal imaging pathway with clear decision points
Nuanced surgical management with evidence-based option selection
commonErrors: d) (5 marks): Options: 1) Endoscopic puncture of ureterocele — minimally invasive, risk of de novo VUR (10-50%). 2) Upper pole nephrectomy — if minimal function, definitive. 3) Ureteric reimplantation + ureterocele excision — definitive reconstruction, more invasive. 4) Ureterocele excision + common sheath reimplantation. Factors: upper pole function; ureterocele type (intravesical better outcomes with puncture); presence/grade of VUR; age; surgeon preference. (2 marks for listing options, 3 marks for factors).

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Antenatally detected urological anomalies: counselling and management

Options:
A. Reassure, need no further follow-up
B. Antenatal USS monitoring every 4 weeks, postnatal USS at day 4-7
C. Offer termination of pregnancy
D. Fetal vesicoamniotic shunting after favourable urine analysis
E. Antenatal USS monthly, postnatal USS at 4-6 weeks, antibiotic prophylaxis after birth
F. Urgent delivery by caesarean section
G. Fetal blood sampling for karyotype
H. Vesicocentesis for urine biochemistry
I. MRI fetal urography
J. No change to routine antenatal care

Scenarios:
1. A fetus at 22 weeks with bilateral hydronephrosis (AP 12 mm each side), thick-walled bladder, keyhole sign, severe oligohydramnios. Karyotype is normal. What is the most appropriate next step before considering intervention?
2. A fetus at 32 weeks with isolated unilateral right hydronephrosis (AP 8 mm, no calyceal or ureteric dilation, normal liquor). What is the appropriate management?
3. A fetus at 34 weeks with unilateral SFU grade 3 hydronephrosis (AP 16 mm, peripheral calyceal dilation, normal parenchyma, normal liquor). What is the appropriate management?
4. A fetus at 18 weeks with anhydramnios, bilateral enlarged echogenic kidneys, and no visible bladder. The karyotype is normal. What is the most appropriate counselling advice?
5. A fetus at 28 weeks with bilateral severe hydronephrosis, thick-walled bladder, keyhole sign, and oligohydramnios. The karyotype is normal and fetal urine biochemistry is favourable (Na 85, Cl 80, osm 180). What is the appropriate management?
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: NICE CG54 guidelines and imaging algorithm
questions.questionText:
A 10-month-old girl presents with a temperature of 39.5°C, vomiting, and irritability. Urine dipstick is positive for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. She has had no previous UTIs. She is admitted for intravenous antibiotics.

(a) [CLASSIFY] this UTI as typical, atypical, or recurrent according to NICE CG54 criteria, with justification. [4 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the NICE-recommended imaging pathway for a child of this age, including the rationale for each investigation. [6 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] the Royal College of Physicians (RCPCH) fever in under-5s guideline modifications to the NICE UTI pathway. [5 marks]
(d) the evidence base underpinning the NICE guidelines, including the limitations and controversies surrounding the imaging recommendations for first febrile UTI. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): NICE CG54 classification: Typical UTI — fever >38°C, vomiting, no atypical features. Atypical — seriously ill, poor urine flow, abdo/bladder mass, raised creatinine, septicaemia, failure to respond to Abx within 48h, non-E. coli organism. Recurrent — ≥2 episodes upper UTI, ≥1 episode upper + ≥1 lower, ≥3 episodes lower. This child: first episode, febrile, no atypical features → typical UTI. (2 marks for classification, 2 marks for justification). Common error: labelling first febrile UTI as atypical without checking criteria.

b) (6 marks): For children <6 months with typical UTI: USS during acute infection (to identify structural anomalies). NICE also recommends: if atypical — MCU and DMSA; if recurrent — MCU and DMSA. For typical first UTI in children <6 months: USS only. For 6 months-3 years: USS within 6 weeks. If atypical/recurrent → DMSA at 4-6 months + MCU. Rationale: USS screens for anatomical abnormalities; DMSA detects scarring; MCU detects VUR but only when management would change. (2 marks for each imaging modality with rationale).

c) (5 marks): RCPCH (NICE NG143) modified the approach: urine testing prioritised in high-risk groups; use of dipstick for initial screening; culture for those meeting criteria. Key change: imaging recommendations simplified — for first UTI under 6 months, USS within 6 weeks (not acutely unless atypical). Reduced emphasis on MCU for first febrile UTI with normal USS. Emphasis on clinical assessment rather than routine imaging. (2 marks for testing changes, 2 marks for imaging changes, 1 mark for clinical emphasis).

d) (5 marks): Evidence: based on systematic reviews showing low incidence of significant renal scarring after first febrile UTI (~5-10%) and spontaneous resolution of low-grade VUR. Controversies: 1) NICE seen as too liberal in some areas (withholding MCU) — Scandinavian data show higher scarring rates. 2) DMSA is gold standard but not always available. 3) Ultrasound misses significant VUR. 4) Lack of high-quality RCT evidence for imaging-based management improving outcomes. 5) Top-down vs bottom-up approach debate. (2 marks for evidence, 3 marks for controversies).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) This is a typical first febrile UTI according to NICE CG54. She has fever >38°C and vomiting consistent with acute pyelonephritis. There are no atypical features (no poor urine flow, no abdominal/bladder mass, no raised creatinine, no septicaemia, no failure to respond to antibiotics within 48 hours, and the organism is likely E. coli). This is her first episode.

b) NICE CG54 recommends for a child aged 10 months (<3 years) with a typical first UTI: renal ultrasound within 6 weeks of the infection. If ultrasound is normal, no further imaging is required. MCU and DMSA are reserved for recurrent or atypical UTIs, or children under 6 months. The rationale: USS identifies structural abnormalities requiring intervention; DMSA detects renal scarring; MCU detects VUR, but the evidence does not support routine MCU after first typical UTI because the risk of significant VUR is low and most low-grade VUR resolves spontaneously without increased risk of scarring.

c) The RCPCH fever in under-5s guideline (NICE NG143, updated) refines UTI diagnosis: urine dipstick is the initial screening test; culture is required only if dipstick is positive or the child is high-risk. For imaging, USS should be performed within 6 weeks (not acutely) for first UTI in children under 6 months, and is not routinely recommended for children over 6 months with a first typical UTI unless there are atypical features. The emphasis shifted from mandatory imaging to clinical judgement.

d) NICE CG54 was based on systematic evidence review showing low rates of significant pathology after first febrile UTI. Key evidence: the risk of renal scarring after first febrile UTI is 5-15% with prompt treatment; most VUR (especially grades I-III) resolves spontaneously and the benefit of detection is unclear; prophylaxis does not reduce scarring (RIVUR). Controversies include: Scandinavian studies showing higher scarring rates, suggesting NICE may miss some children with high-grade VUR; ultrasound has poor sensitivity for VUR detection; the lack of RCT evidence showing that imaging-based management improves long-term renal outcomes. The debate between 'top-down' (DMSA-first) and 'bottom-up' (USS-first) approaches continues.
discriminators: Accurate classification of UTI according to NICE criteria with justifications
Detailed knowledge of age-specific imaging pathways
Awareness of NICE/RCPCH guideline evolution
Critical appraisal of evidence base including international controversies
commonErrors: a) (4 marks): NICE CG54 classification: Typical UTI — fever >38°C, vomiting, no atypical features. Atypical — seriously ill, poor urine flow, abdo/bladder mass, raised creatinine, septicaemia, failure to respond to Abx within 48h, non-E. coli organism. Recurrent — ≥2 episodes upper UTI, ≥1 episode upper + ≥1 lower, ≥3 episodes lower. This child: first episode, febrile, no atypical features → typical UTI. (2 marks for classification, 2 marks for justification). Common error: labelling first febrile UTI as atypical without checking criteria.

questions.title: Antibiotic prophylaxis evidence: the RIVUR trial
questions.questionText:
A 14-month-old girl is found to have grade III left VUR after her second febrile UTI. The DMSA shows a small scar in the upper pole of the left kidney. The parents ask whether antibiotic prophylaxis would prevent further infections and scarring.

(a) [CRITICALLY EVALUATE] the RIVUR trial design, primary outcomes, and key findings regarding the efficacy of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis in preventing recurrent UTI and renal scarring. [7 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] the RIVUR trial results with those of the PREDICT trial and the Swedish Reflux Study, highlighting differences in population, intervention, and conclusions. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the issue of antimicrobial resistance as a consequence of prophylaxis and its clinical implications. [4 marks]
(d) [RECOMMEND] a management plan for this child, justifying whether or not you would prescribe prophylaxis. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (7 marks): RIVUR: multicentre RCT (n=607), children 2-71 months with grade I-IV VUR after first/second UTI. Randomised to TMP-SMX or placebo. Primary outcome: febrile UTI recurrence. Secondary: scarring (DMSA at 2 years). Results: 39% reduction in febrile UTI recurrence (13% vs 24%, p<0.001). No difference in renal scarring (11% vs 10%, NS) — underpowered for scarring. More resistant organisms in prophylaxis group. (2 marks for design, 2 marks for primary outcome, 2 marks for secondary outcome, 1 mark for resistance data).

b) (6 marks): PREDICT (2018): n=292, children 1 month-3 years with grade I-III VUR after first febrile UTI. Prophylaxis vs no prophylaxis. Outcome: recurrence of febrile UTI and renal scarring. Result: no significant difference in UTI recurrence (OR 0.75, NS) or scarring (OR 0.83, NS). Key differences: younger population, lower-grade VUR (I-III vs I-IV), co-amoxiclav used. Swedish Reflux Study (2010): compared prophylaxis, endoscopic treatment, and surveillance in grade III-IV VUR. Found prophylaxis reduced UTI in girls but not boys; no difference in scarring. (2 marks per study).

c) (4 marks): RIVUR: 63% of breakthrough UTIs in prophylaxis group had TMP-SMX-resistant E. coli vs 19% in placebo (p<0.001). PREDICT: similar resistance patterns. Clinical implications: prophylaxis selects for resistant organisms; breakthrough infections are harder to treat (need broader-spectrum Abx, IV therapy); community resistance patterns affected; alternative prophylactic agents limited (nitrofurantoin avoided in <3 months due to concerns). (2 marks for data, 2 marks for implications).

d) (3 marks): This child: female, grade III VUR, existing scar (at risk). Consensus: prophylaxis reduces febrile UTI recurrence in girls with grade III-IV VUR (RIVUR). Given existing scar, preventing further pyelonephritis is important. Recommend TMP-SMX prophylaxis (or nitrofurantoin if <3 months). Discuss: risk of resistance, breakthrough infections, 2-year course then reassess. Alternative: endoscopic injection if parents prefer. (1 mark for recommendation, 1 mark for justification, 1 mark for alternative).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The RIVUR (Randomized Intervention for Children with Vesicoureteral Reflux) trial was a multicentre double-blind RCT of 607 children aged 2-71 months with grades I-IV VUR after a first or second UTI. Participants were randomised to TMP-SMX prophylaxis or placebo. The primary outcome was febrile UTI recurrence, which was significantly reduced with prophylaxis (13% vs 24%, hazard ratio 0.61, p<0.001). The secondary outcome, renal scarring on DMSA at 2 years, showed no significant difference (11% vs 10%, p=NS), though the study was underpowered for this endpoint. Importantly, breakthrough UTIs in the prophylaxis group were more likely to be caused by antibiotic-resistant organisms (63% vs 19%, p<0.001).

b) The PREDICT trial (2018) enrolled 292 children aged 1 month to 3 years with grades I-III VUR after their first febrile UTI and found no significant reduction in UTI recurrence (OR 0.75, 95% CI 0.38-1.49) or renal scarring with co-amoxiclav prophylaxis. Differences from RIVUR: younger age, lower VUR grade, different antibiotic, and all children had a DMSA-proven first pyelonephritis. The Swedish Reflux Study (2010) included children with grades III-IV VUR and compared prophylaxis, endoscopic treatment, and surveillance. It found that prophylaxis reduced UTI recurrence in girls but not boys, and there was no difference in new renal scarring among the three arms.

c) Prophylaxis significantly increases antimicrobial resistance. In RIVUR, 63% of breakthrough UTIs in the prophylaxis group were TMP-SMX-resistant, compared to 19% in the placebo group. PREDICT confirmed similar findings with co-amoxiclav. Clinical implications: breakthrough infections require broader-spectrum intravenous antibiotics, hospitalisation, and have higher morbidity; selection of resistant organisms within the gut flora and community; limited alternative prophylactic agents (nitrofurantoin is effective but contraindicated under 3 months, cephalexin has poor Gram-negative coverage, ciprofloxacin avoided in children).

d) Given this child is female with grade III VUR and an existing renal scar, I would recommend TMP-SMX prophylaxis at 2 mg/kg nocte. The evidence from RIVUR supports a reduction in febrile UTI recurrence in this subgroup. The existing scar identifies her as at higher risk for progressive renal damage from further pyelonephritis. I would counsel the parents on the risk of antibiotic resistance and breakthrough infections, prescribe a 2-year course with annual review, and consider Deflux endoscopic injection as an alternative if they prefer to avoid long-term antibiotics.
discriminators: In-depth knowledge of RIVUR with critical appraisal of design, outcomes, limitations
Comparative analysis of three landmark VUR trials
Detailed understanding of antimicrobial resistance implications
Evidence-based personalised management recommendation
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Urine collection methods in children
questions.questionText:
(a) [LIST] the four methods of urine collection available in a pre-continent child. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the advantages, disadvantages, and contamination rates for each method. [4 marks]
(c) [INTERPRET] the following urine culture results from a bag specimen: mixed growth of >10^5 CFU/mL of two organisms. What is the next step? [2 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (4 marks): 1) Clean-catch urine (midstream when possible, otherwise clean-catch). 2) Bag specimen (adhesive bag applied to perineum). 3) Catheter specimen. 4) Suprapubic aspiration (SPA). 1 mark each.

b) (4 marks): Clean-catch: advantages — non-invasive, low contamination (<10%); disadvantages — requires patience, may be difficult in young infants. Bag specimen: advantages — simple, convenient; disadvantages — high contamination rate (25-60%), false positives, only useful if negative. Catheter: advantages — low contamination (<5%), reliable; disadvantages — invasive, discomfort, risk of introducing infection. SPA: advantages — gold standard, least contamination (<1%), definitive diagnosis; disadvantages — invasive, requires skill, may fail if bladder empty. 1 mark per method.

c) (2 marks): Mixed growth of two organisms from a bag specimen is almost certainly contamination. The next step is to repeat the urine culture using a catheter specimen or SPA (clean catch if feasible) before making a diagnosis of UTI. Bag specimens showing mixed growth should never be treated as UTI. (1 mark for stating contamination, 1 mark for next step).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The four urine collection methods in pre-continent children are: clean-catch urine, bag specimen, catheter specimen, and suprapubic aspiration (SPA).

b) Clean-catch: low contamination (~10%), non-invasive, but requires patience. Bag specimen: easy but high contamination (25-60%), only useful to rule out UTI if negative. Catheter: reliable (contamination <5%), but invasive and can introduce infection. SPA: gold standard (<1% contamination), definitive, but requires skill and may fail if the bladder is empty.

c) Mixed growth of two organisms from a bag specimen indicates contamination. No treatment should be started. The next step is to collect a catheter specimen or SPA for culture before making any diagnosis or starting antibiotics.
discriminators: Comprehensive comparison of all four collection methods
Accurate contamination rate gradation
Correct interpretation of contaminated bag specimen
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Organism spectrum in paediatric UTI
questions.questionText:
A 6-week-old uncircumcised boy presents with fever (38.5°C) and a urine culture growing a Gram-negative rod. Imaging reveals bilateral hydronephrosis and a urinary catheter is inserted.

(a) [LIST] the three most common causative organisms of paediatric UTI and their approximate proportions. [3 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] why Proteus mirabilis is more commonly isolated in boys than in girls with UTI. [3 marks]
(c) [INTERPRET] the significance of a non-E. coli organism in a febrile infant with abnormal imaging findings. [2 marks]
(d) [STATE] the recommended empiric antibiotic choices for febrile UTI in a 6-week-old infant, considering local resistance patterns. [2 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (3 marks): 1) Escherichia coli — 70-85% of first UTIs. 2) Proteus mirabilis — 5-10% (higher in boys). 3) Klebsiella pneumoniae — 5-10%. 1 mark each. Other acceptable: Enterococcus faecalis, Enterobacter, Pseudomonas (especially in catheterised/complex).

b) (3 marks): Proteus is more common in boys due to: 1) presence of urease enzyme → splits urea → alkaline urine → struvite stone formation. 2) Foreskin colonisation — Proteus colonises the subpreputial space more readily than E. coli. 3) Uncircumcised boys (especially <1 year) have higher rates of Proteus UTI. (1 mark each). Common error: candidates forget to mention the role of the foreskin.

c) (2 marks): Non-E. coli organisms (especially Proteus, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas) in a febrile infant with abnormal imaging is classified as an 'atypical' UTI per NICE criteria, which requires more extensive imaging (MCU and DMSA). It suggests possible underlying structural abnormality (VUR, obstruction). (1 mark for atypical classification, 1 mark for structural association).

d) (2 marks): Empiric choice: co-amoxiclav (Augmentin) or third-generation cephalosporin (cefotaxime or ceftriaxone) for intravenous therapy. For oral step-down: co-amoxiclav or cephalexin. TMP-SMX avoided in infants <6 weeks due to bilirubin displacement. Nitrofurantoin not used in young infants <3 months for acute treatment. Choice depends on local resistance patterns — >20% E. coli resistance to co-amoxiclav in some areas. (1 mark for IV, 1 mark for oral).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) The most common organisms are: Escherichia coli (70-85%), Proteus mirabilis (5-10%), and Klebsiella pneumoniae (5-10%). Other organisms include Enterococcus, Enterobacter, and Pseudomonas.

b) Proteus mirabilis is more common in boys due to the urease enzyme it produces, which splits urea into ammonia, creating alkaline urine and promoting struvite stone formation. Additionally, the foreskin in uncircumcised boys provides a reservoir for Proteus colonisation, and uncircumcised male infants have higher rates of Proteus UTI compared to girls.

c) A non-E. coli organism in a febrile infant with abnormal imaging is classified as an atypical UTI according to NICE CG54. This necessitates more extensive imaging (MCU and DMSA) because non-E. coli organisms are more commonly associated with underlying structural anomalies such as high-grade VUR or obstructive uropathy.

d) For a 6-week-old with febrile UTI requiring IV therapy, empiric co-amoxiclav or a third-generation cephalosporin (cefotaxime/ceftriaxone) is appropriate. For oral step-down, co-amoxiclav or cephalexin. TMP-SMX is avoided in neonates due to the risk of bilirubin displacement and kernicterus. Local resistance patterns should guide the final choice.
discriminators: Knowledge of organism-specific epidemiology
Understanding of Proteus predilection in boys including foreskin and urease mechanisms
Link between non-E. coli organisms and atypical UTI classification
Age-appropriate antibiotic selection with safety considerations
commonErrors: b) (3 marks): Proteus is more common in boys due to: 1) presence of urease enzyme → splits urea → alkaline urine → struvite stone formation. 2) Foreskin colonisation — Proteus colonises the subpreputial space more readily than E. coli. 3) Uncircumcised boys (especially <1 year) have higher rates of Proteus UTI. (1 mark each). Common error: candidates forget to mention the role of the foreskin.

questions.title: Recurrent febrile UTI with renal scarring
questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old girl presents with her third febrile UTI in 12 months. She has known bilateral grade III VUR and was commenced on TMP-SMX prophylaxis after her second UTI. On this admission, she is febrile (39.5°C), has rigors, and is vomiting. Urine culture grows E. coli resistant to TMP-SMX. DMSA shows new scarring in the right upper pole and a pre-existing left lower pole scar. The parents are distraught and request definitive treatment.

(a) [ANALYSE] the reasons for prophylaxis failure in this child and discuss the significance of the resistant organism. [5 marks]
(b) [RECOMMEND] the acute management for this admission, including choice of antibiotics and intravenous fluid management. [5 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the definitive surgical options available (endoscopic injection vs open reimplantation), including success rates, risks, and indications for each. [6 marks]
(d) [OUTLINE] the long-term monitoring plan for this child, including surveillance for hypertension, proteinuria, and renal function. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
a) (5 marks): Prophylaxis failure reasons: 1) TMP-SMX-resistant organism (63% of breakthrough UTIs in RIVUR). 2) Existing VUR (grade III) may be persistent. 3) Compliance may be variable. 4) True failure despite compliance — some children are breakthrough regardless of prophylaxis. Resistant organism means the prophylaxis provided no coverage and may have selected for resistant strains. This is a breakthrough UTI meeting criteria for change of management. (2 marks for resistance, 2 marks for failure analysis, 1 mark for significance).

b) (5 marks): Acute: IV ceftriaxone (or cefotaxime) 50-100 mg/kg/day — Gram-negative coverage including resistant E. coli. IV fluids (dextrose-saline) for resuscitation if dehydrated/not tolerating oral. Blood cultures before antibiotics. Monitor urine output, temperature, inflammatory markers. Switch to oral based on sensitivities when afebrile for 24-48 hours (e.g., oral cephalosporin or amoxicillin if sensitive). Analgesia and antipyretics. (2 marks for antibiotics, 1 mark each for fluids, monitoring, step-down).

c) (6 marks): Endoscopic injection (Deflux): Success rates variable — grade III VUR ~70-80% after single injection, 85-90% after two. Advantages: day case, minimal downtime, no incisions, low morbidity. Disadvantages: less successful in higher grades, duplicated systems, pre-existing ureterocele. Open reimplantation (Cohen or Lich-Gregoir): Success >95%. Advantages: definitive, all grades, anatomical correction. Disadvantages: longer hospital stay, catheterisation required, postoperative haematuria, rare ureteric obstruction, failure rate <5%. Indications for reimplantation: failed endoscopic injection, high-grade VUR (IV-V), complex anatomy (duplex), breakthrough UTIs despite prophylaxis, parental preference for definitive treatment. (2 marks for injection, 2 marks for reimplantation, 2 marks for comparison).

d) (4 marks): Blood pressure at every visit (annual minimum, more frequently if abnormal). First-morning urine for protein-to-creatinine ratio (PCR) annually. eGFR by Schwartz formula 6-12 monthly. If proteinuria develops → ACE inhibitor. DMSA only if new breakthrough UTI or deteriorating function. Annual height/weight/head circumference. Counselling about pregnancy risks in future (UTI, hypertension, pre-eclampsia). Lifelong follow-up even after VUR correction due to existing scarring. (1 mark each for 4 components).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
a) Prophylaxis failure in this child is likely due to infection with a TMP-SMX-resistant E. coli strain, a well-documented consequence of prophylaxis. The RIVUR trial demonstrated that 63% of breakthrough UTIs in the prophylaxis arm were caused by resistant organisms. Additional factors include possible non-compliance, the presence of persistent grade III VUR providing a pathway for bacteria to reach the upper tracts, and individual host susceptibility. A breakthrough UTI while on prophylaxis, especially with a resistant organism and new scarring, represents a definitive indication to change management strategy — either switching to a different prophylactic agent or proceeding with definitive surgical correction.

b) Acute management: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily (or cefotaxime 100 mg/kg/day divided) for adequate Gram-negative coverage, including TMP-SMX-resistant E. coli. Blood cultures prior to antibiotics. IV fluids (0.45% NaCl + 5% dextrose) at maintenance if tolerating oral fluids, or bolus 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline if clinically dehydrated. Monitor urine output, temperature, CRP. Switch to oral antibiotics based on culture sensitivities (e.g., cephalexin if sensitive) once afebrile for 24 hours. Complete 10-14 days total antibiotic course.

c) Definitive options: Endoscopic injection of Deflux (dextranomer/hyaluronic acid) is a day-case procedure with ~70-80% success after one injection for grade III VUR. It is less invasive but has lower success in higher grades, duplicated systems, and pre-existing scarring. Open ureteric reimplantation (cross-trigonal Cohen technique) has success rates >95% and is definitive. It requires 2-3 days hospital stay, urethral catheter for 24-48 hours, and carries rare risks of ureteric obstruction (1-2%) and haematuria. Given this child has bilateral VUR, new scarring, and breakthrough UTIs on prophylaxis, I would recommend open reimplantation as definitive treatment given the need for certainty and the presence of bilateral disease.

d) Long-term monitoring: annual blood pressure measurement (risk of hypertension 5-10% with unilateral scar, higher with bilateral); annual first-morning urine albumin-to-creatinine or protein-to-creatinine ratio for proteinuria detection; eGFR by Schwartz formula every 6-12 months; DMSA only indicated if new breakthrough UTI or unexplained decline in GFR; growth monitoring; and counselling for future pregnancy (increased risk of UTI, hypertension, and pre-eclampsia). Lifelong follow-up is essential even after successful VUR correction because the existing scars confer ongoing risk.
discriminators: Detailed analysis of prophylaxis failure mechanisms with trial evidence
Comprehensive acute management including antibiotic stewardship
Balanced surgical decision-making with evidence-based comparison
Complete long-term surveillance plan with pregnancy counselling
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Imaging and management after paediatric UTI

Options:
A. No further imaging required
B. Ultrasound within 6 weeks
C. Ultrasound + DMSA at 4-6 months
D. Ultrasound + MCU + DMSA at 4-6 months
E. Ultrasound during acute infection + DMSA at 4-6 months + MCU
F. DMSA only
G. MCU only
H. MR urography
I. Repeat ultrasound in 3 months
J. MAG3 renogram

Scenarios:
1. A 6-month-old boy with a first typical febrile UTI that responds to antibiotics within 24 hours. Urine culture grows E. coli. Clinical examination is normal. What is the appropriate imaging according to NICE CG54?
2. An 18-month-old girl with her second febrile UTI (recurrent). USS shows mild left hydronephrosis (AP 9 mm). What imaging is recommended?
3. A 3-month-old boy with a first febrile UTI, poor urine stream, and a palpable bladder. Creatinine is elevated at 80 micromol/L. What is the appropriate imaging?
4. A 2-year-old girl with her first febrile UTI, otherwise well. Urine grows E. coli. She responds promptly to oral antibiotics. Clinical examination is normal. What is the appropriate imaging?
5. An 8-month-old girl with a first febrile UTI. She has a sibling with VUR. Ultrasound shows right AP 7 mm. What is the appropriate next imaging step?
