questions.title: International Grading System and Spontaneous Resolution of VUR
questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old girl is referred with a first febrile urinary tract infection. Her mother reports no other urinary symptoms. Renal ultrasound shows mild left pelvicalyceal dilation. A MCUG is requested.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the International Classification of Vesicoureteral Reflux (Grades I–V) and illustrate each grade with a line diagram description of the anatomical findings. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the expected spontaneous resolution rates for each grade of VUR and the factors that influence resolution, including age at presentation, gender, laterality, and the presence of bladder bowel dysfunction. [8 marks]
(c) [EVALUATE] the evidence for and against routine antibiotic prophylaxis in a child with grade III VUR, citing findings from the RIVUR trial and the Swedish Reflux Trial. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Grade I – contrast into ureter only (1); Grade II – into renal pelvis without calyceal dilation (1); Grade III – mild/moderate dilation of ureter and pelvis, normal fornices (1); Grade IV – moderate ureteral tortuosity, blunted fornices (1); Grade V – gross dilation, tortuous ureter, loss of papillary impressions (1); Each grade with accurate description (1 each for IV–V clarity) (3). Deduct 1 mark if fornices vs calyces confused.

Part (b) 8 marks: Grade I+II ~80–90% resolution over 4–5 years (1); Grade III ~50–60% (1); Grade IV ~30–40% (1); Grade V ~10–20% (1); Age <2 years resolves faster (1); BBD reduces resolution by ~50% (1); Bilateral reflux lower resolution (1); Unilateral higher resolution (1). Common error: quoting adult resolution rates.

Part (c) 9 marks: RIVUR – continuous TMP-SMX reduced febrile UTI by 50% (RR 0.5) (2); Number needed to treat = 8 (1); Higher risk of antibiotic resistance (1); Swedish trial – girls with grade III–IV benefit; boys do not (1); No difference in renal scarring on DMSA at 2 years (1); BBD blunts prophylactic benefit (1); Individualize decision (1); Consider circumcision in boys (1). Must weigh both sides for full marks.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The International Classification of VUR (International Reflux Study Committee, 1985) defines Grade I as contrast reflux into a non-dilated ureter only; Grade II as reflux into a non-dilated renal pelvis and calyces; Grade III as mild-to-moderate dilation of the ureter and pelvis with normal fornices; Grade IV as moderate ureteral tortuosity and blunting of the fornices; and Grade V as gross dilation with tortuous ureter and loss of papillary impressions.

Spontaneous resolution is inversely related to grade: Grade I ~85%, Grade II ~75%, Grade III ~55%, Grade IV ~35%, Grade V ~15% over 4–5 years. Younger age at diagnosis (<2 years) strongly favours resolution. Unilateral reflux resolves more frequently than bilateral. Bladder bowel dysfunction is the single most important modifiable factor — untreated BBD reduces resolution rates by up to 50%.

The RIVUR trial (2014) demonstrated that continuous TMP-SMX prophylaxis reduced the risk of recurrent febrile UTI by approximately 50% (HR 0.50; 95% CI 0.34–0.74). However, the Swedish Reflux Trial found benefit primarily in girls with grade III–IV reflux. Neither trial showed a significant difference in new renal scarring on DMSA at 2 years. Prophylaxis must therefore be individualised, with careful counselling of families regarding the trade-off between UTI reduction and antibiotic resistance emergence.
discriminators: Quotes exact resolution percentages by grade
Distinguishes fornices from calyces in grading
Integrates RIVUR and Swedish trial contrasting findings
Recognises BBD as critical effect modifier
Discusses NNT and resistance trade-off
commonErrors: Part (b) 8 marks: Grade I+II ~80–90% resolution over 4–5 years (1); Grade III ~50–60% (1); Grade IV ~30–40% (1); Grade V ~10–20% (1); Age <2 years resolves faster (1); BBD reduces resolution by ~50% (1); Bilateral reflux lower resolution (1); Unilateral higher resolution (1). Common error: quoting adult resolution rates.

questions.title: Surgical Management of VUR: Endoscopic versus Open Techniques
questions.questionText:
A 4-year-old boy with bilateral grade IV VUR diagnosed after a febrile UTI has persistent high-grade reflux despite 18 months of antibiotic prophylaxis. His parents request definitive surgical correction. They ask about 'keyhole' versus open surgery.

(a) [COMPARE] the mechanisms of action, injection materials, and technique of the STING, HIT, and double-HIT endoscopic methods for VUR correction. [9 marks]
(b) [ANALYSE] the published success rates of endoscopic injection versus open ureteric reimplantation (Cohen and Lich-Gregoir techniques) for grade IV VUR, citing evidence from systematic reviews and randomised trials. [8 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the role of bladder bowel dysfunction assessment and treatment in the perioperative period, including how untreated BBD affects surgical outcomes for both endoscopic and open approaches. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 9 marks: STING – subureteric injection at 6 o'clock beneath ureteric orifice (1); HIT – intraureteric hydrodistension with injection into the intramural tunnel (1); double-HIT – proximal and distal intramural injections (1); Dextranomer/hyaluronic acid (Deflux) is most common agent (1); Polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) – historical, migration concerns (1); Vantris (polyacrylate polyalcohol copolymer) – higher bulking, less migration (1); Silicone – used historically (1); Each material mention (2); Complications: ureteric obstruction, calcification, contralateral de novo reflux (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Endoscopic success for grade IV – ~70–75% single session (1); Cohen cross-trigonal – ~95–98% success (1); Lich-Gregoir extravesical – ~92–96% success (1); Systematic reviews show open superior for grade IV+V (1); IRSC data support open for high grade (1); RCTs (e.g. Capozza 2017) – endoscopic inferior for dilated ureters (1); Repeat injection improves endoscopic success to ~85% (1); Cost-effectiveness favours endoscopic as first-line despite lower single-session success (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: BBD present in 30–50% of VUR children (1); Preoperative BBD treatment essential (1); Untreated BBD reduces endoscopic success by 15–20% (1); Open reimplantation success less affected but postoperative voiding dysfunction may worsen (1); Postoperative urinary retention more common with bilateral extravesical reimplant (1); Urotherapy, bowel regimen, anticholinergics before surgery (1); Postoperative voiding re-education (1); Must document BBD assessment in clinical record for medicolegal completeness (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The STING (Subureteric Transurethral Injection) technique places bulking agent beneath the bladder mucosa at the 6 o'clock position of the ureteric orifice, creating a nipple-like elevation that coapts the orifice during bladder filling. The HIT (Hydrodistension Implantation Technique) relies on hydrodistension of the intramural tunnel, with injection directly into the tunnel lumen. Double-HIT involves two injection points within the tunnel. Dextranomer/hyaluronic acid (Deflux) is the most commonly used agent; Vantris offers higher viscosity and lower migration but carries a higher risk of ureteric obstruction.

For grade IV VUR, single-session endoscopic success is approximately 70–75%, rising to ~85% with repeat injection. Open ureteric reimplantation (Cohen cross-trigonal) achieves 95–98% success in a single procedure. Extravesical Lich-Gregoir reimplantation has comparable success (92–96%) with the advantage of avoiding a cystotomy but carries a risk of postoperative urinary retention when performed bilaterally.

Bladder bowel dysfunction is present in 30–50% of children with VUR and is the strongest predictor of surgical failure for both endoscopic and open approaches. Preoperative BBD treatment with urotherapy, timed voiding, treatment of constipation, and anticholinergic medication when indicated, should be completed before any surgical intervention. Untreated BBD reduces endoscopic success by 15–20% and increases the risk of persistent postoperative dysfunctional voiding after open reimplantation.
discriminators: Differentiates STING, HIT, double-HIT mechanisms precisely
Quotes evidence-based success rates for each modality
Compares materials with biophysical rationale
Emphasises BBD as critical perioperative variable
Provides nuanced cost-effectiveness analysis
commonErrors: Part (a) 9 marks: STING – subureteric injection at 6 o'clock beneath ureteric orifice (1); HIT – intraureteric hydrodistension with injection into the intramural tunnel (1); double-HIT – proximal and distal intramural injections (1); Dextranomer/hyaluronic acid (Deflux) is most common agent (1); Polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) – historical, migration concerns (1); Vantris (polyacrylate polyalcohol copolymer) – higher bulking, less migration (1); Silicone – used historically (1); Each material mention (2); Complications: ureteric obstruction, calcification, contralateral de novo reflux (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: BBD present in 30–50% of VUR children (1); Preoperative BBD treatment essential (1); Untreated BBD reduces endoscopic success by 15–20% (1); Open reimplantation success less affected but postoperative voiding dysfunction may worsen (1); Postoperative urinary retention more common with bilateral extravesical reimplant (1); Urotherapy, bowel regimen, anticholinergics before surgery (1); Postoperative voiding re-education (1); Must document BBD assessment in clinical record for medicolegal completeness (1).

questions.title: Imaging Strategy in Febrile UTI: DMSA vs MCU Timing
questions.questionText:
A 6-week-old neonate presents with a fever of 39.2°C, irritability, and malodorous urine. Urine culture grows >10^5 CFU/mL E. coli. The paediatrician orders a renal ultrasound and asks your advice on further imaging.

(a) [OUTLINE] the current evidence-based imaging algorithm following a first febrile UTI in an infant under 2 years, referencing the NICE, AAP, and ESPU guidelines. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the rationale for timing of DMSA scintigraphy relative to the acute infection, and how late DMSA (4–6 months) versus acute-phase DMSA (1–2 weeks) findings are interpreted differently. [5 marks]
(c) [JUSTIFY] the optimal timing of MCUG in this neonate relative to the UTI and antibiotic treatment, and discuss the evidence for 'top-down' versus 'bottom-up' imaging approaches. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: NICE – US in all <6 months; MCU only if US abnormal or atypical/recurrent UTI (1); AAP – US and MCU in all 2–24 months after first febrile UTI (1); ESPU – individualised, US for all, DMSA as reference standard (1); Top-down approach (ESPU) preferred by many to reduce unnecessary MCUs (1); Controversy over routine circumcision after first UTI (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: Acute DMSA (1–2 weeks) – detects acute pyelonephritis; defects represent inflammation ± permanent scar (1); Cannot differentiate reversible from permanent damage (1); Late DMSA (4–6 months) – only permanent scarring remains (1); ~50% of acute defects resolve by 6 months (1); Late DMSA is the reference standard for diagnosing renal scarring (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: MCUG should be performed 2–4 weeks after UTI treatment, once urine is sterile (1); Earlier MCU risks false-positive reflux from inflammation (1); Top-down approach – DMSA first, MCU only if DMSA abnormal (1); Reduces MCU rate by ~30–40% (1); Bottom-up – MCU first; finds low-grade reflux that would otherwise be missed but exposes more children to radiation (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
NICE guidelines recommend renal ultrasound for all infants <6 months with first febrile UTI, with MCUG reserved for those with abnormal ultrasound or atypical/recurrent infections. The AAP recommends both US and MCUG for children 2–24 months after first febrile UTI. The ESPU advocates an individualised approach with a 'top-down' strategy: DMSA scan first, with MCUG only if DMSA shows abnormalities.

Acute DMSA (performed 1–2 weeks after infection) shows photopenic defects representing acute pyelonephritis. Approximately 50% of these acute defects resolve by 4–6 months. Late DMSA (4–6 months post-infection) therefore serves as the reference standard for diagnosing permanent renal scarring.

MCUG should be delayed until urine is sterile (typically 2–4 weeks after antibiotic completion) to avoid false-positive results from inflammation-induced transient reflux. The top-down approach reduces the number of MCUGs performed by 30–40% compared with the bottom-up approach, without missing clinically significant scarring, but will miss low-grade reflux that would otherwise be detected incidentally.
discriminators: Compares three guideline bodies (NICE, AAP, ESPU)
Explains why acute and late DMSA give different information
Quantifies defect resolution rate (~50%)
Justifies MCUG timing with pathophysiology
Provides numeric reduction rate for top-down approach
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Bladder Bowel Dysfunction as a Cofactor in VUR
questions.questionText:
A 7-year-old girl with known grade II VUR presents with breakthrough febrile UTIs despite antibiotic prophylaxis. She has been on prophylaxis for 3 years. Her mother describes daytime wetting, urgency, and stool soiling.

(a) [DEFINE] bladder bowel dysfunction (BBD) and list its diagnostic criteria using the Rome IV criteria for paediatric functional constipation and the DVSS (Dysfunctional Voiding Symptom Score) questionnaire. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiological mechanisms by which BBD perpetuates or worsens vesicoureteral reflux and increases the risk of febrile UTIs. [5 marks]
(c) [CREATE] a step-by-step management plan for addressing BBD in this child before any surgical intervention for reflux is considered, including expected timeframes for improvement. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: BBD = combined bladder and bowel dysfunction (1); Rome IV – ≥2 of: ≤2 defecations/week, faecal incontinence, stool retention, painful bowel movements, large rectal mass (1); DVSS >6 in girls, >9 in boys indicates abnormal voiding (1); Criteria include: urgency, frequency, daytime incontinence, straining, dysuria, holding manoeuvres (1); Must have duration ≥2 months (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: Rectal distension causes extrinsic bladder compression and detrusor instability (1); High-pressure voiding from pelvic floor dyssynergia (1); Elevated intravesical pressure overcomes ureterovesical junction competence (1); Incomplete bladder emptying → residual urine → bacterial growth (1); Constipation alters bowel microbiota and periurethral colonisation (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: Step 1 – ensure regular bowel evacuation: laxatives (polyethylene glycol 1–2 g/kg/day) (1); Step 2 – timed voiding q2–3h (1); Step 3 – treat constipation first (4–8 weeks) before assessing bladder symptoms (1); Step 4 – urotherapy, biofeedback for pelvic floor dyssynergia (1); Step 5 – anticholinergics (e.g. oxybutynin) if detrusor overactivity persists after bowel treatment (1). Expect 6–12 months for full improvement.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Bladder bowel dysfunction is the concurrent presence of lower urinary tract symptoms and functional constipation. The Rome IV criteria for paediatric constipation require ≥2 of the following for ≥2 months: ≤2 defecations per week, faecal incontinence episodes, stool retention, painful bowel movements, or a large rectal faecal mass. The DVSS questionnaire quantifies voiding dysfunction; scores >6 in girls or >9 in boys are abnormal.

The pathophysiology involves a 'cross-talk' mechanism: rectal distension from faecal loading causes posterior bladder wall compression, inducing detrusor instability and reducing functional bladder capacity. Pelvic floor dyssynergia during voiding generates high intravesical pressures that overcome the flap-valve mechanism at the ureterovesical junction, perpetuating or worsening reflux. Incomplete bladder emptying leaves residual urine that serves as a culture medium.

Management follows a stepped approach: (1) bowel disimpaction and maintenance with polyethylene glycol (1–2 g/kg/day) for 8–12 weeks; (2) timed voiding every 2–3 hours; (3) urotherapy and biofeedback; (4) anticholinergics only if detrusor overactivity persists after constipation is resolved. Families should be counselled that 6–12 months of BBD treatment may be needed before assessing whether VUR has resolved.
discriminators: Integrates Rome IV and DVSS diagnostic framework
Explains mechanical cross-talk pathophysiology
Specifies laxative dosing and duration
Recommends treating bowel BEFORE bladder
Provides realistic timeline for improvement
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Sibling Screening Controversy and Ethical Considerations in VUR
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old boy is diagnosed with bilateral grade V VUR after presenting with urosepsis. He undergoes successful bilateral Cohen reimplantation. His parents are now concerned about his asymptomatic 6-month-old baby sister. They request a MCUG to 'screen' her. The family has no other children. There is a strong family history of renal disease — the father was diagnosed with 'kidney scarring' as a child.

(a) [ARGUE] the case for and against routine sibling screening for VUR, citing evidence from family cohort studies including the Birmingham Reflux Study and the meta-analysis by Sweeney et al. on sibling reflux prevalence. [8 marks]
(b) [DEBATE] the ethical framework you would use to counsel these parents, incorporating the principles of beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, and justice as they relate to screening an asymptomatic infant. [8 marks]
(c) [PROPOSE] a pragmatic, evidence-based screening and surveillance protocol for asymptomatic siblings of children with high-grade VUR, and justify each component against alternative strategies. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Sibling VUR prevalence ~30–50% (1); Higher when index case has high-grade reflux (1); Birmingham Reflux Study – siblings with reflux often have lower grades that resolve spontaneously (1); Sweeney meta-analysis – screening leads to overdiagnosis (1); Most screened siblings never develop UTI or scarring (1); Renal scarring on DMSA in screened siblings is rare (~5–10%) (1); Screening leads to unnecessary radiation, anxiety, and potential overtreatment (1); No RCT shows outcome benefit of early detection in asymptomatic siblings (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Beneficence – potential to prevent renal damage (1); Non-maleficence – radiation exposure from MCUG (effective dose ~0.5 mSv) (1); Anxiety from false positives and labelling (1); Autonomy – parents' right to choose after informed counselling (1); Justice – resource allocation for low-yield screening (1); Ethical framework – shared decision-making (1); Offer DMSA as less invasive alternative (1); Document counselling clearly (1).

Part (c) 9 marks: Option A – no screening, watchful waiting (1); Option B – renal US at 1 month, MCUG only if US abnormal or febrile UTI occurs (2); Option C – all siblings have US + DMSA at 1 year (1); Recommend – individualised, discuss options, offer US first (1); If US abnormal (hydronephrosis, renal size discrepancy) → MCUG (1); Counsel parents on UTI symptoms and prompt treatment (1); No role for prophylactic antibiotics in screened asymptomatic siblings (1); DMSA as baseline at 12 months if high concern (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The prevalence of VUR in asymptomatic siblings of children with known reflux is 30–50%, with higher rates when the index child has high-grade (IV–V) reflux. However, the sibling reflux is typically lower grade and more likely to resolve spontaneously. The Birmingham Reflux Study demonstrated that most siblings with reflux never develop UTIs or renal scarring. Sweeney et al.'s meta-analysis confirmed that while screening detects reflux, there is no evidence that early detection in asymptomatic siblings improves renal outcomes compared with watchful waiting. Screening therefore risks overdiagnosis, unnecessary radiation (MCUG effective dose ~0.5 mSv), parental anxiety, and potential overtreatment with antibiotics or surgery.

Applying the four principles of biomedical ethics: beneficence would support screening if it prevented renal damage, but current evidence does not demonstrate this benefit. Non-maleficence weighs against MCUG radiation and the psychological harm of labelling a child with a disease. Autonomy supports parental choice after full information, including the option to screen. Justice questions whether healthcare resources should be directed at low-yield population screening. Shared decision-making with clear documentation of the discussion is therefore the most appropriate ethical approach.

A pragmatic protocol would be: (1) counsel all parents of newly diagnosed VUR children about sibling risks and UTI symptoms; (2) perform renal ultrasound in asymptomatic siblings at 1–2 months of age; (3) proceed to MCUG only if ultrasound shows hydronephrosis, abnormal renal size, or parenchymal abnormality; (4) no routine antibiotic prophylaxis for screened siblings; (5) prompt evaluation and treatment of any febrile UTI in siblings with urine culture and DMSA. This balances detection of clinically significant disease against the harms of overinvestigation.
discriminators: Quotes specific sibling VUR prevalence (30–50%)
References Birmingham Reflux Study and Sweeney meta-analysis
Applies principlist ethics framework
Provides radiation dose quantification
Recommends pragmatic tiered screening protocol
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Vesicoureteral Reflux: Clinical Scenarios and Management Decisions

Options:
A. Grade I VUR — observation alone
B. Grade II VUR — observation ± prophylaxis
C. Grade III VUR with BBD — BBD treatment first
D. Grade IV VUR with breakthrough UTI — endoscopic injection
E. Grade V VUR — open ureteric reimplantation
F. Grade III VUR with normal DMSA — continue prophylaxis
G. Sibling of VUR patient, asymptomatic — renal US screening
H. Febrile UTI with normal MCUG — treat UTI, no further imaging
I. Post-endoscopic injection obstruction — JJ stent insertion
J. Bilateral grade IV VUR with BBD — combined BBD treatment + open reimplant

Scenarios:
1. A 2-year-old girl is found to have right grade II VUR on MCUG performed after her first febrile UTI. DMSA scan is normal. She has no voiding symptoms and opens her bowels daily.
2. A 4-year-old boy with known left grade III VUR develops two breakthrough febrile UTIs while on TMP-SMX prophylaxis. He has nocturnal enuresis and faecal soiling. MCUG confirms persistent grade III reflux.
3. A 5-year-old girl undergoes Deflux injection for right grade IV VUR. She presents 2 weeks later with right flank pain and vomiting. Renal US shows new-onset moderate hydronephrosis.
4. A 6-month-old asymptomatic infant is brought by his parents for screening after his older brother required surgery for bilateral grade V reflux. Renal US is normal.
5. A 3-year-old girl with bilateral grade V VUR fails a trial of prophylaxis with two breakthrough UTIs. DMSA shows a left lower pole scar. She has no constipation and voids normally.
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Aetiology and Diagnosis of Ureteropelvic Junction Obstruction
questions.questionText:
A 6-month-old infant is found to have SFU grade III hydronephrosis on antenatal ultrasound. Postnatal ultrasound at 1 week confirms a left renal pelvis AP diameter of 22 mm with calyceal dilation. A MAG3 renogram is requested at 6 weeks of age.

(a) [CLASSIFY] the aetiologies of ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO), distinguishing between intrinsic and extrinsic causes, and describe the histopathological findings of each subtype. [8 marks]
(b) [INTERPRET] the MAG3 renogram drainage curves, explaining the difference between an obstructed, equivocal, and unobstructed pattern and how the T½ (half-time) is calculated and used. [8 marks]
(c) [ASSESS] the role of the Whitaker test in contemporary paediatric urological practice, including its technique, typical pressure thresholds, and indications given the availability of non-invasive renography. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Intrinsic – adynamic segment (1); Aperistaltic narrowed segment at UPJ (1); Histology: disorganised smooth muscle, increased collagen between muscle fibres, reduced pacemaker cells (Cajal-like cells) (1); Extrinsic – crossing vessel (lower pole accessory renal artery) in 15–20% (1); Vessel compresses UPJ (1); High insertion of ureter into renal pelvis (1); Fibrous bands/adhesions (1); Polyp (fibroepithelial polyp) – rare intrinsic cause (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Furosemide (Lasix) given at 15–20 min (1); T½ = time for 50% of tracer to drain (1); Obstructed: T½ > 20 min, rising/plateau curve, poor response to furosemide (2); Equivocal: T½ 10–20 min (1); Unobstructed: T½ < 10 min, rapid drainage after furosemide (1); Well-tempered renogram – patient well-hydrated, bladder empty, proper furosemide timing (1); Diuretic challenge essential (1).

Part (c) 9 marks: Whitaker test = antegrade pressure-perfusion study under fluoroscopy (1); Percutaneous nephrostomy access (1); Saline infused at 10 mL/min (1); Normal = renal pelvic pressure < 12 cm H2O (1); Equivocal 12–22 cm H2O (1); Obstructed > 22 cm H2O (1); Indications: equivocal renogram, discrepancy between anatomy and function, post-pyeloplasty assessment (1); Invasive – requires GA and radiation (1); Now rarely used due to high-quality renography but remains gold standard for equivocal cases (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
UPJO may be intrinsic or extrinsic. Intrinsic UPJO results from an adynamic segment of the ureter at the pelviureteric junction, histologically characterised by disorganised smooth muscle architecture, increased collagen deposition between muscle fibres, and reduced interstitial cells of Cajal that serve as pacemaker cells. Extrinsic causes include a crossing lower pole accessory renal artery (present in 15–20% of cases), high ureteric insertion, and fibrous bands.

On MAG3 renography, the obstructed pattern shows rising or plateau tracer activity in the renal pelvis after furosemide administration, with a T½ > 20 minutes. The unobstructed pattern shows prompt washout (T½ < 10 minutes). Equivocal curves show intermediate drainage (T½ 10–20 minutes). The well-tempered renogram requires adequate hydration, an empty bladder, and correct timing of furosemide (usually 15–20 minutes post-tracer).

The Whitaker test is an antegrade pressure-perfusion study performed under fluoroscopic guidance. Through a percutaneous nephrostomy, saline is infused at 10 mL/min while monitoring renal pelvic pressure. Pressures <12 cm H2O are normal, 12–22 cm H2O are equivocal, and >22 cm H2O indicate obstruction. While rarely used today due to the reliability of MAG3 renography, it remains the gold standard for resolving equivocal cases where renography and imaging findings are discordant.
discriminators: Distinguishes intrinsic vs extrinsic with histology
Quotes exact T½ thresholds (10, 20 min)
Uses 'well-tempered renogram' terminology
Provides precise Whitaker pressure thresholds
Defines current role and limitations of Whitaker test
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Pyeloplasty Techniques: Open, Laparoscopic, and Robotic
questions.questionText:
A 4-year-old boy with antenatally diagnosed left SFU grade IV hydronephrosis is confirmed to have obstructed drainage on MAG3 renogram (T½ 45 minutes, split function 38%). The family ask about 'scarless surgery' and want to know which operation is best for their son.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the steps of the Anderson-Hynes dismembered pyeloplasty, explaining how the technique addresses both intrinsic and extrinsic causes of obstruction. [9 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] the outcomes of open, laparoscopic, and robotic-assisted pyeloplasty in children, citing success rates, operative times, length of stay, and complication rates from published comparative series. [8 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] how the presence of a crossing vessel alters surgical decision-making, and whether a laparoscopic or robotic approach offers any advantage over open surgery in this specific scenario. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 9 marks: Incision through skin, muscle, retroperitoneal approach (1); Mobilise renal pelvis and proximal ureter (1); Excise narrowed UPJ segment (1); Spatialate ureter laterally (1); Reduce excess renal pelvis if required (1); Anastomosis: posterior wall first (1); Place JJ stent or nephrostomy tube (1); Relocate ureter anterior to crossing vessel if present (1); Watertight closure with 5-0 or 6-0 absorbable suture (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Open pyeloplasty success 95–98% (1); Mean operative time ~90–120 min (1); Hospital stay 2–4 days (1); Laparoscopic success 90–95% (1); Longer learning curve, operative time ~150–200 min (1); Shorter hospital stay 1–2 days (1); Robotic success 94–98%, comparable to open (1); Shorter hospital stay, less analgesia, higher cost (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: Crossing vessel present in 15–20% of UPJO (1); Open allows visual identification and transposition (1); Laparoscopic/robotic provides superior visualisation of retroperitoneal vessels (1); Vessel transposition (dismembered pyeloplasty with ureter anterior to vessel) essential regardless of approach (1); Robotic: 3D vision + wristed instruments aid dissection around vessel (1); No RCT shows superiority of any approach for crossing vessel (1); Surgeon experience most important factor (1); Conversion rates low (<3%) for laparoscopic/robotic (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The Anderson-Hynes dismembered pyeloplasty begins with a retroperitoneal approach through a flank incision. The renal pelvis is mobilised to expose the UPJ and proximal ureter. The narrowed segment is excised, and the ureter is spatialated laterally to create a wide anastomosis. Excess renal pelvis may be reduced. The anastomosis is performed with absorbable sutures (5-0 or 6-0), posterior wall first, over a JJ stent placed antegradely. When a crossing vessel is identified, the ureter is transposed anterior to the vessel to prevent ongoing compression.

Open pyeloplasty achieves 95–98% success, with operative times of 90–120 minutes and hospital stays of 2–4 days. Laparoscopic pyeloplasty has success rates of 90–95% with longer operative times (150–200 minutes) but shorter hospital stays (1–2 days). Robotic-assisted pyeloplasty has success rates of 94–98%, approaching open surgery, with the shortest hospital stays and lowest analgesia requirements, albeit at higher cost. No approach has demonstrated superiority in randomised trials.

A crossing vessel is present in 15–20% of UPJO cases. The key principle — ureteric transposition anterior to the vessel — applies regardless of approach. Robotic assistance provides superior 3D visualisation and wristed instrumentation that facilitates precise dissection around the crossing vessel. However, surgeon experience and comfort remain the most important determinants of outcome.
discriminators: Provides complete step-by-step operative description
Quotes success rates with precise ranges
Compares operative time and length of stay across all three approaches
Addresses crossing vessel-specific management
States surgeon experience as key determinant
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Drainage Curves on Renography: Interpretation and Pitfalls
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old girl with antenatal hydronephrosis undergoes a MAG3 renogram at 3 months of age. The curve shows a T½ of 18 minutes with moderate tracer retention in the renal pelvis after furosemide. The left split renal function is 44%.

(a) [DEFINE] the three standard renographic curve patterns (obstructed, equivocal, unobstructed) and sketch each pattern with labelled axes. [5 marks]
(b) [LIST] the factors that may produce a false-positive obstructed drainage curve, including patient-related and technical factors. [5 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the management options for this child with an equivocal MAG3 result, integrating clinical assessment, further imaging options, and a monitoring schedule. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: Obstructed – rising/plateau curve with T½ > 20 min (1); Equivocal – gradual drainage, T½ 10–20 min (1); Unobstructed – rapid washout, T½ < 10 min (1); Axes: time (x) vs counts/activity (y) (1); Furosemide injection marked on curve (1). Accept sketch description if drawn in text.

Part (b) 5 marks: Dehydration (1); Full bladder causing extrinsic compression on ureter (1); Immature renal function in neonates (<1 month) (1); Inadequate furosemide dose (<1 mg/kg) (1); Poor injection technique/extravasation (1); Large redundant renal pelvis (reservoir effect) (1); Gravity effect if patient supine (1). Any 5 for full marks.

Part (c) 5 marks: Clinical assessment – well, no UTI, normal growth (1); Repeat US in 3–6 months to assess hydronephrosis stability/improvement (1); Repeat MAG3 in 6–12 months if US stable (1); Consider Whitaker test if worsening function or symptoms develop (1); if declining split function (<35%) or febrile UTI → surgical intervention (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The obstructed pattern shows a rising or plateau curve after furosemide with T½ > 20 minutes. The unobstructed pattern shows rapid washout (T½ < 10 minutes). The equivocal pattern falls in between (T½ 10–20 minutes).

False-positive obstruction can result from patient dehydration, an overfull bladder causing extrinsic compression, immature neonatal renal function (<1 month of age), inadequate furosemide dosing (<1 mg/kg), technical issues with injection, a large redundant renal pelvis acting as a reservoir, or supine positioning negating gravity-assisted drainage.

For this child with equivocal drainage (T½ 18 min) and preserved function (44%), conservative management is appropriate: clinical monitoring for UTIs and abdominal pain, repeat renal ultrasound in 3–6 months, and repeat MAG3 in 6–12 months. If split function deteriorates below 35%, symptoms develop, or breakthrough UTIs occur, surgical intervention with pyeloplasty would be indicated. The Whitaker test is reserved for cases where renography and imaging are discordant.
discriminators: Defines three curve patterns with T½ cut-offs
Lists ≥5 causes of false-positive obstruction
Recognises neonatal renal immaturity as confounder
Uses 35% split function as intervention threshold
Describes reservoir effect of redundant pelvis
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: PUJO versus Primary Obstructed Megaureter
questions.questionText:
A 6-week-old neonate with antenatal hydronephrosis has a postnatal ultrasound showing a left renal pelvis AP diameter of 14 mm, mild calyceal dilation, and a dilated left ureter (8 mm throughout its course) down to the vesicoureteric junction. The bladder appears normal.

(a) [DIFFERENTIATE] between the pathophysiological mechanisms, anatomical level of obstruction, and histological findings in PUJO (pelviureteric junction obstruction) versus primary obstructed megaureter (POM). [5 marks]
(b) [INTERPRET] the expected findings on contrast-enhanced voiding MCUG in this child and explain how this investigation distinguishes POM from primary refluxing megaureter. [5 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a management algorithm for a child with POM, with reference to the role of conservative management, indications for surgery, and surgical options (endoscopic dilatation vs ureteric reimplantation with tapering). [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: PUJO – obstruction at pelviureteric junction (1); POM – obstruction at distal 1–2 cm of juxtavesical ureter (1); PUJO – adynamic segment histology: disordered muscle, increased collagen (1); POM – distal ureteric segment shows hypo- or aplastic longitudinal muscle, circumferential fibrosis (1); PUJO involves the proximal ureter and renal pelvis; POM involves the entire ureter with maximal dilation in the distal third (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: MCUG shows no reflux (1); If reflux present → primary refluxing megaureter (1); POM shows narrow distal ureteric segment with proximal dilation (1); Delayed images may show contrast hold-up at VUJ (1); Bladder appears normal, no trabeculation (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: Conservative – observation with US/MAG3 monitoring (1); Most POM resolves spontaneously in first 2 years (1); Surgery if: deteriorating split function <35%, febrile UTI, symptoms, increasing hydroureteronephrosis (1); Endoscopic balloon dilatation of distal ureter – success ~70–80% (1); Ureteric reimplantation with excisional tapering – success >90% but more invasive (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
PUJO involves obstruction at the UPJ due to an adynamic segment with disordered smooth muscle and collagen excess. Primary obstructed megaureter involves obstruction at the distal 1–2 cm of the juxtavesical ureter, where there is hypo- or aplasia of longitudinal muscle and circumferential fibrosis, causing functional obstruction despite a patent lumen. In POM, the entire ureter above the obstructed segment is dilated, with maximal dilation in the distal third.

MCUG in POM is essential to exclude reflux. The study shows a dilated ureter with a narrow distal segment and no contrast reflux into the ureter. If reflux is present, the diagnosis is primary refluxing megaureter rather than obstructed megaureter.

Most cases of POM resolve spontaneously within the first 2 years of life. Conservative management with serial ultrasound and renography is therefore first-line. Surgical intervention is indicated for deteriorating split renal function (<35%), breakthrough febrile UTIs, increasing hydroureteronephrosis, or symptoms. Surgical options include endoscopic balloon dilatation of the distal ureter (success ~70–80%) and ureteric reimplantation with excisional tapering (success >90%), with the latter preferred for high-grade obstruction.
discriminators: Contrasts histological and anatomical features of both conditions
Clarifies MCUG differential from refluxing megaureter
States spontaneous resolution as expected natural history
Provides surgery threshold criteria
Compares outcomes of endoscopic vs open surgical approaches
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Complex UPJO in a Solitary Kidney with Crossing Vessel
questions.questionText:
A 12-year-old girl presents with intermittent right flank pain associated with drinking large volumes of fluid. She has a history of left nephrectomy at age 3 for symptomatic multicystic dysplastic kidney. Serum creatinine is 85 µmol/L and eGFR is 75 mL/min/1.73m². Ultrasound shows SFU grade IV right hydronephrosis. MAG3 renogram shows an obstructed pattern (T½ 55 min) with split function unmeasurable (solitary kidney). CT angiography reveals a lower pole crossing vessel at the UPJ. She is otherwise well and physically active.

(a) [EVALUATE] the risks of conservative versus surgical management in this patient with a solitary kidney and proven obstruction, considering the impact of the crossing vessel on natural history. [8 marks]
(b) [JUSTIFY] your choice of surgical approach (open vs laparoscopic vs robotic) in this scenario, addressing the specific technical challenges posed by the solitary kidney, the crossing vessel, and the need to preserve maximum functional parenchyma. [9 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] the preoperative counselling you would provide to the patient and her parents, including discussion of risks, success rates, stent management, follow-up protocol, and the long-term outlook for renal function. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Conservative risks – progressive obstruction → further loss of function (2); No contralateral compensation possible (1); Intermittent pain likely due to 'Dietl's crisis' – drinking overloads renal pelvis beyond obstructed drainage capacity (1); Crossing vessel nature – compression is intermittent, positional (1); Risk of UTI/pyelonephritis in obstructed system (1); Spontaneous resolution unlikely at age 12 (1); Risk of hypertension from renin-angiotensin activation (1).

Part (b) 9 marks: Robotic approach preferred for solitary kidney (2); Precise dissection around crossing vessel critical (1); Wristed instruments aid vascular transposition (1); 3D vision helps avoid vascular injury (1); Open approach provides same success but longer recovery (1); Laparoscopic offers less cosmesis but steeper learning curve for precise anastomosis (1); Clamping time must be minimised – warm ischaemia <20 min (1); Relocation of ureter anterior to crossing vessel mandatory (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: Success rate 95–98% for experienced surgeon (1); Risk of anastomotic leak/restenosis (~3–5%) (1); Risk of stent migration/forgotten stent (1); JJ stent in situ for 4–6 weeks (1); Follow-up: US + MAG3 at 3 months, then annually for 2 years (1); Long-term renal monitoring: annual BP, creatinine, eGFR (1); Risk of CKD progression if obstruction recurs (1); Advise maintain hydration, avoid NSAIDs, contact sports caution (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Conservative management in a symptomatic 12-year-old with a solitary kidney and confirmed obstruction carries significant risks: irreversible deterioration of the solitary nephron mass, recurrent Dietl's crises, risk of pyelonephritis, and potential for long-term hypertension. Surgery is clearly indicated.

I would recommend robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty. The solitary kidney demands maximum precision to preserve every nephron. Robotic assistance provides 3D magnified visualisation and wristed instruments that facilitate meticulous dissection around the crossing vessel, precise spatialation of the ureter, and watertight anastomosis with minimal warm ischaemia time. The ureter must be transposed anterior to the crossing vessel to prevent persistent compression. Clamping time should be strictly limited to <20 minutes.

Preoperative counselling would cover: (1) the indication — relief of obstruction and preservation of solitary kidney function; (2) success rate of ~95% in experienced hands; (3) specific risks including anastomotic leak (2–3%), restenosis (3–5%), stent-related complications (discomfort, migration, forgotten stent); (4) JJ stent will remain for 4–6 weeks; (5) postoperative protocol — ultrasound and MAG3 at 3 months, then annually for 2 years; (6) lifelong follow-up of blood pressure and renal function; (7) activity restrictions: maintain good hydration, avoid NSAIDs and contact sports where the solitary kidney could be injured.
discriminators: Identifies Dietl's crisis mechanism with fluid overload
Emphasises solitary kidney as critical modifier
Prioritises robotic approach with technical rationale
Specifies warm ischaemia time threshold <20 min
Provides comprehensive long-term follow-up protocol
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Upper Tract Obstruction: Diagnostic and Management Scenarios

Options:
A. Anderson-Hynes dismembered pyeloplasty
B. Laparoscopic pyeloplasty with vessel transposition
C. Endoscopic balloon dilatation of PUJ
D. Conservative management — serial US and MAG3
E. Whitaker test
F. Endoscopic balloon dilatation of distal ureter
G. Ureteric reimplantation with excisional tapering
H. Nephrectomy
I. Cutaneous ureterostomy
J. Observation with antibiotic prophylaxis only

Scenarios:
1. A 6-month-old infant with SFU grade III hydronephrosis and MAG3 T½ of 14 minutes, split function 45%. No symptoms, no UTIs.
2. A 3-year-old boy with right SFU grade IV hydronephrosis, MAG3 T½ 40 minutes, split function 28%. He has had two febrile UTIs.
3. A 5-year-old with a known obstructed megaureter (distal ureteric diameter 10 mm) develops a febrile UTI and has split function 32% on MAG3.
4. A 10-year-old with intermittent flank pain during sports, normal renal function, and an equivocal MAG3 (T½ 18 min). US shows intermittent hydronephrosis. Initial DMSA shows no scarring.
5. A 2-week-old neonate with antenatal bilateral hydroureteronephrosis and elevated serum creatinine. US shows 10 mm ureters bilaterally. MCUG shows no reflux. MAG3 shows poor drainage bilaterally but renal function is immature for interpretation.
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Weigert-Meyer Rule and Anatomy of Duplex Systems
questions.questionText:
A 4-year-old girl presents with continuous dribbling of urine day and night since birth. She also voids normal volumes per urethra. Her mother reports that she is always 'wet' despite successful toilet training. Her renal function is normal.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the Weigert-Meyer rule and its embryological basis, explaining the relationship between the ureteric bud and the metanephric blastema that leads to the typical anatomical arrangement in complete duplex systems. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] how the Weigert-Meyer rule predicts the clinical presentation of upper versus lower moiety anomalies, including the distinct presentation patterns of ectopic ureter in girls versus boys. [8 marks]
(c) [EVALUATE] the diagnostic imaging pathway for this girl to confirm the suspected diagnosis of an ectopic ureter from the upper moiety of a duplex kidney, including the roles of US, MCUG, MRI urography, and cystovaginoscopy. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Upper moiety ureter inserts inferomedially to lower moiety ureter (Weigert-Meyer rule) (1); Upper moiety ureter drains ectopically (more caudal and medial) (1); Lower moiety ureter inserts orthotopically at trigone (1); Embryology: two separate ureteric buds arise from mesonephric duct (1); Upper bud → upper moiety, lower bud → lower moiety (1); Upper bud involutes later → ureter migrates further caudally (1); 'Meyer-Weigert law' — upper pole ureter enters bladder inferomedial to lower (1); Lower moiety ureter has shorter intramural tunnel → prone to reflux (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Girls: ectopic upper moiety ureter may insert distal to external sphincter (urethra, vestibule, vagina) → continuous dribbling (2); Lower moiety reflux — febrile UTIs (1); Boys: ectopic ureter always inserts proximal to external sphincter (prostatic urethra, seminal vesicle, vas deferens) → no incontinence, but epididymitis, UTIs (2); Upper moiety obstruction (ureterocele) — palpable abdominal mass in neonates if large (1); Vaginal ectopic ureter in girls — pathognomonic continuous dribbling despite normal voiding (1); Bilateral duplex systems may be symmetric or asymmetric (1).

Part (c) 9 marks: US – shows duplex kidney, dilated upper pole moiety, thin parenchyma (1); Dilated ureter behind bladder (retrovesical ureter) (1); MCUG – assess for lower moiety reflux, rule out PUV in boys (1); May show ureterocele as filling defect (1); MRI urography – best for ectopic ureter anatomy, course, and insertion site (2); Cystovaginoscopy – direct visualisation of ectopic ureteric orifice in vagina or urethra (1); DMSA – assess upper vs lower moiety function (may be poor in dysplastic upper pole) (1); Must image entire urinary tract to identify level of duplication (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The Weigert-Meyer rule describes the anatomical arrangement in complete ureteric duplication: the ureter draining the upper moiety inserts inferomedially and ectopically, while the ureter draining the lower moiety inserts superolaterally in the trigone and is prone to reflux. Embryologically, two separate ureteric buds arise from the mesonephric duct. The upper bud undergoes later involution and therefore migrates further caudally with the mesonephric duct, explaining its more distal and ectopic insertion.

In girls, the ectopic upper moiety ureter often inserts distal to the external urethral sphincter — in the distal urethra, vestibule, or vagina — causing continuous dribbling incontinence despite normal volitional voiding. In boys, the ectopic ureter always inserts proximal to the external sphincter (prostatic urethra, seminal vesicle, vas deferens), so incontinence does not occur; instead, boys present with UTIs or epididymitis.

The diagnostic pathway should begin with renal ultrasound to identify the duplex kidney, dilated upper pole, and retrovesical dilated ureter. MCUG is essential to detect lower moiety reflux and rule out posterior urethral valves in boys. MRI urography provides the most comprehensive anatomical delineation of the ectopic ureter's course and insertion. Cystovaginoscopy allows direct visualisation of the ectopic orifice. DMSA scintigraphy quantifies differential function of each moiety.
discriminators: Explains embryological basis of Weigert-Meyer rule
Distinguishes gender-specific presentations clearly
Describes continuous dribbling as pathognomonic sign
Outlines comprehensive multi-modality imaging pathway
Connects anatomy to clinical consequences
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Ureterocele Classification and Surgical Management
questions.questionText:
A 3-week-old female neonate presents with a urinary tract infection. Prenatal ultrasound showed right hydronephrosis. Postnatal US reveals a right duplex kidney with a dilated upper moiety and a thin-walled cystic structure within the bladder. Serum creatinine is 35 µmol/L.

(a) [CLASSIFY] ureteroceles using both anatomical (intravesical vs ectopic) and endoscopic (orthotopic vs ectopic) classification systems, and correlate each type with the expected clinical presentation. [8 marks]
(b) [ANALYSE] the management options for this neonate, including endoscopic incision, upper pole heminephrectomy, and common sheath reimplantation, with reference to the evidence for single-stage versus staged approaches. [9 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the management of the lower moiety reflux that is commonly associated with duplex systems after upper pole surgery, and the indications for common sheath ureteric reimplantation. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Intravesical (orthotopic) — entirely within bladder, at normal trigonal position (1); Usually single system, may present incidentally (1); Ectopic — extends into bladder neck or urethra (1); Always associated with duplex upper pole (1); Endoscopic: orthotopic orifice in normal trigonal position (1); Ectopic orifice located distal to trigone (1); Ectopic ureterocele more likely to cause obstruction, incontinence, UTIs (1); Intravesical ureterocele may be asymptomatic (1).

Part (b) 9 marks: Endoscopic incision — first-line for infected/obstructed ureterocele in neonate (2); Decompresses system, relieves obstruction (1); Risk of de novo VUR (up to 50%) (1); Upper pole heminephrectomy — removes non-functioning upper moiety (2); Laparoscopic approach now standard (1); Staged: incision → delayed heminephrectomy + reimplant if reflux persists (1); Single-stage: common sheath reimplant + upper pole partial nephrectomy (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: Lower moiety reflux present in ~50% of duplex systems (1); Upper pole decompression may unmask or worsen lower pole reflux (1); Common sheath reimplantation — both ureters reimplanted together through same submucosal tunnel (2); Cohen cross-trigonal or Lich-Gregoir extravesical (1); Indications: reflux causing breakthrough UTIs, reflux persisting after upper pole surgery (1); High-grade reflux (IV–V) unlikely to resolve spontaneously (1); Reflux that involves lower moiety into dilated ureter (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Ureteroceles are classified anatomically as intravesical (entirely within the bladder) or ectopic (extending into the bladder neck or urethra). Endoscopically, they are classified as orthotopic (orifice in normal trigonal position) or ectopic (orifice distal to trigone). Ectopic ureteroceles are virtually always associated with the upper moiety of a duplex system and present earlier with UTIs, obstruction, or palpable abdominal mass. Intravesical ureteroceles may be asymptomatic and discovered incidentally.

In an obstructed, infected neonatal ureterocele, the first step is endoscopic incision — a single puncture or 'unroofing' to decompress the system. This relieves obstruction acutely but carries a ~50% risk of creating de novo vesicoureteral reflux. Definitive management may be staged (initial incision followed by delayed heminephrectomy and/or reimplantation) or single-stage (common sheath reimplantation with upper pole partial nephrectomy). There is no level 1 evidence favouring either approach; the choice depends on upper moiety function, degree of reflux, surgeon preference, and institutional protocol.

Lower moiety reflux occurs in approximately 50% of duplex kidneys with upper pole ureterocele. It may become clinically significant after upper pole decompression. Common sheath ureteric reimplantation (Cohen cross-trigonal or extravesical Lich-Gregoir) is indicated when there is high-grade or symptomatic lower moiety reflux, persistent reflux after upper pole surgery, or breakthrough UTIs. Both ureters are mobilised together and reimplanted through a single common submucosal tunnel to preserve their shared blood supply.
discriminators: Provides dual classification (anatomical and endoscopic)
Recommend endoscopic incision as first step in neonate
Quantifies de novo reflux risk after incision (~50%)
Discusses staged vs single-stage with evidence assessment
Explains common sheath principle with surgical rationale
commonErrors: Part (b) 9 marks: Endoscopic incision — first-line for infected/obstructed ureterocele in neonate (2); Decompresses system, relieves obstruction (1); Risk of de novo VUR (up to 50%) (1); Upper pole heminephrectomy — removes non-functioning upper moiety (2); Laparoscopic approach now standard (1); Staged: incision → delayed heminephrectomy + reimplant if reflux persists (1); Single-stage: common sheath reimplant + upper pole partial nephrectomy (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: Lower moiety reflux present in ~50% of duplex systems (1); Upper pole decompression may unmask or worsen lower pole reflux (1); Common sheath reimplantation — both ureters reimplanted together through same submucosal tunnel (2); Cohen cross-trigonal or Lich-Gregoir extravesical (1); Indications: reflux causing breakthrough UTIs, reflux persisting after upper pole surgery (1); High-grade reflux (IV–V) unlikely to resolve spontaneously (1); Reflux that involves lower moiety into dilated ureter (1).

questions.title: Common Sheath Ureteric Reimplantation: Principles and Technique
questions.questionText:
A 5-year-old girl with a left complete duplex kidney is found to have high-grade (grade IV) vesicoureteral reflux into the lower moiety ureter. She has had two breakthrough febrile UTIs despite antibiotic prophylaxis. The upper moiety is non-refluxing. Surgery is planned.

(a) [OUTLINE] the surgical principles of common sheath ureteric reimplantation for duplex systems, including the anatomical basis for why the two ureters must be reimplanted together. [5 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] the Cohen cross-trigonal and Lich-Gregoir extravesical techniques when applied to duplex systems, with specific advantages and disadvantages of each. [5 marks]
(c) [LIST] the potential complications of common sheath reimplantation and how each can be prevented or managed. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: Both ureters share a common adventitial sheath and blood supply — must not be separated (2); Reimplant together through a single submucosal tunnel (1); Tunnel length 5:1 ratio (length 5x ureteric diameter) (1); Anti-reflux mechanism – compression of intramural ureters during bladder filling (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: Cohen — intravesical, cross-trigonal tunnel (1); Advantages: reliable anti-reflux, accessible for bilateral (1); Disadvantages: difficult to access upper tracts endoscopically afterwards, potential for ureteric angulation (1); Lich-Gregoir — extravesical, detrusor tunnel sulcus (1); Advantages: less postoperative haematuria/pain, no cystotomy, shorter catheter time (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: Ureteric obstruction — tunnelling too tight (1); Persistent reflux — tunnel too short (1); Ureteric injury/devascularisation — excessive dissection separating sheaths (1); Ureterovaginal fistula — rare, from ureteric injury (1); Postoperative urinary retention — especially bilateral extravesical approach (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The key principle of common sheath reimplantation is that both ureters share a common adventitial layer and blood supply. Separating them risks devascularisation, particularly of the upper moiety ureter. They must therefore be mobilised en bloc and reimplanted together through a single submucosal tunnel of adequate length (a 5:1 tunnel-to-ureter diameter ratio).

The Cohen cross-trigonal technique provides excellent tunnel length and reliable anti-reflux protection, with access to both orifices through a single cystotomy. Its main disadvantage is that the ureters are tunneled across the trigone, making subsequent endoscopic access to the upper tracts challenging. The Lich-Gregoir extravesical technique creates an extravesical detrusor tunnel without opening the bladder, reducing haematuria and catheter time, but bilateral extravesical reimplantation carries a significant risk of postoperative urinary retention.

Complications include persistent reflux (short tunnel), obstruction (excessively tight tunnel or angulation), ureteric injury or devascularisation from excessive dissection, ureterovaginal fistula, and — specifically with bilateral extravesical approach — urinary retention from pelvic nerve injury.
discriminators: Emphasises shared blood supply as critical anatomical principle
Specifies 5:1 tunnel ratio
Compares Cohen vs Lich-Gregoir with specific pros/cons
Identifies bilateral extravesical retention risk
Lists complications with prevention strategies
commonErrors: Part (a) 5 marks: Both ureters share a common adventitial sheath and blood supply — must not be separated (2); Reimplant together through a single submucosal tunnel (1); Tunnel length 5:1 ratio (length 5x ureteric diameter) (1); Anti-reflux mechanism – compression of intramural ureters during bladder filling (1).

questions.title: Upper Pole Heminephrectomy: Indications and Controversies
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old girl with a right complete duplex kidney and a poorly functioning upper moiety (split function 8% on DMSA) has had recurrent UTIs and dribbling incontinence. The upper moiety ureter is ectopic to the vagina. The lower moiety is normal. You are considering upper pole heminephrectomy.

(a) [DEFINE] the indications for upper pole heminephrectomy in duplex systems, including functional criteria and symptom-based indications. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the technique of laparoscopic upper pole heminephrectomy, including the approach, identification and ligation of the upper pole vessels and ureter, and the management of the transected parenchymal surface. [5 marks]
(c) [DEBATE] the controversy surrounding 'prophylactic' lower moiety anti-reflux surgery at the time of upper pole heminephrectomy, presenting evidence both for and against a concomitant ureteric reimplantation. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: Poor upper pole function (<10–15% of total renal function) (1); Recurrent UTIs despite appropriate medical management (1); Obstructed system causing pain/infection (1); Dribbling incontinence from ectopic ureter (1); Large ureterocele causing bladder outlet obstruction (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: Retroperitoneal or transperitoneal approach (1); Identify upper pole vessels — divide between clips (1); Dissect upper pole ureter distally as far as possible before dividing (1); Transaction of parenchyma — use electrocautery, ultrasonic scalpel, or LigaSure (1); Leave distal ureteric stump open to avoid stump empyema (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: FOR concomitant reimplant: lower moiety reflux present in ~50%, may worsen after upper pole decompression (1); Avoids second surgery if reflux persists (1); AGAINST: no evidence that prophylactic reimplant improves outcome (1); Many cases of de novo reflux after heminephrectomy resolve spontaneously (1); Exposes child to longer anaesthetic and bladder surgery unnecessarily (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Upper pole heminephrectomy is indicated when the upper moiety function is <10–15% on DMSA scintigraphy and the child has recurrent UTIs, obstruction, or incontinence from an ectopic ureter. The goal is to remove the non-functioning moiety while preserving all viable lower pole parenchyma.

Laparoscopic upper pole heminephrectomy can be performed via a retroperitoneal or transperitoneal approach. The upper pole vessels are identified by dissecting the renal hilum and are divided between haemostatic clips. The upper pole ureter is dissected distally as far as possible and divided; leaving a long stump may predispose to stump empyema, so the distal ureter is either completely excised or left open with a drain. The parenchymal transection is performed with electrocautery, ultrasonic scalpel, or LigaSure, with or without selective vessel ligation.

The controversy around simultaneous lower moiety anti-reflux surgery centres on the fact that approximately 50% of duplex kidneys have associated lower moiety reflux. After upper pole heminephrectomy, the distal ureteric stump and its effect on the trigone may alter lower moiety reflux dynamics. Some studies suggest that de novo or worsening reflux occurs in up to 30% after heminephrectomy alone. However, most cases of post-heminephrectomy reflux are low grade and resolve spontaneously. There is no high-level evidence supporting routine prophylactic reimplantation, and the added morbidity of bladder surgery in an already complex procedure is not justified.
discriminators: Specifies <10–15% functional threshold
Details laparoscopic steps with vessel/ureter management
Distinguishes distal ureter management strategy
Quantifies post-heminephrectomy reflux risk (~30%)
Presents balanced debate on prophylactic reimplant
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Complex Duplex System in a Neonate with Ureterocele and Obstruction
questions.questionText:
A 2-day-old female neonate is transferred to the neonatal intensive care unit with a palpable right flank mass and poor feeding. Antenatal ultrasound had shown right hydronephrosis. Postnatal US reveals a right duplex kidney with massive upper moiety hydronephrosis, a large intravesical ureterocele prolapsing through the bladder neck, and a thin rim of upper pole parenchyma. The bladder is distended and a catheter cannot be passed easily. Serum creatinine is 90 µmol/L (elevated).

(a) [ASSESS] the immediate life-threatening issues in this neonate and prioritise your acute management steps, including airway, breathing, circulation, and specific urological interventions. [8 marks]
(b) [FORMULATE] a definitive surgical plan for this neonate, discussing the timing, technique, and rationale for endoscopic incision versus open reconstruction versus upper pole heminephrectomy. [9 marks]
(c) [EVALUATE] the potential impact of the prolapsing ureterocele on the contralateral kidney and the bladder outlet, and explain the long-term follow-up required for this child. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: A – airway (generally unaffected) (1); B – breathing (1); C – circulation, hydration status (1); Check electrolytes, acidosis (1); Urological emergency: bladder outlet obstruction from prolapsed ureterocele (1); Attempt gentle catheterisation — if fails, ultrasound-guided aspiration of ureterocele (2); Intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation (1).

Part (b) 9 marks: Endoscopic puncture (incision) of ureterocele — first-line in obstructed neonate (2); Relieves bladder outlet obstruction and upper tract dilation (1); Decompresses system allowing parenchyma to recover (1); Risk of de novo VUR (30–50%) (1); Delayed reassessment at 3–6 months (1); If upper pole function negligible (<10%) after decompression → laparoscopic heminephrectomy (1); If significant lower moiety VUR develops → common sheath reimplant at ~12 months (1); Open reconstruction rarely needed as first procedure (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: Prolapsed ureterocele may obstruct contralateral ureteric orifice → contralateral hydronephrosis (2); Bladder outlet obstruction → acute urinary retention, post-renal AKI (1); Decompression usually resolves contralateral changes (1); Long-term: monitor for VUR (post-incision) (1); Serial US and functional imaging (MAG3/DMSA) (1); Lower moiety reflux surveillance (1); Growth and renal function monitoring (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This neonate has acute bladder outlet obstruction from a prolapsing ureterocele, causing post-renal acute kidney injury (creatinine 90 µmol/L). Immediate management includes fluid resuscitation, intravenous antibiotics, and gentle urethral catheterisation. If the catheter cannot be passed, ultrasound-guided percutaneous aspiration of the ureterocele provides acute decompression. Electrolytes and acid-base status require urgent assessment.

Definitive management begins with endoscopic ureterocele puncture — a single incision at the most dependent portion to decompress the system. This is the first-line intervention in an obstructed neonate. It relieves both the upper tract obstruction and the bladder outlet obstruction. Following decompression, the child is reassessed at 3–6 months with US and DMSA. If the upper moiety function is negligible (<10%), laparoscopic upper pole heminephrectomy is performed. If significant de novo lower moiety reflux develops with breakthrough UTIs, common sheath ureteric reimplantation is undertaken at approximately 12 months of age.

A large prolapsing ureterocele can obstruct the contralateral ureteric orifice, causing contralateral hydronephrosis, and can also cause bladder outlet obstruction with acute retention and post-renal AKI. These changes are typically reversible once the ureterocele is decompressed. Long-term follow-up includes serial renal ultrasound, functional assessment with MAG3 or DMSA, blood pressure monitoring, and surveillance for lower moiety reflux. The child will require long-term follow-up into adolescence.
discriminators: Identifies bladder outlet obstruction as critical emergency
Provides stepwise acute management with catheter/aspiration strategy
Recommends endoscopic puncture as definitive first-line
Discusses contralateral effects of prolapsing ureterocele
Outlines lifelong follow-up requirement
commonErrors: Part (b) 9 marks: Endoscopic puncture (incision) of ureterocele — first-line in obstructed neonate (2); Relieves bladder outlet obstruction and upper tract dilation (1); Decompresses system allowing parenchyma to recover (1); Risk of de novo VUR (30–50%) (1); Delayed reassessment at 3–6 months (1); If upper pole function negligible (<10%) after decompression → laparoscopic heminephrectomy (1); If significant lower moiety VUR develops → common sheath reimplant at ~12 months (1); Open reconstruction rarely needed as first procedure (1).

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Duplication Anomalies: Clinical Scenarios

Options:
A. Endoscopic ureterocele puncture/incision
B. Upper pole heminephrectomy (laparoscopic)
C. Lower pole ureteric reimplantation (common sheath)
D. Complete ureteric reimplantation (Cohen cross-trigonal)
E. Observation — no intervention required
F. Endoscopic Deflux injection into lower pole ureter
G. Nephroureterectomy
H. Ureteroureterostomy (upper to lower pole)
I. Cutaneous ureterostomy
J. Vaginal anticholinergic therapy

Scenarios:
1. An asymptomatic 6-year-old girl is found to have an incidental left duplex kidney on ultrasound performed for abdominal pain. Both moieties have normal parenchyma. MCUG is normal. She has no UTIs and is continent.
2. A 3-month-old female neonate with a right duplex system and an ectopic ureterocele causing bladder outlet obstruction and raised creatinine. She has not yet had any surgery.

4. A 6-year-old boy with a left duplex kidney and grade IV lower moiety reflux. He has had two breakthrough febrile UTIs despite TMP-SMX prophylaxis. DMSA shows lower pole scarring.
5. A 2-year-old girl with a right duplex system underwent endoscopic incision of an obstructing ureterocele as a neonate. She now has grade II VUR into the lower moiety but has had no UTIs. DMSA shows equal function.
