questions.title: Presentation, Diagnosis, and Valve Ablation Technique
questions.questionText:
A male neonate is born at 37 weeks' gestation with antenatally diagnosed bilateral hydroureteronephrosis and a distended bladder. The amniotic fluid volume was reduced (oligohydramnios). After birth, a 6-French feeding tube cannot be passed beyond the posterior urethra.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the embryological origin and anatomical classification of posterior urethral valves (types I, II, and III according to Young's classification), indicating which types are clinically significant. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the postnatal diagnostic pathway for suspected PUV in this neonate, including the role of ultrasound and voiding cystourethrography (VCUG), and the specific radiographic signs you would look for. [8 marks]
(c) [COMPARE] the techniques of valve ablation using cold knife incision versus Bugbee electrocautery, discussing success rates, complication rates, and factors influencing instrument choice. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Type I (most common, ~95%) — sail-like folds from verumontanum to lateral urethral wall (2); Type II — mucosal folds from verumontanum to bladder neck (not obstructive) (1); Type III (rare, ~5%) — concentric diaphragm distal to verumontanum with central perforation (2); Derived from abnormal insertion of mesonephric duct remnants into posterior urethra (1); Type I and III obstruct; Type II is incidental (1); POP (PCOP) valves — posterior urethral obstruction without valves (variant) (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: US — bilateral hydroureteronephrosis (1); Thickened, trabeculated bladder (keyhole sign — dilated posterior urethra) (1); Echogenic, dysplastic kidneys (1); Oligohydramnios sequence (Potter facies if severe) (1); VCUG — diagnostic (1); Catheter passed, contrast instilled (1); Findings: dilated posterior urethra, abrupt calibre change at level of valve, trabeculated bladder (2); Valve leaflets may be visible as linear filling defects (1).

Part (c) 9 marks: Cold knife — uses paediatric resectoscope with cold knife hook (1); Precision incision of valve leaflets at 5, 7, and 12 o'clock (1); Less thermal injury to urethral mucosa (1); Lower risk of stricture (1); Bugbee — electrocautery wire loop; resectoscope-based (1); Quicker, good haemostasis (1); Higher risk of urethral stricture/cautery injury (1); Choice depends on: urethral calibre (6/8 Fr resectoscope vs 9.5 Fr), surgeon preference (1); Both have ~90% success with careful technique (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Young's classification describes three types of posterior urethral valves. Type I (~95%) consists of sail-like mucosal folds extending from the verumontanum to the lateral urethral wall, which billow distally with antegrade flow and obstruct. Type II are non-obstructive folds running from the verumontanum to the bladder neck. Type III (~5%) is a concentric diaphragm with a central perforation, located distal to the verumontanum. Only types I and III are clinically significant. The POP/POPS valves variant describes a spectrum of posterior urethral obstruction without classic leaflet valves.

Postnatal diagnosis begins with renal ultrasound demonstrating bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, a thick-walled trabeculated bladder, and the 'keyhole sign' (dilated posterior urethra). VCUG is the diagnostic gold standard. A catheter is passed and contrast instilled; the key finding is a dilated posterior urethra with an abrupt calibre change at the level of the valve, a trabeculated bladder, and often secondary reflux.

Valve ablation can be performed with either a cold knife or Bugbee electrocautery. The cold knife hook provides precise mechanical division of valve leaflets with minimal thermal injury and a lower risk of subsequent urethral stricture. Bugbee electrocautery offers quicker ablation with good haemostasis but carries a higher risk of thermal injury and stricture. The choice depends on urethral calibre (smaller infants may only accommodate a 6 Fr or 8 Fr resectoscope for cold knife), available equipment, and surgeon preference. Both techniques achieve approximately 90% success.
discriminators: Complete Young classification with clinical significance
Mentions POP/POPS variant
Describes keyhole sign and VCUG findings
Compares cold knife vs Bugbee with stricture risk differential
Discusses instrument choice based on urethral calibre
commonErrors: Part (a) 8 marks: Type I (most common, ~95%) — sail-like folds from verumontanum to lateral urethral wall (2); Type II — mucosal folds from verumontanum to bladder neck (not obstructive) (1); Type III (rare, ~5%) — concentric diaphragm distal to verumontanum with central perforation (2); Derived from abnormal insertion of mesonephric duct remnants into posterior urethra (1); Type I and III obstruct; Type II is incidental (1); POP (PCOP) valves — posterior urethral obstruction without valves (variant) (1).

questions.title: PLUTO Trial, Prenatal Diagnosis, and Ethical Counselling for Vesicoamniotic Shunting
questions.questionText:
A 20-week antenatal ultrasound reveals a male fetus with severe bilateral hydroureteronephrosis, a distended bladder, and oligohydramnios (amniotic fluid index 3 cm). The parents are counselled about the diagnosis of possible posterior urethral valves and are considering termination of pregnancy versus vesicoamniotic shunt placement. They ask for your expert opinion.

(a) undefined [9 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the fetal assessment protocol to determine prognosis before offering vesicoamniotic shunting, including fetal urine biochemistry, ultrasound findings, and the role of fetal MRI. [8 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] the content of a multidisciplinary counselling session for these parents, including discussion of the three management options (termination, shunting, or continued pregnancy with postnatal palliation) and the ethical framework used. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 9 marks: PLUTO trial — multicentre RCT comparing vesicoamniotic shunting vs conservative management (1); Primary outcome: survival at 28 days (1); Shunting group: 50% survival vs 20% in conservative group (2); BUT trial closed early due to poor recruitment (only 31 randomised) (1); High crossover rate (12/16 in conservative group crossed over) (1); This limits validity of intention-to-treat analysis (1); No significant difference in long-term renal function at 2 years (1); Shunting does not prevent end-stage renal disease (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Fetal US — renal appearance (echogenicity, cysts — 'Potter' phenotype) (1); Amniotic fluid volume (1); Lung volumes (CCAM ratio) (1); Fetal urine biochemistry: serial bladder drainage × 3 (1); Good prognosis: Na < 100 mmol/L, Cl < 90 mmol/L, osmolality < 200 mOsm/L, β2-microglobulin < 4 mg/L (2); Proteinuria marker (1); Fetal MRI — more accurate renal volumetry and parenchymal assessment, lung volumes (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: Multidisciplinary team: fetal medicine, paediatric urology, neonatology, nephrology, palliative care (1); Option 1 — termination: legal, personal choice, discuss within context of poor prognosis (1); Option 2 — vesicoamniotic shunting: modest survival benefit but does not prevent renal failure (1); Risk of shunt complications: dislodgement (~30%), blockage, preterm labour, chorioamnionitis (1); Option 3 — continued pregnancy with postnatal management: dialysis possible but poor long-term outcomes (1); Ethical framework — non-directive counselling, respect autonomy, provide balanced information (1); 'Miracle baby' bias — avoid overpromising (1); Document all discussions (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The PLUTO trial is the only randomised controlled trial comparing vesicoamniotic shunting with conservative management for fetal lower urinary tract obstruction. It demonstrated that shunting improves perinatal survival (50% vs 20%), but the trial had significant limitations: it closed early due to poor recruitment (only 31 randomised), there was a high crossover rate, and no significant improvement in long-term renal function was shown. Shunting therefore appears to convert a lethal condition into a survivable one, but does not prevent progression to end-stage renal disease.

Fetal prognosis assessment includes serial ultrasound to evaluate renal parenchymal echogenicity, cystic changes, and amniotic fluid volume. Fetal urine biochemistry is the most important prognostic tool: favourable values are sodium <100 mmol/L, chloride <90 mmol/L, osmolality <200 mOsm/L, and β2-microglobulin <4 mg/L from third bladder aspiration. Fetal MRI provides superior lung volume assessment (important for predicting pulmonary hypoplasia) and renal parenchymal evaluation.

Counselling should be provided by a multidisciplinary team including fetal medicine, paediatric urology, neonatology, paediatric nephrology, and — if requested — palliative care. Three options are presented non-directively: (1) termination of pregnancy, (2) vesicoamniotic shunting with its modest survival benefit but unchanged renal prognosis, and (3) continued pregnancy without intervention, with postnatal dialysis and potential transplant. The ethical framework centres on respect for parental autonomy, balanced information provision without coercion, and clear documentation of the discussion. Families must understand that even with optimal management, the child may require dialysis and subsequent renal transplantation.
discriminators: Detailed PLUTO critique with recruitment/crossover limitations
Distinguishes survival benefit from renal outcome
Lists fetal urine biochemistry thresholds
Emphasises non-directive counselling
Includes multidisciplinary team composition
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Valve Bladder Syndrome: Pathophysiology and Management
questions.questionText:
A 5-year-old boy who had valve ablation as a neonate now presents with persistent daytime wetting, recurrent UTIs, and deteriorating renal function (creatinine 120 µmol/L, eGFR 45 mL/min/1.73m²). Ultrasound shows a thick-walled bladder with bilateral hydroureteronephrosis. Urodynamics reveals high-pressure, low-compliance bladder.

(a) [DEFINE] valve bladder syndrome and describe the classic urodynamic findings that characterise this condition. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiological consequences of the valve bladder on the upper urinary tracts and renal function, particularly the roles of detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia and high-pressure storage. [5 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a stepwise management plan for this boy, including medical therapy, bladder rehabilitation, surgical options, and strategies to preserve renal function. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: Valve bladder syndrome = persistent lower and upper tract dysfunction after successful valve ablation (1); Urodynamics: low bladder compliance ( <10 mL/cm H2O) (1); High detrusor leak point pressure (>40 cm H2O) (1); Detrusor overactivity (1); Incomplete bladder emptying, high post-void residual (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: High-pressure storage → ureteric obstruction by thickened bladder wall (1); Impaired ureteric drainage → hydroureteronephrosis (1); Pressure transmitted to renal parenchyma → nephron loss (1); DSD → functional obstruction at sphincter level compounding storage pressure (1); Renal concentrating defect → polyuria → further bladder dysfunction cycle (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: Step 1 – optimise bladder emptying: clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC) (1); Step 2 – anticholinergics (oxybutynin) to improve compliance (1); Step 3 – treat constipation (1); Step 4 – if conservative fails, consider botulinum toxin A to detrusor (1); Step 5 – last resort: augmentation cystoplasty ± Mitrofanoff (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Valve bladder syndrome is a persistent state of lower and upper urinary tract dysfunction following successful valve ablation. It is characterised urodynamically by low bladder compliance (<10 mL/cm H2O), high detrusor leak point pressure (>40 cm H2O), detrusor overactivity, and incomplete bladder emptying.

The high-pressure, low-compliance bladder transmits elevated storage pressures to the upper tracts, impairing ureteric drainage and causing hydroureteronephrosis. Sustained high intrarenal pressure leads to progressive nephron loss. Detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia compounds this by creating functional obstruction at the sphincter level. A vicious cycle develops: polyuria from renal concentrating defect worsens bladder overdistension, further impairing compliance.

Management is stepwise: (1) optimise bladder emptying with clean intermittent catheterisation; (2) anticholinergic medication (oxybutynin) to improve compliance; (3) aggressive bowel management for constipation; (4) if poor response, intravesical botulinum toxin A; (5) as a last resort for refractory cases with deteriorating renal function, augmentation cystoplasty with or without a catheterisable Mitrofanoff channel.
discriminators: Provides specific urodynamic thresholds (compliance <10, DLPP >40)
Explains pressure transmission nephropathy mechanism
Identifies polyuria-vicious cycle
Recommends CIC as first-line management
Outlines escalation to augmentation as last resort
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Long-Term Renal Survival and CRF Progression After PUV
questions.questionText:
A 12-year-old boy with a history of posterior urethral valves ablated on day 1 of life now attends clinic. He has had three UTIs in the past year. His creatinine is 150 µmol/L and eGFR is 38 mL/min/1.73m². He is approaching adolescence.

(a) [IDENTIFY] the prognostic factors at birth and in early childhood that predict long-term renal survival in boys with posterior urethral valves. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the pattern of chronic renal failure progression in PUV, including the impact of proteinuria, hypertension, and the 'nadir creatinine' concept. [5 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the transition-of-care plan for this adolescent as he moves from paediatric to adult nephrology and urology services, including specific issues related to sexual function and fertility. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: Nadir creatinine in first year — best predictor (1); Nadir < 50 µmol/L → good prognosis (1); Nadir 50–100 → moderate risk (1); Nadir > 100 → high risk of ESRD (1); Antenatal presentation and oligohydramnios — worse prognosis (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: Nadir creatinine at 6–12 months predicts long-term function (1); Progressive decline after age 5–10 years in those with moderate impairment (1); Proteinuria — marker of hyperfiltration injury, accelerates decline (1); Hypertension — common after age 10, accelerates progression (1); 25–40% will reach ESRD by adolescence (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: Parallel transition: adult nephrologist + urologist involved by age 14–16 (1); Specific PUV issues: ejaculatory dysfunction (absent seminal vesicles, poor ejaculation) (1); Fertility issues: abnormal sperm quality, cryptorchidism association (1); Need for ongoing bladder management (catheterisation, anticholinergics) (1); Blood pressure control, proteinuria management, dialysis access planning (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Nadir creatinine in the first year of life is the single best predictor of long-term renal survival. A nadir <50 µmol/L predicts excellent prognosis, 50–100 µmol/L indicates moderate risk, and >100 µmol/L is associated with a high likelihood of end-stage renal disease by adolescence. Antenatal diagnosis with oligohydramnios, bilateral renal dysplasia, and early presentation all worsen prognosis.

Chronic renal failure in PUV follows a biphasic pattern: a period of stable function (often after successful ablation) followed by progressive decline beginning around age 5–10 years. Proteinuria signals hyperfiltration injury and accelerates functional decline. Hypertension becomes increasingly common after age 10 and is an independent risk factor for progression. Of affected boys, 25–40% will develop ESRD by late adolescence.

Transition of care should begin early, with joint paediatric-adult clinics from age 14. Specific issues to address include: ejaculatory dysfunction from abnormal seminal vesicle development, impaired spermatogenesis (often associated with undescended testes, which are more common in PUV), and the need for continued bladder management. The adult team must be familiar with PUV-specific issues including the risk of urinary retention, need for CIC, and the impact of bladder dysfunction on transplant outcomes.
discriminators: Quotes specific nadir creatinine thresholds
Identifies biphasic renal function decline pattern
Recognises proteinuria as hyperfiltration signal
Provides 25–40% ESRD progression rate
Includes transition and fertility-specific concerns
commonErrors: Part (b) 5 marks: Nadir creatinine at 6–12 months predicts long-term function (1); Progressive decline after age 5–10 years in those with moderate impairment (1); Proteinuria — marker of hyperfiltration injury, accelerates decline (1); Hypertension — common after age 10, accelerates progression (1); 25–40% will reach ESRD by adolescence (1).

questions.title: Severe Neonatal PUV with Renal Dysplasia and Pulmonary Hypoplasia
questions.questionText:
A male infant is born at 35 weeks weighing 2.1 kg. Antenatal history was remarkable for severe oligohydramnios from 18 weeks. The parents declined termination. At birth, the baby has respiratory distress requiring intubation and ventilation. Abdominal examination reveals a palpable distended bladder and bilateral ballotable kidneys. Chest X-ray shows small lung volumes. Creatinine at day 1 is 140 µmol/L.

(a) [PRIORITISE] the immediate neonatal management steps, including respiratory and urological interventions, explaining the rationale for the sequence of these steps. [8 marks]
(b) [ANALYSE] the relationship between posterior urethral valves and renal dysplasia, explaining how the timing and severity of obstruction in utero determines the degree of renal dysplasia and postnatal renal function. [8 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the multidisciplinary approach to counselling this family about the likely long-term outcome, including pulmonary function, renal survival, need for dialysis, and candidacy for transplantation. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Secure airway, respiratory support for pulmonary hypoplasia (1); May require high-frequency ventilation (1); Pass urethral catheter (5 Fr or 6 Fr) to decompress bladder (1); Monitor urine output (1); Check electrolytes, acidosis, calcium, phosphate (1); Start IV fluids, correct acidosis (1); Start prophylactic antibiotics (1); Renal US and echocardiogram when stable (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Early-gestation obstruction (before 20 weeks) → severe renal dysplasia (2); Obstruction impairs nephrogenesis — reduced nephron number (1); Histology: dysplastic tubules, glomerular cysts, immature glomeruli (1); Fibrosis and Bowman's space dilation (1); Later obstruction (after 20 weeks) → hydronephrosis without dysplasia (1); The 'POP' phenomenon — some have no discrete valves but equivalent obstruction (1); Degree of dysplasia determines postnatal creatinine nadir and prognosis (1).

Part (c) 9 marks: Pulmonary hypoplasia — may require prolonged ventilation, tracheostomy possible (1); Pulmonary survival ~60% with severe oligohydramnios (1); Renal prognosis — creatinine 140 at day 1 is very high; likely ESRD (1); Dialysis: peritoneal dialysis possible if abdominal cavity not compromised (1); May require 'top-up' feeds, gastrostomy (1); Transplantation possible — but need to manage bladder first (augmentation if valve bladder) (1); Growth and neurodevelopment concerns (1); Palliative care team involvement if parents choose comfort care (1); Honest, realistic hope without destroying hope (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Immediate management prioritises respiratory support — the pulmonary hypoplasia from severe oligohydramnios is the most immediately life-threatening issue. The infant may require high-frequency ventilation. Simultaneously, urological decompression is achieved by passing a urethral catheter (5 Fr or 6 Fr) to drain the bladder. Serum electrolytes, acidosis, and calcium-phosphate balance require correction. Prophylactic antibiotics are started. Once stabilised, renal ultrasound and echocardiogram are performed.

Renal dysplasia in PUV results from early-gestation obstruction (before 20 weeks) that impairs normal nephrogenesis. Histology shows dysplastic tubules, glomerular cysts, and immature glomeruli with surrounding fibrosis — in contrast to later obstruction (after 20 weeks) where hydronephrosis occurs in a kidney with nearly normal nephron endowment. The severity of dysplasia predicts the nadir creatinine and long-term renal survival.

Multidisciplinary counselling involves neonatology, paediatric urology, nephrology, and — if appropriate — palliative care. The family must understand that pulmonary hypoplasia may require prolonged ventilation and possibly tracheostomy, with a survival rate of approximately 60% in severe cases. The day-1 creatinine of 140 µmol/L portends likely progression to ESRD within the first few years. Dialysis (peritoneal) is feasible if the abdomen is not compromised. Renal transplantation can be offered later, but the bladder must be optimised first (urotherapy ± augmentation). Parents should receive honest, balanced information that acknowledges the severity of the situation while supporting their role as decision-makers.
discriminators: Prioritises respiratory over urological emergency
Explains timing-based dysplasia pathogenesis
Distinguishes early vs late obstruction histology
Provides realistic survival and ESRD statistics
Includes palliative care as legitimate option
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Posterior Urethral Valves: Diagnosis and Management

Options:
A. Cold knife valve ablation
B. Bugbee electrocautery valve ablation
C. Urethral catheterisation and deferred ablation
D. Vesicostomy (Blocksom or Sober)
E. Prenatal vesicoamniotic shunting
F. Clean intermittent catheterisation + anticholinergics
G. Augmentation cystoplasty
H. Urinary diversion (ureterostomy or pyelostomy)
I. Renal transplantation
J. Continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis

Scenarios:
1. A 1-day-old neonate with confirmed PUV has a creatinine of 180 µmol/L. Despite catheter drainage for 48 hours, creatinine remains elevated at 150 µmol/L. Urethra is too small for a resectoscope (5 Fr scope only available).
2. A 2-year-old boy who underwent valve ablation as a neonate now has valve bladder syndrome with high-pressure, low-compliance bladder, recurrent UTIs, and rising creatinine despite CIC and oxybutynin.
3. A 7-year-old boy with PUV has ongoing high bladder pressures (DLPP 45 cm H2O) on urodynamics despite maximal medical therapy with CIC and anticholinergics. He has normal creatinine for age.
4. A 2-week-old male neonate with PUV, good renal function after catheter decompression (creatinine 45 µmol/L), but urethra too small for safe endoscopic ablation. He has no reflux.
5. A 10-year-old boy with PUV has progressed to end-stage renal disease (eGFR 12 mL/min/1.73m²). He has had augmentation cystoplasty. He is medically stable and has a supportive family.
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Multicystic Dysplastic Kidney: Natural History and Surveillance
questions.questionText:
A 2-day-old female neonate is found to have a palpable left flank mass on routine neonatal examination. Antenatal ultrasound had not been performed. Renal ultrasound reveals a left kidney replaced by multiple non-communicating cysts of varying sizes with no identifiable renal parenchyma. The right kidney appears normal.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the diagnostic ultrasound criteria for multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK) and how it is differentiated from severe hydronephrosis and cystic renal disease. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the natural history of MCDK, including rates of spontaneous involution, the expected time course, and the risk of hypertension and malignancy. [8 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] an evidence-based surveillance protocol for this child, including imaging frequency, blood pressure monitoring, and the indications for nephrectomy. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: Multiple non-communicating cysts of varying sizes (2); Absence of identifiable renal sinus/renal pelvis (1); No central hilar arrangement of cysts (1); No detectable parenchyma (1); Doppler: absent main renal artery signal (1); Differentiation from hydronephrosis: cysts do not communicate, no dilated renal pelvis or ureter (1); DMSA: no function in MCDK vs some function in severe hydronephrosis (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: Spontaneous involution in ~50–60% over 5–10 years (1); Complete involution more common in first 2 years (1); ~30% remain stable (1); Hypertension risk: 5–10 per 1000 — very low but higher than general paediatric population (2); Malignancy risk (Wilms tumour): extremely low (~0.07%) — no longer justifies prophylactic nephrectomy (2); Contralateral renal anomalies in 30–40% (VUR, UPJO) (1).

Part (c) 9 marks: US at 3–6 months, then annually until involution or age 5 years (2); Blood pressure at every well-child visit (1); If hypertension develops → confirm with 24-hour ambulatory BP monitor (1); US of contralateral kidney at initial diagnosis (1); MCUG only if contralateral hydronephrosis on US (1); DMSA at baseline if concern about contralateral function (1); Nephrectomy indications: hypertension not controlled medically, massive size causing symptoms, parental preference after counselling (1); No routine nephrectomy for malignancy risk alone (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
MCDK is diagnosed on ultrasound by the presence of multiple non-communicating cysts of varying sizes, absence of identifiable renal sinus or parenchyma, and no central hilar arrangement. Colour Doppler typically shows absent main renal artery signal. DMSA scintigraphy shows no tracer uptake, distinguishing MCDK from severe hydronephrosis where some residual function may be present.

Spontaneous involution occurs in approximately 50–60% of cases over 5–10 years, with most involution occurring within the first 2 years of life. Approximately 30% remain stable in size. The risk of hypertension is low (5–10 per 1000) but is higher than in the general paediatric population. The risk of malignant transformation is approximately 0.07% — comparable to the risk of prophylactic nephrectomy — and therefore does not justify routine prophylactic surgery.

Surveillance should include renal ultrasound at 3–6 months and then annually until involution or until age 5 years. Blood pressure monitoring should be performed at every well-child visit (annually) until at least adolescence. If hypertension is detected, it should be confirmed with 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring. Contralateral renal ultrasound is mandatory at diagnosis to screen for associated anomalies. MCUG is reserved for cases where contralateral hydronephrosis is detected. Nephrectomy is indicated only for hypertension refractory to medical management, massive size causing symptoms, or after thorough counselling and parental preference.
discriminators: Provides specific ultrasound diagnostic criteria
Quotes involution rates and time course precisely
Gives exact hypertension and malignancy risk figures
Explains why routine prophylactic nephrectomy is no longer indicated
Includes imaging frequency with age-dependent protocol
commonErrors: Part (b) 8 marks: Spontaneous involution in ~50–60% over 5–10 years (1); Complete involution more common in first 2 years (1); ~30% remain stable (1); Hypertension risk: 5–10 per 1000 — very low but higher than general paediatric population (2); Malignancy risk (Wilms tumour): extremely low (~0.07%) — no longer justifies prophylactic nephrectomy (2); Contralateral renal anomalies in 30–40% (VUR, UPJO) (1).

questions.title: ARPKD versus ADPKD: Genetics, Presentation, and Management
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old boy presents with abdominal distension, failure to thrive, and polydipsia. Both kidneys are palpable and firm on examination. His blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg (>99th centile for age). Serum creatinine is 60 µmol/L. His parents are healthy and have no known renal disease. An older sister is well.

(a) [COMPARE] the genetics of autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease (ARPKD) and autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), including the responsible genes (PKHD1, PKD1, PKD2), inheritance patterns, and mutation types. [9 marks]
(b) [DIFFERENTIATE] the clinical presentation, renal imaging findings, and liver involvement in ARPKD versus ADPKD presenting in childhood. [8 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a management plan for this boy with suspected ARPKD, including blood pressure control, growth and nutritional support, monitoring of renal function, surveillance for portal hypertension, and the role of the Bowman score in predicting prognosis. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 9 marks: ARPKD — PKHD1 gene (chromosome 6p12) (1); Encodes fibrocystin/polyductin (1); Autosomal recessive — 25% risk, carrier parents (1); ADPKD — PKD1 (chromosome 16p13.3, 85%) or PKD2 (4q21, 15%) (1); Encodes polycystin-1 and polycystin-2 (1); Autosomal dominant — 50% risk (1); ARPKD: typically truncating/splice-site mutations (1); ADPKD: PKD1 mutations more severe than PKD2 (1); ARPKD presents earlier (infancy/childhood), ADPKD typically adulthood (1).

Part (b) 8 marks: ARPKD: enlarged, echogenic kidneys with poor corticomedullary differentiation (1); Multiple small cysts (1–2 mm) — 'salt and pepper' pattern on ultrasound (1); Congenital hepatic fibrosis — always present (1); May have Caroli disease (dilated intrahepatic bile ducts) (1); Portal HTN, varices, hypersplenism (1); ADPKD: asymmetric renal enlargement with discrete, variable-sized cysts (1); Cysts also in liver (30–50%), pancreas (10%), seminal vesicles (1); ADPKD rarely presents in childhood — may be incidental or hypertensive (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: ACE inhibitor/ARB for hypertension (1); High-calorie feeds, gastrostomy if needed (1); Monitor renal function (creatinine, eGFR) 3–6 monthly (1); Monitor for portal hypertension: annual abdominal US, platelet count, splenic size (1); Oesophageal varices screening if splenomegaly/thrombocytopenia (1); Bowman score — ultrasound-based: kidney length, echogenicity, cyst size — predicts renal prognosis (1); Higher Bowman score → worse renal outcome (1); Consider nephrectomy if kidneys massively enlarged causing respiratory compromise (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
ARPKD is caused by mutations in PKHD1 encoding fibrocystin, inherited autosomal recessively with a 25% recurrence risk. ADPKD is caused by mutations in PKD1 (85%, more severe) or PKD2 (15%, milder) encoding polycystin-1 and polycystin-2, inherited autosomal dominantly with 50% recurrence risk. ARPKD typically presents in infancy or early childhood with bilateral flank masses and hypertension, while ADPKD typically presents in adulthood.

On ultrasound, ARPKD shows symmetrically enlarged, echogenic kidneys with poor corticomedullary differentiation and numerous tiny cysts (1–2 mm), giving a 'salt and pepper' appearance. Congenital hepatic fibrosis is always present, with risk of portal hypertension, varices, and Caroli disease (dilated intrahepatic bile ducts). ADPKD in childhood shows discrete, variable-sized renal cysts, often asymmetric, with extrarenal cysts in liver, pancreas, and seminal vesicles.

Management of ARPKD focuses on blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors or ARBs as first-line), optimising nutrition with high-calorie feeds and gastrostomy if needed, and regular renal function monitoring. Surveillance for portal hypertension includes annual abdominal ultrasound for spleen size, platelet count monitoring, and upper GI endoscopy for varices if thrombocytopenia or splenomegaly develop. The Bowman score, based on ultrasound parameters (kidney length, echogenicity, cyst size), stratifies risk of progression to ESRD. Nephrectomy is reserved for symptomatic massive nephromegaly causing respiratory compromise.
discriminators: Compares genetics with specific chromosome loci and protein products
Describes 'salt and pepper' ultrasound pattern
Emphasises congenital hepatic fibrosis as defining feature of ARPKD
References Bowman score as prognostic tool
Includes portal hypertension surveillance protocol
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Nephronophthisis: Genetics, Renal Rickets, and Disease Progression
questions.questionText:
A 10-year-old boy presents with polyuria, polydipsia, and growth failure. His height is below the 3rd centile. Serum creatinine is 200 µmol/L (eGFR 30 mL/min/1.73m²). Urinalysis shows low specific gravity (1.005) and mild proteinuria. Ultrasound shows kidneys of normal size with increased echogenicity and loss of corticomedullary differentiation.

(a) [OUTLINE] the genetic basis of nephronophthisis, listing the NPHP genes and their encoded proteins, and explaining how these relate to the ciliary hypothesis. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiology of renal rickets in children with chronic kidney disease, including the role of the FGF23-klotho axis, vitamin D metabolism, and calcium-phosphate homeostasis. [5 marks]
(c) [CREATE] a management plan addressing this child's growth failure, metabolic bone disease, and progression to end-stage renal disease, including specific medical therapies and preparation for renal replacement therapy. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: NPHP1 (most common, ~20%), NPHP2–20 (1); All encode nephrocystin proteins (1); These localise to primary cilia of renal tubular cells (1); Ciliary dysfunction → abnormal tubular structure and function (1); Autosomal recessive inheritance (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: CKD → hyperphosphataemia stimulates FGF23 secretion (1); FGF23 increases phosphaturia and suppresses renal 1α-hydroxylase (1); Reduced active vitamin D → hypocalcaemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism (1); Renal acidosis worsens bone demineralisation (1); Result: renal rickets — defective mineralisation of growth plate and osteoid (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: Growth: recombinant growth hormone therapy (1); Phosphate restriction + phosphate binders (sevelamer, calcium carbonate) (1); Active vitamin D analogues (calcitriol, paricalcitol) for secondary hyperparathyroidism (1); Consideration of pre-emptive transplantation (1); Dialysis access planning (AV fistula or peritoneal dialysis catheter) (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Nephronophthisis is caused by mutations in at least 20 NPHP genes, most commonly NPHP1 (~20%). These genes encode nephrocystin proteins that localise to the primary cilia of renal tubular epithelial cells. The ciliary hypothesis proposes that dysfunction of these ciliary proteins leads to abnormal tubular structure, disrupted cell signalling, and progressive cyst formation and fibrosis. Inheritance is autosomal recessive.

Renal rickets in CKD results from the FGF23-klotho axis: hyperphosphataemia stimulates FGF23 secretion, which promotes phosphaturia but also suppresses renal 1α-hydroxylase, reducing active vitamin D production. The resulting hypocalcaemia drives secondary hyperparathyroidism. Metabolic acidosis from impaired tubular function further leaches calcium and phosphate from bone, leading to defective mineralisation at the growth plate.

Management of this child includes recombinant growth hormone therapy for growth failure, dietary phosphate restriction with phosphate binders, active vitamin D analogues (calcitriol or paricalcitol) to control secondary hyperparathyroidism, and correction of acidosis with sodium bicarbonate. Early planning for renal replacement therapy is essential, with consideration of pre-emptive transplantation (the best option for children), or dialysis access creation (peritoneal dialysis catheter or AV fistula) with sufficient time for maturation before initiation.
discriminators: Connects NPHP genes to ciliary hypothesis
Explains FGF23-klotho-vitamin D axis in renal rickets
Lists specific therapeutic agents with mechanism
Recommends pre-emptive transplantation as optimal
Includes dialysis access timing consideration
commonErrors: Part (a) 5 marks: NPHP1 (most common, ~20%), NPHP2–20 (1); All encode nephrocystin proteins (1); These localise to primary cilia of renal tubular cells (1); Ciliary dysfunction → abnormal tubular structure and function (1); Autosomal recessive inheritance (1).

questions.title: Tuberous Sclerosis Complex and Renal Angiomyolipomas
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old girl with known tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) presents with acute left flank pain and macroscopic haematuria. CT scan reveals a 6 cm left renal angiomyolipoma (AML) with evidence of active haemorrhage. She also has multiple smaller AMLs in the right kidney.

(a) [DEFINE] the diagnostic criteria for tuberous sclerosis complex (genetic and clinical) and list the renal manifestations of TSC beyond angiomyolipomas. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiological basis of AML formation in TSC, including the role of TSC1 (hamartin) and TSC2 (tuberin) and the mTOR pathway. [5 marks]
(c) [RECOMMEND] the management of this acutely bleeding AML, including immediate intervention (angiography vs surgery), and the role of mTOR inhibitors (everolimus) in both the acute and long-term management of AMLs. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: Genetic: pathogenic TSC1 or TSC2 mutation (1); Clinical: ≥2 major features (facial angiofibromas, ungual fibromas, shagreen patch, cortical tubers, SEGA, renal AML, LAM) (1); Renal manifestations: AMLs (most common) (1); Renal cysts (cysts and RCC in TSC2 contiguous gene syndrome) (1); Renal cell carcinoma (increased risk, often clear cell or papillary) (1).

Part (b) 5 marks: TSC1 (chromosome 9q34) encodes hamartin; TSC2 (16p13) encodes tuberin (1); Hamartin + tuberin form a complex that inhibits mTORC1 (1); Loss of function → mTORC1 constitutive activation (1); Unchecked cell growth → AML development (smooth muscle, fat, blood vessels) (1); TSC2 disease more severe than TSC1 (1).

Part (c) 5 marks: Acute bleeding: selective renal angiographic embolisation — first-line (1); Minimises nephron loss compared to partial nephrectomy (1); Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) reduces AML volume by ~50% over 6–12 months (1); NNT for bleeding reduction: everolimus reduces bleeding events (EXIST-2 trial) (1); Long-term: everolimus for growing AMLs >3 cm or after bleeding (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
TSC is diagnosed genetically by pathogenic TSC1/TSC2 mutation or clinically by the presence of ≥2 major features (facial angiofibromas, ungual fibromas, shagreen patch, hypomelanotic macules, cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, SEGA, renal AMLs, pulmonary LAM). Renal manifestations include AMLs (most common, often multiple and bilateral), renal cysts (especially in TSC2/PKD1 contiguous gene syndrome), and an increased risk of renal cell carcinoma.

TSC1 and TSC2 encode hamartin and tuberin respectively, which form a protein complex that inhibits mTORC1, a key cell growth regulator. Loss of functional hamartin or tuberin results in constitutive mTORC1 activation, leading to unregulated cellular proliferation and the development of AMLs composed of dysplastic smooth muscle, adipose tissue, and abnormal blood vessels.

Acute bleeding from a renal AML is managed first-line with selective angiographic embolisation, which controls haemorrhage while preserving maximal nephron mass. Surgery (partial nephrectomy) is reserved for failed embolisation or suspected malignancy. The EXIST-2 trial demonstrated that the mTOR inhibitor everolimus reduces AML volume by approximately 50% over 6–12 months and reduces the rate of bleeding events. Long-term everolimus therapy is indicated for growing AMLs >3 cm, multiple or bilateral AMLs, or after a bleeding event.
discriminators: Lists TSC diagnostic criteria (genetic + clinical major features)
Explains TSC1/TSC2-mTOR pathway mechanism
Recommends embolisation as first-line for active bleeding
Cites EXIST-2 trial evidence for everolimus
Provides size threshold (>3 cm) for mTOR inhibitor therapy
commonErrors: Part (a) 5 marks: Genetic: pathogenic TSC1 or TSC2 mutation (1); Clinical: ≥2 major features (facial angiofibromas, ungual fibromas, shagreen patch, cortical tubers, SEGA, renal AML, LAM) (1); Renal manifestations: AMLs (most common) (1); Renal cysts (cysts and RCC in TSC2 contiguous gene syndrome) (1); Renal cell carcinoma (increased risk, often clear cell or papillary) (1).

questions.title: Complex Genetic Counselling in ARPKD: Consequential Diagnosis and Family Planning
questions.questionText:
A 6-week-old infant is diagnosed with ARPKD after presenting with bilateral flank masses, hypertension (95th centile), and respiratory distress from massive nephromegaly. Genetic testing reveals compound heterozygous pathogenic mutations in PKHD1. The parents are healthy, consanguineous (first cousins), and wish to have more children. They request counselling about recurrence risk, prenatal testing options (including PGT-M), and the implications for existing family members.

(a) [INTERPRET] the genetic test result for these parents, explaining the recurrence risk in future pregnancies, the carrier frequency in consanguineous unions, and the concept of compound heterozygosity in ARPKD. [8 marks]
(b) [EVALUATE] the available reproductive options for this couple, including prenatal diagnosis (chorionic villus sampling, amniocentesis), preimplantation genetic testing for monogenic disorders (PGT-M), and the ethical considerations around each. [9 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the medical implications for the extended family, including the role of carrier testing for siblings of the parents and whether the asymptomatic older sibling of the affected infant should be tested or screened. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: ARPKD is autosomal recessive — 25% risk per pregnancy (1); Both parents are carriers (1); Compound heterozygosity = two different PKHD1 mutations, one inherited from each parent (2); Consanguinity increases likelihood of recessive disorders (1); Carrier frequency of PKHD1 ~1:70 in general population; higher with consanguinity (1); CYP (couple's recurrence risk) = 25% regardless of previous child's severity (1); Severity in future child may differ due to variable expressivity (1).

Part (b) 9 marks: Prenatal: CVS at 11–14 weeks, amniocentesis at 15–20 weeks (1); Both carry miscarriage risk (~0.5–1%) (1); PGT-M: IVF with embryo biopsy and genetic testing before transfer (2); Avoids pregnancy termination (1); Requires IVF cycle — costly, physically/emotionally demanding (1); Ethical: right to know vs right not to know (1); Disclosure of results to parents (1); Religious/cultural considerations regarding IVF and termination (1).

Part (c) 8 marks: Parental siblings (aunts/uncles): 50% chance of being carriers (1); Carrier testing offered but rarely medically relevant — ARPKD only manifests in homozygotes/compound heterozygotes (1); Siblings of affected child: 25% risk affected, 50% carrier, 25% unaffected non-carrier (1); Health surveillance for asymptomatic older sibling: screening for hypertension, abdominal US for renal/liver involvement (1); Genetic testing of older sibling: controversial due to autonomy — best deferred until adulthood (1); Psychological impact of predictive testing on child (1); MDT approach: clinical genetics, paediatric nephrology, social work (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This couple has a 25% recurrence risk for ARPKD in each future pregnancy, as autosomal recessive inheritance requires both parents to transmit a mutant PKHD1 allele. Both parents are obligate carriers. Compound heterozygosity means the infant inherited two different PKHD1 mutations — one from each parent — which is common in ARPKD given the large number of private mutations. Consanguinity increases the risk of recessive disorders by ~2-fold for first cousins.

Reproductive options include: (1) natural conception with prenatal diagnosis via CVS (11–14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15–20 weeks) with testing of fetal DNA, with the option of termination if affected; or (2) preimplantation genetic testing for monogenic disorders (PGT-M), where embryos created by IVF are biopsied at the blastocyst stage (day 5–6) and only unaffected embryos are transferred. PGT-M avoids the need for termination but requires IVF with associated costs and physical/emotional demands. Ethical counselling should respect the family's cultural and religious values, and the parents should be informed without coercion.

Extended family implications: the parents' siblings have a 50% chance of being carriers. Carrier testing is of limited clinical utility for adults as ARPKD manifests only in homozygotes or compound heterozygotes. The asymptomatic older sibling has a 25% chance of being affected, 50% chance of carrier, and 25% chance of unaffected non-carrier. I would recommend clinical screening (blood pressure, abdominal US) for the older sibling to detect subclinical ARPKD. Predictive genetic testing in minors for adult-onset conditions is generally deferred per guidelines from the ACMG and ESHG, but ARPKD can present in childhood, so testing may be justified if it guides clinical surveillance. A multidisciplinary approach involving clinical genetics, paediatric nephrology, and psychological support is essential.
discriminators: Explains compound heterozygosity concept clearly
Provides recurrence risk with consanguinity context
Compares CVS/amniocentesis vs PGT-M with pros/cons
Addresses ethical considerations for reproductive options
Discusses paediatric predictive genetic testing guidelines
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Untitled
questions.questionText:
Theme: Cystic Renal Disease: Diagnosis and Management

Options:
A. Multicystic dysplastic kidney — observation and serial US
B. Multicystic dysplastic kidney — nephrectomy
C. ARPKD — ACE inhibitor + portal hypertension surveillance
D. ARPKD — bilateral nephrectomy and dialysis
E. ADPKD — blood pressure control + US monitoring
F. Nephronophthisis — genetic counselling and transplant evaluation
G. Tuberous sclerosis with AML — everolimus therapy
H. Tuberous sclerosis with AML — selective angiographic embolisation
I. Simple renal cyst — no follow-up required
J. Medullary sponge kidney — thiazide diuretics

Scenarios:
1. A 4-year-old girl with tuberous sclerosis complex has an asymptomatic 4.5 cm left renal AML found on surveillance imaging. She has never had haematuria or flank pain.
2. A 14-year-old boy with known tuberous sclerosis presents with acute onset left flank pain and macroscopic haematuria. CT shows an 8 cm left renal AML with active contrast extravasation.
3. A 6-month-old infant with antenatally diagnosed right MCDK (no function on DMSA, multiple non-communicating cysts on US). The left kidney is normal. No hypertension.
4. A 2-year-old with bilateral flank masses, hypertension, and normal renal function. US shows symmetrically enlarged, echogenic kidneys with poor corticomedullary differentiation and multiple tiny cysts.
5. A 12-year-old girl with a family history of ADPKD (father affected) is found to have a blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg. US shows bilateral kidneys of normal size with a few scattered cysts.
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Metabolic workup and medical management of paediatric nephrolithiasis
questions.questionText:
A 6-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a two-day history of colicky right flank pain radiating to the groin, associated with macroscopic haematuria. She has no fever, and urinalysis shows pH 6.5 with no nitrites or leucocytes. Renal ultrasound reveals a 7 mm right renal pelvis calculus and a 4 mm left lower pole calculus. She is an only child with no family history of stone disease. Her dietary history reveals excessive salt and negligible dairy intake. Her mother asks whether this is a one-off problem or if she will keep forming stones.

(a) [OUTLINE] the metabolic workup you would perform for this child following the acute episode, including the rationale for each investigation. [8 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] the interpretation of a 24-hour urine collection showing calcium 6 mg/kg/day (elevated), oxalate 55 mg/1.73m²/day (elevated), and citrate 350 mg/g creatinine (low). [6 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a comprehensive management plan for this patient addressing both the acute stones and long-term prevention. [6 marks]
(d) [CRITICALLY APPRAISE] the evidence for dietary versus pharmacological interventions in preventing recurrence of calcium oxalate stones in children. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 8 marks — 1 each for: serum electrolytes + creatinine (renal function), serum calcium + phosphate + PTH (rule out hyperparathyroidism), serum urate, venous bicarbonate (RTA screen), 24h urine calcium, oxalate, citrate (key lithogenic factors), spot urinalysis + cystine screen (if high index), stone analysis if stone available (composition guides therapy). Accept fasting calcium/creatinine ratio on spot sample as alternative. Part (b): 6 marks — Hypercalciuria: discuss absorptive vs renal vs resorptive types. Elevated oxalate: consider enteric hyperoxaluria (dietary) vs primary hyperoxaluria (suspect if >80 mg). Low citrate: hypomagnesiuria? Mention that this pattern suggests mixed metabolic abnormality. Part (c): 6 marks — Acute: hydration, analgesia, α-blocker (tamsulosin) for distal ureteric stones? ESWL vs URS vs PCNL depending on stone location/size. Long-term: increase fluids to achieve urine output >1.5 L/1.73m²/day, dietary modification (reduce salt, increase dairy/calcium, avoid high-oxalate foods, age-appropriate protein). Medical: thiazide for hypercalciuria, potassium citrate for hypocitraturia (± calcium oxalate), pyridoxine trial if primary hyperoxaluria suspected. Part (d): 5 marks — Dietary: DASH-style diet, adequate dietary calcium (paradoxically protective by binding oxalate in gut), low sodium, low animal protein. Pharmacological: thiazides reduce calcium excretion (1 mark), potassium citrate raises urinary citrate and pH (1 mark), allopurinol only if hyperuricosuria (1 mark). Evidence level: mostly adult data extrapolated (1 mark). Few paediatric RCTs (1 mark). Examiner trap: candidates frequently restrict dietary calcium, which increases risk.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The metabolic workup begins after the acute episode resolves. I would check serum electrolytes, creatinine, calcium, phosphate, magnesium, PTH, urate, and venous bicarbonate to rule out hyperparathyroidism and renal tubular acidosis. A 24-hour urine collection (timed or indexed to creatinine) would measure calcium, oxalate, citrate, urate, sodium, magnesium, and volume. Stone analysis is crucial if a stone is available — on the acute side, a 7 mm renal pelvis stone may require ESWL (first-line for stones <20 mm in children) while the 4 mm stone may pass spontaneously with medical expulsive therapy. In this child, the 24-hour urine shows hypercalciuria (>4 mg/kg/day), hyperoxaluria (>50 mg/1.73m²/day), and hypocitraturia (<400 mg/g creatinine). Absorptive hypercalciuria is most common in children and relates to diet; however, fasting calcium/creatinine ratio can distinguish absorptive from renal leak. Management includes aggressive hydration targeting 2–3 L/1.73m²/day, dietary sodium restriction, normalizing dietary calcium (800–1200 mg/day), and potassium citrate 1–2 mEq/kg/day to correct hypocitraturia and reduce calcium oxalate supersaturation. Thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide 0.5–1 mg/kg/day) are reserved for persistent hypercalciuria despite diet alone. Surgical management for the 7 mm stone in a 6-year-old: ESWL under general anaesthesia is first-line, though ureteroscopy with holmium laser is increasingly used for renal pelvic stones. The 4 mm stone likely passes spontaneously. Recurrence prevention requires annual renal ultrasound and periodic 24-hour urine monitoring.
discriminators: Recognises age-appropriate normal values for 24-hour urine constituents in children
Does not restrict dietary calcium in hypercalciuria — explains the calcium-oxalate competition paradox
Integrates acute surgical decision-making with long-term metabolic prevention in a single answer
Mentions that most paediatric stone guidelines are extrapolated from adult data

questions.title: Cystinuria and surgical stone management in a teenager
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old boy presents with recurrent bilateral staghorn calculi. He had his first stone episode at age 9, underwent ESWL twice and ureteroscopy once. He is non-adherent with follow-up. His younger sister passed a stone at age 11. Renal function is normal (creatinine 62 µmol/L, eGFR 112). CT KUB shows a 2.5 cm complete left staghorn calculus and a 1.8 cm right renal pelvis stone. Urine microscopy reveals typical hexagonal crystals. Stone analysis from his previous ureteroscopy confirmed 100% cystine. The family is frustrated with repeated procedures.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiology of cystinuria and the genetic basis, including autosomal recessive inheritance and the role of the SLC3A1 and SLC7A9 genes. [5 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] the medical management options for cystinuria, including targets for urinary pH, volume, and specific pharmacological therapy. [6 marks]
(c) [RECOMMEND] the optimal surgical approach for the bilateral renal stones, justifying your choice of modality and sequence. [8 marks]
(d) [DEBATE] the ethical and practical issues surrounding non-adherence in an adolescent with a chronic stone-forming condition, including strategies to improve compliance during transition to adult services. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — Cystinuria due to defect in dibasic amino acid transporter in renal proximal tubule and small intestine (1). Autosomal recessive with incomplete penetrance (1). SLC3A1 (chromosome 2, type A, encodes rBAT subunit) and SLC7A9 (chromosome 19, type B, encodes b0,+AT subunit) (1). Defect prevents reabsorption of cystine, ornithine, lysine, arginine (COLA) (1). Cystine poorly soluble at physiological pH, precipitates at pH <7.0 (1). Part (b): 6 marks — Target urine pH >7.0–7.5 using potassium citrate 1–2 mEq/kg/day (1). High fluid intake >3 L/1.73m²/day, keeping urine cystine concentration <250 mg/L (1). Titrate fluids to achieve urine specific gravity <1.010 (1). Tiopronin (2-mercaptopropionylglycine) 10–15 mg/kg/day or D-penicillamine 20–30 mg/kg/day as second-line due to side effects (proteinuria, thrombocytopenia, rash) (1). Captopril (ACE inhibitor) as adjunct decreases cystine excretion modestly (1). Monitor urinary cystine capacity (supersaturation) rather than spot cystine levels (1). Part (c): 8 marks — Left staghorn (2.5 cm): PCNL is first-line; cystine stones are ESWL-resistant due to hardness (1). Right renal pelvis (1.8 cm): may also need PCNL or flexible URS with holmium laser — but bilateral simultaneous PCNL is feasible in expert hands (1). Sequence: staged procedures 2–4 weeks apart preferred if single-stage bilateral risky (1). Mini-PCNL (20–24Fr) appropriate for this age (1). Prone positioning, ultrasound-guided puncture to minimise radiation (1). Post-operative CT at 3 months to confirm stone-free status (1). Austere: cystine stones fragment poorly with ESWL — PCNL outperforms ESWL for stones >1.5 cm (1). Part (d): 6 marks — Adolescent non-adherence is multifactorial: denial, body image, fatigue with chronic treatment (1). Motivational interviewing and shared decision-making improve ownership (1). Transition clinic with adolescent-friendly environment, joint paediatric-adult appointments (1). Peer support groups (1). Consequences of non-adherence must be honestly discussed without scare-mongering (1). Written transition plan: treatment goals, when to re-present (1). Common errors: authoritarian approach, expecting perfect adherence, discharging to adult services without handover.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Cystinuria is an autosomal recessive defect of the dibasic amino acid transporter in the proximal tubule, involving SLC3A1 (type A) or SLC7A9 (type B) genes. This prevents reabsorption of filtered cystine, leading to supersaturation and hexagonal crystal formation. Cystine solubility is pH-dependent: maximum solubility requires urinary pH >7.5. Medical management targets three pillars: (1) high-volume fluid intake to maintain cystine concentration <250 mg/L; (2) alkalinisation with potassium citrate to pH 7.5; and (3) thiol-binding drugs like tiopronin that cleave the disulphide bond, increasing solubility tenfold. Tiopronin 10–15 mg/kg/day is better tolerated than D-penicillamine but still carries risks of nephrotic syndrome, thrombocytopenia, and drug-induced lupus. For this 14-year-old with bilateral cystine stones, ESWL is ineffective because cystine stones are hard. Left staghorn (2.5 cm) requires PCNL — mini-PCNL with 20–24Fr access. The right 1.8 cm stone could be managed by PCNL as well or by flexible URS. I would stage the procedures, addressing the larger left stone first, then the right. Bilateral single-stage PCNL is possible but increases risk. The non-adherence issue is critical: adolescents with chronic illnesses often rebel against treatment. A transition clinic with a dedicated adolescent urologist, joint paediatric-adult sessions from age 16, written transition plan, and peer mentoring all improve outcomes.
discriminators: Explains why ESWL fails for cystine stones — links biochemistry to surgical decision-making
Provides specific titration targets for urinary pH (7.5) and concentration (<250 mg/L)
Addresses transition and adolescent psychology as an integral part of stone management
Discusses thiol-binding drug mechanism and side-effect profile in detail

questions.title: Infective (struvite) stones
questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old girl with recurrent urinary tract infections presents with fever, right flank pain, and a palpable abdominal mass. Urine culture grows Proteus mirabilis >10⁵ CFU/mL. Renal ultrasound shows a large echogenic staghorn calculus filling the right renal pelvis. CT confirms a 3.5 cm complete staghorn calculus with mild hydronephrosis. Serum creatinine is 38 µmol/L.

(a) [LIST] the organisms most commonly associated with struvite stone formation and explain the biochemical mechanism. [4 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the essential components of pre-operative assessment and optimisation before PCNL in this child. [4 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the surgical options and explain why PCNL is preferred over ESWL for a complete staghorn in a 3-year-old. [4 marks]
(d) [SPECIFY] the post-operative surveillance protocol, including metabolic workup and imaging, to minimise recurrence. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 4 marks — Urease-producing organisms: Proteus mirabilis (most common) (1), Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, Providencia, Ureaplasma urealyticum (1). Mechanism: urease hydrolyses urea to ammonia + CO₂ → alkaline urine (1) → phosphate precipitates with magnesium and ammonium to form MgNH₄PO₄·6H₂O (struvite) (1). Part (b): 4 marks — Urine culture and sensitivity; treat with targeted antibiotics for ≥7 days pre-op (1). Full blood count, coagulation profile, cross-match (1). Confirm anatomy with CT urogram or MR urography (1). Assess contralateral kidney function (DMSA or MAG3) (1). Part (c): 4 marks — PCNL first-line for complete staghorn (1). Single tract (lower pole calyx) usually sufficient (1). ESWL unsuitable: stone too large, fragments may cause ureteric obstruction ('steinstrasse'), and retained infected fragments lead to rapid recurrence (1). Mini-PCNL reduces blood loss in small children (1). Part (d): 3 marks — Post-op Day 1: KUB ± CT to assess stone-free status (1). Antibiotics for 48h post-op then guided by culture (1). Surveillance: 6-monthly renal US for 2 years, then annually; metabolic workup (24h urine calcium, oxalate, citrate, urate) even though struvite is infection-related (1). Common errors: forgetting to screen for metabolic risk factors that may coexist.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Struvite stones are caused by urease-producing bacteria, most commonly Proteus mirabilis. Urease hydrolyses urea to ammonia and carbon dioxide, raising urinary pH above 7.2, which allows magnesium ammonium phosphate to crystallise. Complete staghorn calculi require PCNL — ESWL is ineffective for large stone burdens and risks sepsis from infected fragments. Pre-operative optimisation includes treating the UTI (culture-specific antibiotics for 7–10 days), checking coagulation, and assessing split renal function. Mini-PCNL (18–22Fr sheath) is appropriate for a 3-year-old. Post-operatively, stone-free status should be confirmed by CT; residual fragments >4 mm require second-look nephroscopy. Recurrence prevention: eradicate infection, correct any metabolic abnormalities, and long-term imaging surveillance.
discriminators: Explains urease biochemistry precisely — the ammonia/alkaline pH mechanism
Recognises that mini-PCNL is feasible and safe even in toddlers
Identifies that struvite stones can recur rapidly if infection persists

ESWL unsuitable: stone too large, fragments may cause ureteric obstruction ('steinstrasse'), and retained infected fragments lead to rapid recurrence (1). Mini-PCNL reduces blood loss in small children (1). Part (d): 3 marks — Post-op Day 1: KUB ± CT to assess stone-free status (1). Antibiotics for 48h post-op then guided by culture (1). Surveillance: 6-monthly renal US for 2 years, then annually; metabolic workup (24h urine calcium, oxalate, citrate, urate) even though struvite is infection-related (1). Common errors: forgetting to screen for metabolic risk factors that may coexist.

questions.title: Endoscopic management of ureteric calculi in children — ESWL vs URS
questions.questionText:
A 9-year-old boy presents with acute left loin pain radiating to the groin, nausea, and vomiting. He is afebrile. Urine dipstick shows blood +++, no nitrites. CT reveals a 9 mm calculus at the left vesicoureteric junction with mild hydroureteronephrosis. His parents are anxious about any procedure and ask about 'shock wave therapy versus a camera inside'.

(a) [COMPARE] the indications, success rates, and complication profiles of ESWL versus ureteroscopy (URS) for a 9 mm distal ureteric stone in a 9-year-old. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the specific technical considerations for performing URS in a child (instrument size, ureteric orifice dilation, laser settings, stenting). [4 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the role of medical expulsive therapy (MET) in paediatric stone disease, including drug choice, dosing, and evidence base. [3 marks]
(d) [SUMMARISE] the key counselling points you would discuss with the parents to obtain informed consent. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — ESWL: success rate 70–85% for distal ureteric stones <10 mm (1). Advantage: non-invasive, no anaesthetic time? Actually requires GA in children (1). Complication: steinstrasse, need for retreatment (1). URS: success rate >90% for stones <15 mm (1). Single-session clearance more likely (1). Complications: ureteric perforation (1–3%), stricture (rare at <2%), need for post-op stent (1). Part (b): 4 marks — Paediatric URS: 6.5–7.5Fr semirigid ureteroscope (1). Ureteric orifice may not require dilation in all; if needed, balloon dilation or second guidewire (1). Holmium laser 0.6–1 J, 5–10 Hz; dusting vs fragmentation settings (1). Post-op ureteric stent (3.7–4.7Fr, 12–16 cm) for 5–14 days — children tolerate stents poorly (1). Part (c): 3 marks — Alpha-blockers: tamsulosin 0.2–0.4 mg once daily (1). Evidence: meta-analysis of small paediatric RCTs shows increased stone expulsion rate, reduced time to passage, fewer analgesic requirements (1). Off-label in many countries — must discuss with parents (1). Part (d): 3 marks — Discuss both modalities (1). ESWL: external, focused soundwaves, multiple sessions may be needed, monitored anaesthesia (1). URS: direct visualisation, higher single-session success, risks of stent discomfort, need for second procedure to remove stent (1). Common errors: not mentioning stent discomfort; failing to discuss the off-label nature of MET in children.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
For a 9 mm distal ureteric stone in a 9-year-old, URS with holmium laser has a higher single-session stone-free rate (>90%) compared with ESWL (70–85%). Both require general anaesthesia in children. URS uses a 6.5–7.5Fr semirigid ureteroscope; the ureteric orifice may need dilation with a second wire or balloon. Laser settings of 0.6–1 J at 5–10 Hz are safe. Post-operative stenting (4.7Fr, 14 cm) for 5–7 days is standard. MET with tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily may facilitate passage of residual fragments, though use is off-label. Counselling includes explaining that ESWL may require multiple sessions while URS offers single-stage clearance but involves a stent with irritative symptoms. Both options are reasonable; shared decision-making is key.
discriminators: Provides specific comparative success rates (high-yield numbers)
Details paediatric-specific technical modifications for URS
Recognises that MET is off-label in children and counsels accordingly

Common errors: not mentioning stent discomfort; failing to discuss the off-label nature of MET in children.

questions.title: Long case: Recurrent staghorn calculi in a child with renal impairment
questions.questionText:
A 12-year-old girl with a history of recurrent urinary tract calculi since age 5 presents with bilateral flank pain and oliguria. She has undergone four previous surgical procedures (two ESWL sessions on the left, one right PCNL, one left URS). Current serum creatinine is 175 µmol/L (eGFR 35 mL/min/1.73m²). CT reveals bilateral staghorn calculi (left 3 cm, right 2.5 cm) with thinned renal parenchyma bilaterally. Urine pH is 8.0, culture grows Proteus. Stone analysis from the prior PCNL showed mixed struvite and calcium carbonate apatite. She has been lost to follow-up for 2 years.

(a) [EVALUATE] the current clinical situation, including the aetiology of her renal impairment and the urgency of intervention. [5 marks]
(b) [DEVELOP] a stepwise management plan addressing metabolic optimisation, infection control, and surgical strategy. [8 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the long-term prognosis, including the risk of end-stage renal disease and requirement for renal replacement therapy. [6 marks]
(d) [PROPOSE] a multidisciplinary team (MDT) approach to prevent recurrence, involving nephrology, microbiology, dietetics, and psychology. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — CKD stage 3b (eGFR 35) (1). Likely multifactorial: recurrent obstruction from stones, pyelonephritis, parenchymal loss from prior PCNL/ESWL (1). Struvite stones imply chronic urease-producing infection — ongoing parenchymal damage (1). Urgent objective: relieve obstruction if present (hydronephrosis? check) (1). Non-urgent but important: complete stone clearance to halt progression (1). Part (b): 8 marks — Metabolic: correct hypocitraturia, hyperoxaluria if present; start potassium citrate (1). Infection: culture-specific antibiotics pre-operatively — 7–14 days before any instrumentation (1). Nephrostomy if obstructed with sepsis (1). Surgical: staged bilateral PCNL — most stone-free rate for staghorns (1). Mini-PCNL or standard with two tracts (1). Consider simultaneous bilateral vs staged (staged safer in CKD due to contrast/fluid load) (1). Post-op: second-look nephroscopy at 48–72h for residual fragments (1). Stone-free status essential to eradicate infection (1). Part (c): 6 marks — Risk of ESRD: up to 15–30% in patients with recurrent struvite staghorns and CKD (1). If eGFR remains <30 despite clearance → prepare for RRT (1). Options: haemodialysis, PD — PD increases infection risk (1). Transplantation: requires bilateral nephrectomy (native) to eliminate infection source and stone formation (1). Living donor vs deceased donor (1). Recurrence in graft is rare but reported if metabolic risk factors persist (1). Part (d): 6 marks — Nephrology: CKD monitoring, blood pressure control, acid-base management (1). Microbiology: long-term suppressive antibiotics if infection cannot be eradicated (1). Dietetics: low protein if uraemic symptoms, low sodium, adequate calcium, high fluid intake (1). Psychology: adherence improvement, body image with stomas/dialysis lines (1). Transition planning to adult services (1). Surgical: committed to achieving stone-free status — multiple procedures may be needed (1). Common errors: forgetting the dietitian, not planning for ESRD.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This girl has CKD stage 3b from recurrent struvite stone disease and probably chronic pyelonephritis. Her eGFR of 35 and thinned parenchyma suggest irreversible damage. Acute management involves checking for obstruction; if hydronephrosis is present, urgent nephrostomy is needed. Otherwise, she requires staged bilateral PCNL with pre-operative antibiotic cover for Proteus. Complete stone clearance is essential because infected fragments drive recurrence and further functional loss. Metabolic evaluation must rule out coexisting hypercalciuria or hypocitraturia. Long-term prognosis: without complete clearance, she risks progression to ESRD. If eGFR declines to <30, she will need RRT counselling. Bilateral native nephrectomy prior to transplantation is standard because infected, stone-forming kidneys pose a sepsis risk in immunosuppressed patients. The MDT should coordinate: nephrologist (CKD management, BP, acid-base), microbiologist (targeted antibiotics, suppressive therapy), dietitian (low-phosphate, low-protein if uraemic), psychologist (adherence, readiness for transition), and surgeon (committed to total stone clearance).
discriminators: Integrates CKD staging with stone-specific prognosis
Recognises necessity of bilateral native nephrectomy before transplantation
Discusses MDT with named disciplines and specific roles
Links struvite biology to progressive renal damage via infection

questions.title: Extended Matching Question — Surgical decision-making in paediatric nephrolithiasis
questions.questionText:
Theme: Management of paediatric urinary tract calculi

Options:
A. Conservative management with hydration and analgesia
B. Medical expulsive therapy (tamsulosin)
C. Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL)
D. Ureteroscopy (URS) with holmium laser lithotripsy
E. Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL)
F. Laparoscopic pyelolithotomy
G. Open nephrolithotomy
H. Antibiotic therapy alone followed by re-imaging
I. Chemolysis via nephrostomy
J. Subtotal nephrectomy

Scenarios:
1. A 4-year-old boy with a 6 mm asymptomatic right renal pelvis calculus (calcium oxalate) and no obstruction. Renal function normal.
2. An 8-year-old girl with a 2 cm right complete staghorn calculus (struvite) and Proteus UTI.
3. A 14-year-old boy with an 8 mm proximal ureteric stone (calcium oxalate) that has not moved despite 4 weeks of MET. No obstruction, GFR 90.
4. A 3-year-old girl with a 4 mm left distal ureteric stone, colic controlled with paracetamol, no UTI, afebrile.
5. A 6-year-old boy with cystinuria, persistently non-adherent, with an 8 mm left renal pelvis stone and 1.5 cm right lower pole stone. Previous ESWL failed on both sides.
6. A 15-year-old girl with a 4 mm right distal ureteric stone, mild hydronephrosis, and intermittent colic that is not responding to ibuprofen.
questions.markingGuide:
Each correct answer = 2 marks. Total 12 marks. Candidates must justify each choice if asked. Common errors: selecting ESWL for cystine stones; choosing intervention for asymptomatic small calyceal stones; failing to choose PCNL for staghorn calculi; not recognising failed MET as an indication for URS.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Stem 1 = A, Stem 2 = E, Stem 3 = D, Stem 4 = A, Stem 5 = D, Stem 6 = B.
discriminators: Tests surgical decision-making across the full age range of paediatric stone disease
Includes cystinuria as a special case requiring modality-specific knowledge
Assesses when NOT to intervene — a crucial clinical skill
Incorporates MET failure as a threshold for intervention
commonErrors: Each correct answer = 2 marks. Total 12 marks. Candidates must justify each choice if asked. Common errors: selecting ESWL for cystine stones; choosing intervention for asymptomatic small calyceal stones; failing to choose PCNL for staghorn calculi; not recognising failed MET as an indication for URS.

questions.title: Dysfunctional voiding and urotherapy in a school-age girl
questions.questionText:
A 7-year-old girl is referred with daytime urinary incontinence, urgency, and a history of recurrent urinary tract infections (3 episodes of culture-proven UTI in the past year). She also has faecal soiling approximately twice per week. She was dry at night by age 4. Her parents describe her as 'always desperate to go and then only a trickle'. She sits on the toilet with her knees tightly together. Urinalysis is negative for infection. Renal ultrasound shows a normal upper tract with post-void residual of 80 mL.

(a) [DEFINE] dysfunctional voiding and explain the pathophysiological mechanism, including the role of pelvic floor overactivity. [6 marks]
(b) [INTERPRET] the findings from non-invasive uroflowmetry with EMG if the flow curve shows a staccato pattern and EMG demonstrates increased pelvic floor activity during voiding. [5 marks]
(c) [CONSTRUCT] a comprehensive behavioural treatment plan (urotherapy) for this child, including management of the associated constipation. [8 marks]
(d) [EVALUATE] the evidence for pharmacological adjuncts (anticholinergics, alpha-blockers) in dysfunctional voiding and when you would escalate from urotherapy alone. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 6 marks — Dysfunctional voiding: learnt voiding pattern of pelvic floor overactivity during micturition (1). Normally, pelvic floor relaxes as detrusor contracts (1); in dysfunctional voiding, the external urethral sphincter paradoxically contracts (1). This produces staccato flow pattern, high voiding pressures, incomplete emptying (1). Predisposes to UTI (residual urine), constipation, and VUR (1). Often associated with bladder bowel dysfunction (BBD) (1). Part (b): 5 marks — Staccato pattern: saw-toothed, intermittent flow peaks and troughs — not to be confused with interrupted (fractionated) voiding (1). Increased EMG activity during voiding confirms pelvic floor overactivity (1). This distinguishes dysfunctional voiding from underactive detrusor (1). Post-void residual >20 mL is abnormal for age (1). Interpretation: the child is contracting her pelvic floor during voiding, likely due to fear of wetting or learned holding manoeuvres (1). Part (c): 8 marks — Urotherapy: standard first-line (1). Step 1: education — explain the condition to child and parents; demystify (1). Step 2: timed voiding — void every 2–3 hours (1). Step 3: optimal voiding posture — feet flat on stool, knees apart, lean forward ('relaxed voiding position') (1). Step 4: double voiding to reduce PVR (1). Step 5: biofeedback — using EMG or flow-toilet training games to teach pelvic floor relaxation (1). Step 6: treat constipation — high-fibre diet, stool softeners (polyethylene glycol 0.5–1 g/kg/day), daily bowel movement target (1). Step 7: reward chart for adherence (1). Part (d): 6 marks — Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, solifenacin) are primarily for overactive bladder with detrusor overactivity, not first-line for dysfunctional voiding (1). In dysfunctional voiding with proven DO (urodynamics), anticholinergics can help (1). Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) may reduce bladder outlet resistance but not standard in girls (1). Evidence: Cochrane review shows urotherapy alone effective in 50–80%; adding pharmacotherapy benefits selected cases (1). Escalate after 3–6 months of full urotherapy without improvement, or if urodynamics confirm DO (1). Common errors: prescribing anticholinergics without urotherapy; ignoring constipation; failing to diagnose the underlying pelvic floor overactivity (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Dysfunctional voiding is a learnt behavioural condition characterised by contraction of the pelvic floor/external sphincter during voiding. Normally, voiding involves coordinated detrusor contraction with complete sphincter relaxation. In this condition, the child 'holds on' by contracting the pelvic floor, producing a staccato flow pattern on uroflowmetry and elevated PVR. Urotherapy is first-line treatment: it involves education, timed voiding, optimal toilet posture (feet supported, knees apart, leaning forward), double voiding, biofeedback for pelvic floor relaxation, and aggressive treatment of constipation. Anticholinergics are not first-line unless urodynamics confirm detrusor overactivity. Biofeedback using EMG-based computer games has strong evidence in this age group. Constipation must be treated simultaneously — bladder and bowel dysfunction are linked. If symptoms persist after 6 months of compliant urotherapy or if UTI recurrence continues, escalation to urodynamics and consideration of anticholinergics or neuromodulation is warranted.
discriminators: Clearly distinguishes dysfunctional voiding from overactive bladder
Provides specific, actionable urotherapy advice (posture, timing, bowel management)
Resists over-prescribing anticholinergics — appropriately reserves them for confirmed DO
Integrates biofeedback as an evidence-based intervention
