questions.title: Nocturnal enuresis — evaluation and management in a treatment-resistant adolescent
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old boy who has never achieved sustained nighttime dryness presents with his mother. He has approximately 4–5 wet nights per week and is now refusing to attend sleepovers or school camps. He was treated with desmopressin nasal spray at age 8 with initial response but relapsed. An alarm was tried but discontinued after 2 weeks because it 'woke the whole house and he slept through it'. He drinks approximately 500 mL of water before bed. He has no daytime symptoms, no polyuria, no polydipsia, no history of UTI, and no snoring. He is struggling with self-esteem.

(a) [DIFFERENTIATE] between monosymptomatic and non-monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis and justify why this distinction matters for management. [5 marks]
(b) [CRITICALLY APPRAISE] the mechanisms of action, evidence, and limitations of desmopressin therapy versus alarm therapy in monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis. [8 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a rescue treatment plan for this adolescent, explaining how you would re-introduce combination therapy and address adherence. [7 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the psychological impact of persistent enuresis in adolescence and the ethical considerations of treatment withdrawal in a teenager who refuses further intervention. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — Monosymptomatic: nighttime wetting only, no daytime LUTS, no history of UTI or neurological abnormality (1). Non-monosymptomatic: presence of daytime symptoms (urgency, frequency, incontinence, dysfunctional voiding) or constipation (1). Distinction is critical because management differs: MNE responds to desmopressin/alarm; NMNE requires treating underlying bladder dysfunction first (1). 80% of enuretic children have some form of daytime dysfunction if carefully assessed (1). Misclassification leads to treatment failure (1). Part (b): 8 marks — Desmopressin: synthetic vasopressin analogue, reduces nocturnal urine production by concentrating urine (1). Evidence: 60–70% initial response; but only 20–30% achieve full dryness; high relapse rate after withdrawal (1). Strengths: rapid effect, easy to use, good for sleepovers (1). Limitations: nasal spray has higher hyponatraemia risk (monitor Na), sublingual tablet form safer; requires fluid restriction 1h before and 8h after (1). Alarm: operant conditioning, teaches child to wake to full bladder sensation (1). Evidence: 65–80% success, lower relapse rate than desmopressin; takes 6–12 weeks; requires commitment (1). Strengths: lasting effect, no drug side effects. Limitations: stressful for family, requires child motivation, poor compliance if child is deep sleeper (1). Combination therapy superior to either alone (1). Part (c): 7 marks — Re-assess: ensure it is truly MNE (query daytime symptoms, constipation, polydipsia, diabetes insipidus) (1). Address modifiable factors: restrict evening fluids (1). Re-start alarm with motivational support — involve child in choosing alarm, reward chart for waking, not just dryness (1). Add desmopressin 120–240 µg sublingual at bedtime — less hyponatraemia risk than nasal (1). Fluid restriction after dose (1). Combination alarm + desmopressin shown in RCT to be superior to either alone for severe enuresis (1). Plan for gradual withdrawal: once 14 consecutive dry nights, every-other-night desmopressin then stop (1). Part (d): 5 marks — Psychological impact: low self-esteem, social isolation, bullying, avoidance of activities (sleepovers, camps), depression risk (1). Enuresis is one of the most socially disabling chronic conditions in childhood (1). Ethical considerations: the adolescent's autonomy must be respected — he has capacity to refuse treatment (Gillick competence / Fraser guidelines) (1). However, fully informed decision requires understanding of consequences and that alternatives exist (1). MDT approach: involve psychologist, enuresis nurse specialist, explore reasons for refusal — sometimes perceived side effects, lack of efficacy, or stigma (1). Common errors: dismissing adolescent refusal as 'non-compliance' without exploring reasons; insisting on treatment against the child's will.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This adolescent likely has monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis (MNE) but re-assessment must confirm by ruling out daytime LUTS and constipation. Desmopressin (DDAVP) reduces nocturnal urine production by concentrating urine via V2 receptor agonism; sublingual 120–240 µg is first-line pharmacological therapy. The alarm works by classical conditioning — the child must wake to the alarm. He failed alarm likely because of short trial duration (2 weeks is insufficient — 6–12 weeks needed) and a deep sleep pattern. Combination therapy (alarm + desmopressin) has the highest success rate and is appropriate here. Starting with desmopressin alone initially over a few weeks to achieve some dry nights, then adding the alarm, often improves motivation. The psychological toll is significant — he avoids social activities. If he refuses treatment, his autonomy must be respected, but a skilled enuresis nurse can explore the barriers. Transition to adult enuresis services is available if needed.
discriminators: Distinguishes MNE from NMNE and links classification to treatment outcomes
Provides specific evidence percentages for desmopressin vs alarm (high-yield numbers)
Addresses adolescent psychology, consent, and Gillick competence
Recommends combination alarm + desmopressin as rescue for severe/refractory cases
commonErrors: Part (a): 5 marks — Monosymptomatic: nighttime wetting only, no daytime LUTS, no history of UTI or neurological abnormality (1). Non-monosymptomatic: presence of daytime symptoms (urgency, frequency, incontinence, dysfunctional voiding) or constipation (1). However, fully informed decision requires understanding of consequences and that alternatives exist (1). questions.title: Overactive bladder in children
questions.questionText:
A 5-year-old boy presents with urinary urgency, frequency (voiding 12 times daily), and occasional urge incontinence. He squats and crosses his legs to hold on (Vincent's curtsy). He is dry at night. His parents are concerned he may have a behavioural problem. Urinalysis is normal. Renal ultrasound and post-void residual are normal.

(a) [DEFINE] overactive bladder (OAB) in children and describe the expected urodynamic finding. [3 marks]
(b) [DISTINGUISH] OAB from dysfunctional voiding based on history, voiding pattern, and flow curve. [4 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the initial management (urotherapy) and specify when you would escalate to pharmacotherapy. [4 marks]
(d) [COMPARE] oxybutynin and solifenacin in terms of efficacy, side effect profile, and tolerability in children. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 3 marks — OAB: symptom complex of urgency, frequency ± urge incontinence (1). Pathophysiology: involuntary detrusor contractions during filling phase (1). Urodynamics: detrusor overactivity (phasic or terminal) during cystometry with or without leakage (1). Part (b): 4 marks — OAB: history of urgency, squatting, normal or bell-shaped flow curve (may be high amplitude, short duration), normal PVR (1). Dysfunctional voiding: staccato pattern, EMG-positive, elevated PVR, often associated with constipation (1). Squatting (Vincent curtsy) is a typical holding manoeuvre for OAB, NOT dysfunctional voiding (1). The distinction matters because OAB responds to anticholinergics; dysfunctional voiding requires biofeedback (1). Part (c): 4 marks — Urotherapy first: timed voiding (2–3 hourly), bladder diary, optimal toilet posture, treat constipation (1). Behavioural modification for 4–8 weeks (1). If inadequate response or persistent QoL impact → add pharmacotherapy (1). Anticholinergics (oxybutynin 0.2–0.4 mg/kg/day TDS or solifenacin weight-based dosing) after discussing side effects (1). Part (d): 4 marks — Oxybutynin: immediate-release has high anticholinergic burden — dry mouth (60%), constipation, blurred vision, CNS effects (somnolence, cognitive slowing) (1). Extended-release improves tolerability (1). Solifenacin: once-daily dosing, more M3-receptor selective, fewer CNS side effects, better tolerability profile (1). Both equally effective for OAB symptoms in children (1). Common errors: thinking anticholinergics are first-line; not counselling about CNS side effects of oxybutynin.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Overactive bladder is a symptom complex of urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence due to involuntary detrusor contractions (detrusor overactivity on urodynamics). It is distinct from dysfunctional voiding, where the pelvic floor contracts during voiding, producing staccato flow. Initial treatment is urotherapy (timed voiding, posture, bowel management) for 4–8 weeks. If symptoms persist, anticholinergics are added. Oxybutynin (0.2–0.4 mg/kg/day) is effective but has significant adverse effects including dry mouth, constipation, and CNS effects (somnolence). Solifenacin is M3-selective, once daily, with fewer CNS side effects. Extended-release formulations improve tolerability.
discriminators: Correctly distinguishes OAB from dysfunctional voiding
Recognises that first-line treatment is behavioural, not pharmacological
Compares anticholinergic profiles specifically in children

Urodynamics: detrusor overactivity (phasic or terminal) during cystometry with or without leakage (1). Part (b): 4 marks — OAB: history of urgency, squatting, normal or bell-shaped flow curve (may be high amplitude, short duration), normal PVR (1). questions.title: Biofeedback and non-pharmacological therapies for incontinence
questions.questionText:
A 9-year-old girl with non-monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis and daytime urge incontinence has failed 6 months of standard urotherapy and two anticholinergic trials (oxybutynin and solifenacin) due to side effects. Uroflowmetry shows a staccato pattern with increased EMG activity. Post-void residual is 60 mL. She has no neurological abnormality. Her mother is keen to avoid more medication.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the principle of biofeedback therapy for pelvic floor retraining in children with dysfunctional voiding. [4 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] how biofeedback is delivered in a paediatric setting, including practical techniques and typical treatment duration. [4 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the evidence base for biofeedback, including success rates and predictors of response. [4 marks]
(d) [RECOMMEND] alternative non-pharmacological strategies if biofeedback is unavailable or fails, including neuromodulation. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 4 marks — Biofeedback provides real-time visual/auditory feedback of a physiological process (pelvic floor EMG activity or flow pattern) (1). Child learns to recognise the sensation of pelvic floor relaxation and contract appropriately (1). Goal: teach 'inhibitory control' — pelvic floor relaxation before and during voiding (1). Positive reinforcement when correct relaxation pattern is achieved (1). Part (b): 4 marks — Typically 6–12 weekly sessions of 30 minutes (1). Perianal EMG electrodes or intra-anal probe (in adolescents) connected to a computer with animated games (1). Games reward pelvic floor relaxation (e.g. a fish swimming when relaxing, bubbles when contracting) (1). Home exercises between sessions using a handheld device or diary (1). Part (c): 4 marks — Success rate: 70–85% improvement in incontinence episodes (1). Predictors of response: child motivation, parental support, fewer UTIs, treatment-naive (1). Better results in dysfunctional voiding than OAB alone (1). Weaknesses: resource-intensive, requires trained therapist, younger children may not cooperate (1). Part (d): 3 marks — Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) — parasacral or posterior tibial nerve stimulation — shown to reduce OAB symptoms (1). Sacral neuromodulation (InterStim) — reserved for severe, refractory cases in older children/adolescents (1). Percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation (PTNS) — alternative if TENS fails (1). Common errors: not trying biofeedback before invasive therapies; dismissing non-pharmacological options as 'too slow'.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Biofeedback uses real-time EMG or uroflow signals to teach pelvic floor relaxation during voiding. Animated computer games provide reward-based learning. The child learns to recognise the sensation of a relaxed pelvic floor and to inhibit sphincter contraction during voiding. Therapy involves 6–12 weekly sessions. Evidence supports 70–85% improvement. If biofeedback fails or is unavailable, parasacral TENS is a good next step — it modulates sacral reflexes to reduce detrusor overactivity. Posterior tibial nerve stimulation (PTNS) is another option. In severe refractory cases, sacral neuromodulation can be considered, but requires surgical implantation of electrodes and is rarely used in young children.
discriminators: Explains biofeedback mechanism in practical, understandable terms
Provides evidence-based success rates
Discusses neuromodulation options as escalation pathway

Child learns to recognise the sensation of pelvic floor relaxation and contract appropriately (1). Goal: teach 'inhibitory control' — pelvic floor relaxation before and during voiding (1). questions.title: Long case: Complex incontinence in a child with associated bowel dysfunction and treatment fatigue
questions.questionText:
A 10-year-old girl presents with a 4-year history of daytime urinary incontinence and recurrent UTIs. She has been treated with three different anticholinergics, each stopped due to side effects or lack of efficacy. She has been on prophylactic antibiotics (trimethoprim) for 18 months. She is passing stool only every 3–4 days, often with painful defecation. She is overweight (BMI 28). Her parents are frustrated and considering seeking a second opinion privately. She says she 'doesn't want any more medicine' and reports feeling 'different' from her friends.

(a) [ASSESS] this patient's clinical situation, identifying all contributing factors to her refractory incontinence including biological and psychosocial elements. [6 marks]
(b) [DESIGN] a multidisciplinary treatment algorithm prioritising interventions in order of importance. [8 marks]
(c) [APPRAISE] the evidence for discontinuing prophylactic antibiotics once bladder-bowel dysfunction is managed, including risk of UTI recurrence. [5 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a communication strategy to manage parental frustration and the child's treatment fatigue, including setting realistic expectations. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 6 marks — Biological: chronic constipation (every 3–4 days) causing rectal distension → bladder compression → reduced functional capacity → detrusor instability (1). Possibly untreated or undertreated BBD (1). Overweight → increased intra-abdominal pressure → stress component? (1). Failed multiple anticholinergics → may need review of true bladder dysfunction type (urodynamics) (1). Psychosocial: treatment fatigue, poor adherence, peer stigma, parental frustration (1). Family dynamic: conflict, pressure on child, potential for over-medicalisation (1). Part (b): 8 marks — Step 1: Bowel management first — disimpact (polyethylene glycol 1–1.5 g/kg/day for 3 days) then maintenance (0.5 g/kg/day) to achieve daily soft stool (1). Step 2: Washout period off all anticholinergics (1). Step 3: Re-education — urotherapy refresher (1). Step 4: Consider urodynamics to classify bladder dysfunction (DO vs DV vs underactive) before restarting any medication (1). Step 5: If DO confirmed, consider solifenacin once daily (best-tolerated) or darifenacin (1). Step 6: If DV, biofeedback (1). Step 7: Consider stopping prophylactic antibiotics after bowel regimen established (1). Step 8: Weight management referral (dietitian, exercise), psychologist support (1). Part (c): 5 marks — Prophylactic antibiotics controversial in BBD; evidence does not show benefit once bladder-bowel dysfunction is treated (1). RIVUR trial showed that in children with VUR and BBD, antibiotic prophylaxis reduced UTI but at cost of resistance (1). Once bowel is managed and PVR normalises, the UTI risk decreases significantly (1). Stopping prophylaxis reduces antimicrobial resistance and side effects (1). Shared decision-making: if UTIs recur after stopping, can restart (1). Part (d): 6 marks — Acknowledge parental frustration and child's exhaustion with treatment (1). Explain that the focus will shift from medication to bowel management, which is often the missing piece (1). Set realistic goals: not 'cure' but number of dry hours, number of UTIs, daily bowel movements (1). Involve child in decision-making: ask what she wants (dry during school, sleepovers) (1). Consider psychologist for body image, self-esteem, and adherence (1). Arrange a joint meeting with school nurse to support her at school (1). Common errors: ignoring the psychosocial dimensions; escalating anticholinergics without addressing constipation.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This child has refractory daytime incontinence driven primarily by chronic constipation (BBD). The rectal distension from constipation reduces functional bladder capacity and triggers detrusor overactivity — no anticholinergic can overcome a stool-distended rectum. Step 1 is aggressive bowel management: disimpacting with high-dose PEG, then maintenance to achieve daily soft stools. Second, stop all anticholinergics and prophylactic antibiotics. Third, perform video-urodynamics to correctly classify bladder dysfunction. Fourth, restart appropriate targeted pharmacotherapy if needed (solifenacin once daily for tolerability). The RIVUR trial suggests that once BBD is treated, UTI risk falls and prophylaxis can be stopped. The parents need validation of their concerns, a clear plan with measurable goals (daily bowel movement, fewer wetting episodes), and involvement of a psychologist for the child's treatment fatigue and low self-esteem. The goal is not perfect dryness but functional improvement and quality of life.
discriminators: Prioritises bowel management over all other interventions
Recommends urodynamics to avoid misclassification after anticholinergic failure
Addresses treatment fatigue and parental frustration as clinical problems requiring active management
Supports discontinuation of antibiotic prophylaxis when BBD is controlled

questions.title: Extended Matching Question — Incontinence symptom recognition and initial management
questions.questionText:
Theme: Initial management of childhood incontinence

Options:
A. Standard urotherapy (timed voiding, posture, hydration, bowel management)
B. Alarm therapy alone
C. Desmopressin therapy alone
D. Combination alarm + desmopressin
E. Oxybutynin immediate-release
F. Solifenacin once daily
G. Biofeedback therapy
H. Urodynamic investigation before treatment
I. Clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC)
J. Referral to paediatric psychology
K. Urology referral for possible surgery

Scenarios:
1. A 6-year-old boy with only nighttime wetting (5–7 nights/week), no daytime symptoms, normal urinalysis, no constipation.
2. A 7-year-old girl with urgency, frequency (14 voids/day), and urge incontinence. Uroflow shows bell-shaped curve, normal PVR.
3. A 9-year-old girl with daytime incontinence, staccato flow pattern on uroflow, elevated EMG during voiding, and PVR 90 mL.
4. A 14-year-old boy with nighttime wetting who has tried desmopressin alone and alarm alone both with initial improvement but relapse. He is motivated to try again.
5. An 8-year-old girl with continuous dribbling incontinence (not in a stream), normal voiding frequency, and a normal neurological exam.
6. A 6-year-old boy with daytime incontinence who also has urinary frequency, polydipsia, and polyuria (3000 mL/day). He has no nocturia.
questions.markingGuide:
Each correct answer = 2 marks. Key knowledge points: alarm vs desmopressin indications, distinguishing DO from DV, recognising red flags for structural/neurological causes. Common errors: giving biofeedback for OAB, treating MNE with anticholinergics, missing the ectopic ureter presentation.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Stem 1 = C, Stem 2 = A, Stem 3 = G, Stem 4 = D, Stem 5 = H, Stem 6 = H.
discriminators: Tests ability to distinguish structural from functional incontinence
Includes a red flag stem (ectopic ureter — continuous dribbling)
Includes a medical mimic stem (diabetes insipidus)
Requires knowledge of combination therapy evidence
commonErrors: Each correct answer = 2 marks. Key knowledge points: alarm vs desmopressin indications, distinguishing DO from DV, recognising red flags for structural/neurological causes. Common errors: giving biofeedback for OAB, treating MNE with anticholinergics, missing the ectopic ureter presentation.

questions.title: Myelomeningocele and neurogenic bladder — initial assessment and management in a newborn
questions.questionText:
A newborn girl (day 2 of life) is diagnosed with a lumbosacral myelomeningocele at L4 level. She has a flaccid lower limb paralysis and absent anal tone. The myelomeningocele was closed on day 1. A renal ultrasound shows mild bilateral hydronephrosis (renal pelvic diameter 8 mm). Serum creatinine is normal at 45 mumol/L. She has not yet voided spontaneously. The neonatology team requests your advice on urological management.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the expected bladder and sphincter dysfunction based on the level of the spinal lesion (L4) and the neurological examination findings. [6 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the immediate urological investigations you would perform in the neonatal period, including imaging and urodynamics, and their timing. [7 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a management plan for this newborn including CIC initiation, anticholinergic therapy, and prevention of renal deterioration. [7 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the role of the multidisciplinary team in the neonatal period and how you would counsel the parents about long-term prognosis. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 6 marks — L4 lesion: generally incomplete cord injury; sacral reflex arc may be partially spared (1). Typically produces a balanced or areflexic bladder early; may develop DSD later (1). Lower motor neuron signs (flaccid paralysis, absent anal tone) suggest sacral root involvement — likely acontractile bladder with denervated sphincter (1). Cannot predict bladder type from level alone — urodynamics essential (1). Early DSD and high-pressure storage is the dangerous pattern (1). Part (b): 7 marks — Renal US already done: document baseline hydronephrosis (1). MCU at 4-6 weeks: assess for VUR, bladder contour, post-void residual (1). Video-urodynamics ideally at 3 months: assess detrusor filling pressure, compliance, leak-point pressure, DSD (1). DMSA scan at 3-6 months: baseline renal parenchyma (1). Ultrasound repeated at 3 months (1). Urodynamics before starting anticholinergics (1). Some units perform early urodynamics at 4-6 weeks (1). Part (c): 7 marks — CIC: start if PVR >50% bladder capacity or DLPP >40 cmH2O or hydronephrosis (1). CIC 4-6 hourly, 8-10Fr catheter, clean technique (1). Anticholinergics: oxybutynin 0.2 mg/kg TDS increasing to 0.4 mg/kg (1). Goal: low-pressure storage (<40 cmH2O), adequate capacity, complete emptying (1). Prophylactic antibiotics not routinely indicated (1). Monitor with serial US and urine dipstick (1). Deterioration despite CIC and anticholinergics leads to escalation: botulinum toxin, surgery (1). Part (d): 5 marks — MDT: neurosurgeon (spinal closure, VP shunt), urologist, orthopaedic surgeon, physical therapist, occupational therapist, social worker, psychologist (1). Lifelong bladder management (CIC) likely needed (1). Goals: preserve renal function (primary), achieve continence (secondary) (1). Bowel management (transanal irrigation, ACE procedure in future) (1). Introduce family to support groups (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
At L4 level, the lesion may produce either an areflexic or a hyperreflexic bladder depending on sacral root involvement. Clinical examination shows lower motor neuron signs below L4, but urodynamics is required for definitive classification. Immediate investigations: renal US done; next, MCU at 4-6 weeks to assess VUR, then video-urodynamics at 3 months. The presence of bilateral hydronephrosis indicates high-pressure storage or incomplete emptying. CIC should be started 4-6 hourly with a 10Fr catheter using clean technique. Oxybutynin 0.2 mg/kg TDS is initiated to improve compliance and lower detrusor pressure. The target is DLPP <40 cmH2O and adequate bladder capacity for age. MDT involvement is critical. Parental counselling should be honest but hopeful: preservation of renal function is the primary goal; social continence can often be achieved with CIC plus or minus surgery later in childhood.
discriminators: Recognises the limitation of predicting bladder type from lesion level alone
Provides specific timing for urodynamics and imaging in neonates
Links hydronephrosis directly to elevated detrusor pressure and need for CIC
Emphasises MDT coordination from birth
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Augmentation cystoplasty and Mitrofanoff in adolescence — transition and shared decision-making
questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old boy with myelomeningocele (thoracolumbar level, VP shunt in situ) is referred for consideration of augmentation cystoplasty. He has been managed with CIC and oxybutynin since infancy but has developed progressively deteriorating bladder compliance despite maximum anticholinergic therapy. His current bladder capacity is 150 mL (expected capacity approximately 400 mL). Detrusor leak-point pressure is 45 cmH2O. He has had two febrile UTIs in the past year with new right-sided VUR grade II on MCU. He is wheelchair-dependent, cognitively normal, and has been performing his own CIC since age 12. He is beginning to think about university and independent living.

(a) [EVALUATE] the indications for augmentation cystoplasty in this adolescent, referencing urodynamic parameters and clinical deterioration. [6 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the surgical technique of clam ileocystoplasty and the Mitrofanoff procedure, including important technical considerations in a previously operated abdomen. [8 marks]
(c) [ANALYSE] the long-term metabolic, infective, and neoplastic complications of enterocystoplasty and how you would counsel the patient about these risks. [6 marks]
(d) [PROPOSE] a transition plan to adult urology services, including timing, documentation, and key aspects of self-management. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 6 marks — Indications: refractory DSD/high-pressure bladder despite maximal medical therapy (1). DLPP >40 cmH2O (1). Low bladder capacity (<50% expected for age) (1). New-onset VUR or renal deterioration (1). Recurrent febrile UTIs due to high-pressure storage (1). Failed or unsuitable for botulinum toxin A (1). Part (b): 8 marks — Clam ileocystoplasty: 20-25 cm of terminal ileum isolated on vascular pedicle (1). Bowel continuity restored (1). Bladder bivalved in coronal plane from one ureteric orifice across dome to the other (1). Ileal patch anastomosed to bivalved bladder (1). Mitrofanoff: appendix (preferred) or ileum tunnelled into the bladder, brought to umbilicus or lower abdomen (1). Appendix must be of adequate length and lumen (1). Stoma at umbilicus for cosmesis (1). In redo abdomen: adhesiolysis, identification of right colon and appendix, careful entry to bladder (1). Part (c): 6 marks — Metabolic: hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis — 15-20% require bicarbonate supplementation (1). Bone demineralisation (rickets, osteomalacia) (1). Infection: bacteriuria universal — treat only symptomatic UTIs (1). Mucus production may cause obstruction — regular bladder washouts (1). Stones: 10-30% incidence — washouts and surveillance (1). Malignancy: adenocarcinoma at enterovesical anastomosis — 1-4% at 10-20 years; annual cystoscopy from 10 years post-op (1). Part (d): 5 marks — Transition starts at age 14-16 with structured programme (1). Transition clinic with paediatric and adult urologist present (1). Written summary: diagnosis, operations, medications, CIC regimen (1). Adolescent takes increasing responsibility (1). Discuss fertility and sexual health — men with myelomeningocele often have erectile dysfunction, ejaculatory issues; refer to fertility specialist if desired (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This adolescent has clear indications for augmentation: DLPP >40 cmH2O, low capacity, new VUR, and febrile UTIs despite maximal anticholinergics. Botulinum toxin A is an alternative but has limited duration (6-12 months) and is less suitable for a young man planning transition who needs a durable solution. Clam ileocystoplasty with Mitrofanoff (appendix) is appropriate. The ileal segment must be detubularised to reduce contractility. Post-operatively, CIC via the Mitrofanoff stoma (catheterisable channel) makes self-catheterisation easier. Long-term risks include metabolic acidosis (monitor serum bicarbonate, treat with oral bicarbonate/citrate), recurrent UTIs (treat only febrile/symptomatic), stone formation (10-30% — annual US, bladder washouts), and malignancy (adenocarcinoma risk — annual cystoscopy from 10 years post-op). He must understand these risks before surgery. Transition should begin now (age 16) with a joint paediatric-adult clinic. He should be able to independently manage his CIC, know which medications he takes, and understand when to seek help. Fertility counselling: men with myelomeningocele may have erectile dysfunction and retrograde ejaculation; sperm retrieval and assisted reproduction are options.
discriminators: Provides objective urodynamic thresholds for augmentation
Details surgical technique for both clam and Mitrofanoff
Addresses malignancy risk with specific surveillance protocol
Integrates transition planning and sexual health counselling
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: CIC technique and timing in neurogenic bladder
questions.questionText:
A 6-week-old infant with myelomeningocele (L5 level) is referred for urological assessment. Renal US is normal. MCU shows no VUR. The parents are trained in CIC by the clinical nurse specialist. You are asked to review the CIC protocol and provide a management plan.

(a) [STATE] the recommended CIC regimen for a 6-week-old infant, including catheter size, frequency, and technique (clean vs sterile). [4 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] how you would determine whether CIC is necessary in this infant, referencing objective urodynamic criteria. [4 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] the complications of CIC in infancy and strategies to minimise them. [4 marks]
(d) [COMPARE] clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC) with indwelling catheterisation and suprapubic catheterisation in the paediatric neurogenic bladder population. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 4 marks — Catheter size: 8-10Fr (smallest that ensures adequate drainage) (1). Frequency: every 3-4 hours (6-7 times daily) (1). Technique: clean (not sterile) — washed hands, clean catheter, soap and water for meatus (1). Single-use disposable catheters vs reusable — single-use preferred but cost may dictate (1). Part (b): 4 marks — CIC indicated if: detrusor leak-point pressure >40 cmH2O (1), post-void residual >50% of expected bladder capacity (1), or evidence of upper tract deterioration (hydronephrosis, VUR) (1). Prophylactic CIC in all myelomeningocele infants remains controversial — some units start selectively based on urodynamics (1). Part (c): 4 marks — UTI: most common — risk reduced by clean technique, complete emptying, frequency (1). Urethral trauma/false passage: use smallest adequate catheter, gentle insertion, lubricant (1). Bleeding: usually self-limiting (1). Bacteriuria is common and does not require treatment unless symptomatic (1). Part (d): 3 marks — CIC: preferred — lowest infection rate, preserves body image, independence (1). Indwelling urethral catheter: worst option — biofilm, recurrent UTIs, bladder stones, urethral erosion (1). Suprapubic catheter: alternative if CIC not possible (poor dexterity, caregiver burden) — fewer UTIs than urethral but still higher than CIC; requires surgical placement and stoma care (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
For a 6-week-old, CIC with 8-10Fr catheter every 3-4 hours using clean (not sterile) technique is standard. CIC is indicated if urodynamics show DLPP >40 cmH2O, poor compliance, or PVR >50% of bladder capacity. In this infant with normal US and no VUR, baseline urodynamics at 2-3 months will determine need. CIC carries UTI risk (minimised by frequency, complete emptying, and clean technique). Asymptomatic bacteriuria is expected and not treated. CIC is superior to indwelling catheters which have high infection and stone rates.
discriminators: Specifies exact catheter size (8-10Fr) and frequency (3-4 hourly)
Clarifies clean vs sterile debate in community setting
Distinguishes CIC from indwelling alternatives with rationale
commonErrors: Part (a): 4 marks — Catheter size: 8-10Fr (smallest that ensures adequate drainage) (1). Frequency: every 3-4 hours (6-7 times daily) (1). Technique: clean (not sterile) — washed hands, clean catheter, soap and water for meatus (1). Single-use disposable catheters vs reusable — single-use preferred but cost may dictate (1). Part (b): 4 marks — CIC indicated if: detrusor leak-point pressure >40 cmH2O (1), post-void residual >50% of expected bladder capacity (1), or evidence of upper tract deterioration (hydronephrosis, VUR) (1). Bacteriuria is common and does not require treatment unless symptomatic (1). Part (d): 3 marks — CIC: preferred — lowest infection rate, preserves body image, independence (1). Indwelling urethral catheter: worst option — biofilm, recurrent UTIs, bladder stones, urethral erosion (1). Suprapubic catheter: alternative if CIC not possible (poor dexterity, caregiver burden) — fewer UTIs than urethral but still higher than CIC; requires surgical placement and stoma care (1).

questions.title: Pharmacological escalation in neurogenic detrusor overactivity
questions.questionText:
A 4-year-old boy with myelomeningocele (sacral level) has been on CIC and oxybutynin 0.4 mg/kg/day (TDS) since infancy. Recent video-urodynamics show persistent detrusor overactivity with poor compliance (bladder capacity 80 mL, expected approximately 150 mL) and DLPP 42 cmH2O. He has had two breakthrough UTIs. His mother reports that he experiences flushing and drowsiness after oxybutynin doses.

(a) [LIST] the available pharmacological options for managing refractory detrusor overactivity in neurogenic bladder after failure of immediate-release oxybutynin. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the mechanism of action, evidence, and protocol for botulinum toxin A (Botox) injection into the detrusor in children. [5 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the risks, benefits, and consent process for botulinum toxin A in a 4-year-old boy requiring repeat injections. [3 marks]
(d) [RECOMMEND] when surgical augmentation should be considered over continued pharmacological escalation. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 4 marks — Options after failed immediate-release oxybutynin: extended-release oxybutynin (once daily, better tolerability) (1). Solifenacin (once daily, M3-selective, fewer CNS effects) (1). Trospium (non-CNS-penetrating, fewer cognitive effects) (1). Botulinum toxin A intra-detrusor injection (1). Part (b): 5 marks — Botulinum toxin A: cleaves SNAP-25 protein, preventing acetylcholine release at presynaptic parasympathetic nerve terminals, reducing detrusor contraction (1). Dose: 10-12 U/kg (max 300 U) in 20-30 injections divided across detrusor (avoid trigone) (1). Cystoscopic injection under GA (1). Effect lasts 6-12 months (1). Evidence: improvement in bladder compliance, capacity, DLPP; reduces incontinence episodes (1). Part (c): 3 marks — Risks: haematuria, UTI, urinary retention (already on CIC), general anaesthetic (1). Benefits: may delay or avoid augmentation surgery, improves continence, low systemic side effect profile (1). Consent: explain repeat injections needed, discuss alternatives (higher-dose anticholinergics, neuromodulation, augmentation) (1). Part (d): 3 marks — Consider augmentation if: 2+ botulinum toxin A treatments fail (1), or bladder compliance <20 mL/cmH2O with DLPP >40 cmH2O (1), or renal deterioration persists despite maximal therapy (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
After failed immediate-release oxybutynin at maximum dose, options include extended-release oxybutynin, solifenacin (once daily), trospium (non-CNS-penetrating), or intra-detrusor botulinum toxin A. Botulinum toxin A (200-300 U divided into 20-30 injections sparing the trigone) improves bladder compliance and capacity, reducing DLPP and breakthrough UTIs. The effect lasts 6-12 months; repeat injections are safe. In this 4-year-old, a trial of solifenacin or extended-release oxybutynin (given his side effects) would be appropriate before or alongside botulinum toxin. Surgical augmentation is indicated if botulinum toxin fails twice or renal function deteriorates.
discriminators: Provides anticholinergic escalation ladder with rationale for each switch
Gives specific botulinum toxin A dosing parameters in children
Defines clear threshold for augmentation after botulinum failure
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Long case: Adolescent with a previously augmented bladder — complications and bladder cancer risk
questions.questionText:
A 22-year-old woman with myelomeningocele underwent clam ileocystoplasty with Mitrofanoff (appendix) at age 10. She has been well and continent on CIC via Mitrofanoff. She presents with a 3-month history of intermittent haematuria and right flank pain. Urine culture is negative. Renal ultrasound shows a 2 cm right renal pelvis stone and a 1.5 cm stone in the augmented bladder. Serum creatinine is 80 umol/L. She has not attended follow-up for 5 years. She now presents to the adult urology clinic for the first time.

(a) [INVESTIGATE] the potential causes of haematuria in a patient with enterocystoplasty, listing the most important diagnoses to exclude. [6 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] the association between enterocystoplasty and bladder cancer, including histology, latency, risk factors, and surveillance recommendations. [7 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a management plan for the renal and bladder calculi in this augmented bladder, including the technical challenges of stone surgery in this setting. [6 marks]
(d) [PROPOSE] a long-term follow-up protocol for this patient, including monitoring for metabolic complications, stones, and malignancy. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 6 marks — Most important: adenocarcinoma at enterovesical anastomosis (1). Stones (both renal and bladder) are common — 10-30% (1). Urinary tract infection (including PUC — pouchitis) (1). Stomal stenosis or false passage causing haematuria from Mitrofanoff (1). Metabolic acidosis-related bone disease (rare cause of haematuria) (1). Menstrual bleeding must be excluded in women of reproductive age (1). Part (b): 7 marks — Adenocarcinoma (most common, 60-70%), squamous and transitional cell also reported (1). Latency: typically 10-20 years (mean 15 years) after augmentation (1). Risk factors: chronic inflammation, bacteriuria, nitrosamine production, use of colonic vs ileal segments (colonic higher risk) (1). Incidence: 1-4% at 10-20 years (1). Surveillance: annual ultrasound and urine cytology (1). Cystoscopy + biopsy of Augmentation at 10 years post-op, then annually (1). Some advocate screening starting at 5 years if colonic segment used (1). Part (c): 6 marks — Renal stone (2 cm): PCNL or ESWL depending on location and hardness (1). Bladder stone (1.5 cm): cystolitholapaxy via Mitrofanoff stoma or via urethra (1). Technical challenges: augmented bladder has mucus, potentially irregular contour, stone fragments can be hidden in ileal segment (1). Mitrofanoff channel may be narrow — may need dilation (1). Ensure complete clearance to prevent recurrence (1). Post-operative CT to confirm stone-free (1). Part (d): 6 marks — Annual: renal US, serum creatinine, electrolytes, bicarbonate, urine culture (only if symptomatic) (1). 6-monthly: urine cytology (1). 10 years post-op: annual cystoscopy with biopsy of augmentation (1). Vitamin B12 monitoring (terminal ileum resected) (1). Bone density if metabolic acidosis (1). Transition documentation complete — ensure she understands the surveillance protocol (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This patient presents with haematuria and bilateral stones. The most concerning diagnosis is adenocarcinoma at the enterovesical anastomosis, which has a latency of 10-20 years. She is now 12 years post-augmentation. Workup includes CT urogram, urine cytology, and cystoscopy with biopsy of the augmentation. Cancer risk is 1-4% at 10-20 years; surveillance should now include annual cystoscopy. The stones (2 cm renal, 1.5 cm bladder) likely result from chronic bacteriuria, mucus, and metabolic disturbances. Renal stone can be managed by PCNL; bladder stone by cystolitholapaxy via the Mitrofanoff. Complete clearance is essential. Long-term: annual US, serum creatinine and bicarbonate, vitamin B12 monitoring, and annual cystoscopy. She must understand the importance of lifelong follow-up.
discriminators: Recognises adenocarcinoma as the most serious cause of haematuria in augmented bladders
Provides specific surveillance protocol with cystoscopy timing
Addresses technical challenges of stone surgery in augmented bladders
Includes vitamin B12 monitoring as a metabolic consequence of terminal ileal resection

questions.title: Extended Matching Question — Neurogenic bladder management
questions.questionText:
Theme: Management of neurogenic bladder in children and adolescents

Options:
A. Start CIC 4-6 hourly with 8-10Fr catheter
B. Start oxybutynin 0.2 mg/kg TDS
C. Add solifenacin (switch from oxybutynin) due to side effects
D. Refer for intra-detrusor botulinum toxin A injection
E. Refer for augmentation cystoplasty
F. Perform video-urodynamics before any intervention
G. Start prophylactic antibiotics
H. Perform renal US and MCU in 3 months
I. Refer to adult urology for transition
J. Start desmopressin for nocturnal polyuria

Scenarios:
1. A 3-month-old infant with myelomeningocele (L4 level), normal renal US, normal MCU. No hydronephrosis. Parent has been taught CIC but is unsure if it is needed yet.
2. A 5-year-old boy with myelomeningocele on CIC and oxybutynin 0.4 mg/kg/day. Urodynamics show DLPP 48 cmH2O, capacity 60% expected. He has drowsiness after oxybutynin.
3. A 10-year-old girl with myelomeningocele on CIC, oxybutynin, and solifenacin at maximal doses. Urodynamics still show DLPP 45 cmH2O with poor compliance. She has had two febrile UTIs in 6 months.
4. A 6-week-old with myelomeningocele (L3 level), bilateral hydronephrosis (APD 10 mm), and VUR grade III on MCU. Creatinine normal.
5. A 17-year-old boy with myelomeningocele who has been stable on CIC and solifenacin for 5 years. He is about to finish school and has questions about managing his bladder independently at university.
6. A 7-year-old with myelomeningocele on CIC and oxybutynin who develops breakthrough febrile UTI. Urine culture grows ESBL E. coli. Renal US shows new mild hydronephrosis.
questions.markingGuide:
Each correct answer = 2 marks. Key testing points: when to start CIC, when to switch anticholinergics, thresholds for surgical escalation, importance of transition, and need for urodynamics before changing management.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Stem 1 = F, Stem 2 = C, Stem 3 = E, Stem 4 = A, Stem 5 = I, Stem 6 = F.
discriminators: Tests appropriate escalation through anticholinergics to surgery
Includes transition planning for adolescents
Assesses when to perform urodynamics vs when to act empirically
Requires knowledge of oxybutynin side effect profile and switching strategies
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Pena classification and initial urological assessment of a newborn with ARM
questions.questionText:
A newborn male infant is diagnosed with an anorectal malformation (ARM). He has no visible anal opening and passes meconium through the urethra. He has a palpable abdominal mass. Spinal ultrasound reveals a tethered cord. Renal ultrasound shows left renal agenesis and right hydronephrosis (APD 12 mm). The neonatology team requests your urological assessment on day 1 of life.

(a) [CLASSIFY] this anorectal malformation according to the Pena system, explaining the basis for your classification from the clinical findings. [5 marks]
(b) [EVALUATE] the urological anomalies detected and describe the embryological basis for the association between ARM and the VACTERL spectrum. [6 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the immediate urological investigations required in the first week of life and the rationale for each. [6 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a staged urological surveillance protocol from birth through adolescence for a child with ARM and known urological anomalies. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — Passing meconium through urethra indicates a rectourethral fistula (1). This is a high ARM (above the levator ani) according to the Pena classification (1). In the Pena system: high = rectovesical or rectoprostatic fistula; intermediate = rectobulbar fistula; low = perineal fistula, anal stenosis (1). This patient has a rectoprostatic/rectobulbar fistula — high/intermediate (1). Palpable abdominal mass may represent dilated rectum or associated hydronephrosis/renal agenesis (1). Part (b): 6 marks — VACTERL: Vertebral, Anorectal, Cardiac, Tracheo-Esophageal, Renal, Limb (1). At least 3 anomalies needed for diagnosis — this infant has ARM + renal agenesis + hydronephrosis + tethered cord (vertebral/sacral) (1). Embryology: urorectal septum divides cloaca into urogenital sinus and anorectum at 4-8 weeks (1). Failure of septation leads to persistent cloaca or ARM with fistula (1). The mesonephric duct (Wolffian) contributes to both urinary tract and hindgut development — explains concurrent anomalies (1). Sacral agenesis/dysgenesis commonly associated (1). Part (c): 6 marks — Day 1: renal US (already done) — assess hydronephrosis, renal agenesis (1). MCU (voiding cystourethrogram) — look for VUR, confirm fistula type (rectourethral), assess bladder emptying (1). Spinal US — assess tethered cord, sacral ratio (1). Serum creatinine — baseline renal function (1). MAG3/DMSA — if hydronephrosis significant, assess split renal function (1). Echocardiogram — VACTERL screening includes cardiac anomalies (1). Part (d): 8 marks — Neonatal period: as above (1). Age 3 months: repeat renal US, consider VCUG if not done (1). Age 6 months: DMSA if hydronephrosis or VUR was present (1). Age 1 year: renal US, assess for UTIs, continence (1). Age 2-3 years: assess bladder function (voiding diary, uroflow if possible), MCU if recurrent UTIs (1). Age 5 years: uroflowmetry + PVR, continence assessment, renal US (1). Puberty: reassess renal function, blood pressure, continence, sexual function counselling (1). Lifelong: BP monitoring, annual urinalysis, renal US every 2-3 years if structural anomaly present (1). Common errors: forgetting to screen for associated anomalies outside the urinary tract (cardiac, spinal).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This male infant has a high ARM with rectourethral fistula (meconium in urine). The Pena classification differentiates high (supralevator), intermediate, and low (translevator) based on the relationship to the levator ani. He has left renal agenesis, right hydronephrosis, and tethered cord — likely VACTERL association (vertebral, ARM, cardiac, TE, renal, limb). The embryological basis relates to abnormal development of the urorectal septum at 4-8 weeks gestation. Immediate urological workup: renal US, MCU (to define fistula and VUR), serum creatinine, and spinal US. Tethered cord requires neurosurgical evaluation. Long-term: serial renal US and MCU scheduled through childhood, bladder function monitoring, and transition to adult care. Children with ARM require lifelong urological surveillance because of the risk of progressive renal damage from VUR, bladder dysfunction, and neurogenic bladder from tethered cord.
discriminators: Correctly classifies ARM using Pena system based on clinical presentation
Links embryology to concurrent urogenital anomalies
Provides a staged surveillance protocol from birth to adulthood
Integrates VACTERL screening into the assessment

questions.title: Tethered cord, caudal regression, and long-term urological outcome in ARM
questions.questionText:
A 12-year-old girl born with a cloacal anomaly (common channel 3 cm) that was repaired in infancy is referred for ongoing urinary incontinence. She has never achieved complete daytime dryness and has intermittent UTIs. She has a neurogenic bladder pattern on urodynamics (detrusor overactivity, poor compliance, DLPP 35 cmH2O). Spinal MRI shows tethered cord with a fatty filum terminale and a low-lying conus at L3-L4 level. She also has sacral agenesis (partial, S3-S5 absent). Her parents are distressed that she still wets despite multiple surgeries.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the association between anorectal malformations, sacral anomalies, tethered cord, and neurogenic bladder dysfunction. [6 marks]
(b) [EVALUATE] the role of neurosurgical detethering in improving bladder function in this child, citing the evidence for its efficacy and timing. [6 marks]
(c) [DESIGN] a comprehensive bladder management plan for this adolescent girl, addressing both the neurogenic component and the previous cloacal reconstruction. [8 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the ethical and practical issues around consent for treatment in an adolescent with complex congenital anomalies, including the transition to adult services. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 6 marks — ARM and sacral anomalies share embryological origin (caudal cell mass, notochord) (1). Incidence: 30-50% of ARM patients have spinal anomalies (tethered cord, sacral agenesis, lipomeningocele) (1). Caudal regression syndrome: spectrum from sacral agenesis to sirenomelia, associated with maternal diabetes (1). Tethered cord causes traction on conus medullaris, leading to ischaemia and progressive neurological deficit (1). Neurogenic bladder results from impaired sacral reflex arc (detrusor-sphincter dysfunction) (1). Sacral ratio <0.4 predicts neurogenic bladder (1). Part (b): 6 marks — Detethering releases the conus by dividing the fatty filum (1). Evidence for urological benefit: retrospective series show improvement/stabilisation in 40-60% (1). Improvement more likely if performed early (<2 years) and before significant neurological deterioration (1). In an adolescent with established neurogenic bladder (12 years old), detethering is less likely to reverse bladder dysfunction (1). However, it may prevent further deterioration of lower limb neurology and bladder function (1). Should be performed in symptomatic tethered cord (orthopaedic, neurological, urological decline) (1). Part (c): 8 marks — Start: optimise CIC regimen (4-6 hourly via urethra or Mitrofanoff if previous reconstruction) (1). Anticholinergics: solifenacin once daily (better tolerability) (1). Consider botulinum toxin A if DLPP remains >40 cmH2O (1). Augmentation cystoplasty if refractory (1). Bowel management: antegrade continence enema (ACE) via appendix or Malone stoma (1). Vaginal dilation counselling if needed (cloacal anomaly may have vaginal septum/stenosis) (1). Address previous reconstruction: may need revision of catheterisable channel (1). Psychological support for body image and continence (1). Part (d): 5 marks — Adolescent consent: Gillick competence — she must be involved in all decisions (1). Treatment fatigue is common — explore her goals (what does she want? dryness, independence, sleepovers?) (1). Ethics: balancing pressure from parents for further surgery vs adolescent autonomy (1). Transition: structured transition to adult urology by age 16-18, including joint clinic, written summary, and introduction to adult team (1). Fertility and sexual health counselling — cloacal anomalies may affect fertility, vaginal function (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This adolescent has a neurogenic bladder from tethered cord and sacral agenesis associated with her cloacal anomaly. The risk of neurogenic bladder in ARM is highest when the sacral ratio is <0.4 or there is an obvious spinal anomaly. Tethered cord causes progressive neurological damage; detethering improves bladder function in 40-60% of children if performed early. However, at age 12 with established bladder dysfunction, the goal of detethering is to prevent further deterioration rather than achieve reversal. Her bladder management should include CIC (possibly via Mitrofanoff if previous cloacal reconstruction makes urethral access difficult), anticholinergics (solifenacin), and consideration of botulinum toxin A or augmentation. Bowel management with an ACE procedure is often needed. She must be at the centre of all decisions — her goals for continence and independence should guide treatment. Transition planning should start now with joint paediatric-adult clinics.
discriminators: Explains the embryological link between ARM, sacral anomalies, and neurogenic bladder
Provides evidence-based efficacy of detethering and recognises its limitations in older children
Designs a comprehensive bladder and bowel management plan integrated with previous reconstruction
Addresses adolescent consent, treatment fatigue, and transition as core clinical issues

questions.title: Urologic surveillance in anorectal malformations
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old girl born with a rectovestibular fistula (low ARM) underwent posterior sagittal anorectoplasty (PSARP) as a newborn. She had a normal spinal ultrasound and normal renal US at birth. She has been well with no UTIs. Her parents ask whether she needs any further kidney or bladder checks.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the recommended urological surveillance schedule for a child with a low ARM and no identified anomalies at birth. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] why children with even low ARMs can develop bladder dysfunction later in childhood. [3 marks]
(c) [STATE] the components of a clinical continence assessment in a 3-5 year old with ARM. [4 marks]
(d) [LIST] the indications for performing a spinal MRI in a child with ARM. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 4 marks — Neonatal: renal US, spinal US (1). Age 3 months: renal US (1). Age 6-12 months: renal US, consider MCU if hydronephrosis (1). Age 3-5 years: continence assessment, uroflow + PVR, renal US (1). Part (b): 3 marks — Low ARMs have lower but not zero risk of neurogenic bladder (1). Occult tethered cord can present later with growth (1). Constipation (common after ARM repair) can cause bladder dysfunction via BBD (1). Part (c): 4 marks — Daytime dryness by age 5 (for low/intermediate ARM, achieve in 60-80%) (1). Voiding frequency and urgency assessment (1). Uroflowmetry + PVR (normal bell-shaped curve, PVR <20 mL) (1). History of UTIs (1). Part (d): 4 marks — Sacral abnormality on imaging (agenesis, hemivertebra) (1). Neuro-orthopaedic signs (lower limb weakness, abnormal gait, foot deformity) (1). Abnormal neurological examination (anal tone, perianal sensation, bulbocavernosus reflex) (1). Deteriorating bladder function or new-onset incontinence (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Even with a low ARM and normal initial imaging, surveillance is recommended: renal US at 3 and 12 months, then continence assessment with uroflow + PVR at 3-5 years. Bladder dysfunction can develop from occult tethered cord (may present later with growth) or from chronic constipation (bladder bowel dysfunction). Clinical continence assessment at 3-5 years should evaluate dryness, voiding pattern, UTIs, and include uroflow + PVR. Spinal MRI is indicated if there are sacral anomalies, neurological signs, or deteriorating bladder function.
discriminators: Recognises that even low ARMs need urological surveillance
Explains the mechanism of late-presenting bladder dysfunction
Defines specific indications for spinal MRI
commonErrors: Part (a): 4 marks — Neonatal: renal US, spinal US (1). Age 3 months: renal US (1). Age 6-12 months: renal US, consider MCU if hydronephrosis (1). Age 3-5 years: continence assessment, uroflow + PVR, renal US (1). Part (b): 3 marks — Low ARMs have lower but not zero risk of neurogenic bladder (1). questions.title: Genitourinary fistulas in anorectal malformations
questions.questionText:
A newborn female is diagnosed with a cloacal anomaly. She has a single perineal opening, a 2.5 cm common channel, and a distended abdomen. Renal US shows bilateral hydronephrosis (APD 9 mm left, 11 mm right). A vaginostomy is performed on day 2 for hydrocolpos. You are asked to assess and plan the urological aspects of her reconstruction.

(a) [DEFINE] the types of genitourinary fistulas seen in anorectal malformations and describe how the fistula type determines the surgical approach in males versus females. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] why hydrocolpos occurs in cloacal anomalies and how it affects the upper urinary tract. [3 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the preoperative imaging needed to define the anatomy before definitive cloacal reconstruction. [4 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the long-term urological outcomes and complications after cloacal repair. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — In males: rectourethral (bulbar, prostatic) and rectovesical fistulas (1). Rectovesical is high (supralevator), rectobulbar is low (1). In females: rectovestibular (most common), rectovaginal, cloacal (common channel) (1). High fistulas require abdominal approach; low fistulas can be repaired via PSARP (1). Cloacal repair requires total urogenital mobilisation (TUM) or vaginal replacement (1). Part (b): 3 marks — Hydrocolpos: fluid accumulation in the vagina due to obstruction at the common channel (1). It causes extrinsic compression on the bladder neck and trigone, contributing to hydronephrosis (1). Also distorts pelvic anatomy, making reconstruction more complex (1). Part (c): 4 marks — Cystovaginoscopy with antegrade/retrograde contrast studies (1). Distal colostogram via mucous fistula (if colostomy) (1). MRI pelvis (best for delineating complex anatomy) (1). Renal US + MCU (assess VUR, bladder function) (1). Part (d): 3 marks — Urinary incontinence: common even after successful repair — up to 50% may need CIC (1). VUR resolves spontaneously in 30-50% after obstruction relieved (1). Urinary retention: may need CIC; if persistent, consider Mitrofanoff (1). Common errors: focusing on the colorectal repair while neglecting urological outcomes; not anticipating the need for CIC.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Genitourinary fistulas in ARMs are classified by type and level. In males, rectourethral fistulas can be bulbar or prostatic; rectovesical fistulas are supralevator. In females, rectovestibular fistulas are most common, followed by rectovaginal and cloacal anomalies. The fistula determines the surgical approach: low lesions via PSARP, high lesions requiring abdominal or laparoscopic approach. Hydrocolpos in cloacal anomalies compresses the bladder trigone, causing hydronephrosis; vaginostomy relieves this. Preoperative imaging (cystovaginoscopy, MRI pelvis, colostogram) is essential. Long-term, many children with cloacal anomalies require CIC for bladder emptying; urinary incontinence is common.

Explains mechanism of hydrocolpos-induced hydronephrosis
Links fistula type to surgical approach

questions.title: Long case: Adolescent with cloacal anomaly — continence, sexuality, and transition
questions.questionText:
A 17-year-old girl born with a cloacal anomaly (common channel 4 cm) underwent complex reconstruction in infancy including PSARP, total urogenital mobilisation, and appendicovesicostomy (Mitrofanoff). She has been managing with CIC via Mitrofanoff and an ACE for bowel management. She is continent of urine between CIC (4 hourly) but has persistent faecal soiling. She is sexually active but reports dyspareunia and has never had a gynaecological examination. She is about to transition to adult services.

(a) [ASSESS] the current urological, gynaecological, and bowel function status of this young woman, identifying unmet needs. [7 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] the potential gynaecological issues associated with cloacal anomalies, including vaginal stenosis, Mullerian anomalies, and fertility implications. [6 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a multidisciplinary transition plan involving urology, gynaecology, colorectal surgery, and psychology. [7 marks]
(d) [EVALUATE] the options for managing persistent faecal incontinence in an adolescent with an ACE procedure. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 7 marks — Urology: good urinary continence with CIC via Mitrofanoff — positive (1). Needs monitoring: stomal stenosis, UTI, bladder stones (1). Serum creatinine, renal US, and bladder surveillance (1). Gynaecology: dyspareunia suggests vaginal stenosis or inadequate introitus (1). Never examined — needs gentle examination under anaesthesia by gynaecologist (1). Bowel: persistent faecal soiling despite ACE — evaluate ACE patency, washout regimen, stool consistency (1). Psychological: body image, sexual function, self-esteem (1). Part (b): 6 marks — Cloacal anomaly associated with Mullerian anomalies: uterine didelphys, vaginal septum, vaginal agenesis (1). Vaginal stenosis common after reconstruction — may need dilation or revision vaginoplasty (1). Dyspareunia from stenosis, dryness (lack of Lubrication from abnormal vaginal mucosa), or psychological factors (1). Fertility: Mullerian anomalies may affect fertility; however, pregnancy is possible — high-risk obstetric management needed (1). Contraception counselling (1). HPV vaccination (1). Part (c): 7 marks — Transition clinic with: urology (CIC, Mitrofanoff surveillance, renal monitoring) (1). Gynaecology (vaginal assessment, dilation, sexual health, fertility counselling) (1). Colorectal surgery (ACE management, faecal incontinence evaluation, possible ACE revision) (1). Psychology (body image, sexual counselling, treatment fatigue, transition anxiety) (1). Joint appointments with paediatric and adult specialists simultaneously (1). Written transition plan and summary (1). Self-management goals: she should book own appointments, order supplies, know her medications (1). Part (d): 5 marks — First: evaluate ACE regimen — volume, frequency, type of irrigant (tap water vs saline vs phosphate enema) (1). Assess for ACE stomal stenosis — dilate or revise (1). Colonic transit study if unclear (1). Dietary manipulation: fibre adjustment, stool bulking agents (1). If ACE fails: consider colostomy as last resort — but must counsel that this does not cure the underlying problem (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This young woman has several unmet needs despite good urinary continence. The Mitrofanoff and ACE have provided functional independence, but persistent faecal soiling, dyspareunia, and lack of gynaecological follow-up are significant issues. Cloacal anomalies are associated with Mullerian anomalies (uterine didelphys, vaginal septum) and vaginal stenosis — she requires examination under anaesthesia by a specialist gynaecologist. The dyspareunia may be from vaginal stenosis requiring dilation or revision vaginoplasty. Fertility potential is reduced but not absent; if she desires pregnancy, this would be high-risk. The faecal soiling may be from incomplete ACE washouts, stomal stenosis, or underlying colonic dysmotility. A structured multidisciplinary transition clinic should bring together urology, gynaecology, colorectal surgery, and psychology. She must be at the centre of decision-making.
discriminators: Addresses gynaecological and sexual health issues often overlooked in ARM follow-up
Discusses Mullerian anomalies and their impact on fertility and sexual function
Proposes a specific multidisciplinary transition team composition
Evaluates ACE failure systematically before recommending colostomy

questions.title: Extended Matching Question — Urological associations and management of ARM
questions.questionText:
Theme: Anorectal malformations — urological assessment and management

Options:
A. Renal ultrasound alone
B. Renal ultrasound + MCU
C. Renal ultrasound + spinal MRI
D. Renal ultrasound + MCU + spinal MRI
E. Video-urodynamics
F. Start CIC and anticholinergics
G. Refer for ACE procedure
H. Vaginal dilation under anaesthesia
I. Transition to adult multidisciplinary clinic
J. Renal ultrasound + DMSA scan

Scenarios:
1. A newborn male with high ARM (rectourethral fistula), normal renal US, no hydronephrosis.
2. A 6-year-old boy with repaired low ARM and new-onset daytime urinary incontinence. Spinal US was normal at birth.
3. A newborn female with cloacal anomaly, bilaterally palpable kidneys, and a tense lower abdominal mass.
4. A 14-year-old girl with repaired cloacal anomaly, continent on CIC via Mitrofanoff, now reporting dyspareunia.
5. A 5-year-old with repaired high ARM, recurrent UTIs, and hydronephrosis on US. Previous MCU showed no VUR.
6. A 16-year-old with repaired ARM who is stable on CIC and ACE. He is about to start college and needs ongoing care.
questions.markingGuide:
Each correct answer = 2 marks. Key themes: importance of spinal imaging, role of urodynamics in new incontinence, recognition of hydrocolpos, need for gynaecological follow-up in females, and transition planning.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Stem 1 = C, Stem 2 = E, Stem 3 = D, Stem 4 = H, Stem 5 = E, Stem 6 = I.
discriminators: Tests knowledge of ARM screening protocols by risk category
Requires recognition of late-presenting neurogenic bladder
Includes gynaecological complications often missed in follow-up
Emphasises transition to adult care
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Complete Primary Repair of Exstrophy (CPRE) vs Staged Repair — surgical decision-making
questions.questionText:
A newborn male is delivered at 38 weeks with classic bladder exstrophy. The bladder plate appears adequate (approximately 3 cm diameter) with no associated anomalies. Renal ultrasound and serum creatinine are normal. The parents are distressed and have been counselled by the neonatology team. They ask whether a single operation or multiple operations would be better for their son. You are the paediatric urologist consulted on day 1.

(a) [COMPARE] the philosophy and surgical principles of the modern staged repair (Jeffs) versus complete primary repair of exstrophy (CPRE / Mitchell), including the key steps of each approach. [8 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] the indications for pelvic osteotomy at the time of primary closure and the factors that determine its necessity. [5 marks]
(c) [CRITICALLY APPRAISE] the outcomes for continence, upper tract preservation, and penile development for each approach, citing published series. [7 marks]
(d) [CONSTRUCT] a counselling framework for the parents that explains the pros and cons of each approach and supports shared decision-making. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 8 marks — Staged repair (Jeffs): Stage 1 — bladder closure ± osteotomy in newborn (1). Stage 2 — epispadias repair at 6-12 months (Cantwell-Ransley or Mitchell) (1). Stage 3 — bladder neck reconstruction (Young-Dees-Leadbetter) at 4-5 years when bladder capacity adequate (>60 mL) and child motivated for continence (1). CPRE (Mitchell): single-stage closure + epispadias repair in newborn (1). Bladder, posterior urethra, and penis are reconstructed simultaneously (1). Bladder neck not formally reconstructed in newborn — continence depends on bladder growth and outlet resistance (1). Advantages of CPRE: one hospitalisation, less total anaesthesia, earlier penile reconstruction (1). Disadvantage: more technically demanding in newborn, risk of penile loss if vascular compromise (1). Part (b): 5 marks — Osteotomy indicated if: pubic diastasis >4 cm (1), primary closure beyond 48-72 hours (1), or redo closure (1). Newborns closed within 72 hours may not need osteotomy due to ligamentous laxity from maternal relaxin (1). Osteotomy (anterior innominate or posterior iliac) allows tension-free approximation of pubic symphysis and pelvic floor (1). Part (c): 7 marks — Staged repair: continence 60-80% socially dry (dry intervals >3h) (1). BNR success depends on adequate bladder capacity (>60 mL, ideally >100 mL pre-BNR) (1). Upper tract deterioration: VUR develops in 30-50% but rarely causes renal impairment (1). CPRE: continence rates 40-70% socially dry in reported series (1). Some series show higher continence and fewer secondary procedures (1). Penile development: CPRE may give better cosmetic result (neophallic urethra via glans) but risk of glans dehiscence or urethral fistula (1). Both approaches require long-term follow-up and may need additional surgeries (1). Part (d): 5 marks — Acknowledge parental distress and urgency — reassure that closure will happen safely regardless of approach chosen (1). Explain both approaches with balanced information: staged repair is traditional with more data; CPRE is a single operation but newer with less long-term data (1). Factors specific to this baby: adequate bladder plate, closed within 48h, surgeon experience with CPRE (1). Emphasise that both approaches aim for the same goals: closed bladder, continence, normal renal function, functional/ cosmetic penis (1). Recommend referral to a centre with experience in the chosen approach (1). Common errors: being dogmatic about one approach; failing to acknowledge the importance of surgeon experience.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
The two main surgical strategies for classic bladder exstrophy are the modern staged repair (Jeffs) and CPRE (Mitchell). Staged repair separates closure, epispadias repair, and bladder neck reconstruction into three distinct procedures. CPRE performs closure and epispadias repair in one neonatal operation. Pelvic osteotomy is needed if the pubic diastasis is >4 cm, closure occurs after 48-72 hours, or it is a redo closure. For this newborn with an adequate bladder plate closed early, osteotomy may not be necessary. Published continence rates (social dryness) range from 60-80% for staged repair and 40-70% for CPRE, but definitions vary. Both approaches can achieve good outcomes in experienced hands. The parents need to understand that regardless of approach, long-term follow-up is required and additional procedures may be needed. The most important factor is that the primary closure is performed by a surgeon experienced in the chosen technique.
discriminators: Provides balanced comparison of staged vs CPRE with specific published outcomes
Links osteotomy decision-making to timing and diastasis width
Acknowledges that surgeon expertise is a critical determinant of outcome
Constructs a counselling framework that recognises parental distress

questions.title: Bladder neck reconstruction (Young-Dees-Leadbetter) — principles, outcomes, and alternatives
questions.questionText:
A 5-year-old boy with classic bladder exstrophy underwent staged repair: primary closure at day 2 of life and Cantwell-Ransley epispadias repair at 14 months. He now has an adequate bladder capacity of 120 mL on cystogram under anaesthesia. He has no VUR. However, he remains completely incontinent despite timed voiding. His parents are keen to proceed with continence surgery before he starts school. They have read about the 'Young-Dees-Leadbetter' procedure online.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the anatomical basis for incontinence in bladder exstrophy after successful primary closure. [5 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the surgical principles and technique of the Young-Dees-Leadbetter bladder neck reconstruction (BNR). [7 marks]
(c) [EVALUATE] the prerequisites for successful BNR, including bladder capacity, upper tract stability, and patient motivation. [6 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the alternatives if BNR fails or is contraindicated, including bladder neck closure with Mitrofanoff, artificial urinary sphincter, and bladder augmentation. [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — Classic exstrophy: bladder neck and posterior urethra are open — the trigone is exposed and the bladder neck is incompetent (1). Even after closure, the bladder neck lacks the normal circumferential sphincter mechanism (1). The intrinsic sphincter deficiency persists because the bladder neck musculature (trigonal and detrusor loops) does not form a competent sphincter (1). Hence, the bladder acts as a low-pressure reservoir but urine leaks continuously (1). Continence after exstrophy requires creation of outlet resistance (BNR) (1). Part (b): 7 marks — YDL-BNR: exposure of bladder neck and proximal urethra via midline cystotomy (1). Bilateral mucosal strips (triangular flaps) are raised from the trigone down to the verumontanum (1). The detrusor muscle is mobilised and wrapped around the proximal urethra and bladder neck (1). The mucosal tube is reconstructed over a catheter (1). The detrusor wrap is imbricated and reinforced (1). The ureteric orifices are often reimplanted (Cohen cross-trigonal) to prevent VUR (1). A suprapubic catheter is placed for post-op drainage (1). Part (c): 6 marks — Prerequisites: bladder capacity >60 mL (ideally >100 mL) on cystogram under anaesthesia (1). Absence of VUR or corrected VUR (1). Adequate renal function and stable upper tracts (1). Child readiness: motivated, understands the need for CIC if necessary post-op (1). Family support for intensive post-operative catheter and voiding programme (1). Bladder capacity is the single most important predictor — <60 mL almost guarantees failure (1). Part (d): 7 marks — If BNR fails: repeat BNR has lower success (1). Options: bladder neck closure (BNC) with Mitrofanoff for CIC — guarantees dryness (stoma needed) (1). Artificial urinary sphincter (AUS) — limited paediatric data, risk of erosion, but option in older children (1). Augmentation cystoplasty (if bladder capacity inadequate) can be combined with BNR or BNC (1). Bladder neck injection therapy (bulking agents) — poor results in exstrophy (1). Slings (rectus fascia or synthetic) — limited experience (1). Choice depends on bladder capacity, upper tract status, family preference, and surgeon expertise (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
After primary closure, the bladder neck remains incompetent because the sphincter mechanism is deficient. Bladder neck reconstruction using the Young-Dees-Leadbetter technique creates outlet resistance by tubularising the trigone and wrapping detrusor muscle around the proximal urethra. Success depends critically on pre-operative bladder capacity: >100 mL gives 70-80% chance of social continence; <60 mL almost always fails. This child has 120 mL capacity, making him a good candidate. Pre-operative cystography must rule out VUR — if present, ureteric reimplantation is added. If BNR fails, options include repeat BNR, bladder neck closure with Mitrofanoff (highly effective but requires CIC via stoma), or augmentation cystoplasty. Artificial urinary sphincter is rarely used in children. The parents should understand that even with successful BNR, some stress incontinence may persist, and CIC may be needed if the child cannot empty adequately.
discriminators: Explains the anatomical sphincter deficiency that persists after closure
Provides specific bladder capacity thresholds for BNR success
Lists alternatives with indications and limitations for each
Emphasises the role of pre-operative VUR assessment and reimplantation
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Pelvic osteotomy in bladder exstrophy
questions.questionText:
A 6-hour-old male with classic bladder exstrophy is being prepared for primary closure. The pubic diastasis measures 5 cm on plain radiograph. The neonatology team asks why osteotomy is sometimes needed and what the options are.

(a) [STATE] the role of pelvic osteotomy in the primary closure of bladder exstrophy. [3 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the types of pelvic osteotomy used, their timing, and the advantages/disadvantages of each. [5 marks]
(c) [LIST] the complications of pelvic osteotomy in the newborn. [3 marks]
(d) [DESCRIBE] the post-operative immobilisation and nursing care required after closure with osteotomy. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 3 marks — Osteotomy allows medial rotation of the pubic rami to approximate the pubic symphysis (1). This provides tension-free closure of the bladder, posterior urethra, and anterior abdominal wall (1). Reduces risk of bladder dehiscence and prolapse (1). Part (b): 5 marks — Anterior innominate osteotomy (most common): supra-acetabular iliac bone cut bilaterally (1). Posterior iliac osteotomy: bone cut behind sacroiliac joint (1). Combined anterior and posterior: for severe diastasis (1). Timing: at the time of closure (day 1-3) or delayed (if late closure) (1). Advantages: anterior gives direct access, can be combined with closure through same incision; posterior more effective rotation but requires repositioning (1). Part (c): 3 marks — Prolonged ileus (most common) (1). Pin site infection if external fixation used (1). Nerve injury (femoral or sciatic) (1). Bleeding, compartment syndrome (rare). Part (d): 4 marks — Post-op: modified Buck traction or double hip spica cast for 4-6 weeks (1). Prone or lateral positioning to avoid pressure on closure (1). External fixator pins require daily cleaning (1). Assess neurovascular status of lower limbs regularly (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Pelvic osteotomy is indicated for pubic diastasis >4 cm, closure after 72 hours, or redo closure. For this newborn with a 5 cm diastasis, anterior innominate osteotomy is appropriate and can be performed during the same anaesthetic as bladder closure. The osteotomy allows tension-free pubic approximation, reducing the risk of dehiscence, bladder prolapse, and wound breakdown. Post-operative immobilisation with a spica cast or Buck traction for 4-6 weeks is required. Complications include ileus, pin-site infection, and nerve injury.
discriminators: Provides specific diastasis threshold for osteotomy
Compares osteotomy types with technique-specific advantages
Details post-operative immobilisation and nursing care

This provides tension-free closure of the bladder, posterior urethra, and anterior abdominal wall (1). Reduces risk of bladder dehiscence and prolapse (1).
