questions.title: Epispadias repair — Cantwell-Ransley vs Mitchell technique
questions.questionText:
A 10-month-old boy with classic bladder exstrophy who underwent successful primary closure on day 1 is now being prepared for epispadias repair. He has dorsal chordee and a bifid glans. The parents ask what the operation involves and what the expected outcome is.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the anatomical deformity in male epispadias, including the urethral plate, corpora, and glans configuration. [4 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] the Cantwell-Ransley epispadias repair and the Mitchell technique (complete penile disassembly) in terms of surgical steps and outcomes. [5 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the complications of epispadias repair and their management. [3 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the long-term cosmetic and functional outcomes, including erectile function and ejaculation. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 4 marks — Epispadias: urethral meatus on dorsal aspect of penis (1). Urethral plate is short and on the dorsal surface (1). Corpora are separated (bifid) and attach to the pubic rami causing dorsal chordee (1). Glans is open dorsally (bifid glans) (1). Part (b): 5 marks — Cantwell-Ransley: mobilisation of urethral plate, division of dorsal chordee, tubularisation of urethral plate, transposition of urethra ventrally, glanuloplasty (1). Mitchell (Complete penile disassembly): penis divided into three components — ventral halves of corpora, dorsal halves of corpora with glans, and urethral plate (1). Allows complete straightening of chordee (1). Urethra is reconstructed and placed ventrally (1). Mitchell gives better chordee correction and cosmetic appearance but higher complication rate (urethrocutaneous fistula 15-25%) (1). Part (c): 3 marks — Fistula: most common (10-25%) — repair after 6 months (1). Glans dehiscence — redo glanuloplasty (1). Urethral stricture — dilation or urethroplasty (1). Part (d): 3 marks — Erectile function: generally preserved — chordee recurrence in 5-10% (1). Ejaculation: most patients have normal antegrade ejaculation (1). Penile length: usually adequate but may be shorter than normal — parents must have realistic expectations (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Male epispadias features a dorsally positioned urethral meatus, separated corpora, and a bifid glans with dorsal chordee. The Cantwell-Ransley repair mobilises and transposes the urethra ventrally, while the Mitchell complete disassembly separates the penis into three components for complete chordee correction. The Mitchell technique offers superior straightening but has a higher fistula rate. Complications include urethrocutaneous fistula (most common), glans dehiscence, and stricture. Long-term outcomes are good: erectile function is preserved in most patients, and antegrade ejaculation is usually normal. Parents should be counselled that the penis may be shorter than average but functional.
discriminators: Compares the two techniques with specific complication rates
Explains the anatomical basis for the surgical approach
Addresses long-term functional outcomes including sexual function

Penile length: usually adequate but may be shorter than normal — parents must have realistic expectations (1).

questions.title: Long case: Adult with exstrophy — fertility, sexual function, and long-term outcomes
questions.questionText:
A 28-year-old man born with classic bladder exstrophy presents to the adult urology clinic. He underwent staged repair as a child: primary closure at day 2, Cantwell-Ransley epispadias repair at 18 months, and Young-Dees-Leadbetter bladder neck reconstruction at age 5. He is continent with timed voiding (dry intervals 3-4 hours) but has occasional stress incontinence with coughing or lifting. He has been diagnosed with infertility after 2 years of trying to conceive with his partner. He has erectile function adequate for intercourse but has noticed that his ejaculate volume is very small and he has retrograde ejaculation confirmed on post-ejaculatory urinalysis. He is contemplating starting a family.

(a) [ASSESS] the long-term urological outcomes in this patient, including continence, upper tract status, and surgical history. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the mechanisms of infertility in men born with bladder exstrophy, including the anatomical, surgical, and functional factors. [7 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a fertility management plan, including sperm retrieval options and assisted reproductive techniques. [6 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the psychological and relational impact of exstrophy on adult sexual function and intimacy, and the role of psychosexual counselling. [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a): 5 marks — Continence: social dryness with timed voiding — good outcome (1). Some stress incontinence common after YDL-BNR (20-30%) (1). Upper tract: must assess renal function, US, and VUR status (1). Potential issues: bladder stones, recurrent UTIs (1). Need for long-term surveillance of augmentation if performed — not in this case (1). Part (b): 7 marks — Infertility in exstrophy is multifactorial: (1) Retrograde ejaculation — most common — due to bladder neck reconstruction/BNR — bladder neck does not close during ejaculation (1). (2) Aspermia or low-volume ejaculate (1). (3) Obstructive azoospermia from ejaculatory duct obstruction or prior pelvic surgery (1). (4) Cryptorchidism (10-20% of exstrophy patients have undescended testes) (1). (5) Reduced spermatogenesis from testicular maldescent or prior orchidopexy (1). (6) Psychosexual factors — reduced libido, performance anxiety (1). Part (c): 6 marks — First: confirm retrograde ejaculation — post-ejaculatory urine analysis for sperm (1). Sperm retrieval: electroejaculation or penile vibratory stimulation (if antegrade ejaculation can be achieved) (1). Testicular sperm extraction (TESE) or micro-TESE if needed (1). Sperm cryopreservation (1). Assisted reproduction: IVF/ICSI using retrieved sperm (1). Consider bladder neck medication (sympathomimetics like pseudoephedrine) to convert retrograde to antegrade ejaculation — limited success (1). Part (d): 7 marks — Exstrophy has lifelong impact on body image, sexual confidence, and relationships (1). Men with exstrophy may have a shorter penis, penile curvature, and visible scars — leading to embarrassment and avoidance of intimacy (1). Retrograde ejaculation and infertility cause profound psychological distress (1). Communication with partner is often challenging (1). Psychosexual counselling should be offered proactively, not only when problems emerge (1). Support groups and peer mentoring (like the Association for Bladder Exstrophy Community) are valuable (1). The urologist should initiate these discussions — patients rarely volunteer sexual concerns (1).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
This man has a good functional outcome from his staged exstrophy repair — social continence with timed voiding and preserved erectile function. His infertility is primarily due to retrograde ejaculation from the bladder neck reconstruction, which prevents antegrade seminal emission. Additional contributing factors may include obstructive azoospermia from prior surgery or reduced spermatogenesis. Management starts with post-ejaculatory urine analysis to confirm and quantify sperm in urine. Options include electroejaculation or penile vibratory stimulation for sperm retrieval, TESE if needed, and IVF/ICSI. Sympathomimetics (pseudoephedrine) may help some patients convert to antegrade ejaculation. Psychosexual counselling is essential — the burden of living with exstrophy affects body image, confidence, and relationships. The urologist should proactively discuss sexual health at every consultation, as patients rarely volunteer these concerns.
discriminators: Provides a comprehensive analysis of exstrophy-related infertility mechanisms
Formulates a stepwise fertility plan from diagnosis to ART
Addresses psychosexual counselling as a core component of adult exstrophy care
Discusses specific sperm retrieval techniques and their indications

questions.title: Extended Matching Question — Exstrophy-epispadias complex management
questions.questionText:
Theme: Exstrophy-epispadias complex — management decisions

Options:
A. Newborn primary bladder closure without osteotomy
B. Newborn primary bladder closure with anterior innominate osteotomy
C. CPRE (complete primary repair of exstrophy)
D. Bladder neck reconstruction (Young-Dees-Leadbetter) now
E. Delayed bladder neck reconstruction after achieving adequate bladder capacity
F. Cantwell-Ransley epispadias repair
G. Mitchell complete penile disassembly epispadias repair
H. Bladder neck closure with Mitrofanoff
I. Artificial urinary sphincter placement
J. Referral for psychosexual counselling

Scenarios:
1. A newborn with classic bladder exstrophy, pubic diastasis 2.5 cm, born at 39 weeks. Closure on day 1.
2. A 3-day-old with classic bladder exstrophy, pubic diastasis 5.5 cm, born at 41 weeks after prolonged labour.
3. A 4-year-old boy with classic exstrophy who had primary closure at birth and now has bladder capacity 80 mL on cystogram. He is completely incontinent.
4. A 12-month-old boy with classic exstrophy who had primary closure at birth. He now needs epispadias repair. He has severe dorsal chordee and a short urethral plate.
5. A 7-year-old girl with classic bladder exstrophy who had primary closure and BNR but remains completely incontinent. Bladder capacity is 50 mL. She has had 3 febrile UTIs in the past year.
6. An 18-year-old man with exstrophy who is continent and potent but reports significant distress about penile appearance and avoids intimate relationships.
questions.markingGuide:
Each correct answer = 2 marks. Key themes: osteotomy indications, capacity thresholds for BNR, choice of epispadias repair based on chordee severity, management of failed BNR, and importance of psychosexual support in adolescents and adults.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Stem 1 = A, Stem 2 = B, Stem 3 = E, Stem 4 = G, Stem 5 = H, Stem 6 = J.
discriminators: Tests nuanced decision-making for osteotomy based on diastasis width and timing
Requires knowledge of bladder capacity thresholds for BNR
Distinguishes epispadias repair techniques based on chordee severity
Includes psychosexual counselling as a valid management option
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Hypospadias Classification and Preoperative Assessment
questions.questionText:
A 14-month-old boy is referred by his GP with a history of the urinary stream spraying from the ventral aspect of the penis since birth. His parents have noticed that the foreskin is hooded dorsally and the penis appears slightly curved during erection. On examination in clinic, the urethral meatus is noted to be on the distal shaft. You plan to take the child to the operating theatre for assessment under anaesthesia and definitive correction.

(a) [CLASSIFY] the anatomical subtypes of hypospadias based on meatal location, listing the structures encountered along the ventral penile surface from glans to perineum. [6 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the GAP (glans-urethral meatus angle) classification and its relevance to assessing chordee in hypospadias. [6 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the step-by-step technique for performing an artificial erection test under anaesthesia to quantify chordee. Include the expected findings for mild, moderate, and severe ventral curvature. [12 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the key anatomical features that must be documented before planning surgical correction in this patient. Address the significance of glans size, urethral plate width, and degree of chordee. [12 marks]
(e) [FORMULATE] a management algorithm for a child found to have 40 degrees of ventral chordee on artificial erection with a mid-shaft meatus. Include decision points for single-stage versus two-stage repair. [14 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 6 marks: 1 mark for each correct level: (1) glanular, (2) coronal, (3) distal penile, (4) mid-penile, (5) proximal penile, (6) penoscrotal/scrotal/perineal. Accept either 5 or 6 levels; credit any logical subdivision. Must mention ventral ectopic meatus. Common error: omitting penoscrotal junction. Part (b) — 6 marks: 2 marks for defining GAP as angle between glans axis and urethral meatus, 2 marks for normal erection (straight vs curved), 2 marks for clinical utility in objectively measuring chordee. Distinction candidates relate GAP to risk of persistent curvature after degloving. Part (c) — 12 marks: 1 each for: (1) prep and drape with tourniquet, (2) 25G butterfly needle, (3) sterile saline injection into corpus cavernosum, (4) gradual injection, (5) observe erection quality, (6) measure chordee with goniometer, (7) <10° mild, (8) 10–30° moderate, (9) >30° severe, (10) document after degloving, (11) repeat after dorsal plication if performed, (12) photograph for records. Trap: injecting into corpus spongiosum produces false erection. Part (d) — 12 marks: 2 each for: (1) meatal position and quality (stenotic vs wide), (2) urethral plate width and elasticity, (3) glans size and configuration (flat vs conical vs deep groove), (4) degree of chordee (ventral vs lateral), (5) penile length and scrotal anomalies (transposition, penoscrotal webbing), (6) prepuce quality (dorsal hood vs circumcised). Common error: forgetting to assess prepuce quality for preputial onlay flaps. Part (e) — 14 marks: Award 2 marks for distinguishing degloving alone vs formal correction. Algorithm: (1) degloving alone — re-assess chordee; (2) if <30° after degloving — dorsal plication (Nesbit or Baskin) TIP; (3) if 30–45° after degloving — consider urethral plate transection and two-stage (Bracka); (4) if >45° — two-stage repair; (5) single-stage TIP acceptable if plate wide and chordee resolves; (6) single-stage with dorsal plication if mild residual; (7) two-stage with buccal graft if proximal and severe. Trap: attempting TIP with narrow plate and severe chordee leads to fistula/stenosis.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Hypospadias is classified by meatal position following penile degloving. From distal to proximal: glanular (within glans), coronal (at corona), distal penile (subcoronal to midshaft), mid-penile, proximal penile, penoscrotal (at penoscrotal junction), scrotal, and perineal (most proximal). The ventral structures that fail to fuse include the corpus spongiosum, Buck fascia, dartos fascia, and skin. The foreskin is classically deficient ventrally with a dorsal hood. Part (b): The GAP classification measures the angle between the long axis of the glans and the urethral meatus during erection. Normal males have a GAP of 0–10° (straight). Hypospadias patients with chordee have an increased GAP, reflecting ventral tethering. This provides an objective, reproducible measure of chordee severity. A GAP >30° correlates with significant chordee requiring correction. Part (c): After induction and aseptic preparation, a tourniquet is applied at the penile base. A 25-gauge butterfly needle is inserted into one corpus cavernosum. Sterile normal saline (or 1:1000 heparinised saline) is injected slowly until full erection is achieved. The degree of ventral curvature is measured using a goniometer or protractor. Mild = <10°, moderate = 10–30°, severe = >30°. The artificial erection is repeated after penile degloving to determine if curvature persists (intrinsic chordee) or resolves (cutaneous chordee). Findings are documented photographically. Part (d): Key features: (1) Meatal position and morphology — the meatus may be stenotic, wide, or slit-like; meatal calibre determines whether meatotomy is needed. (2) Urethral plate — width and quality from meatus to glans tip; a plate <8 mm may not tubularise easily and a narrow plate risks stricture. (3) Glans size and shape — a small, flat glans makes TIP repair difficult; a deep urethral groove is favourable. (4) Chordee — degree and whether it persists after degloving determines the need for dorsal plication or urethral plate transaction. (5) Penile length and scrotal anomalies — penoscrotal transposition or webbing influences surgical approach. (6) Prepuce — if present, may be used for preputial onlay or tubularised flaps. Part (e): Algorithm: (1) Perform initial artificial erection → 40° chordee. (2) Penile degloving — reflect skin and dartos to penile base. (3) Repeat artificial erection → if chordee <30°, proceed with dorsal plication (midline plication at 12 o'clock or Baskin technique) and perform TIP urethroplasty if urethral plate width >8 mm and glans adequate. (4) If chordee 30–45° after degloving, transect the urethral plate at the point of maximal curvature and measure the resultant gap. (5) If gap <1.5 cm and plate proximal quality good, consider staged repair: first stage — straightening with urethral plate transection and glans wings; second stage — tubularisation of neourethra after 6 months. (6) If gap >1.5 cm or severe chordee >45°, perform first-stage Bracka with buccal mucosal graft inlay. (7) After 6 months proceed to second-stage tubularisation. A single-stage TIP with dorsal plication is appropriate only if chordee is <30° after degloving and urethral plate is healthy. Single-stage must not compromise chordee correction.
discriminators: Uses objective chordee measurement (GAP/goniometer) rather than subjective grading
Describes decision algorithm that acknowledges degloving alone can resolve apparent chordee
Recognises that urethral plate quality (width >8 mm) determines suitability for TIP
Correctly identifies that >30° residual chordee after degloving warrants plate transection and two-stage repair

Common error: forgetting to assess prepuce quality for preputial onlay flaps. Part (e) — 14 marks: Award 2 marks for distinguishing degloving alone vs formal correction. Trap: attempting TIP with narrow plate and severe chordee leads to fistula/stenosis.

questions.title: Surgical Techniques in Distal Hypospadias Repair
questions.questionText:
A 10-month-old boy is brought to the paediatric urology clinic with glanular hypospadias. The meatus is at the tip of the glans but ventrally placed within a shallow groove. There is no chordee on examination and the prepuce is intact. The parents are very anxious and have researched MAGPI and Snodgrass (TIP) procedures online. They ask you which technique is best for their son and what the expected outcomes and complication rates are.

(a) [COMPARE] the indications and contraindications for MAGPI (meatal advancement and glanuloplasty) versus tubularised incised plate (TIP/Snodgrass) urethroplasty for distal hypospadias. [10 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the key steps of the Snodgrass (TIP) urethroplasty, including the incision of the urethral plate and why it is necessary. [10 marks]
(c) [CRITICALLY EVALUATE] the published evidence comparing complication rates (fistula, meatal stenosis, glans dehiscence, urethral stricture) between MAGPI and TIP procedures for distal hypospadias. [10 marks]
(d) [EXPLAIN] the principles of fistula prevention in hypospadias surgery. Detail the role of multilayer closure, flap coverage, and urinary diversion in reducing fistula formation. [10 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the long-term outcomes of hypospadias repair from the patient's perspective, including cosmetic appearance, urinary function, and sexual function. How should you counsel the parents preoperatively? [10 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 10 marks: 2 each for: (1) MAGPI indicated for glanular hypospadias with non-stenotic, mobile meatus and good urethral plate mobility, (2) MAGPI contraindicated when chordee >15°, stenotic meatus, or shallow glans groove, (3) TIP suitable for glanular to distal penile with deep urethral groove and plate width >8 mm, (4) TIP can correct mild chordee (<30°) if plate is healthy, (5) both techniques require adequate glans size. Trap: MAGPI in a shallow groove results in meatal regression. Part (b) — 10 marks: 1 per step: (1) stay suture in glans, (2) U-shaped incision around meatus with parallel limbs in glans groove, (3) degloving penile shaft, (4) artificial erection to confirm no chordee, (5) midline incision of urethral plate from meatus to tip, (6) tubularise plate over 8Fr stent with 7/0 absorbable suture, (7) second layer (dartos flap) coverage, (8) glansplasty with interrupted sutures, (9) meatoplasty at tip, (10) dressing and stent. Why incision: relaxes plate, widens neourethra to 14–16Fr. Part (c) — 10 marks: 2 each: (1) fistula rates: TIP 5–10% vs MAGPI <3% for distal cases, (2) meatal stenosis: TIP 2–5% vs MAGPI 5–10% (plication effect), (3) glans dehiscence: TIP 3–5% vs MAGPI 1–2%, (4) urethral stricture: TIP 1–2% (rare), MAGPI essentially 0%, (5) reoperation rate: TIP 7–12% vs MAGPI 5–8%. Credit reference to ESPU/BAPS audit data. Common error: quoting fistula rates above 20% for primary distal TIP — recall bias for complex cases. Part (d) — 10 marks: 2 each: (1) multilayer closure with at least two layers of epithelial and subepithelial tissue, (2) dartos flap coverage (de-epithelialised or island flap) to separate neourethra from skin suture lines, (3) tension-free anastomosis with spatulation, (4) appropriate stent size (8–10Fr) for 5–7 days, (5) avoid urethral catheter traction and early voiding against resistance. Trap: not using dartos flap increases fistula rate 2–3×. Part (e) — 10 marks: 2 each: (1) cosmetic: straight erection, meatus at tip, normal slit-like urinary stream — 80–90% good/excellent, (2) urinary: peak flow rates comparable to controls, but reduced flow in 10–15% (not usually symptomatic), (3) sexual: long-term data show normal ejaculation and sensation in >90% after TIP, (4) psychological: boys with hypospadias have higher rates of anxiety about genital appearance — sensitive preoperative counselling essential, (5) realistic expectations: 70–80% single-stage success, up to 20% may need secondary procedure.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): MAGPI is indicated for glanular hypospadias with a mobile, non-stenotic meatus situated at or within the glans, a good urethral plate that advances easily, and no significant chordee. It is contraindicated when there is a shallow glans groove, >15° chordee, a stenotic meatus that would cause tension, or a meatus that cannot be advanced to the tip. TIP (Snodgrass) urethroplasty is indicated for glanular to distal penile hypospadias with a deep urethral groove and a plate width >8 mm. It can correct mild chordee (<30°) if the chordee disappears after degloving. TIP is contraindicated when the urethral plate is narrow, atretic, or severely scarred (e.g., previous surgery), or when chordee >30° persists after degloving, in which case plate transection and two-stage repair is preferred. Part (b): Key steps: (1) Stay suture in glans for traction. (2) Mark U-shaped incision around the meatus with parallel limbs extending to the glans tip within the urethral groove. (3) Penile degloving: incise ventrally and reflect skin and dartos to penile base. (4) Artificial erection: confirm no or minimal chordee. (5) Midline relaxing incision of the urethral plate from within the meatus to the glans tip, deepening until Buck fascia is visible — this widens the plate from 8–10 mm to 14–16 Fr calibre. (6) Tubularise the incised plate over an 8Fr silicone stent using 7/0 or 6/0 absorbable suture (e.g., Monocryl or PDS) in a continuous subepithelial fashion. (7) Harvest a dorsal dartos flap (de-epithelialised preputial or shaft skin) and transpose ventrally to cover the neourethra. (8) Glansplasty: approximate glans wings over the neourethra in two layers. (9) Meatoplasty: suture neourethra to glans epithelium at the tip. (10) Dressing and stent secured for 5–7 days. The relaxing incision is necessary because the native urethral plate is usually too narrow (8–10 mm) to tubularise to an adequate calibre; incising it down to corpus spongiosum allows the plate to epithelialise to a wider calibre (up to 14–16 Fr) by secondary healing. Part (c): Published meta-analyses (e.g., Wilkinson et al. 2012, Huang et al. 2016) show fistula rates of 5–10% for TIP vs <3% for MAGPI in primary distal hypospadias. Meatal stenosis is higher with MAGPI (5–10%) due to the circumferential plication effect, vs 2–5% with TIP if the relaxing incision is adequate. Glans dehiscence is 3–5% for TIP vs 1–2% for MAGPI — often related to glans size <14 mm. Urethral stricture is rare for both (1–2% TIP, essentially 0% MAGPI). Overall reoperation rates are 7–12% for TIP and 5–8% for MAGPI. Important caveat: these data reflect experienced surgeons; TIP has been shown to be reproducible by trainees with comparable outcomes. The choice should be individualised based on anatomy rather than assumed superiority of one technique. Part (d): Fistula prevention begins with (1) tension-free anastomosis — spatulate the neourethra to prevent a narrow-calibre junction. (2) Multilayer closure: a running subepithelial layer inverts epithelium; a second layer of spongiosum or subcutaneous tissue reinforces the suture line. (3) A well-vascularised dartos flap (de-epithelialised dorsal preputial or lateral shaft skin) is rotated ventrally to interpose between the neourethra and skin closure, which reduces fistula rate by 50–70%. (4) Urinary diversion: an 8Fr silicone stent diverts urine away from the repair for 5–7 days; transurethral catheter may be used for complex repairs. (5) Avoid over-distension of the neourethra — appropriate stent size and prevention of catheter traction. The most critical factor is the interposition dartos flap; lack of flap coverage increases fistula risk 2–3 fold. Part (e): Long-term outcomes: (1) Cosmetic appearance — 80–90% of patients and parents rate outcome as good or excellent after TIP for distal hypospadias. The meatus should be slit-like and at the tip. (2) Urinary function — uroflowmetry shows peak flow rates within normal range in most patients, but up to 15% have mildly reduced flow, usually asymptomatic. (3) Sexual function — long-term studies show normal ejaculation, sensation, and erectile function in >90% of TIP patients. (4) Psychosexual — some adolescents report embarrassment about penile appearance even after successful repair; preoperative counselling should address body image. Preoperative counselling should include: 70–80% success in a single stage, up to 20% may require secondary procedure (fistula repair, meatoplasty); appearance will be near-normal but not identical to an uncircumcised penis; urinary function is expected to be normal; long-term sexual function is normal; and the child should be informed about his surgery at an appropriate developmental stage.
discriminators: Provides objective comparison of MAGPI vs TIP with evidence-based complication rates
Explains the physiological rationale for the midline relaxing incision in TIP
Emphasises dartos flap interposition as the critical step in fistula reduction
Addresses patient-reported outcomes and psychological aspects of genital surgery

questions.title: Fistula Management After Hypospadias Repair
questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old boy presented 6 months ago with a proximal penile hypospadias and underwent a Snodgrass (TIP) urethroplasty. He passed urine well initially. His mother now reports a second urinary stream from the ventral aspect of the penis during micturition. On examination a 2 mm fistula is identified at the coronal sulcus. The meatus appears normal and there is no evidence of meatal stenosis or urethral stricture.

(a) [LIST] four predisposing factors associated with fistula formation after hypospadias repair. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] why a minimum waiting period of 6 months is recommended before attempting fistula repair. [4 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the preoperative investigations required to assess the urethra before fistula closure. [5 marks]
(d) [DESCRIBE] the surgical principles of fistula closure, including excision, multilayer closure, and flap coverage. [6 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the management options if the fistula is found to be associated with distal urethral stricture at the time of surgery. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks (1 each): (1) distal obstruction (meatal stenosis or urethral stricture), (2) inadequate flap coverage between neourethra and skin suture lines, (3) infection or ischaemia of the neourethra, (4) narrow urethral plate (<8 mm) necessitating tight tubularisation or high-tension closure. Also accept: poor vascularity, use of mono-filament vs braided suture, or postoperative catheter trauma. Part (b) — 4 marks (2 each): (1) wound maturity — oedema and inflammation from the primary repair need to resolve fully to allow healthy vascularised tissue for reoperation, (2) to distinguish persistent fistula from delayed healing — small fistulae may close spontaneously within 6 months if there is no distal obstruction. Trap: operating earlier risks recurrence due to poor tissue quality. Part (c) — 5 marks (1 each): (1) careful physical examination including meatal calibration with 8Fr feeding tube, (2) observation of voiding stream, (3) retrograde urethrogram (RUG) to delineate urethral anatomy, (4) voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) to assess for stricture or diverticulum, (5) urethroscopy under anaesthesia immediately before repair. Common error: proceeding without imaging and missing an unrecognised stricture. Part (d) — 6 marks (1–2 each): (1) elliptical incision around fistula tract excising scarred epithelium to healthy tissue, (2) closure of neourethra in 2 layers (subepithelial running 7/0 absorbable, second layer of spongiosum/subcutaneous), (3) dartos or tunica vaginalis flap interposition between urethral closure and skin, (4) ensure no distal obstruction before closure — calibrate meatus and urethra, (5) tension-free skin closure with fine absorbable sutures and urine diversion (stent) for 5–7 days. Trap: simple closure without flap coverage has >50% recurrence. Part (e) — 6 marks: (1) perform urethroscopy to confirm stricture location and severity, (2) for short (<5 mm) stricture — visual internal urethrotomy (VIU) or dilation, followed by fistula repair, (3) for long (>5 mm) or recurrent stricture — formal urethral reconstruction with excision of stricture and redo urethroplasty, (4) staged approach may be needed — correct stricture first, allow healing, then repair fistula at a separate sitting, (5) buccal mucosa onlay graft for long anterior urethral strictures, (6) urine diversion with suprapubic catheter if repair is complex. Trap: repairing fistula without addressing distal stricture guarantees recurrence.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Four predisposing factors: (1) distal urethral obstruction (meatal stenosis or stricture), (2) absence of a dartos/tunica vaginalis barrier flap between neourethra and skin closure, (3) infection or haematoma compromising neourethral vascularity, (4) high-tension tubularisation of a narrow urethral plate (<8 mm). (b) A minimum 6-month waiting period is recommended because (1) it allows complete resolution of postoperative oedema and inflammation, providing well-vascularised healing tissue for reoperation, and (2) some small fistulae close spontaneously within this period if there is no distal obstruction — operating before 6 months risks operating on a temporary problem and leads to higher recurrence. (c) Preoperative investigations: (1) clinical examination with meatal calibration using an 8Fr feeding tube to exclude distal stenosis; (2) observation of voiding to assess for a poor stream; (3) retrograde urethrogram to delineate the full urethral anatomy and identify stricture, diverticulum, or other fistulae; (4) voiding cystourethrogram to assess for posterior urethral pathology; (5) urethroscopy immediately before repair to directly visualise the fistula, check for synchronous strictures, and confirm urethral plate quality. (d) Surgical principles: (1) excise the fistula tract in an elliptical fashion through healthy tissue; (2) close the neourethra in two layers using 7/0 absorbable sutures — first a subepithelial running suture to invert the epithelium, then a second layer of spongiosal or subcutaneous tissue; (3) harvest an interposition flap — either a local dartos flap or tunica vaginalis flap — to separate the urethral closure from the skin suture line; (4) confirm absence of distal obstruction by calibrating the meatus and distal urethra before committing to closure; (5) close skin without tension and divert urine with a 6–8Fr stent for 5–7 days. (e) If a distal urethral stricture is found: (1) confirm with urethroscopy; (2) for a short (<5 mm) stricture, perform visual internal urethrotomy or serial dilation and then proceed with fistula repair; (3) for a longer (>5 mm) or recurrent stricture, excise the strictured segment and perform a formal redo urethroplasty (onlay buccal mucosal graft); (4) consider a staged approach: repair the stricture first, allow 6 months of healing, then close the fistula in a second stage; (5) in complex cases, divert urine with a suprapubic catheter and schedule staged reconstruction. The fistula must not be closed until the stricture is definitively corrected, or the fistula will inevitably recur.

Highlights mandatory preoperative imaging to exclude distal obstruction
Stresses the absolute requirement for flap interposition to prevent recurrence
Recognises that distal stricture must be treated simultaneously or fistula will recur

questions.title: Preoperative Hormone Therapy in Hypospadias
questions.questionText:
A 14-month-old boy with proximal penoscrotal hypospadias is assessed for surgery. The glans is small (diameter 10 mm) and flat. The urethral plate is narrow (6 mm). The surgeon is considering the use of preoperative testosterone or HCG to augment penile size before the planned single-stage TIP urethroplasty.

(a) [STATE] the physiological rationale for using androgen stimulation before hypospadias repair. [4 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] the regimens for preoperative testosterone and HCG, including dose, route and timing relative to surgery. [5 marks]
(c) [SUMMARISE] the published evidence from randomised trials and meta-analyses on the effect of preoperative androgens on surgical outcomes (complication rates, glans dimensions, penile length). [6 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the potential adverse effects of preoperative androgen therapy in infants and young children. [5 marks]
(e) [RECOMMEND] whether you would use preoperative androgen therapy in this patient. Justify your decision based on the anatomy, evidence, and risk-benefit balance. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks: (1) Hypospadias is a form of incomplete virilisation; the penis and glans are often hypoplastic for age, (2) androgens stimulate penile growth by increasing penile length, glans circumference, and vascularity, (3) larger glans allows better glansplasty and neourethra accommodation, (4) improved penile length and skin quality facilitates flap harvesting and wound closure. Common error: suggesting hormones repair the underlying embryological defect — they only augment existing tissues. Part (b) — 5 marks: Testosterone: (1) testosterone enanthate 2 mg/kg IM every 2–4 weeks × 3 doses, OR topical testosterone 2.5% cream applied twice daily to penile shaft for 4–6 weeks, (2) last dose/application 4–6 weeks before surgery to allow any transient growth plate effects to resolve. HCG: (1) 500 IU IM every 5–7 days × 3–4 doses, (2) last dose 3–4 weeks before surgery. Both equivalent in effect. Trap: giving hormones too close to surgery (<4 weeks) may cause tissue oedema and friability increasing complications. Part (c) — 6 marks: (1) Meta-analyses (e.g., Wright et al. 2013, Gorduza et al. 2011) show significant increase in penile length (mean +1.2 cm) and glans diameter (+1.5 mm) after therapy, (2) no consistent reduction in overall complication rate (fistula, stenosis) in randomised trials, (3) some series suggest improved glans dehiscence rates in proximal hypospadias with glans <14 mm, (4) no difference in reoperation rate, (5) heterogeneity in dosing and timing limits pooled analysis, (6) current ESPU guidelines state preoperative androgens may be considered for proximal hypospadias with hypoplastic glans but evidence level is C (expert opinion / small studies). Trap: quoting a proven complication benefit when RCTs have not demonstrated this. Part (d) — 5 marks: (1) temporary penile growth and pubic hair regrowth (reversible within 6 months), (2) transient acceleration of linear growth and bone age advancement, (3) behavioural changes (irritability, aggression), (4) local skin reactions with topical cream (rash, erythema), (5) theoretical risk of premature epiphyseal plate closure — not observed with short courses. Reassure that all effects are reversible. Part (e) — 5 marks: Recommend use in this patient. Justification: (1) glans diameter is only 10 mm — below the 14 mm threshold associated with higher dehiscence rates, (2) narrow urethral plate (6 mm) may benefit from increased vascularity, (3) proximal hypospadias with hypoplasia is the subgroup most likely to benefit, (4) evidence is limited but the risk profile of short-course androgens is well-established as safe, (5) discuss risks with parents and obtain informed consent. Alternative: proceed without hormones but counsel on higher reoperation risk. Distinction candidates cite the need for properly designed multicentre RCT.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) The physiological rationale is that hypospadias represents partial androgen insufficiency during development, leading to a hypoplastic penis with small glans and narrow urethral plate. Androgen stimulation increases penile length, glans circumference, and local vascularity, facilitating surgical reconstruction. A larger glans allows better glans wings approximation with reduced dehiscence risk, while increased penile skin vascularity improves healing. (b) Testosterone: intramuscular testosterone enanthate 2 mg/kg every 2–4 weeks for 3 doses, with the final dose 4–6 weeks before surgery, OR topical testosterone 2.5% cream applied to the penile shaft twice daily for 4–6 weeks. HCG: 500 IU intramuscularly every 5–7 days for 3–4 doses, finishing 3–4 weeks preoperatively. Both are equally effective. Treatment should not be given closer than 4 weeks from surgery to avoid tissue oedema and friability. (c) Meta-analyses (Wright 2013, Gorduza 2011, Kaya 2016) confirm that preoperative androgens significantly increase penile length (mean 1.0–1.5 cm) and glans diameter (1.5–2 mm). However, randomised controlled trials (e.g., the only double-blind placebo-controlled trial by Gorduza 2011) showed no statistically significant reduction in fistula (25% vs 22%), meatal stenosis (8% vs 10%), or reoperation rates. Subgroup analyses suggest improved glans dehiscence in proximal hypospadias with glans diameter <14 mm. The overall complication reduction has not been proven. (d) Potential adverse effects: (1) temporary penile growth and pubic hair that regress after stopping; (2) transient linear growth acceleration with minor bone age advancement (1–3 months); (3) mild behavioural changes (irritability, increased activity) reported in 10–15%; (4) topical cream may cause local skin irritation. All effects are reversible within 3–6 months of cessation. No long-term effect on final adult height or fertility has been documented with short courses. (e) I would recommend preoperative testosterone therapy in this patient. The glans diameter is 10 mm, well below the 14 mm threshold associated with increased dehiscence, and the urethral plate is narrow at 6 mm. The best available evidence, although limited, shows clear anatomical benefit. The risk profile is acceptable with reversible side effects. I would use IM testosterone enanthate 2 mg/kg at weeks –8, –6, and –4 before surgery and explain to the parents that while it may reduce certain complications (especially glans dehiscence), the overall reoperation rate data are not definitive. A well-designed multicentre RCT is needed.
discriminators: Clearly states that evidence shows anatomical benefit but no proven reduction in overall complications
Distinguishes appropriate timing (finish 4–6 weeks preoperatively) to avoid tissue friability
Identifies glans diameter <14 mm as the threshold that may benefit most
Recommends therapy in selected proximal cases while acknowledging limited evidence quality
commonErrors: Part (a) — 4 marks: (1) Hypospadias is a form of incomplete virilisation; the penis and glans are often hypoplastic for age, (2) androgens stimulate penile growth by increasing penile length, glans circumference, and vascularity, (3) larger glans allows better glansplasty and neourethra accommodation, (4) improved penile length and skin quality facilitates flap harvesting and wound closure. Common error: suggesting hormones repair the underlying embryological defect — they only augment existing tissues. Trap: quoting a proven complication benefit when RCTs have not demonstrated this. questions.title: Redo Hypospadias with BXO – Two-Stage Bracka Repair
questions.questionText:
A 6-year-old boy presents for review. He underwent a TIP urethroplasty at 18 months of age for distal penile hypospadias. The repair broke down within the first year with complete glans dehiscence and the meatus has now retracted to a mid-shaft position. On examination there is a white, sclerotic, firm ring of tissue around the meatus extending proximally along the ventral shaft. The prepuce was used in the initial repair so is not available. There is 20° of ventral curvature on erection. The clinical appearance is consistent with lichen sclerosus (BXO). He has been passing urine with difficulty and a narrow stream.

(a) [DIAGNOSE] the clinical condition and explain the pathological relationship between BXO (lichen sclerosus) and failed hypospadias repair. [8 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the principles of the Bracka two-stage repair technique, detailing the first stage (excision, buccal mucosal graft harvest and inlay) and the second stage (tubularisation). [10 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the alternative reconstructive options for redo hypospadias with BXO when buccal mucosa is insufficient or unavailable. [8 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a postoperative care plan including catheter management, wound care, and follow-up surveillance for recurrence in a child with BXO undergoing staged reconstruction. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: Diagnosis: balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO) / lichen sclerosus. 2 marks for describing the clinical features (white sclerotic ring, firm texture, meatal stenosis, progressive nature). 3 marks for pathological relationship: (1) BXO is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis of unknown aetiology that causes progressive fibrosis of the glans, meatus, and urethra, (2) it may be unmasked or exacerbated by the initial surgery — trauma and inflammation can trigger latent BXO, (3) it causes progressive scarring, neourethral contracture, and repair breakdown. 3 marks for clinical significance: BXO is found in up to 30% of redo hypospadias cases and is the single most important risk factor for recurrent failure if not recognised and treated with complete excision of affected tissue. Trap: treating BXO conservatively without excision leads to 100% recurrence. Part (b) — 10 marks: First stage: (1) complete excision of all BXO-affected skin and urethral plate until healthy vascularised tissue is reached (frozen section may guide), (2) correct chordee with dorsal plication if needed (± urethral plate transection), (3) harvest buccal mucosa graft from lower lip/cheek (2–3 cm × 1–1.5 cm) — defat and fenestrate, (4) graft is quilted and sutured into the ventral defect from neomeatus to glans tip over a stent, (5) bolster dressing for 5 days to immobilise graft. Second stage (≥6 months later): (1) confirm adequate graft take and pliability, (2) U-shaped incision around graft, (3) tubularise graft over 10–12Fr stent with 2-layer closure, (4) dartos flap coverage if local tissue available, (5) glansplasty and meatoplasty. Credit mention of buccal graft as the gold standard in BXO because genital skin is also affected. Part (c) — 8 marks: 2 marks each: (1) post-auricular Wolfe graft (full-thickness skin graft) — good colour match and less hair-bearing than genital skin but less robust than buccal mucosa, (2) lingual mucosa graft (from lateral tongue) — similar properties to buccal, useful if buccal insufficient, (3) tunica vaginalis flap — from scrotum, provides well-vascularised tissue but may have cosmetic mismatch, (4) Two-stage with preputial skin graft — only if prepuce spared by BXO (rare). Trap: using genital skin in BXO will re-disease and fail. Part (d) — 4 marks: (1) catheter (8–10Fr silicone) for 7–10 days to divert urine, (2) suprapubic catheter for complex cases where transurethral catheter may disrupt graft, (3) antibiotic prophylaxis while catheterised, (4) regular follow-up to monitor for BXO recurrence (annually for 5 years) as late recurrence may occur. BXO tends to recur in the graft bed at a rate of 5–15% over long-term follow-up, requiring lifelong surveillance.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) This patient has balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO), the genital manifestation of lichen sclerosus, superimposed on failed hypospadias repair. BXO is characterised by a white, sclerotic, parchment-like skin with follicular plugging and progressive fibrosis. Pathologically, it is a chronic lymphocytic dermatosis of unknown aetiology that results in epidermal atrophy and dermal sclerosis. In the context of hypospadias, BXO may be triggered or unmasked by surgical trauma. It causes progressive fibrosis of the meatus, neourethra, and glans, leading to the characteristic white ring, meatal stenosis, and eventual repair breakdown. Up to 30% of redo hypospadias repairs show BXO on histology. Complete excision of all affected tissue down to healthy, bleeding tissue is essential because BXO invariably recurs if any affected skin remains; buccal mucosa is the preferred substitute because it is not susceptible to BXO. (b) Bracka two-stage repair — Stage 1: (1) Excise all BXO-affected ventral skin and the diseased urethral plate; extend excision into healthy, bleeding tissue. (2) Perform artificial erection; correct residual chordee with dorsal midline plication (Baskin technique) if >20° persists. (3) Harvest buccal mucosa from the lower lip (avoiding the parotid duct); defat the graft with fine scissors and make small fenestrations to prevent haematoma. (4) Suture the buccal graft into the ventral defect with 6/0 absorbable sutures from the neomeatus proximally to the glans tip distally; quilt the graft to the underlying tunica albuginea with several quilting sutures. (5) Place a silicone stent and a bolster dressing for 5 days to immobilise the graft. Stage 2 (≥6 months later): (1) Assess graft for adequate take — it should be pink, pliable, and well-integrated. (2) Make a U-shaped incision along the borders of the graft. (3) Tubularise the graft over a 10–12Fr stent in two layers using running subepithelial 6/0 absorbable sutures. (4) Cover with a dartos flap if available. (5) Perform glansplasty and meatoplasty. (c) Alternative grafts when buccal mucosa is unavailable: (1) Post-auricular full-thickness skin graft — excellent colour match for penile shaft, low hair-bearing potential, but less durable than buccal. (2) Lingual mucosal graft — similar histological properties to buccal, harvested from lateral tongue, useful when prior buccal harvest has been performed. (3) Tunica vaginalis flap — pedicled flap from parietal tunica vaginalis, can cover long defects, well-vascularised, but may have cosmetic mismatch and has been associated with graft contracture. (4) Colonic or bladder mucosa grafts — rarely used, reserved for pan-urethral stricture disease. The key principle is that genital skin must not be used in BXO because it will undergo identical pathological change. (d) Postoperative care: (1) Transurethral 8–10Fr silicone catheter for 7–10 days until initial graft take is secure. (2) In complex cases, a suprapubic catheter provides optimal urinary diversion without risking graft disruption. (3) Oral or intravenous antibiotics while catheterised. (4) Review at 2 weeks for stent removal, 6 weeks for clinical assessment, then annually. Lifelong surveillance is required because BXO can recur in the graft bed in 5–15% of cases, sometimes years later, and may present as delayed stricture or meatal stenosis. Early recurrence can be managed with topical corticosteroids, but established recurrence requires re-excision and regrafting.
discriminators: Recognises BXO as the single most important risk factor for recurrent hypospadias failure
States that complete excision of BXO tissue is mandatory, and genital skin must not be reused
Places buccal mucosa as the gold standard graft with clear justification (not susceptible to BXO)
Emphasises lifelong surveillance for late BXO recurrence in the graft bed

questions.title: Consent and Ethical Issues in Hypospadias Surgery
questions.questionText:
Theme: 

Options:


Scenarios:
questions.markingGuide:
Each part 3 marks. Must select the single best option. Part (a) — A (Gillick competence assessment): at 15, the boy is likely Gillick-competent; his refusal should be respected if competence is confirmed. Common error: D (court-ordered) — court would not overrule a competent child's refusal of non-life-saving surgery. Part (b) — A (Gillick competence assessment): the 14-year-old's wishes should be independently assessed; his desire for surgery should be considered. Trap: assuming parents' reluctance overrides the adolescent's wishes. Part (c) — B (parental responsibility): if both parents have parental responsibility, they must agree; if not, MDT discussion may help, but ultimately court may decide. Part (d) — C (best interests determination): MDT assessment of best interests considering clinical need, parental views, and the child's future autonomy. Part (e) — E (MDT discussion including child psychologist): psychological support and MDT review for body dysmorphia after apparently successful surgery. Part (f) — H (child protection referral): refusal of life-saving treatment is a child protection concern; emergency social services involvement. Trap: B (parental responsibility) does not extend to refusing life-saving care. Part (g) — J (assessment under Mental Capacity Act) / A (Gillick): at 16, presumed competent under MCA; if competent, his consent is sufficient. Part (h) — B (parental responsibility): the teenage mother has parental responsibility unless a court orders otherwise; no legal requirement for grandparent consent. Part (i) — E (MDT discussion/psychology): request for reversal requires psychological evaluation to understand motivations and expectations. Part (j) — G (second surgical opinion): extended family have no legal standing; offer a second opinion to address concerns but parents' decision is final.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) A — Gillick competence assessment. At 15, the boy is likely competent to make this decision. His refusal of elective surgery should be respected. The medical team should document his competence assessment and counsel the family. (b) A — Gillick competence assessment. The 14-year-old's wishes must be independently assessed. He may be Gillick-competent to request revision surgery for functional or cosmetic reasons. (c) B — Parental responsibility and consent. If parents disagree and both have parental responsibility, attempt mediation. If no agreement, the court may need to decide. The surgeon should not proceed without either both parties' consent or a court order. (d) C — Best interests determination. For an infant with proximal hypospadias and micropenis, the MDT (paediatric urologist, endocrinologist, psychologist, clinical ethics committee) should determine the best interests, incorporating clinical necessity, parental views, and the child's future autonomy. (e) E — MDT discussion including child psychologist. Depression and body dysmorphia after technically successful surgery require psychological assessment and support. The MDT should explore his concerns, offer cognitive behavioural therapy, and only consider revision after psychological clearance. (f) H — Child protection referral. Refusal of life-saving treatment for adrenal crisis constitutes neglect. Emergency social services and child protection procedures must be initiated. (g) J — Capacity assessment under Mental Capacity Act. At 16, the patient is presumed to have capacity. If capacity is confirmed, his consent alone is legally sufficient, even if parents disagree. (h) B — Parental responsibility. The teenage mother retains parental responsibility. Her consent alone is sufficient. No other family member has automatic legal standing unless they hold a parental responsibility order. (i) E — MDT discussion with psychologist. Reversal of circumcision is an unusual request. Psychological evaluation is essential to understand underlying motivations, body image concerns, and to set realistic expectations about surgical outcomes. (j) G — Second surgical opinion. Extended family have no legal authority over the child's medical care. Offer a second surgical opinion to address their concerns, but the parents' decision regarding technique is final.
discriminators: Differentiates Gillick competence from Fraser guidelines and Mental Capacity Act for 16-year-olds
Recognises that parental refusal of life-saving care triggers child protection, not deferral to parental rights
Distinguishes adolescent consent from parental consent in elective genital surgery
Emphasises MDT and psychological input for body image concerns after hypospadias repair
commonErrors: Each part 3 marks. Must select the single best option. Part (a) — A (Gillick competence assessment): at 15, the boy is likely Gillick-competent; his refusal should be respected if competence is confirmed. Common error: D (court-ordered) — court would not overrule a competent child's refusal of non-life-saving surgery. Part (b) — A (Gillick competence assessment): the 14-year-old's wishes should be independently assessed; his desire for surgery should be considered. Trap: assuming parents' reluctance overrides the adolescent's wishes. Part (c) — B (parental responsibility): if both parents have parental responsibility, they must agree; if not, MDT discussion may help, but ultimately court may decide. Part (d) — C (best interests determination): MDT assessment of best interests considering clinical need, parental views, and the child's future autonomy. Part (e) — E (MDT discussion including child psychologist): psychological support and MDT review for body dysmorphia after apparently successful surgery. Part (f) — H (child protection referral): refusal of life-saving treatment is a child protection concern; emergency social services involvement. Trap: B (parental responsibility) does not extend to refusing life-saving care. Part (g) — J (assessment under Mental Capacity Act) / A (Gillick): at 16, presumed competent under MCA; if competent, his consent is sufficient. Part (h) — B (parental responsibility): the teenage mother has parental responsibility unless a court orders otherwise; no legal requirement for grandparent consent. Part (i) — E (MDT discussion/psychology): request for reversal requires psychological evaluation to understand motivations and expectations. Part (j) — G (second surgical opinion): extended family have no legal standing; offer a second opinion to address concerns but parents' decision is final.

questions.title: Physiological vs Pathological Phimosis and the Foreskin
questions.questionText:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to the paediatric urology clinic by his mother who is concerned that he cannot retract his foreskin. She has tried to retract it gently during bathing but he complains of pain. He has never had balanitis or urinary tract infections. On examination the foreskin is healthy in appearance with no scarring or whitening. The preputial opening is pinhole but the boy can pass urine with a good stream.

(a) [DEFINE] the terms physiological phimosis and pathological phimosis. Explain the embryological development of foreskin separation from the glans (balano-preputial lamina). [10 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the natural history of physiological phimosis, including the expected rates of complete foreskin retractability at ages 1, 3, 5, 10 and 16 years. [8 marks]
(c) [DIFFERENTIATE] between the clinical features of physiological phimosis and pathological phimosis due to balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO). Include inspection, palpation, and history. [12 marks]
(d) [RECOMMEND] the management strategy for this 5-year-old. Justify your plan with reference to published guidelines on the non-surgical management of physiological phimosis. [10 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the indications for circumcision in children, distinguishing medical from cultural/religious indications. Address the ethical framework for obtaining valid consent. [10 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 10 marks: 3 marks for definition of physiological phimosis: non-retractile foreskin due to congenital adhesions between glans and prepuce without pathological scarring; resolves spontaneously with age. 3 marks for pathological phimosis: inability to retract due to scarring, fibrosis, or inflammation — typically BXO. 4 marks for embryology: (1) at 8–12 weeks gestation the prepuce forms as a ridge of ectoderm on the dorsal aspect of the glans, (2) it grows ventrally and fuses with the glans via the balano-preputial lamina, (3) the lamina undergoes squamous metaplasia and desquamation from late gestation onwards, (4) separation is complete in most boys by puberty but varies widely. Common error: describing phimosis as always pathological in children under 5. Part (b) — 8 marks: Rates of full retractability: age 1 year — 10–20% boys can retract fully; age 3 — 40–50%; age 5 — 60–70%; age 10 — 80–85%; age 16 — 95–99%. Accept ranges within 10% of these figures. Important: most boys will have a non-retractile foreskin at birth; this is normal. Trap: quoting retractability below 50% at 3 years as abnormal. Part (c) — 12 marks: Physiological: (1) foreskin appears normal with no scarring, (2) preputial opening may be pinhole but is soft and elastic, (3) no white discolouration, (4) ballooning during urination may occur but is transient and harmless, (5) no history of balanitis or pain on retraction. Pathological (BXO): (1) white, sclerotic, well-demarcated ring at the tip of the prepuce, (2) firm, fibrotic, non-elastic phimotic ring, (3) on eversion — the classic 'porcelain-white' appearance, (4) may be associated with meatal stenosis if BXO involves glans, (5) history of progressive difficulty retracting previously retractile foreskin, (6) may cause poor stream, discomfort, or urinary retention. 2 marks per well-described differentiating feature up to 12. Part (d) — 10 marks: (1) Reassure parents that this is physiological phimosis — the child has no BXO, no UTI, and passes urine well. (2) Advise against forcible retraction which causes pain, fissuring, and may lead to pathological scarring (iatrogenic BXO). (3) Recommend gentle hygiene only — washing the outside of the foreskin with water. (4) Discuss topical steroid therapy as an option if parents are keen to accelerate resolution: betamethasone 0.05% or clobetasol 0.05% cream twice daily for 4–6 weeks, applied to the phimotic ring with gentle retraction after 2 weeks. (5) Refer to BAPS/ESPU guidelines: physiological phimosis does not require surgery; only 1% of boys ultimately need circumcision for persistent pathological phimosis. Trap: recommending circumcision for physiological phimosis at age 5 is inappropriate. Part (e) — 10 marks: Medical indications: (1) pathological phimosis (BXO), (2) recurrent balanoposthitis unresponsive to medical treatment, (3) recurrent UTIs with phimosis, (4) paraphimosis that cannot be reduced, (5) ballooning causing urinary retention — rare. Cultural/religious: Jewish brit milah (8th day), Islamic khitan (any age before puberty). Ethical consent framework: (1) parents must give informed consent (parental responsibility), (2) the child's best interests must be the primary consideration — non-therapeutic circumcision remains controversial; GMC and BAPS recommend it should only be performed if in the child's best interests, (3) Gillick competence should be considered in older children — their refusal should be respected, (4) discussion of risks: bleeding (1–2%), infection (2%), meatal stenosis (5–10% in newborn circumcision), and psychological impact, (5) adequate analgesia and anaesthesia must be provided.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Physiological phimosis is the normal inability to retract the foreskin in young boys due to congenital adhesions between the glans and the inner preputial epithelium. These adhesions represent the normal embryological fusion that gradually separates through desquamation of the balano-preputial lamina. Pathological phimosis is the inability to retract due to a scarred, fibrotic foreskin, most commonly caused by BXO (lichen sclerosus). Embryologically, the prepuce develops from a ridge of ectoderm at 8–12 weeks that grows ventrally, covering the glans and fusing via the balano-preputial lamina. This lamina separates by squamous metaplasia and keratinisation starting in the third trimester and continuing through childhood. (b) At birth, only ~4% of boys have a fully retractile foreskin. By 1 year ~20% are retractile, by 3 years ~50%, by 5 years ~70%, by 10 years ~85%, and by 16 years ~99% of boys have a fully retractile foreskin. This natural history underscores that non-retractility in a young boy is almost always normal and does not require intervention. (c) Physiological phimosis: the foreskin is soft and elastic with no visible scar; the opening may be pinhole but will dilate with gentle traction; the glans may be visible as a bulge during ballooning. There is no history of progressive tightening. Pathological phimosis (BXO): the classic finding is a firm, white, sclerotic ring at the tip of the foreskin that does not stretch. On eversion, there is a porcelain-white appearance of the inner prepuce. The phimosis is progressive — a previously retractile foreskin becomes non-retractile. There may be associated meatal stenosis, fissuring, bleeding, and a poor urinary stream. Pain on urination or during erections may be reported. (d) This child has physiological phimosis — the foreskin is healthy with no scarring, he voids with a good stream, and has had no infections. Management: (1) Reassure the mother that this is normal for his age and will likely resolve spontaneously. (2) Advise against forcible retraction as this causes trauma, pain, and can induce scarring (iatrogenic BXO). (3) Advise normal hygiene — washing the exterior only. (4) If parents are anxious, offer a trial of topical betamethasone 0.05% cream applied twice daily to the phimotic ring for 4–6 weeks with gentle retraction after 2 weeks — this achieves resolution in 60–80% of cases. (5) Reassure that fewer than 1% of boys ultimately require circumcision for medical reasons. (e) Medical indications for circumcision: pathological phimosis (BXO, confirmed clinically or histologically), recurrent balanoposthitis (>3 episodes/year despite hygiene and medical therapy), recurrent UTIs in boys with phimosis (weak evidence), paraphimosis not amenable to reduction, and rare cases of ballooning causing urinary retention. Cultural/religious indications: Jewish brit milah on day 8, Islamic khitan, and certain African and Pacific cultural practices. Ethical framework: The surgeon must ensure that (1) the child's best interests are paramount — for non-therapeutic circumcision the benefit must be clearly demonstrated; (2) valid consent is obtained from someone with parental responsibility, who has been fully informed of risks (bleeding 1–2%, infection 2%, meatal stenosis up to 10% in neonates, need for reoperation 2–5%); (3) for older children, their views should be sought and a Gillick competence assessment performed if they refuse; (4) adequate anaesthesia (general anaesthetic with local anaesthetic block) must be provided; (5) the procedure should be performed or directly supervised by a trained surgeon — BAPS recommends against ritual circumcision by non-medical practitioners due to complication rates.
discriminators: Clearly separates physiological from pathological phimosis with age-appropriate retractability rates
Provides evidence-based management with steroid therapy as a non-surgical option
Distinguishes medical from cultural/religious indications with an ethical consent framework
Warns against forcible retraction which can cause iatrogenic BXO

questions.title: BXO (Lichen Sclerosus) — Diagnosis and Comprehensive Management
questions.questionText:
A 9-year-old uncircumcised boy presents with a 12-month history of progressive difficulty retracting his foreskin. He now can barely retract to reveal a tiny portion of the glans. His mother reports he sometimes has a split-second 'catch' in his stream and occasionally has to strain to pass urine. On examination there is a whitish, sclerotic ring at the preputial tip with a 'porcelain-white' appearance on gentle eversion. The glans appears normal. There is no balanitis.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the typical clinical features of balanitis xerotica obliterans (lichen sclerosus) affecting the prepuce and glans in prepubertal boys. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiology and histology of lichen sclerosus, including why it is important to distinguish from physiological phimosis. [8 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the indications for and technique of preputioplasty as an alternative to circumcision in selected cases of BXO. [10 marks]
(d) [COMPARE] the evidence for preputioplasty versus circumcision in the treatment of BXO. Include recurrence rates, cosmetic outcomes, and patient satisfaction. [12 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the long-term management of BXO, including the risk of meatal involvement, malignant transformation, and indications for surveillance. [12 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: 2 marks each: (1) whitish/sclerotic ring at preputial tip well-demarcated from normal skin, (2) firm, non-elastic phimotic ring that does not stretch, (3) 'porcelain-white' appearance of inner prepuce on eversion, (4) may include glans involvement (erythematous, atrophic patches, meatal stenosis). Also accept: fissuring, bleeding with retraction, urethral discharge. Common error: diagnosing BXO without the characteristic white sclerotic appearance — balanitis alone is not BXO. Part (b) — 8 marks: Pathophysiology: chronic inflammatory dermatosis of unknown aetiology, possibly autoimmune. 4 marks for histology: (1) epidermal atrophy with hyperkeratosis, (2) basal cell liquefaction degeneration, (3) dermal oedema with homogenisation of collagen (sclerosis), (4) lymphocytic band-like infiltrate. 1 mark for significance — immunological studies may show anti-BP180 antibodies. 3 marks for why important: misdiagnosis as physiological phimosis leads to inappropriate conservative management, progression, and potential meatal involvement. Part (c) — 10 marks: Indications: (1) mild BXO limited to the prepuce without glans involvement, (2) normal meatus and distal urethra, (3) good stream without straining, (4) family desire for foreskin preservation. Technique (1–2 marks each): (1) Dorsal slit at 12 o'clock through phimotic ring, (2) transverse closure with 5/0 or 6/0 absorbable sutures (Heineke-Mikulicz principle), (3) multiple longitudinal incisions (triple incision preputioplasty) with transverse closure, (4) balloon dilation and steroid injection — alternative less effective, (5) postoperative topical steroid to prevent re-scarring. Trap: preputioplasty in established BXO with glans involvement has high recurrence. Part (d) — 12 marks: Circumcision: (1) definitive cure — recurrence rate <5% when BXO limited to prepuce, (2) allows histological confirmation, (3) complication rate 2–5% (bleeding, infection, meatal stenosis), (4) cosmetic outcome good but foreskin permanently removed. Preputioplasty: (1) preserves foreskin, (2) recurrence rate 20–40% at 2 years, (3) lower patient satisfaction if recurrence occurs, (4) may be appropriate for mild BXO where preservation is strongly desired. Evidence: studies (e.g., Kikiros 2000, Wilkinson 2012) show circumcision is superior in long-term resolution; preputioplasty has higher recurrence but is a reasonable alternative with shared decision-making. Common error: quoting preputioplasty as equivalent to circumcision for BXO. Part (e) — 12 marks: Long-term management: (1) After circumcision, monitor for meatal stenosis (occurs in 10–15% of boys with BXO post-circumcision) — treat with meatal dilation or meatotomy, (2) urethral involvement risk: 10–20% of boys with penile BXO develop anterior urethral stricture — consider uroflowmetry if symptoms develop, (3) malignant transformation: <1% risk of squamous cell carcinoma with long-standing untreated BXO in adults; risk in boys is extremely low but long-term surveillance is still recommended, (4) annual follow-up until transition to adult services, (5) topical corticosteroids for early recurrence or glans patches, (6) patient and family education about symptoms of recurrence (stream change, difficulty voiding).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) BXO presents with a classic triad: (1) a white, sclerotic, well-demarcated ring at the preputial orifice, (2) phimosis that is firm, fibrotic, and non-elastic (unlike the soft, elastic pinhole of physiological phimosis), (3) on eversion of the prepuce, the inner layer has a 'porcelain-white' colour with possible fissuring. The glans may be involved, showing erythematous, atrophic, or ivory-white patches. Associated features include balanitis, urethral discharge, and a history of progressive tightening that may include a previously retractile foreskin becoming non-retractile. (b) Lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis of unknown aetiology, thought to have an autoimmune component (associated with HLA-DQ7, anti-BP180 antibodies). It can be triggered by trauma, infection, or hormonal factors. Histology shows epidermal atrophy with hyperkeratosis, basal cell liquefaction degeneration, dermal oedema with homogenisation of collagen, and a band-like lymphocytic infiltrate in the upper dermis. Distinguishing from physiological phimosis is critical because BXO is a progressive condition that can extend to the glans, meatus, and anterior urethra if untreated, causing meatal stenosis, urethral stricture, and, rarely, malignant transformation. Conservative management or topical steroids alone will not halt progression of established BXO. (c) Preputioplasty is indicated for mild BXO limited to the prepuce with a normal meatus, good urinary stream, and strong family preference for foreskin preservation. The technique involves a dorsal longitudinal incision (or multiple radial incisions) through the phimotic ring, which is then closed transversely with 5/0 or 6/0 absorbable sutures (Heineke-Mikulicz principle). This widens the preputial orifice. A postoperative course of topical corticosteroid cream reduces the risk of re-scarring. It is not suitable if there is glans involvement, extensive scarring, or meatal stenosis. (d) Circumcision is the gold standard treatment for BXO. It provides definitive cure with a recurrence rate of <5% when disease is limited to the prepuce. Histological examination confirms the diagnosis. However, it carries risks of bleeding (1–2%), infection (1–2%), and meatal stenosis (5–10%, higher in BXO patients). Preputioplasty preserves the foreskin but has a recurrence rate of 20–40% at 1–2 years, often requiring eventual circumcision. Patient satisfaction is lower with preputioplasty if recurrence occurs, but initial satisfaction is high in those who value foreskin preservation. Current evidence (Kikiros, Wilkinson, ESPU guidelines) supports circumcision as the more definitive treatment; preputioplasty is a second-line option for carefully selected mild cases after shared decision-making. (e) Long-term management: (1) After circumcision, boys need surveillance for meatal stenosis, which occurs in 10–15% of BXO cases. Uroflowmetry should be performed if the stream deteriorates. Meatal dilation or meatotomy is effective. (2) Anterior urethral stricture develops in 10–20% of BXO patients — urethroscopy or urethrography is indicated if flow deteriorates. (3) Squamous cell carcinoma risk: <1% in adults with long-standing untreated BXO; the risk in childhood is extremely low but patients should be counselled about lifelong surveillance for changes in the glans or meatus. (4) Annual review should continue into adolescence. (5) Recurrence on the glans can be managed with topical corticosteroids (clobetasol 0.05%). (6) On transition to adult services, patients should be informed about the small malignant potential and told to report any new lesions, ulceration, or bleeding.

Recognises circumcision as the gold standard with lowest recurrence rate for BXO
Highlights meatal stenosis risk after circumcision in BXO (10–15%)
Addresses malignant potential and the need for surveillance into adulthood
