questions.title: Paraphimosis Reduction and Circumcision Complications
questions.questionText:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the Emergency Department by his mother. His foreskin was retracted during bathing 4 hours ago and cannot be replaced. The glans is swollen, dusky, and tender. The retracted prepuce forms a tight constricting band proximal to the corona. There is no history of previous episodes. The boy has had some difficulty passing urine but has voided small amounts.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the step-by-step technique for closed manual reduction of paraphimosis in a child. Include preparation, analgesia, and the specific manoeuvres. [7 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the indications for dorsal slit or emergency circumcision when closed reduction fails. [5 marks]
(c) [LIST] six complications of circumcision in children, briefly describing the aetiology and prevention of each. [6 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the management of meatal stenosis after circumcision, including the role of meatotomy versus meatoplasty. [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 7 marks: 1 mark each: (1) assess for tissue viability — pink glans can be reduced; dusky/purple requires urgent reduction; if non-viable, emergency surgery, (2) topical anaesthetic (lidocaine-prilocaine cream) applied for 20–30 minutes + oral analgesia, (3) gentle sustained compression of the glans with gauze soaked in iced saline for 3–5 minutes to reduce oedema, (4) both thumbs placed on glans, index fingers behind the constricting band, (5) apply steady, sustained pressure on glans while pulling the prepuce forward (towards the penis), (6) the 'Ely's manoeuvre' — wrapping a Penrose drain or elastic bandage around the glans from distal to proximal to express oedema, then reattempt reduction, (7) after reduction, ensure prepuce can be returned to normal position and advise against retraction for 1 week. Common error: using forceful retraction — causes more swelling and makes reduction harder. Part (b) — 5 marks: Indications for dorsal slit: (1) failed closed reduction after adequate analgesia and oedema reduction, (2) evidence of impending glans ischaemia (severe swelling, discolouration), (3) inability to void with progressive retention. Technique: (1) after anaesthesia, (2) the constricting band is identified, (3) a dorsal longitudinal incision is made through the constricting band at 12 o'clock, (4) the foreskin is then reduced, (5) the incision can be closed transversely (preputioplasty) or the wound left open for delayed repair or circumcision. Emergency circumcision may be performed if the prepuce is non-viable or heavily scarred. Trap: performing emergency circumcision without first attempting simple dorsal slit. Part (c) — 6 marks (1 each): (1) Meatal stenosis — aetiology: post-circumcision meatal ulceration from ammoniacal dermatitis → epithelial scarring; prevention: apply barrier cream (Vaseline or topical oestrogen) to exposed meatus for 2–3 weeks post-circumcision, (2) Skin bridge — aetiology: adhesions between raw edges of the circumcision wound and glans; prevention: regular retraction of skin by parent postoperatively, daily wound care, (3) Haemorrhage — aetiology: inadequate haemostasis, usually from frenular artery; prevention: careful ligation/fulguration of frenular and dorsal vessels, (4) Infection — aetiology: wound contamination; prevention: aseptic technique, parent education on wound care, (5) Excess skin / inadequate circumcision — aetiology: poor marking of incision lines; prevention: accurate preoperative marking with pen, (6) Urethral injury — aetiology: catching the urethra in the suture or clamp; prevention: careful dissection, visualisation of the glans before suture placement. Part (d) — 7 marks: Meatal stenosis presents with a narrow, slit-like urinary stream, upward deflection, and prolonged voiding. Diagnosis is confirmed by pin-point meatus. Management: (1) mild cases — meatal dilation with topical anaesthetic and serial dilation, (2) meatotomy — most common: clamp the ventral meatus with straight mosquito forceps for 30 seconds, then incise with scissors; suture meatal edges to glans epithelium with 6/0 absorbable sutures, (3) meatoplasty (e.g., Mathieu or Heineke-Mikulicz) for more severe stenosis — involves advancing a V-flap of para-meatal skin into the meatal incision to create a wider lumen, (4) postoperative care: topical oestrogen cream to prevent restenosis, +/– serial self-dilation. Meatotomy has a success rate of >90%; meatal stenosis recurrence is 5–10%.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Closed manual reduction: (1) Assess viability — if glans is dusky/purple, proceed urgently. (2) Apply topical lidocaine-prilocaine cream to the glans and constricting band, with oral analgesia. (3) Apply gentle sustained compression with gauze soaked in iced saline for 3–5 minutes to express oedema. (4) Place both thumbs on the glans and the index/middle fingers behind the constricting band. (5) Push the glans proximally while pulling the prepuce distally with steady, sustained pressure — not jerky movements. (6) Alternative: wrap a Penrose drain around the glans from distal to proximal, expressing oedema, then reduce. (7) After reduction, confirm the prepuce lies in its normal position and advise the family to avoid retraction for 1 week, and to retract gently thereafter during bathing. (b) Indications for dorsal slit: failed closed reduction despite adequate oedema reduction and analgesia; evidence of glans ischaemia (purple colour, capillary refill >3 seconds); urinary retention. The dorsal slit is made at 12 o'clock through the constricting band, releasing the constriction. The wound may be closed transversely or left open for formal circumcision after oedema resolves. Emergency circumcision is indicated only if the prepuce is non-viable, heavily scarred, or if there is recurrence after dorsal slit. (c) Six complications of circumcision: (1) Meatal stenosis — occurs from ammoniacal irritation of the exposed meatus; prevent with barrier ointment postoperatively. (2) Skin bridge — raw surfaces of the shaft and glans heal together; prevent with regular penile traction and separation of raw surfaces. (3) Haemorrhage — from frenular artery or dorsal vessels; prevent with careful ligation or diathermy. (4) Wound infection — prevent with aseptic technique and hygiene. (5) Inadequate/excess skin removal — prevent with accurate marking. (6) Urethrocutaneous fistula — rare, from catching the urethra; prevent by careful suture placement under direct vision. (d) Management of meatal stenosis: Diagnosis is clinical — a pinpoint meatus with upward deflection and prolonged voiding. Uroflowmetry shows a flat, low-flow curve. (1) Mild cases: topical anaesthetic with gentle serial dilation using sounds or a lubricated 8Fr catheter. (2) Meatotomy (standard treatment): under general anaesthesia, insert a straight mosquito forceps into the stenotic meatus to the level of the groove of the glans, clamp for 30 seconds to crush tissue, then incise with scissors in the midline. The resultant raw edges are sutured to the glans epithelium with 6/0 absorbable sutures (interrupted) to prevent re-stenosis. (3) Meatoplasty: for recurrent or severe stenosis, a V-flap of ventral parameatal skin (or a Y-V advancement) is used to create a wide, slit-like meatus. (4) Postoperative: topical oestrogen or steroid cream applied for 2 weeks reduces recurrence. Success of meatotomy is >90% with low recurrence if sutures are placed carefully.
discriminators: Provides step-by-step paraphimosis reduction with evidence-based technique including alternative (Penrose drain)
Distinguishes dorsal slit from emergency circumcision as sequential escalation
Lists specific and relevant complications of circumcision with preventive strategies
Differentiates meatotomy from meatoplasty with clear indications for each
commonErrors: Part (a) — 7 marks: 1 mark each: (1) assess for tissue viability — pink glans can be reduced; dusky/purple requires urgent reduction; if non-viable, emergency surgery, (2) topical anaesthetic (lidocaine-prilocaine cream) applied for 20–30 minutes + oral analgesia, (3) gentle sustained compression of the glans with gauze soaked in iced saline for 3–5 minutes to reduce oedema, (4) both thumbs placed on glans, index fingers behind the constricting band, (5) apply steady, sustained pressure on glans while pulling the prepuce forward (towards the penis), (6) the 'Ely's manoeuvre' — wrapping a Penrose drain or elastic bandage around the glans from distal to proximal to express oedema, then reattempt reduction, (7) after reduction, ensure prepuce can be returned to normal position and advise against retraction for 1 week. Common error: using forceful retraction — causes more swelling and makes reduction harder. Part (b) — 5 marks: Indications for dorsal slit: (1) failed closed reduction after adequate analgesia and oedema reduction, (2) evidence of impending glans ischaemia (severe swelling, discolouration), (3) inability to void with progressive retention. Technique: (1) after anaesthesia, (2) the constricting band is identified, (3) a dorsal longitudinal incision is made through the constricting band at 12 o'clock, (4) the foreskin is then reduced, (5) the incision can be closed transversely (preputioplasty) or the wound left open for delayed repair or circumcision. Emergency circumcision may be performed if the prepuce is non-viable or heavily scarred. Trap: performing emergency circumcision without first attempting simple dorsal slit. Part (c) — 6 marks (1 each): (1) Meatal stenosis — aetiology: post-circumcision meatal ulceration from ammoniacal dermatitis → epithelial scarring; prevention: apply barrier cream (Vaseline or topical oestrogen) to exposed meatus for 2–3 weeks post-circumcision, (2) Skin bridge — aetiology: adhesions between raw edges of the circumcision wound and glans; prevention: regular retraction of skin by parent postoperatively, daily wound care, (3) Haemorrhage — aetiology: inadequate haemostasis, usually from frenular artery; prevention: careful ligation/fulguration of frenular and dorsal vessels, (4) Infection — aetiology: wound contamination; prevention: aseptic technique, parent education on wound care, (5) Excess skin / inadequate circumcision — aetiology: poor marking of incision lines; prevention: accurate preoperative marking with pen, (6) Urethral injury — aetiology: catching the urethra in the suture or clamp; prevention: careful dissection, visualisation of the glans before suture placement. Part (d) — 7 marks: Meatal stenosis presents with a narrow, slit-like urinary stream, upward deflection, and prolonged voiding. Diagnosis is confirmed by pin-point meatus. Management: (1) mild cases — meatal dilation with topical anaesthetic and serial dilation, (2) meatotomy — most common: clamp the ventral meatus with straight mosquito forceps for 30 seconds, then incise with scissors; suture meatal edges to glans epithelium with 6/0 absorbable sutures, (3) meatoplasty (e.g., Mathieu or Heineke-Mikulicz) for more severe stenosis — involves advancing a V-flap of para-meatal skin into the meatal incision to create a wider lumen, (4) postoperative care: topical oestrogen cream to prevent restenosis, +/– serial self-dilation. Meatotomy has a success rate of >90%; meatal stenosis recurrence is 5–10%.

questions.title: Consent, Gillick Competence and Cultural Considerations in Circumcision
questions.questionText:
A 13-year-old Muslim boy is referred by his GP for circumcision. He has never had any problems with his foreskin — no phimosis, no balanitis, and he can retract fully. The family request circumcision for religious reasons. On examination the foreskin and glans are entirely normal. When seen alone, the boy tells you he does not want the operation. He says his parents are pressuring him and he is scared of the pain and potential complications. His parents are in the waiting room and are insistent the procedure goes ahead.

(a) [DEFINE] Gillick competence and explain how and when it should be assessed in a 13-year-old. [5 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the legal framework for consent to surgery in a child where the child and parents disagree regarding non-therapeutic circumcision. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the role of the multidisciplinary team (MDT) and psychological support in managing this clinical scenario. [5 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a management plan for this case that respects the boy's autonomy, the parents' cultural/religious beliefs, and the legal framework. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 2 marks for definition: Gillick competence — the ability of a child under 16 to consent to medical treatment if they have sufficient intelligence, maturity, and understanding to comprehend what is involved. 3 marks for assessment: (1) determine if the child understands the nature, purpose, and consequences of the proposed treatment, (2) assess their understanding of the alternatives and risks of not treating, (3) ensure they are not acting under duress or undue influence. Must be assessed on an individual basis — not an age-based threshold. Document the assessment in the medical record. Common error: assuming competence is an all-or-nothing status — it is decision-specific. Part (b) — 6 marks: (1) A Gillick-competent child can consent to or refuse treatment; their refusal of a non-therapeutic procedure should be respected. (2) Parents' consent alone is insufficient if a competent child refuses because the child's autonomy takes precedence in non-life-saving treatment. (3) The case is different from an emergency or life-saving intervention where parental consent may suffice. (4) If parents insist, the surgeon should not proceed — this would constitute battery and assault. (5) The GMC and BAPS guidelines state that where a competent child refuses non-therapeutic treatment, that refusal should be respected. (6) If parents challenge this, they can apply to the court, but the court is very unlikely to overrule a competent child's refusal of non-therapeutic surgery. Trap: proceeding with circumcision based on parental consent alone when the competent child refuses. Part (c) — 5 marks: (1) Involve a clinical psychologist or child and adolescent mental health service (CAMHS) to assess the boy's understanding and explore his fears, (2) involve a hospital chaplain or cultural liaison if needed to counsel the family on religious requirements, (3) MDT meeting with urology, psychology, and nursing to agree a consistent message, (4) provide written information for the family about the risks and benefits of circumcision and the legal position, (5) a social worker or hospital legal team may be needed if conflict escalates. Part (d) — 9 marks: (1) See the boy alone to assess Gillick competence in detail — discuss his understanding of the procedure, risks, benefits, alternatives, and explore his reasons for refusal, (2) document his competence assessment thoroughly, (3) meet with the family together with the boy to explain that the child's views are paramount and that you have assessed him as competent to refuse, (4) explain that performing surgery against his wishes would be unlawful (assault/battery), (5) offer a second surgical opinion if the family remains dissatisfied, (6) offer cultural/chaplaincy support to explain that Islamic law does not mandate circumcision after the age of consent if the child refuses, (7) offer a deferred plan — revisit discussion at 16 or 18 when the adolescent can consent independently, (8) if family is insistent and the boy remains adamant, consider involving the hospital legal team and/or an independent advocate for the child, (9) document all discussions and the outcome in the medical record; if necessary, write to the GP to advise the family not to seek circumcision elsewhere without the child's consent.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Gillick competence describes a child under 16's ability to consent to medical treatment if they have sufficient understanding and intelligence to understand the nature, purpose, and consequences of the proposed treatment. It must be assessed on a decision-specific basis. For a 13-year-old considering circumcision, the assessment should explore: his understanding of the surgical procedure (what it involves, recovery time), the risks (bleeding, infection, meatal stenosis, unsatisfactory cosmesis), the benefits (religious compliance, hygiene), and the consequences of not proceeding. He must demonstrate that he is not acting under duress. The assessment must be documented. (b) In English law, a Gillick-competent child's refusal of treatment is generally binding for non-therapeutic procedures. Parents cannot override a competent child's refusal. The surgeon cannot rely on parental consent alone — to proceed would be battery. The GMC (2007) and BAPS (2012) statements confirm that where a competent child refuses a non-therapeutic intervention such as circumcision, that refusal should be respected. If parents disagree, they can apply to the High Court using the inherent jurisdiction of the court, but the court will prioritise the child's autonomy. The surgeon should not proceed while the dispute is unresolved. (c) The MDT approach: (1) a child psychologist should assess the boy's fears and determine if they can be addressed with counselling, (2) the psychologist can also assess for coercion and ensure the boy's views are expressed freely, (3) a cultural liaison or chaplain can educate the family on religious flexibility — Islamic scholars confirm circumcision is recommended (Sunnah) but not obligatory, and can be deferred until the child chooses, (4) the nursing team can support the family with information and coordinate care, (5) if needed, social services can be involved to advocate for the child's best interests. (d) Management plan: (1) Interview the boy alone and assess Gillick competence — document his understanding of the procedure, risks, and his reasons for refusal. He appears Gillick-competent. (2) Meet jointly with the boy and his parents. Explain that the boy has been assessed as competent to make this decision and that his refusal must be respected. (3) Explain the legal position: proceeding against his wishes would be unlawful and the hospital cannot support this. (4) Validate the parents' religious concerns — offer to meet with the hospital chaplain or an imam to clarify that circumcision can be deferred. (5) Offer a deferred plan: revisit the discussion at age 16 when the boy can consent as a mature minor, or earlier if his views change. (6) Provide the family with written information about the decision and an open appointment if the boy later requests the procedure. (7) Offer ongoing psychological support for both the boy and family to address the conflict. (8) Document all discussions in the medical record. (9) If parents are insistent and consider taking the child elsewhere, contact the GP and consider a safeguarding referral to protect the child from non-consensual surgery.
discriminators: Demonstrates that a Gillick-competent child's refusal of non-therapeutic circumcision is binding
Recognises that parental consent alone is insufficient when a competent child refuses
Integrates cultural/religious awareness with legal obligations
Provides a practical step-by-step de-escalation plan with deferred option

Must be assessed on an individual basis — not an age-based threshold. Document the assessment in the medical record. (6) If parents challenge this, they can apply to the court, but the court is very unlikely to overrule a competent child's refusal of non-therapeutic surgery. Part (d) — 9 marks: (1) See the boy alone to assess Gillick competence in detail — discuss his understanding of the procedure, risks, benefits, alternatives, and explore his reasons for refusal, (2) document his competence assessment thoroughly, (3) meet with the family together with the boy to explain that the child's views are paramount and that you have assessed him as competent to refuse, (4) explain that performing surgery against his wishes would be unlawful (assault/battery), (5) offer a second surgical opinion if the family remains dissatisfied, (6) offer cultural/chaplaincy support to explain that Islamic law does not mandate circumcision after the age of consent if the child refuses, (7) offer a deferred plan — revisit discussion at 16 or 18 when the adolescent can consent independently, (8) if family is insistent and the boy remains adamant, consider involving the hospital legal team and/or an independent advocate for the child, (9) document all discussions and the outcome in the medical record; if necessary, write to the GP to advise the family not to seek circumcision elsewhere without the child's consent.

questions.title: Complex BXO with Pan-urethral Involvement in an Adolescent
questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old boy presents with a 2-year history of progressive difficulty in passing urine. He was circumcised at age 7 for biopsy-proven BXO of the prepuce. He has had two previous meatal dilatations for meatal stenosis but these provided only temporary relief. His urinary stream is described as 'thin', he takes a long time to empty his bladder, and he has had two episodes of acute urinary retention requiring catheterisation. Uroflowmetry shows a Qmax of 5 mL/s with a flat plateau curve. Retrograde urethrogram shows a tight stenosis of the fossa navicularis extending 2 cm into the anterior urethra. The remainder of the urethra appears normal.

(a) [INTERPRET] the clinical picture and investigation findings. Explain the natural history of BXO involving the urethra after circumcision. [8 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the surgical options for management of anterior urethral stricture secondary to BXO, including the indications for and outcomes of buccal mucosal urethroplasty. [10 marks]
(c) [FORMULATE] a long-term management and surveillance plan for this patient, including the transition from paediatric to adult urology services. [8 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the ethical considerations regarding fertility, sexual function, and body image when counselling an adolescent about major urethral reconstruction. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: 3 marks for interpretation: BXO is not cured by circumcision — it continues to affect the glans and urethra. After preputial BXO is excised, the disease process can involve the glans (atrophic patches), meatus (stenosis), and anterior urethra (stricture). Meatal dilation alone fails because the underlying pathology is progressive fibrosis. The Qmax of 5 mL/s with plateau curve indicates severe stricture. RUG shows a 2 cm stricture of the fossa navicularis. 5 marks for natural history: (1) BXO is a pan-urethral disease in 10–20% of cases, (2) meatal stenosis develops in 10–15% of circumcised BXO patients, (3) untreated meatal stenosis progresses to anterior urethral stricture, (4) the stricture is typically inflammatory, progressive, and tends to be longer over time, (5) recurrence after dilation alone is the rule, not the exception. Trap: assuming circumcision cures BXO — it removes the preputial disease but glans and urethral disease may persist or progress. Part (b) — 10 marks: Options: (1) visual internal urethrotomy (VIU) — high failure rate in BXO-related stricture, not recommended as definitive treatment, (2) dilation — only palliative, stricture will recur, (3) buccal mucosal graft urethroplasty — gold standard. Buccal mucosal urethroplasty technique: (1) buccal mucosa harvested from lower lip or cheek (2 × 3 cm), (2) stricture is exposed via a ventral sagittal approach or perineal route, (3) the strictured segment is opened along its length, (4) buccal graft is placed as an onlay patch quilted to the underlying corpora cavernosum with 5/0 PDS, (5) graft is tubularised over a 16Fr catheter if the entire urethral plate is diseased, (6) a second layer of dartos or tunica vaginalis covers the graft, (7) suprapubic or urethral catheter for 3 weeks. Outcomes: success rate for primary buccal graft in BXO stricture is 80–90% at 5 years in specialist centres. (4) Staged urethroplasty for pan-urethral disease, (5) perineal urethrostomy as a last resort. Common error: recommending VIU for BXO stricture — recurrence >80%. Part (c) — 8 marks: Long-term plan: (1) after successful urethroplasty, perform uroflowmetry at 3, 6, and 12 months, then annually, (2) if flow deteriorates — investigate with urethroscopy or RUG, (3) lifelong surveillance because BXO can recur in the graft — buccal mucosa is not immune to BXO, (4) manage recurrences with dilation, topical steroids, or regrafting, (5) counsel about lifelong risk of stricture recurrence (10–20% at 10 years), (6) ensure transition to adult urology with a handover clinic that includes a summary of surgical history, histology, and surveillance plan, (7) provide the patient with a written summary of his condition, surgery, and the importance of long-term follow-up, (8) address psychosexual aspects during transition. Trap: discharging to GP follow-up — BXO requires specialist lifelong surveillance. Part (d) — 4 marks: (1) Fertility: urethral reconstruction does not directly affect spermatogenesis, but ejaculation may be affected if the stricture or surgery damages the bulbospongiosus or verumontanum — counsel that antegrade ejaculation is usually preserved with onlay techniques, (2) Sexual function: graft harvest and penile dissection may affect penile sensation or cause curvature — advise that most patients have normal erectile function postoperatively, (3) Body image: the scars from graft harvest (buccal, penile) and the altered appearance of the genitalia can cause distress — preoperative counselling with a psychologist is essential, (4) Consent: the patient must understand the magnitude of the procedure, the recovery time, the high initial success rate, and the lifelong need for surveillance; a Gillick competence assessment may be needed if he is under 16.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) This patient has BXO-related pan-urethral disease manifesting as a 2 cm fossa navicularis stricture despite prior circumcision and meatal dilations. BXO is a chronic, progressive inflammatory dermatosis; circumcision removes the preputial disease but does not halt glans or urethral involvement. The natural history is that meatal stenosis develops in 10–15% of BXO patients post-circumcision due to ongoing inflammation of the glans and meatus. Repeated dilation temporarily disrupts fibrosis but the underlying inflammatory process continues, leading to progressive scarring and extension into the anterior urethra. The Qmax of 5 mL/s with plateau curve confirms severe stricture. The RUG delineates a 2 cm stricture amenable to buccal mucosal onlay grafting. (b) Surgical options: (1) VIU has >80% recurrence in BXO strictures and is not recommended as definitive treatment — it may be used as a temporising measure. (2) Buccal mucosal graft urethroplasty is the gold standard. For a 2 cm fossa navicularis stricture, the technique involves harvesting a 2–3 cm buccal graft from the cheek (after submucosal infiltration with 1:100,000 adrenaline). The stricture is exposed via a ventral penile incision (or a perineal approach if more proximal). The strictured segment is opened along its length through healthy urethra proximally and distally. The buccal graft is trimmed to size, defatted, and quilted onto the underlying tunica albuginea as an onlay patch using 5/0 PDS. It is not tubularised unless the entire plate is diseased. A catheter (14–16Fr) is left for 2–3 weeks. Success rates for primary buccal graft in BXO stricture are 80–90% at 5 years. (3) Two-stage urethroplasty (Bracka) is used for pan-urethral disease >4 cm. (4) Perineal urethrostomy is reserved for patients who fail all other options. (c) Long-term surveillance: (1) Uroflowmetry at 3, 6, 12 months, then annually. (2) If Qmax drops below 10 mL/s or symptoms recur, perform urethroscopy or RUG. (3) Lifelong follow-up is mandatory — BXO recurs in grafts in 5–15% of cases and may require dilation, topical steroids, or regrafting. (4) At transition (around age 16–18), a formal handover to an adult reconstructive urologist is essential. The transition should include a written summary of all procedures, biopsy results confirming BXO, and the surveillance schedule. (5) Provide the patient with his own copy of this summary and advise him to inform any future urologist about the BXO diagnosis. (6) Address sexual health, body image, and the need for lifelong surveillance during the transition consultation. (d) Ethical considerations: (1) Fertility — reassure that buccal onlay grafting preserves ejaculatory function in the vast majority; however, the underlying BXO does not affect fertility. (2) Sexual function — the procedure preserves erectile function. Penile sensation may be altered in the distribution of the dorsal nerve but usually recovers. (3) Body image — an adolescent facing major urethral reconstruction is vulnerable to body image concerns. Preoperative psychological counselling should address expectations about penile appearance, graft harvest site scars (buccal), and the catheter period. (4) Consent — if under 16, Gillick competence must be assessed. The adolescent must understand the magnitude of surgery, the 80–90% success rate, the 10–20% risk of long-term recurrence, and the need for lifelong surveillance. His consent, not just his parents', must be obtained. A deferred decision is acceptable if he is not ready.
discriminators: Recognises that circumcision does not cure BXO — glans and urethral disease may progress
Identifies buccal mucosal onlay urethroplasty as the gold standard, not VIU or dilation
Emphasises lifelong specialist surveillance with transition to adult services
Addresses adolescent-specific psychosexual and consent issues in complex reconstruction

questions.title: Circumcision Complications — Recognition and Management
questions.questionText:
Theme: 

Options:


Scenarios:
questions.markingGuide:
Each part 3 marks. Part (a) — B (Skin bridge). The tethering band between shaft skin and glans is a classic post-circumcision adhesion/skin bridge. Common error: diagnosing meatal stenosis. Part (b) — A (Meatal stenosis). Pin-point meatus with narrow stream post-circumcision is meatal stenosis. Often occurs weeks to months after circumcision. Trap: confusing with congenital hypospadias meatus. Part (c) — C (Haemorrhage from frenular artery). Immediate bright red bleeding from frenular area post-circumcision is arterial haemorrhage, most commonly from the frenular artery. Part (d) — D (Wound infection). Pain, erythema, and purulent discharge 5 days postop indicates wound infection. Part (e) — E (Urethrocutaneous fistula). Urine from two sites — meatus and ventral shaft — is a fistula. Rare after circumcision, usually from catching the urethra in a suture. Part (f) — G (Concealed penis). The penis hidden within suprapubic fat after circumcision is a concealed/buried penis, often iatrogenic from removal of too much shaft skin. Part (g) — H (Excess skin / inadequate circumcision). Ballooning and uneven appearance indicates inadequate circumcision with remaining foreskin. Part (h) — I (Meatitis / meatal ulcer). Raw, erythematous meatus with ulcer and pain on voiding is meatal ulcer (ammoniacal dermatitis), often preceding meatal stenosis. Differentiate from meatal stenosis which is scarred, not raw. Part (i) — F (Glans injury / partial glans amputation). Visible partial glans loss during circumcision under local anaesthesia is a catastrophic iatrogenic injury. Requires immediate reconstructive surgical referral. Part (j) — J (Chordee due to excess skin removal). New-onset curvature after circumcision suggests chordee from excess skin removal — the ventral shaft is shorter causing the penis to bend.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) B — Skin bridge. This results from raw edges of the circumcision wound healing in contact with the glans. It is prevented by regular retraction of the healing skin edge by parents. Treatment is surgical division under general anaesthetic. (b) A — Meatal stenosis. Occurring at 4 weeks old, 10 days after circumcision, this is the classic presentation of post-circumcision meatal stenosis. The pathophysiology is ammoniacal irritation of the unprotected meatus leading to ulceration and scarring. Prevention: barrier ointment. Treatment: meatotomy or dilation. (c) C — Haemorrhage from frenular artery. Immediate postoperative bright red bleeding from the frenulum is arterial. Management: apply pressure; if continued, explore and ligate the frenular artery under anaesthesia. (d) D — Wound infection. Purulent discharge with erythema and pain indicates infection. Management: wound swab, oral antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or according to local policy), and wound care. (e) E — Urethrocutaneous fistula. A second opening on the ventral shaft through which urine passes is a fistula, an uncommon complication from inadvertent urethral suture placement. Requires formal fistulectomy and multilayer closure. (f) G — Concealed penis. The penis retracts into the suprapubic fat pad after excessive shaft skin removal. Management: release of trapped penis with skin reconstruction, often needs revision surgery. (g) H — Excess skin / inadequate circumcision. Incomplete removal of the prepuce leaves a redundant hood that balloons during voiding. Indication for revision circumcision if symptomatic. (h) I — Meatitis / meatal ulcer. A raw, ulcerated meatus with dysuria, distinct from meatal stenosis which is a white, scarred, stenotic meatus. Treatment: topical barrier cream (Vaseline or oestrogen cream) and reassurance. It resolves in 1–2 weeks. (i) F — Glans injury / partial glans amputation. This is a surgical emergency. Refer to specialist paediatric urology for microsurgical repair or reconstruction. (j) J — Chordee due to excess skin removal. Penile curvature from imbalance of skin coverage — the ventral shaft skin is deficient. Manage with Z-plasty or skin graft if symptomatic.
discriminators: Distinguishes meatal ulcer (raw, erythematous) from meatal stenosis (scarred, stenotic) as distinct sequential complications
Includes both common (skin bridge, meatal stenosis) and rare catastrophic (glans amputation) complications
Recognises concealed penis as a specific complication of excess skin removal
Differentiates ischaemic chordee from inadequate circumcision as separate post-surgical issues
commonErrors: Each part 3 marks. Part (a) — B (Skin bridge). The tethering band between shaft skin and glans is a classic post-circumcision adhesion/skin bridge. Common error: diagnosing meatal stenosis. Part (b) — A (Meatal stenosis). Pin-point meatus with narrow stream post-circumcision is meatal stenosis. Often occurs weeks to months after circumcision. Trap: confusing with congenital hypospadias meatus. Part (c) — C (Haemorrhage from frenular artery). Immediate bright red bleeding from frenular area post-circumcision is arterial haemorrhage, most commonly from the frenular artery. Part (d) — D (Wound infection). Pain, erythema, and purulent discharge 5 days postop indicates wound infection. Part (e) — E (Urethrocutaneous fistula). Urine from two sites — meatus and ventral shaft — is a fistula. Rare after circumcision, usually from catching the urethra in a suture. Part (f) — G (Concealed penis). The penis hidden within suprapubic fat after circumcision is a concealed/buried penis, often iatrogenic from removal of too much shaft skin. Part (g) — H (Excess skin / inadequate circumcision). Ballooning and uneven appearance indicates inadequate circumcision with remaining foreskin. Part (h) — I (Meatitis / meatal ulcer). Raw, erythematous meatus with ulcer and pain on voiding is meatal ulcer (ammoniacal dermatitis), often preceding meatal stenosis. Differentiate from meatal stenosis which is scarred, not raw. Part (i) — F (Glans injury / partial glans amputation). Visible partial glans loss during circumcision under local anaesthesia is a catastrophic iatrogenic injury. Requires immediate reconstructive surgical referral. Part (j) — J (Chordee due to excess skin removal). New-onset curvature after circumcision suggests chordee from excess skin removal — the ventral shaft is shorter causing the penis to bend.

questions.title: Cryptorchidism — Guidelines and Clinical Assessment
questions.questionText:
An 8-month-old boy is referred by his GP who noted that the right testis was not palpable in the scrotum at the 8-week and 8-month routine checks. The GP documented that the left testis is fully descended and normal. The boy was born at term by normal vaginal delivery with a birth weight of 3.4 kg. He has no other medical problems. On examination in your clinic, the right testis is not palpable in the scrotum or inguinal canal. The left testis is normal and fully descended.

(a) [CLASSIFY] the types of undescended testis (palpable vs non-palpable, intra-abdominal, inguinal, gliding, ectopic, and retractile). [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the normal embryological descent of the testis from the urogenital ridge to the scrotum, including the roles of the gubernaculum, intra-abdominal pressure, and hormonal factors. [10 marks]
(c) [STATE] the current BAPS and ESPU guidelines for the optimal timing of orchidopexy for congenital cryptorchidism. Provide the evidence base for these recommendations. [10 marks]
(d) [DESCRIBE] your examination technique to differentiate between retractile testis, gliding testis, and truly undescended testis. Include the use of the 'soap test' and other useful manoeuvres. [10 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the potential consequences of late or untreated cryptorchidism on fertility and testicular malignancy risk. Include histological evidence. [12 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: 1 mark each — (1) palpable (inguinal, prescrotal, superficial inguinal pouch), (2) non-palpable (intra-abdominal, atrophic/absent), (3) intra-abdominal (testis within abdomen, not palpable), (4) inguinal (testis in inguinal canal, may be palpable), (5) gliding/retractile (testis can be brought to scrotum but retracts immediately or doesn't stay), (6) ectopic (perineal, femoral, prepubic, or transverse scrotal), (7) retractile (can be brought to scrotum and stays for a moment then retracts; normal testis, not pathological), (8) ascending testis (previously in scrotum, now retracted). Common error: calling all non-scrotal testes 'undescended' without considering retractile. Part (b) — 10 marks: (1) testes develop at urogenital ridge (10–12 weeks), (2) transabdominal phase (8–15 weeks): gubernaculum enlarges under INSL3 influence, holds testis near internal inguinal ring, (3) transinguinal phase (25–35 weeks): gubernaculum regresses, testis passes through inguinal canal under androgen control, (4) scrotal phase (35–40 weeks): testis reaches scrotum, gubernaculum shortens, (5) roles: gubernaculum (guides descent), intra-abdominal pressure (pushes testis through canal), androgens and INSL3 (hormonal drivers), (6) failure at any stage leads to cryptorchidism at respective level. Part (c) — 10 marks: (1) Current ESPU/BAPS guidelines (2016/2018): orchidopexy should be performed between 6–12 months of age; (2) Desirable window: 6–12 months; (3) No later than 12–18 months; (4) Evidence: by 12 months, germ cell loss is already detectable — histology shows decreased germ cell count per tubule after 12 months; (5) By 18 months, the number of Leydig cells declines; (6) Fertility potential decreases with delayed surgery; (7) Early surgery (<12 months) improves paternity rates in bilateral cases; (8) The risk of malignancy is the same regardless of age at orchidopexy (1 in 200–500 for unilateral, 1 in 10–50 for bilateral intra-abdominal) — but early orchidopexy facilitates self-examination; (9) BAPS audit: only 30% of UK cases are currently performed within the 6–12 month window; (10) Referral should be made by 4 months so that surgery can be scheduled within the recommended window. Trap: suggesting 2 years is acceptable — outdated. Part (d) — 10 marks: (1) Warm room, relaxed child, supine with frog-leg position; (2) Start from the anterior superior iliac spine, sweep along inguinal canal towards scrotum; (3) Use soap or ultrasound gel (the 'soap test') — glide fingers over the inguinal region to feel the testis; (4) If not palpable, try cross-legged sitting position; (5) For retractile testis — can be brought to the bottom of scrotum and remains transiently; does not retract immediately; testis is normal size; (6) For gliding testis — can be brought to scrotum but retracts immediately; often pathological and may need surgery; (7) For truly undescended — cannot be manipulated into scrotum or comes with tension and retracts immediately; (8) Document position and whether the testis can reach the scrotum; (9) If non-palpable — consider laparoscopy; (10) Examine with warm hands and patience. Part (e) — 12 marks: Fertility: (1) Histology: germ cell numbers decrease after 1 year; by 2 years, 30–50% of cryptorchid testes show absent germ cells; (2) Spermatogonia/tubule ratio declines; (3) Ad spermatogonia (dark type) — precursors of adult spermatogonia — are reduced; (4) Bilateral cryptorchidism: paternity rate 35–65% even after surgery; (5) Unilateral: paternity rate 80–90% (near normal); (6) Fertility index (Johnsen score) is lower in cryptorchid testes. Malignancy: (1) Relative risk of testicular germ cell tumour: 2.5–8× for unilateral cryptorchidism; (2) Higher for intra-abdominal testis (RR 5–10×); (3) Bilateral cryptorchidism RR up to 32×; (4) orchidopexy does not reduce malignancy risk but makes self-examination possible; (5) Seminoma is the most common tumour; (6) Risk remains in contralateral descended testis (slightly increased).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Cryptorchidism classification: (1) Palpable — testis is felt on examination, usually in the inguinal canal or superficial inguinal pouch. (2) Non-palpable — not felt; may be intra-abdominal, canalicular but impalpable, atrophic, or absent. (3) Intra-abdominal — lies above the internal inguinal ring. (4) Inguinal — lies within the inguinal canal. (5) Gliding — can be brought to the scrotum but retracts immediately; often has a short spermatic cord. (6) Ectopic — lies outside the normal path of descent (perineum, femoral, prepubic region). (7) Retractile — can be brought to the scrotum by gentle traction and remains there momentarily; normal size; caused by active cremasteric reflex. (8) Ascending testis — previously documented in the scrotum, now found to be undescended; often around age 5–10 due to inadequate cord length. (b) Embryological descent: The testis develops at the urogenital ridge on the posterior abdominal wall (week 10–12). INSL3 from Leydig cells stimulates gubernacular enlargement (transabdominal phase, 8–15 weeks), holding the testis near the internal inguinal ring. The transinguinal phase (25–35 weeks) is driven by androgens (testosterone from Leydig cells) — the gubernaculum regresses, intra-abdominal pressure pushes the testis through the inguinal canal into the scrotum. The scrotal phase completes by 35–40 weeks. The processus vaginalis precedes the testis into the scrotum. Failure of any phase — deficient INSL3, androgen insufficiency, or mechanical obstruction — results in cryptorchidism. (c) Current BAPS (2016) and ESPU (2018) guidelines recommend that orchidopexy for congenital cryptorchidism should be performed between 6 and 12 months of age, and certainly before 18 months. The evidence is based on histological studies showing that germ cell loss begins after 6 months and is significant by 12–18 months. Spermatogonia count per tubule and the number of Ad spermatogonia (stem cells for adult spermatogenesis) decline progressively after 12 months. Boys who undergo orchidopexy before 12 months have better fertility indices (paternity rates 90% for unilateral, 65% for bilateral) than those operated later. The malignancy risk is not altered by timing of orchidopexy but early placement allows future self-examination. (d) Examination technique: In a warm room with the child supine and legs frog-legged, start palpation from the ASIS, sweeping medially along the inguinal canal toward the scrotum. The 'soap test' uses ultrasound gel on the examiner's fingers to reduce friction, making it easier to feel a small testis. If the testis is not palpable, have the child sit cross-legged to relax the cremasteric reflex. A retractile testis can be milked gently down to the scrotum and stays momentarily after release. A gliding testis reaches the scrotum but retracts immediately. A truly undescended testis cannot be brought into the scrotum or reaches it only under tension and retracts immediately upon release. Non-palpable testis requires ultrasound or laparoscopy for localisation. (e) Consequences of late or untreated cryptorchidism: Fertility — the adult germ cell pool is established in infancy from Ad spermatogonia. Histological studies show that the total germ cell count per tubule declines after 12 months. In unilateral cryptorchidism, the contralateral descended testis may undergo compensatory hypertrophy but also shows reduced spermatogonia counts in some studies. Bilateral cryptorchidism has a paternity rate of only 35–65% despite surgery. Unilateral has paternity rate of 80–90%. Malignancy — the relative risk of testicular seminoma is 2.5–8× for unilateral cryptorchidism and up to 32× for bilateral. Intra-abdominal testes carry the highest risk (RR 5–10×). The presence of Y chromosome material in the testis confers risk for gonadoblastoma in DSD patients. Orchidopexy does not reduce the malignancy risk but facilitates self-examination. Annual self-examination should be advised from adolescence onwards. Contralateral testicular biopsy may be considered in bilateral cases for risk stratification.
discriminators: Integrates embryological understanding with clinical classification
Quotes evidence for 6–12 month surgical window with histological basis
Differentiates retractile from gliding from truly undescended with examination technique details
Provides age-specific fertility and malignancy risk data

questions.title: Orchidopexy Techniques and Fowler-Stephens Procedure
questions.questionText:
A 14-month-old boy with a non-palpable left testis is taken to the operating theatre. Examination under anaesthesia confirms the testis is non-palpable. Diagnostic laparoscopy is performed. The left testis is identified just inside the internal inguinal ring with a short gonadal vessel that appears to tether the testis 2 cm above the ring. The vas deferens loops into the inguinal canal. The right testis is normally descended and appears healthy.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the laparoscopic findings and classify the position of the left testis. State the vanishing testis syndrome and how to identify testicular remnants. [8 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the standard open inguinal orchidopexy technique (Shoemaker approach), including incision, mobilisation of the testis, and fixation in the scrotum. [12 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] the Prentiss manoeuvre and when it is indicated. Describe how it differs from the Shoemaker approach. [10 marks]
(d) [DESCRIBE] the principles of the Fowler-Stephens single-stage and two-stage laparoscopic orchidopexy for the intra-abdominal testis. [12 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the controversies regarding testicular atrophy rates after Fowler-Stephens orchidopexy versus standard orchidopexy, and how the decision between the two is made. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: 2 marks for laparoscopic findings: testis 2 cm above internal ring with short gonadal vessels and looping vas — this is a 'peeping testis' — testis that is intra-abdominal but near the ring (low intra-abdominal). 3 marks for classification: level 1 — testis at or within 2 cm of internal ring; level 2 — testis 2–5 cm above ring; level 3 — high intra-abdominal (>5 cm). 3 marks for vanishing testis: (1) blind-ending gonadal vessels and vas entering internal ring indicate testicular agenesis/vanishing; (2) nubbin of testicular remnant may be found in the inguinal canal; (3) histology confirms hemosiderin-laden macrophages (testicular infarction). Trap: leaving a nubbin in situ without biopsy — may harbour germ cell elements (low risk but documented). Part (b) — 12 marks: 1–2 marks each: (1) inguinal skin crease incision, (2) open external oblique aponeurosis to mobilise cord, (3) identify and isolate the hernia sac/patent processus vaginalis, (4) ligate and divide PPV at internal ring, (5) mobilise the testis by dissecting cord structures to the retroperitoneum, (6) divide the gubernaculum, (7) perform the Shoemaker manoeuvre: pass a curved clamp from the scrotum through the scrotal and subcutaneous tissue to exit the inguinal incision, (8) pull the testis through the newly created dartos pouch, (9) fix the testis in the subdartos pouch with absorbable suture, (10) close subcutaneous tissue and skin, (11) ensure testis is in a dependent position without tension, (12) check the contralateral testis is descended. Common error: placing testis in a superficial pouch without dartos creation — testis may retract. Part (c) — 10 marks: Prentiss manoeuvre: (1) division of the inferior epigastric vessels, (2) the spermatic cord is then routed medially and directly to the scrotum through the newly created hiatus, (3) creates a straight line from the retroperitoneum to the scrotum, reducing the distance by 2–3 cm. Indications: (1) when standard mobilisation gives insufficient length for tension-free scrotal placement, (2) high inguinal testis with short cord, (3) after failed previous orchidopexy. Difference from Shoemaker: Shoemaker creates a scrotal/subdartos tunnel but does not reroute the cord; Prentiss reroutes the cord medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, straightening the course to the scrotum. Part (d) — 12 marks: Single-stage Fowler-Stephens: (1) testicular vessels are divided at a high level (above the testis), (2) testicular viability relies on the collateral blood supply from the vasal artery and cremasteric artery (the 'long-loop' anastomosis between the three arterial systems), (3) the testis is mobilised on the vas and gubernaculum only and brought to the scrotum via the Prentiss route, (4) performed laparoscopically or open. Two-stage Fowler-Stephens: (1) Stage 1 — testicular vessels are clipped or ligated laparoscopically (first stage), (2) wait 6 months for collateral circulation to develop, (3) Stage 2 — the testis is mobilised on the vas alone and brought to the scrotum using a Prentiss manoeuvre, (4) the two-stage approach has better testicular survival rates (85–90%) vs single-stage (50–70%). Trap: dividing the vas during mobilisation — the vasal artery is the critical collateral; the vas must be preserved with its mesentery. Part (e) — 8 marks: (1) Atrophy rate: single-stage F-S 30–50% vs two-stage F-S 10–15% vs standard orchidopexy 2–5%; (2) Decision depends on testis position and length of gonadal vessels at laparoscopy: if testis is >2 cm from internal ring with short vessels not reachable after Prentiss, F-S is needed; (3) A peeping testis near the ring (<2 cm) may be treated with standard laparoscopic orchidopexy with or without Prentiss — no need for F-S; (4) The 'long loop' vas (vas looping into inguinal canal) is favourable for F-S because the collateral supply is robust; (5) Two-stage F-S is now preferred over single-stage; (6) Some advocate microvascular autotransplantation for the highest intra-abdominal testis — but requires specialist expertise; (7) Preoperative Doppler ultrasound may help assess testicular size and vascularity; (8) Atrophy risk must be discussed with parents — if testis is very small or dysplastic, orchidectomy may be preferred.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) The testis is a low intra-abdominal testis — within 2 cm of the internal ring — classified as a 'peeping testis'. It has short gonadal vessels tethering it above the ring. The vas deferens loops into the inguinal canal (long-looping vas). Vanishing testis syndrome: if the testis has infarcted in utero (often due to neonatal torsion), laparoscopy shows blind-ending gonadal vessels with or without a nubbin entering the internal ring. A nubbin should be excised and sent for histology to identify hemosiderin-laden macrophages and to exclude residual germ cell elements. (b) Open inguinal orchidopexy (Shoemaker): (1) Inguinal skin crease incision. (2) Open external oblique aponeurosis in the line of its fibres. (3) Identify the spermatic cord and isolate the hernia sac/PPV. (4) Dissect and ligate the PPV at the internal ring. (5) Mobilise the testis with blunt dissection of the cord to the retroperitoneum. (6) Divide the gubernaculum to free the testis. (7) Create a subdartos pouch in the scrotum through a separate scrotal incision. (8) The Shoemaker manoeuvre: pass a curved clamp from the scrotum through the dartos layer to the inguinal wound and bring the testis down. (9) Place the testis in the pouch without tension and fix with a 4/0 or 5/0 absorbable suture through the tunica albuginea and dartos. (10) Close the scrotal and inguinal incisions. (c) The Prentiss manoeuvre involves dividing the inferior epigastric vessels to create a new, more direct path for the spermatic cord to reach the scrotum. The spermatic cord is rerouted medially through the divided inferior epigastric vessels, creating a straight line from the retroperitoneum to the scrotum. It shortens the required cord length by 2–3 cm. Indications: high inguinal testis that reaches the scrotum only under tension, or redo orchidopexy. The Shoemaker approach does not reroute the cord — it uses the standard anatomical course through the inguinal canal. (d) Fowler-Stephens orchidopexy: Single-stage — the gonadal vessels are divided high (above the point where the collateral supply enters), and the testis is mobilised on the deferential artery and the cremasteric collaterals, then brought to the scrotum via the Prentiss route. Two-stage — Stage 1 (laparoscopic): the testicular vessels are clipped/ligated to induce collateral circulation via the vasal and cremasteric arteries. After 6 months, Stage 2: the testis is mobilised on the vas and brought to the scrotum. The two-stage approach is preferred as the testicular survival rate is 85–90% versus 50–70% for single-stage. The gubernaculum should be preserved during Stage 1. The vas and its mesentery must be preserved intact as it carries the critical blood supply. (e) Testicular atrophy after Fowler-Stephens: single-stage 30–50%, two-stage 10–15%, standard orchidopexy 2–5%. The decision between F-S and standard orchidopexy depends on the intra-operative assessment: if the testis can reach the scrotum without tension after cord mobilisation and/or Prentiss manoeuvre, standard orchidopexy is performed. If the vessels are too short (testis >2 cm above ring), F-S is indicated. The two-stage approach is almost always preferred over single-stage. Some centres advocate microvascular autotransplantation for the highest intra-abdominal testis (atrophy rate 5–10% but requires significant expertise). Parents must be counselled preoperatively about the risk of testicular loss and the possibility of orchidectomy if the testis is atrophic or dysplastic at exploration.
discriminators: Distinguishes Shoemaker vs Prentiss vs Fowler-Stephens with clear indications for each
Provides evidence-based atrophy rates for each approach
Explains the two-stage F-S rationale (collateral vessel development) with timing
Integrates laparoscopic findings to guide intra-operative decision-making

questions.title: Communicating Hydrocoele — Diagnosis and Management
questions.questionText:
A 14-month-old boy presents with a 3-month history of a painless swelling of the right scrotum. The swelling is fluctuant, transilluminates brilliantly, and is non-tender. It is more prominent in the evening and reportedly smaller in the morning. The left scrotum is normal. The testis cannot be clearly palpated separately from the swelling. There is no history of vomiting, abdominal pain, or constipation.

(a) [DIFFERENTIATE] between a communicating hydrocoele and a non-communicating hydrocoele on the basis of history, examination, and ultrasonography findings. [6 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the embryological basis of a communicating hydrocoele, relating it to the processus vaginalis. [5 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the natural history of communicating hydrocoeles in infants and the evidence-based indications for surgical intervention. [5 marks]
(d) [DESCRIBE] the surgical technique for ligation of a patent processus vaginalis for a communicating hydrocoele. [5 marks]
(e) [OUTLINE] the postoperative complications of hydrocoele surgery and how they compare with inguinal hernia repair. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 6 marks (2 each): Communicating: (1) history of swelling that varies in size — bigger during activity/crying/evening, smaller after lying down/morning, (2) examination — transilluminates brilliantly, usually non-tender, reducible, cough impulse may be positive, (3) ultrasound — fluid surrounding the testis with a visible patent processus vaginalis connecting to the peritoneal cavity; may see bowel if internal ring is widely patent. Non-communicating: (1) constant size, no diurnal variation, (2) tense, may transilluminate but less brilliantly, not reducible, (3) ultrasound — fluid only in the tunica vaginalis without communication. Trap: in young infants, a tense non-communicating hydrocoele can be difficult to distinguish clinically from a communicating hydrocoele — ultrasound helps. Part (b) — 5 marks: (1) At 12 weeks gestation, the processus vaginalis (PV) evaginates through the internal inguinal ring, (2) it precedes the testis through the inguinal canal and into the scrotum (25–35 weeks), (3) after testicular descent, the PV normally obliterates from the internal ring downwards, (4) in a communicating hydrocoele, the PV fails to obliterate, leaving a narrow communication between the peritoneal cavity and the tunica vaginalis testis, (5) peritoneal fluid passes through the patent PV and accumulates around the testis. Trap: confusing the obliteration sequence — PV obliteration occurs from the internal ring downward, not from the scrotum upward. Part (c) — 5 marks: Natural history: (1) up to 50% of communicating hydrocoeles diagnosed in the first year of life close spontaneously, (2) closure is most likely before 12 months, (3) the rate of spontaneous closure declines after 12–18 months. Indications for surgery: (1) persistence beyond 12–18 months, (2) size causing discomfort or difficulty examining the testis, (3) suspicion of concomitant inguinal hernia (wide communication), (4) failure of the swelling to reduce when recumbent (suggests a non-communicating component or thick fluid), (5) development of pain or rapid increase in size. Common error: operating on all communicating hydrocoeles before 12 months — conservative management is appropriate. Part (d) — 5 marks: (1) Inguinal skin crease incision on the side of the hydrocoele, (2) open external oblique aponeurosis, (3) identify the cord and isolate the PPV (which may be thin and translucent), (4) carefully separate the PPV from the vas and vessels — use traction and counter-traction, (5) open the PPV to confirm communication (express fluid or squeeze the scrotum to see fluid escape), then twist and ligate the PPV with 4/0 absorbable suture at the internal ring level. (6) Distal end may be left open or partially excised — complete excision risks injury to the vas and vessels. Trap: mistaking the PPV for an indirect hernia sac — they are the same structure but a hydrocoele PPV is narrow. Part (e) — 4 marks: (1) Recurrence (2–5%) — higher if PPV is not ligated at the level of the internal ring, (2) haematoma and scrotal oedema (2–5%), (3) wound infection (<2%), (4) vas deferens injury (<1%) — rare but the most serious; caused by difficulty identifying the vas in a narrow PPV with dense adhesions. Compared to inguinal hernia repair: risks are similar; hernia repair has a slightly higher risk of ischaemic orchitis (from strangulated contents), while hydrocoele repair has higher risk of recurrence if the PPV is not adequately ligated.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Communicating hydrocoele: history of scrotal swelling that varies — increases with activity, crying, or in the evening; reduces or disappears when lying down or in the morning. Examination reveals a fluctuant, brilliantly transilluminating mass that is reducible and may have a cough impulse. The testis is often not palpable separately. Ultrasound shows peritesticular fluid that changes with position, with a visible communicating tract to the peritoneal cavity. Non-communicating (simple) hydrocoele: constant size, no diurnal variation. More tense, less brilliantly transilluminating, not reducible. Ultrasound shows fluid confined to the tunica vaginalis without communication. (b) Embryologically, the processus vaginalis is a peritoneal diverticulum that precedes testicular descent through the inguinal canal. Normally it obliterates after descent is complete, starting at the internal ring and moving distally. Failure of complete obliteration leaves a narrow channel between the peritoneal cavity and the tunica vaginalis. Peritoneal fluid flows into the tunica vaginalis via this channel, causing the intermittent scrotal swelling characteristic of a communicating hydrocoele. (c) Natural history: 40–50% of communicating hydrocoeles resolve spontaneously by 12–18 months as the PPV obliterates. Surgery is indicated if: (1) the hydrocoele persists beyond 12–18 months, (2) it is large and causes discomfort or difficulty examining the testis, (3) there is suspicion of an inguinal hernia (wide communication, bowel in sac), (4) it becomes tense and non-reducible suggesting a loculated component, (5) it increases rapidly or becomes painful. An expectant approach up to 18 months is appropriate. (d) Surgical technique: (1) Inguinal crease incision. (2) External oblique opened — the cord is identified. (3) The PPV is identified on the anteromedial aspect of the cord, carefully dissected from the vas and gonadal vessels. (4) The PPV is opened to confirm patency — gentle pressure on the scrotum expresses fluid. (5) The PPV is twisted and suture-ligated at the level of the internal inguinal ring with 4/0 absorbable suture. (6) The distal sac is left open or partially excised; complete excision risks injury to the vas and vessels. (7) The wound is closed in layers. (e) Complications: recurrence (2–5%) — most common if the PPV is not ligated at the internal ring. Haematoma (2–5%) — minimise with careful haemostasis. Wound infection (<2%). Vas deferens injury (<1%) — catheterising the vas or crushing with forceps; prevention requires meticulous dissection. Compared with inguinal hernia repair, the complication profile (especially recurrence and vas injury) is similar; however, hernia repair carries additional risk of ischaemic orchitis from strangulated bowel, while hydrocoele surgery has a slightly higher rate of recurrence from inadequate PPV ligation.
discriminators: Clearly distinguishes communicating from non-communicating hydrocoele with diurnal variation as key history point
Provides embryological basis linking PPV patency to hydrocoele formation
Emphasises expectant management up to 12–18 months before surgical intervention
Details safe surgical technique with emphasis on vas identification and preservation

Non-communicating: (1) constant size, no diurnal variation, (2) tense, may transilluminate but less brilliantly, not reducible, (3) ultrasound — fluid only in the tunica vaginalis without communication. questions.title: Varicocoele and Fertility in Adolescents
questions.questionText:
A 15-year-old boy is referred by his GP with a 6-month history of a 'bag of worms' feeling in his left scrotum. He has no pain but is aware of the swelling. On examination there is a grade 3 left varicocoele that fills when standing and with Valsalva, and does not decompress when supine. The right testis appears normal. The left testis is notably smaller (approximately 2 mL less volume than the right). He has no other medical problems. He is concerned about his fertility and asks if he needs surgery.

(a) [DEFINE] the Dubin and Amelar grading system for varicocoele. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the proposed pathophysiological mechanisms by which a varicocoele impairs testicular function and fertility. [6 marks]
(c) [REVIEW] the indications for varicocoele treatment in adolescents as recommended by ESPU and paediatric urology consensus guidelines. [5 marks]
(d) [COMPARE] the Palomo (open high ligation) technique versus laparoscopic varicocelectomy. Include outcomes, recurrence rates, and complication profiles. [6 marks]
(e) [OUTLINE] a management plan for this adolescent, including the role of semen analysis, the decision to operate, and long-term follow-up. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks (1 each): Grade 0 — subclinical (only detectable on ultrasound), Grade 1 — palpable only with Valsalva, Grade 2 — palpable without Valsalva but not visible, Grade 3 — visible through scrotal skin ('bag of worms'). Trap: calling a visible varicocoele grade 2. Part (b) — 6 marks (1–2 each): (1) Hyperthermia — increased venous blood (37°C) impairs spermatogenesis (optimal 33–35°C), (2) Venous congestion and hypoxia — stasis of deoxygenated blood leads to Leydig cell dysfunction, (3) Reflux of renal/adrenal metabolites — reflux of catecholamines and cortisol from the renal vein (via left testicular vein) causes vasoconstriction and Leydig cell toxicity, (4) Oxidative stress — reactive oxygen species damage sperm DNA, (5) Hormonal imbalance — Leydig cell dysfunction leads to reduced testosterone, (6) Germ cell apoptosis — the combined effects lead to progressive germ cell loss and testicular atrophy. Common error: stating the mechanism is 'venous obstruction' — it is venous reflux, not obstruction. Part (c) — 5 marks (1 each): Indications for treatment in adolescents: (1) Persistent varicocoele with ipsilateral testicular hypotrophy (volume difference >2 mL or >20% discrepancy), (2) Grade 2–3 varicocoele, (3) Bilateral varicocoeles, (4) Abnormal semen analysis if obtainable, (5) Pain/discomfort. Also accept: (6) Bilateral palpable varicocoele, (7) Other testis is absent or abnormal. The presence of testicular hypotrophy is the strongest indication for intervention. ESPU guidelines (2022) recommend intervention when there is >20% volume discrepancy and/or abnormal serial semen parameters. Trap: operating on all grade 3 varicocoeles regardless of testicular size difference — careful selection is needed. Part (d) — 6 marks: Palomo: (1) Open technique via inguinal incision, (2) mass ligation of the testicular vein(s) and artery at the level of the internal inguinal ring (above where they separate), (3) recurrence rate 2–5%, (4) hydrocoele formation 5–15% (from ligated lymphatics), (5) artery ligation may cause testicular atrophy if the testis is dependent on the testicular artery. Laparoscopic: (1) Minimally invasive, (2) precise dissection with artery-sparing approach, (3) lymphatic-sparing technique reduces hydrocoele rate to <2%, (4) recurrence rate 2–10% (higher with artery-sparing because small collateral veins may be missed), (5) longer operative time but faster recovery. Both have equivalent success rates; laparoscopic allows better visualisation and artery-sparing. Trap: using the Palomo technique without lymphatic sparing in adolescents — hydrocoele rate is high. Part (e) — 4 marks: (1) Discuss the findings with the patient and family — explain the significance of testicular hypotrophy (2 mL volume loss), (2) Offer semen analysis for baseline assessment (if he is able to produce a sample), (3) Discuss surgical options: laparoscopic varicocelectomy with artery and lymphatic sparing is the preferred approach in this age group, (4) Plan for postoperative follow-up at 3, 6, and 12 months to document testicular catch-up growth (ultrasound), (5) Repeat semen analysis at 12 months if obtained preoperatively; (6) Long-term follow-up should continue into adulthood, with the patient informed about fertility options. If he does not wish surgery, document the discussion and offer annual clinical and ultrasound surveillance.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Dubin and Amelar grading: Grade 0 — subclinical, not palpable, only seen on ultrasound. Grade 1 — palpable only during Valsalva. Grade 2 — palpable without Valsalva but not visible. Grade 3 — visible through the scrotal skin ('bag of worms') and palpable. (b) Pathophysiology: (1) Hyperthermia from reflux of warm abdominal blood impairs spermatogenesis. (2) Venous congestion and hypoxia cause Leydig cell dysfunction and reduced testosterone. (3) Reflux of renal metabolites (catecholamines, cortisol) causes vasoconstriction of the testicular microvasculature. (4) Oxidative stress from reactive oxygen species damages sperm DNA and cell membranes. (5) These factors collectively induce germ cell apoptosis, progressive loss of spermatogonia, and testicular atrophy. The effect is progressive over time. (c) Indications for varicocoele treatment in adolescents: (1) Ipsilateral testicular hypotrophy — volume difference >2 mL or >20% compared to the contralateral testis (most important indicator). (2) Grade 2–3 varicocoele with hypotrophy. (3) Bilateral palpable varicocoele. (4) Abnormal semen analysis (if obtainable). (5) Discomfort or pain attributable to the varicocoele. (6) An abnormal solitary testis. The presence of testicular atrophy is the strongest predictor of future fertility impairment and the most compelling indication for intervention. (d) Palomo technique: open high ligation of the testicular vein and artery at the level of the internal ring. Recurrence 2–5%. Hydrocoele formation 5–15% (often from ligated lymphatics). Artery ligation risks testicular atrophy. Laparoscopic varicocelectomy: minimally invasive, allows artery-sparing and lymphatic-sparing dissection. Recurrence 2–10%. Hydrocoele rate <2% with lymphatic sparing. Faster recovery. Both have comparable success rates. The laparoscopic approach is preferred in adolescents because of the better visualisation and ability to perform artery and lymphatic sparing, reducing the hydrocoele rate and testicular atrophy risk. (e) This patient has a grade 3 left varicocoele with significant testicular hypotrophy (2 mL volume loss) and is concerned about fertility. Management: (1) Offer semen analysis for baseline assessment. (2) Given the significant testicular size discrepancy, surgical intervention is indicated. (3) Offer laparoscopic varicocelectomy with artery and lymphatic sparing. (4) Postoperative follow-up with scrotal ultrasound at 3, 6, and 12 months to document catch-up growth. (5) Repeat semen analysis at 12 months if baseline was obtained. (6) Advise about long-term follow-up and inform him that varicocoeles can recur. (7) If he refuses surgery, offer annual clinical and ultrasound surveillance with semen analysis every 1–2 years.
discriminators: Provides pathophysiological mechanisms beyond hyperthermia (oxidative stress, renal metabolite reflux, hormonal effects)
Quotes specific threshold for testicular hypotrophy (>2 mL or >20%) as key surgical indication
Compares Palomo vs laparoscopic with evidence-based recurrence and hydrocoele rates
Integrates semen analysis into adolescent decision-making with practical management plan
