questions.title: Intra-abdominal Testis in an Adolescent — Complex Decision-Making
questions.questionText:
A 17-year-old male presents with a left non-palpable testis. He was adopted from Eastern Europe at age 4 and has no prior medical records. He has never undergone any surgery. He has a normally descended right testis that appears normal on examination. He has never had any hormonal testing. He is otherwise healthy and has gone through normal puberty (Tanner stage 5). He is aware that his left testis has never been in his scrotum and is concerned about the risk of cancer and his future fertility. He wants to know 'what should be done'.

(a) [INTERPRET] the clinical picture. What is the differential diagnosis for a non-palpable testis in a 17-year-old with normal secondary sexual characteristics? Discuss the role of imaging (ultrasound and MRI) versus diagnostic laparoscopy. [8 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] the treatment options if an intra-abdominal testis is found at laparoscopy. Include orchidopexy, orchidectomy, and the evidence on which to choose. [8 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the risk of testicular malignancy in the intra-abdominal testis and the evidence for prophylactic orchidectomy versus surveillance. [6 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a counselling plan for this patient addressing the risks and benefits of each option, including the impact on fertility, the need for hormone replacement, and the psychological aspects. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: Differential: (1) intra-abdominal testis (most likely given never been in scrotum), (2) inguinal but non-palpable (atrophic), (3) vanishing testis (antenatal torsion, atrophic remnant in inguinal canal), (4) testicular agenesis (anorchia — but normal puberty suggests at least one testis is functioning). Normal secondary sexual characteristics indicate adequate Leydig cell function from the contralateral testis. Imaging: Ultrasound (1) low sensitivity for intra-abdominal testis (can miss up to 50%), (2) may identify a testis near the internal ring or an atrophic nubbin. MRI: (1) higher sensitivity (70–90%), (2) can identify testis in the abdomen or inguinal canal, (3) but cannot reliably differentiate a testis from lymph node or other structure. Diagnostic laparoscopy: (1) gold standard, (2) directly visualises the testis, vas, and gonadal vessels, (3) allows immediate therapeutic intervention, (4) sensitivity and specificity >95%. Trap: relying on negative ultrasound to exclude intra-abdominal testis — laparoscopy is mandatory. Part (b) — 8 marks: If an intra-abdominal testis is found: Options: (1) Laparoscopic orchidopexy — options: standard mobilisation (if testis is low, near internal ring, with adequate vessel length), two-stage Fowler-Stephens (clip vessels now, mobilise 6 months later). Success (viable testis in scrotum at 1 year) — 50–70% for single-stage F-S, 85–90% for two-stage F-S. (2) Orchidectomy — remove the testis if it is atrophic, dysplastic, or cannot be brought to the scrotum without tension. (3) Microvascular autotransplantation — requires specialist centre, testicular survival 80–90%. Decision factors: (a) testicular size and appearance at laparoscopy — if atrophic (<1 mL) or dysplastic, orchidectomy is reasonable; (b) ability to bring to scrotum; (c) the patient's fertility concerns — orchidopexy preserves spermatogenesis potential; (d) age: in a 17-year-old, a normal-appearing intra-abdominal testis may still have some spermatogenic potential, albeit reduced. Common error: attempting orchidopexy on a high, atrophic testis in an adolescent — high atrophy rate and likely low fertility potential. Part (c) — 6 marks: Malignancy risk: (1) Intra-abdominal testis has a relative risk of 5–10× of developing testicular germ cell tumour (seminoma). (2) Absolute risk: 1 in 20 to 1 in 50 for an intra-abdominal testis (vs 1 in 500 for a normal descended testis). (3) Orchidopexy does not reduce the malignancy risk but makes the testis accessible for self-examination. (4) Surveillance — if orchidopexy is performed, teach self-examination, consider annual ultrasound. (5) Prophylactic orchidectomy — eliminates malignancy risk but removes potential for spermatogenesis and (if bilateral) hormone production; in this case, with a normal right testis, the fertility and endocrine function of the left is likely minimal. (6) The risk-benefit ratio of orchidopexy vs orchidectomy in an adolescent with a normal contralateral testis is debated: many would recommend orchidectomy for a high intra-abdominal testis at this age given the malignancy risk and limited fertility potential. Trap: keeping a dysplastic intra-abdominal testis 'for fertility reasons' — its spermatogenic potential is negligible and cancer risk is substantial. Part (d) — 8 marks: Counselling plan: (1) Explain the diagnosis and findings expected at laparoscopy. (2) Discuss the two main options: orchidopexy (to preserve natural position and potential, albeit reduced, function) vs orchidectomy (to eliminate cancer risk). (3) For orchidopexy — benefits: preserves testis, potential for spermatogenesis (though limited), scrotal position allows monitoring; risks: 10–15% atrophy rate (two-stage F-S), does not eliminate malignancy risk, need for lifelong self-examination. (4) For orchidectomy — benefits: eliminates cancer risk entirely, no need for surveillance; risks: loss of any spermatogenic potential, impact on body image. (5) Explain that his fertility with a single normal right testis is expected to be normal — unilateral cryptorchidism has paternity rates of 80–90%. (6) Discuss the need for hormone replacement ONLY if both testes are lost — with one normal testis, he will not need testosterone. (7) Address psychological concerns — body image with a single testis; consider testicular prosthesis. (8) Give him time to discuss with family and decide. Document the discussion thoroughly.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Differential diagnosis for a non-palpable testis in a 17-year-old with normal puberty: intra-abdominal testis (most likely given the history of never scrotal), vanishing testis (antenatal torsion causing regression), or testicular agenesis. Normal puberty confirms adequate Leydig cell function from the right testis. Imaging: ultrasound has poor sensitivity for intra-abdominal testis (<50%) and cannot reliably exclude an intra-abdominal testis. MRI has higher sensitivity (70–90%) but cannot differentiate testicular tissue from lymph nodes with certainty. Diagnostic laparoscopy is the gold standard — it directly visualises the testis and allows concurrent intervention (orchidopexy or orchidectomy). A negative ultrasound does not obviate the need for laparoscopy. (b) Treatment options at laparoscopy: (1) Standard laparoscopic orchidopexy — if the testis is low (within 2 cm of internal ring) and the gonadal vessels are adequate, the testis can be mobilised and placed in the scrotum. (2) Two-stage Fowler-Stephens orchidopexy — if the testis is high (>2 cm from ring) with short vessels, the vessels are clipped at Stage 1, and Stage 2 is performed 6 months later. Success rate for two-stage F-S is 85–90% testicular survival. (3) Microvascular autotransplantation — for the highest testes, survival 80–90% but requires specialist microsurgical expertise. (4) Orchidectomy — indicated if the testis is atrophic, dysplastic, or the cord is too short to reach the scrotum. In a 17-year-old with a normal contralateral testis and a high intra-abdominal testis, orchidectomy is a reasonable option given the limited fertility potential and elevated malignancy risk. (c) Malignancy risk in intra-abdominal testis: RR 5–10× for germ cell tumours (seminoma). Absolute lifetime risk is 2–5% (1 in 20 to 1 in 50). Orchidopexy does not reduce this risk but facilitates self-examination and ultrasound surveillance. Prophylactic orchidectomy eliminates this risk entirely. In an adolescent with a normal contralateral testis, the fertility benefit of preserving a high intra-abdominal testis is minimal — the spermatogenic function is typically very poor after prolonged exposure to body temperature. Therefore, many urologists recommend prophylactic orchidectomy for a high intra-abdominal testis in a post-pubertal patient with a normal contralateral testis. If the testis appears normal and can be brought to the scrotum easily, orchidopexy with surveillance is an acceptable alternative after full counselling. (d) Counselling plan: (1) Start by explaining the anatomy and the options. (2) Lay out both paths clearly: orchidopexy preserves the testis but requires lifelong self-examination and carries a 10–15% atrophy risk; orchidectomy removes the testis and cancer risk but may affect body image. (3) Fertility: reassure that with a single normal right testis, his fertility is likely normal (80–90% paternity rate in unilateral cryptorchidism). (4) Hormones: he will NOT need testosterone replacement if one testis is preserved. (5) Body image: offer a testicular prosthesis if orchidectomy is chosen. (6) Cancer risk: explain that the risk is real (2–5% lifetime) and that surveillance requires a motivated patient who will perform regular self-examination. (7) Give him written information and time to decide. (8) Offer a follow-up consultation with his parents or partner present. Document the discussion and his decision. If he chooses orchidectomy, arrange for the procedure. If he chooses orchidopexy, schedule a two-stage Fowler-Stephens if the testis is high.
discriminators: Explains the malignancy risk-benefit analysis for an intra-abdominal testis at age 17
Distinguishes between orchidopexy and orchidectomy with reasoned criteria for each
Provides fertility counselling basis (unilateral cryptorchidism has near-normal paternity rate)
Integrates psychological support and testicular prosthesis option into decision-making

(2) Orchidectomy — remove the testis if it is atrophic, dysplastic, or cannot be brought to the scrotum without tension. (3) Microvascular autotransplantation — requires specialist centre, testicular survival 80–90%. Decision factors: (a) testicular size and appearance at laparoscopy — if atrophic (<1 mL) or dysplastic, orchidectomy is reasonable; (b) ability to bring to scrotum; (c) the patient's fertility concerns — orchidopexy preserves spermatogenesis potential; (d) age: in a 17-year-old, a normal-appearing intra-abdominal testis may still have some spermatogenic potential, albeit reduced. Common error: attempting orchidopexy on a high, atrophic testis in an adolescent — high atrophy rate and likely low fertility potential. Part (c) — 6 marks: Malignancy risk: (1) Intra-abdominal testis has a relative risk of 5–10× of developing testicular germ cell tumour (seminoma). (2) Absolute risk: 1 in 20 to 1 in 50 for an intra-abdominal testis (vs 1 in 500 for a normal descended testis). (3) Orchidopexy does not reduce the malignancy risk but makes the testis accessible for self-examination. (4) Surveillance — if orchidopexy is performed, teach self-examination, consider annual ultrasound. (5) Prophylactic orchidectomy — eliminates malignancy risk but removes potential for spermatogenesis and (if bilateral) hormone production; in this case, with a normal right testis, the fertility and endocrine function of the left is likely minimal. (6) The risk-benefit ratio of orchidopexy vs orchidectomy in an adolescent with a normal contralateral testis is debated: many would recommend orchidectomy for a high intra-abdominal testis at this age given the malignancy risk and limited fertility potential. Trap: keeping a dysplastic intra-abdominal testis 'for fertility reasons' — its spermatogenic potential is negligible and cancer risk is substantial. Part (d) — 8 marks: Counselling plan: (1) Explain the diagnosis and findings expected at laparoscopy. (2) Discuss the two main options: orchidopexy (to preserve natural position and potential, albeit reduced, function) vs orchidectomy (to eliminate cancer risk). (3) For orchidopexy — benefits: preserves testis, potential for spermatogenesis (though limited), scrotal position allows monitoring; risks: 10–15% atrophy rate (two-stage F-S), does not eliminate malignancy risk, need for lifelong self-examination. (4) For orchidectomy — benefits: eliminates cancer risk entirely, no need for surveillance; risks: loss of any spermatogenic potential, impact on body image. (5) Explain that his fertility with a single normal right testis is expected to be normal — unilateral cryptorchidism has paternity rates of 80–90%. (6) Discuss the need for hormone replacement ONLY if both testes are lost — with one normal testis, he will not need testosterone. (7) Address psychological concerns — body image with a single testis; consider testicular prosthesis. (8) Give him time to discuss with family and decide. Document the discussion thoroughly.

questions.title: Consent and Ethics in Testicular Surgery in Children
questions.questionText:
Theme: 

Options:


Scenarios:
questions.markingGuide:
Each part 3 marks. Part (a) — A (Gillick competence assessment): at 14, the boy may be Gillick-competent. His refusal must be assessed. If competent, his refusal should be respected; parents cannot override a competent child's refusal for elective surgery. Trap: proceeding on father's consent alone. Part (b) — A (Gillick competence assessment): at 12, the boy's wishes should be assessed. If he is competent and wants surgery, his consent combined with parental agreement is sufficient. If parents refuse, they can be counselled about the BAPS/ESPU guidelines supporting timely surgery. Part (c) — B (Parental consent alone — child too young): at 18 months, the child lacks capacity. Parental consent is required. One parent with parental responsibility can consent. Part (d) — F (Child protection referral): grandparents have no automatic parental responsibility. The parents are unavailable due to drug addiction — this raises safeguarding concerns. Social services must be involved to determine who has parental responsibility and to ensure the child's best interests. Part (e) — D (Court-ordered application): removal of a solitary testis is a non-therapeutic, irreversible procedure with major lifelong implications (loss of hormone production, fertility). Even with a Gillick-competent child and parental consent, this is a 'grave' decision that likely requires court approval (Gillick competence does not extend to consenting to non-therapeutic sterilisation or organ removal). Part (f) — H (Consent from one parent is sufficient): in England and Wales, consent from one parent with parental responsibility is sufficient. The father's objection does not bar the surgery; however, if there is a shared residence order, the court may need to decide. Typically, one parent's consent is enough. Part (g) — G (Best interests meeting): a 16-year-old with intellectual disability who cannot communicate must have a best interests determination under the Mental Capacity Act. An MDT meeting including the family, a psychiatrist, and an ethics committee should determine if surgery is in his best interests. Part (h) — J (Transition planning): at 18, he is an adult. He has a right to his medical records. He also needs transition from paediatric to adult urology services if long-term varicocoele follow-up is needed. Part (i) — C (MDT discussion): a 10-year-old with previously conservative management now questions the decision. An MDT discussion should involve a psychologist to help him understand his condition and treatment options. If surgery is chosen, a standard orchidopexy or F-S may be offered depending on testis position. Part (j) — H (Consent from one parent is sufficient): one parent's consent is sufficient for surgery on the child. Prophylactic orchidopexy for the contralateral testis is not routine but can be discussed; testicular prosthesis placement is reasonable at the time of orchidectomy. The mother's consent is sufficient if she has parental responsibility.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) A — Gillick competence assessment. The 14-year-old's refusal must be assessed; if competent, it should be respected for elective surgery. Medical, psychological and cultural counselling should be offered to both the boy and his father to reach an understanding. (b) A — Gillick competence assessment. At 12, assess his competence. If he understands the procedure and its benefits, his views are important. Discuss with the parents that the optimal window (6–12 months) is past but that current guidelines still recommend timely surgery; deferred surgery may be acceptable but the boy's wishes should be considered. (c) B — Parental consent alone. The child is too young to contribute. One parent with parental responsibility can give valid consent for inguinal hernia repair. (d) F — Child protection referral. The grandparents lack parental responsibility; the parents are unavailable due to addiction. Social services must assess the situation and determine the child's welfare. Surgery can proceed only after the legal guardian is identified. (e) D — Court-ordered application. Removal of a solitary testis is a 'grave' irreversible procedure involving sterilisation and lifelong hormone dependency. The court's inherent jurisdiction should determine if this is in the child's best interests, regardless of Gillick competence. (f) H — Consent from one parent is sufficient. In England, one parent with parental responsibility can consent to surgery. The father's objection, while important to discuss, does not legally bar the procedure. If conflict persists, mediation should be offered. (g) G — Best interests meeting. Under the Mental Capacity Act, a 16-year-old who lacks capacity requires a best interests decision. An MDT with the family, a psychiatrist (to assess capacity), and the clinical ethics committee should determine if orchidopexy is in his best interests. (h) J — Transition planning. At 18, he has a right to his records. He should be transitioned to adult urology if follow-up is needed. Provide a full summary of his surgical history. (i) C — MDT discussion. The 10-year-old's distress warrants a psychologist's involvement to explore his feelings about his condition and past management. The MDT should then consider the best surgical option (likely orchidopexy) and offer it with appropriate counselling. (j) H — Consent from one parent is sufficient. The mother's consent allows the surgery. Prophylactic contralateral orchidopexy for a descended testis is not standard but may be discussed; testicular prosthesis placement is reasonable. Document the discussion.
discriminators: Distinguishes between routine parental consent for infants and Gillick competence assessment for adolescents
Recognises that court approval may be needed for 'grave' decisions like solitary testis removal
Addresses child protection when parents are unavailable due to addiction
Highlights that one parent's consent is sufficient even if the other disagrees (with exceptions)

questions.title: Testicular Torsion — Diagnosis and Emergency Management
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a 3-hour history of sudden onset, severe pain in the right testicle. The pain woke him from sleep. He has vomited twice. On examination, the right testis is swollen, tender, and lies high in the scrotum with a horizontal lie. The left testis is normal. There is no urinary frequency or dysuria. He has no fever.

(a) [DESCRIBE] the typical clinical presentation of testicular torsion, distinguishing it from other causes of acute scrotum. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the anatomical basis of testicular torsion — the bell-clapper deformity. Describe the normal fixation of the testis within the tunica vaginalis and why some boys are predisposed to torsion. [8 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the evidence-based relationship between the duration of torsion and testicular salvage rates (0–6h, 6–12h, 12–24h, >24h). Explain the pathophysiology of ischaemia-reperfusion injury. [12 marks]
(d) [DESCRIBE] the technique for manual detorsion, including the direction of rotation and post-manipulation assessment. [10 marks]
(e) [FORMULATE] an emergency management plan for this patient from arrival in the ED to the operating theatre, including time-critical interventions. [12 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: (1) Sudden onset severe testicular pain — often waking from sleep, (2) nausea/vomiting (50–70%), (3) testis is swollen, tender, and high-riding with horizontal lie (absolutely pathognomonic — the epididymis may be anterior or the testis may be transverse), (4) absence of fever, dysuria, or urinary symptoms. Distinguishing features: (1) In epididymitis — gradual onset, dysuria, fever, preserved testicular lie (vertical), (2) In torsion of appendix testis — gradual onset, blue-dot sign, tender at upper pole, (3) In testicular torsion — rapid onset, severe, horizontal lie. Common error: describing a patient with torsion as having a normal testicular lie. Trap: waiting for a Doppler ultrasound before exploring — clinical suspicion alone warrants exploration. Part (b) — 8 marks: Bell-clapper deformity: (1) Normally, the tunica vaginalis envelops the testis and epididymis but attaches to the posterior aspect of the testis, fixing it to the scrotal wall (prevents rotation). (2) In bell-clapper deformity, the tunica vaginalis envelops the entire testis and epididymis (including the posterior aspect), leaving the testis suspended within the tunica vaginalis like a 'clapper in a bell'. (3) This allows the testis to rotate freely on the spermatic cord, (4) It is bilateral in 80% of cases — hence the need to fix the contralateral testis. (5) Prevalence: 12% of males have bell-clapper deformity, but only a small proportion develop torsion. (6) Torsion is usually medial rotation (90% rotate inward/medially). (7) The degree of torsion (360° vs 540° vs 720°) determines the severity of ischaemia. Part (c) — 12 marks: Salvage rates: 0–6h — up to 90–100%; 6–12h — 50–70%; 12–24h — 0–20%; >24h — <10%. Use these ranges (exact figures may vary by study). Pathophysiology: (1) Torsion twists the spermatic cord, occluding the testicular vein first (low-pressure system) → venous congestion → oedema → arterial occlusion. (2) Ischaemia → ATP depletion → failure of Na+/K+ pump → cellular swelling → irreversible injury. (3) Reperfusion (after detorsion) → influx of oxygen → formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) → lipid peroxidation → further cellular damage (ischaemia-reperfusion injury). (4) The degree of damage depends on duration and whether torsion is intermittent or complete. (5) Germ cells are most sensitive (spermatogonia) — Leydig cells are relatively resistant to ischaemia. Trap: thinking that salvage rates are still good >12 hours — they are poor. Part (d) — 10 marks: Manual detorsion: (1) Explain procedure and obtain verbal consent, (2) Give analgesia (IV morphine or NSAID), (3) Position patient supine, (4) Stand on the patient's right side to manipulate the RIGHT testis, (5) Grasp the testis and rotate it from medial to lateral (as if 'opening a book') — i.e., externally/ laterally. (6) If pain increases, try the opposite direction (lateral to medial, as 90% rotate inward, but 10% outward). (7) 'Rotate to open' — think of opening a door. (8) After rotation, assess: pain relief, testis descends to a more normal position, the lie improves. (9) Successful manual detorsion does NOT obviate the need for surgery — surgical exploration and orchiopexy is mandatory. (10) Document the procedure, direction, and post-manipulation findings. Alternative: some authors recommend 'to the prone position' (feet up) or 'book orientation' (open a book from the binding). Trap: thinking that successful manual detorsion is definitive treatment. Part (e) — 12 marks: Emergency plan: (1) ED triage: immediate triage to a cubicle — 'time is testis'. (2) History and examination (fast) — do NOT wait for urine/blood results. (3) No imaging needed unless diagnosis is genuinely unclear after clinical assessment (even then, exploration threshold should be low). (4) Analgesia: IV paracetamol + NSAID (diclofenac/ketorolac) + IV morphine if severe. (5) IV fluids if vomiting. (6) Attempt manual detorsion after analgesia (not mandatory — some go straight to theatre). (7) Contact paediatric urology/on-call surgeon immediately — arrange emergency theatre. (8) Consent: discuss risks of exploration, orchidectomy (if non-viable), orchiopexy, and contralateral fixation. (9) Bloods: FBC, U+E, coagulation screen (for GA). (10) Transfer to theatre — aim for <6 hours from onset. (11) If >12 hours and testis may be non-viable, counsel re orchidectomy. (12) Post-op: wound care, analgesia, discharge with advice. Score at least 1 mark for each well-described step. Trap: ordering an ultrasound — this delays surgery and is unnecessary.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Testicular torsion presents with sudden onset severe unilateral testicular pain, often waking the patient, associated with nausea/vomiting in 50–70%. On examination, the affected testis is swollen, tender, high-riding, and has a horizontal lie (the epididymis may be palpable anteriorly). The cremasteric reflex is absent. There is no fever, dysuria, or urinary frequency. In epididymitis, the pain is gradual, there is urinary tract symptoms, fever, and the testis is vertically oriented with a normal lie. In torsion of the appendix testis, pain is gradual, and the pathognomonic blue-dot sign may be present. Absence of the cremasteric reflex is the most sensitive sign for torsion. (b) Bell-clapper deformity: normally, the tunica vaginalis encloses the testis and epididymis but attaches posteriorly to the scrotal wall, fixing the testis in a vertical position. In the bell-clapper deformity, the tunica vaginalis completely encircles the testis and epididymis (including the posterior aspect), so the testis is not anchored to the scrotal wall. Instead, it is suspended freely within the tunica vaginalis like a clapper in a bell, allowing it to rotate on the spermatic cord. This deformity is bilateral in 80% of boys with torsion. It is present in about 12% of the male population but only a minority develop torsion. The typical rotation is medial (90% rotate inward). (c) Salvage rates correlate inversely with ischaemia time: 0–6 hours: 90–100% salvage; 6–12 hours: 50–70%; 12–24 hours: 0–20%; >24 hours: <10%. Pathophysiology: Torsion occludes the low-pressure venous system first, causing venous congestion, oedema, and eventually arterial occlusion and testicular ischaemia. Ischaemia leads to ATP depletion, failure of the Na+/K+ pump, cellular swelling, and acidosis. Upon detorsion, the sudden influx of oxygen generates reactive oxygen species (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals), causing lipid peroxidation and additional cellular injury — ischaemia-reperfusion injury. Germ cells (spermatogonia) are the most sensitive to ischaemia; Leydig cells are relatively resistant, so hormonal function may be preserved even if spermatogenesis is lost. (d) Manual detorsion: (1) Give adequate analgesia (IV morphine or NSAID). (2) Stand on the patient's right side. (3) Grasp the right testis. (4) Rotate the testis from medial to lateral (externally) — think of 'opening a book' or 'rotating to the prone position'. (5) If pain increases, rotate in the opposite direction (lateral to medial). (6) Successful detorsion results in immediate pain relief, the testis descends, and the lie becomes more vertical. (7) The testis is then supported with a scrotal support. (8) Manual detorsion is a temporising measure; emergency surgical exploration is still mandatory to confirm viability and perform orchiopexy. (9) Document the direction of rotation and post-detorsion findings. (e) Emergency management plan: (1) Immediate triage to a monitored bed. (2) Focused history and exam (under 5 minutes). (3) No need for imaging — clinical diagnosis. (4) IV analgesia — paracetamol + ketorolac + morphine as needed. (5) IV fluids if vomiting. (6) Attempt manual detorsion (not essential if theatre is imminent). (7) Contact paediatric urology immediately — request emergency theatre slot (urgent <6h from onset). (8) Consent for scrotal exploration, possible orchidectomy, right orchiopexy, and mandatory left orchiopexy. (9) Bloods: FBC, U+E, coagulation. (10) Transfer to theatre as soon as possible. (11) If >12 hours since onset, counsel the family that the testis may be non-viable and orchidectomy may be necessary. (12) Postoperative: scrotal support, analgesia, wound care, follow-up in 4–6 weeks with ultrasound to assess testicular viability and consider endocrine function if orchidectomy was performed.
discriminators: Emphasises horizontal testicular lie as the most specific clinical sign
Provides evidence-based salvage rates with clear time thresholds
Describes manual detorsion technique with direction and post-detorsion assessment
Emphasises 'time is testis' — no imaging, straight to theatre

questions.title: Differential Diagnosis of the Acute Scrotum
questions.questionText:
A 9-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a 12-hour history of gradual-onset right testicular pain. He has not vomited. He reports mild dysuria. On examination he has a temperature of 38.1°C, the right testis is tender with mild swelling, and there is a normal vertical lie. There is a visible tender nodule at the upper pole of the testis. The left testis is normal. Urine dipstick is positive for nitrites and leukocytes.

(a) [DIFFERENTIATE] the clinical features of testicular torsion, torsion of the appendix testis, and epididymo-orchitis in prepubertal boys. Create a table comparing age, onset, pain quality, systemic symptoms, examination findings, and urinalysis. [12 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the significance of the blue-dot sign in torsion of the appendix testis, and discuss the role of conservative management. [10 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the microbiology and antibiotic management of epididymo-orchitis in prepubertal boys versus adolescents and sexually active males. [10 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a diagnostic algorithm for the acute scrotum in a 9-year-old boy, including which patients require immediate surgical exploration, which can be managed conservatively, and when imaging is appropriate. [10 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the role of Doppler ultrasound in the acute scrotum: its sensitivity, specificity, and limitations in distinguishing torsion from other causes. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 12 marks: Table format. Award 4 marks per condition: Testicular torsion: (1) age 12–18 peak (neonatal peak also), (2) sudden onset, (3) severe pain, (4) nausea/vomiting, (5) high-riding horizontal testis, (6) absent cremasteric reflex, (7) no fever, (8) normal urinalysis. Torsion of appendix testis: (1) age 7–12 peak, (2) gradual onset over 12–24h, (3) mild-moderate pain, (4) N&V rare, (5) blue-dot sign at upper pole, (6) tender nodule at upper pole, (7) ± low-grade fever, (8) normal urinalysis. Epididymo-orchitis: (1) any age, (2) gradual onset, (3) mild-moderate pain, (4) N&V rare, (5) testis normal lie, (6) tender epididymis, (7) fever, (8) positive urinalysis. Trap: confusing appendix testis with testicular torsion. Part (b) — 10 marks: Blue-dot sign: (1) Deep blue or black nodule visible through the skin at the upper pole of the testis, (2) Represents the torted, ischaemic appendix testis (hydatid of Morgagni) — a Müllerian remnant, (3) Present in 15–30% of cases — diagnostic when visible, (4) More common in lighter-skinned boys. Conservative management: (1) If diagnosis of torsion of appendix testis is certain (gradual onset, blue-dot sign, normal testicle), conservative management is appropriate, (2) NSAIDs for pain, (3) Scrotal support, (4) Activity restriction (no sports) for 1–2 weeks, (5) Analgesia as needed, (6) Follow-up in 1 week — symptoms usually resolve in 5–10 days, (7) Indications for surgery: diagnostic uncertainty (cannot exclude torsion), severe pain, or failure of conservative management. Trap: giving antibiotics for appendix torsion — they are not indicated. Part (c) — 10 marks: Prepubertal boys (<12): (1) Most cases are idiopathic or associated with a viral illness, (2) Common organisms: enteric bacteria (E. coli, Proteus, Klebsiella) — usually from urinary tract infection, (3) Obtain urine culture, (4) Treat with oral antibiotics according to sensitivities — e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or amoxicillin-clavulanate for 7–10 days, (5) Consider imaging to exclude underlying urological abnormality (VCUG if recurrent). Adolescent/sexually active (>12–14): (1) Consider sexually transmitted organisms: Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, (2) Obtain first-void urine for NAAT (chlamydia, gonorrhoea), (3) Treat empirically with doxycycline 100 mg BD × 14 days + ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose, (4) Contact tracing, (5) Advise sexual abstinence during treatment. Common error: giving same antibiotic regimen for all ages without considering sexual activity. Part (d) — 10 marks: Algorithm: (1) History and examination. (2) If testicular torsion suspected (sudden onset, nausea/vomiting, horizontal testis, absent cremasteric reflex) → emergency scrotal exploration. (3) If blue-dot sign present, gradual onset, normal testis lie, no urinary symptoms → conservative management for torsion of appendix testis. (4) If gradual onset, fever, dysuria, abnormal urinalysis → urine culture, antibiotics for epididymitis; if no improvement in 48–72h, re-examine or consider ultrasound. (5) If diagnosis uncertain after clinical assessment — perform urgent Doppler ultrasound. (6) If ultrasound shows absent or reduced testicular blood flow → exploration. (7) If increased or normal flow → likely epididymitis or appendix torsion → manage accordingly. (8) Reassess any patient whose symptoms worsen or do not improve within 24–48h. Trap: exploring all patients with acute scrotum is unnecessary — but if any doubt, explore. Part (e) — 8 marks: Doppler ultrasound: (1) Sensitivity for torsion: 85–95%, (2) Specificity: 90–98%, (3) false negatives: 5–15% — in early torsion (first 4–6 hours), a low-flow state may be misinterpreted as normal (especially in young boys with small testes), (4) false positives: 2–10% — epididymitis can sometimes show reduced flow (severe inflammation with oedema compressing vessels), (5) Limitations: operator-dependent; in children under 5, testicular vessels are small and difficult to interrogate; intermittent torsion with normal flow may be missed; it should not delay exploration if clinical suspicion is high. Role: confirm clinical suspicion but NOT a substitute for clinical judgment. The exploration threshold should be low — any clinical suspicion of torsion warrants exploration even with a normal ultrasound.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Differentiation of acute scrotum (tabular form): Testicular torsion — age 12–18 (and neonates), sudden onset, severe pain, N&V common, high-riding horizontal lie, absent cremasteric, no fever, normal urinalysis. Torsion of appendix testis — age 7–12, gradual onset, mild-moderate pain, N&V rare, blue-dot sign, tender upper pole nodule, normal lie, ± low-grade fever, normal urinalysis. Epididymo-orchitis — any age, gradual onset, mild-moderate, N&V rare, normal lie, tender epididymis, fever, dysuria, positive urinalysis/nitrites. (b) The blue-dot sign is a pathognomonic finding in torsion of the appendix testis — a small (2–5 mm) blue-black nodule visible through the scrotal skin at the upper pole of the testis, representing the ischaemic appendage. It is only visible in 15–30% of cases depending on skin pigmentation. If the diagnosis of appendix torsion is certain (gradual onset, blue-dot present, normal testicular position and axis, no urinary symptoms), conservative management is appropriate: NSAIDs, scrotal support, restricted activity for 1–2 weeks, and follow-up in 7 days. Symptoms typically resolve within 5–10 days. Surgery is indicated if there is diagnostic uncertainty, severe pain, or failure of conservative treatment. (c) Microbiology: Prepubertal boys — most cases are associated with UTI or viral illness. Common organisms: E. coli, Proteus, Klebsiella, enterococci. Treat according to urine culture — trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or amoxicillin-clavulanate for 7–10 days. Screen for underlying urological anomalies (VCUG) if recurrent. Adolescents/sexually active — consider Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Obtain first-void urine NAAT. Treat empirically with doxycycline 100 mg BD × 14 days + ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose. Contact tracing is essential. (d) Algorithm: Clinical assessment first. Features of torsion (sudden onset, vomiting, horizontal testis, absent cremasteric) → emergency exploration — no imaging. Features of appendix torsion (gradual onset, blue-dot, normal axis) → conservative management. Features of epididymitis (fever, dysuria, abnormal urinalysis) → urine culture, antibiotics, reassess. If clinical uncertainty persists after evaluation, perform Doppler ultrasound. Ultrasound showing absent/reduced flow → explore. Ultrasound showing normal/increased flow → manage as epididymitis or appendix torsion. Reassess any worsening or failure to improve within 24–48 hours. (e) Doppler ultrasound: sensitivity 85–95%, specificity 90–98%. However, 5–15% false-negative rate — in early torsion (<6h), spontaneous detorsion, or intermittent torsion, flow may appear normal. False positives (2–10%) occur with severe epididymitis causing increased intra-testicular pressure. Limitations include operator dependence, difficulty in prepubertal boys (small vessels), and inability to exclude intermittent torsion. The risk of missing a torsion on ultrasound (with potential testicular loss) is greater than the risk of a negative exploration. Therefore, ultrasound should not delay exploration if clinical suspicion exists. The threshold for exploration should be low.
discriminators: Uses tabular comparison to clearly differentiate three common diagnoses
Explains the blue-dot sign and conservative management criteria for appendix torsion
Differentiates microbiology and antibiotic regimens by age and sexual activity
Provides a practical algorithm emphasising low threshold for exploration when in doubt
commonErrors: Part (a) — 12 marks: Table format. Award 4 marks per condition: Testicular torsion: (1) age 12–18 peak (neonatal peak also), (2) sudden onset, (3) severe pain, (4) nausea/vomiting, (5) high-riding horizontal testis, (6) absent cremasteric reflex, (7) no fever, (8) normal urinalysis. Torsion of appendix testis: (1) age 7–12 peak, (2) gradual onset over 12–24h, (3) mild-moderate pain, (4) N&V rare, (5) blue-dot sign at upper pole, (6) tender nodule at upper pole, (7) ± low-grade fever, (8) normal urinalysis. Epididymo-orchitis: (1) any age, (2) gradual onset, (3) mild-moderate pain, (4) N&V rare, (5) testis normal lie, (6) tender epididymis, (7) fever, (8) positive urinalysis. Trap: confusing appendix testis with testicular torsion. Part (b) — 10 marks: Blue-dot sign: (1) Deep blue or black nodule visible through the skin at the upper pole of the testis, (2) Represents the torted, ischaemic appendix testis (hydatid of Morgagni) — a Müllerian remnant, (3) Present in 15–30% of cases — diagnostic when visible, (4) More common in lighter-skinned boys. Conservative management: (1) If diagnosis of torsion of appendix testis is certain (gradual onset, blue-dot sign, normal testicle), conservative management is appropriate, (2) NSAIDs for pain, (3) Scrotal support, (4) Activity restriction (no sports) for 1–2 weeks, (5) Analgesia as needed, (6) Follow-up in 1 week — symptoms usually resolve in 5–10 days, (7) Indications for surgery: diagnostic uncertainty (cannot exclude torsion), severe pain, or failure of conservative management. Trap: giving antibiotics for appendix torsion — they are not indicated. Part (c) — 10 marks: Prepubertal boys (<12): (1) Most cases are idiopathic or associated with a viral illness, (2) Common organisms: enteric bacteria (E. coli, Proteus, Klebsiella) — usually from urinary tract infection, (3) Obtain urine culture, (4) Treat with oral antibiotics according to sensitivities — e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or amoxicillin-clavulanate for 7–10 days, (5) Consider imaging to exclude underlying urological abnormality (VCUG if recurrent). Adolescent/sexually active (>12–14): (1) Consider sexually transmitted organisms: Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, (2) Obtain first-void urine for NAAT (chlamydia, gonorrhoea), (3) Treat empirically with doxycycline 100 mg BD × 14 days + ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose, (4) Contact tracing, (5) Advise sexual abstinence during treatment. Common error: giving same antibiotic regimen for all ages without considering sexual activity. Part (d) — 10 marks: Algorithm: (1) History and examination. (2) If testicular torsion suspected (sudden onset, nausea/vomiting, horizontal testis, absent cremasteric reflex) → emergency scrotal exploration. (3) If blue-dot sign present, gradual onset, normal testis lie, no urinary symptoms → conservative management for torsion of appendix testis. (4) If gradual onset, fever, dysuria, abnormal urinalysis → urine culture, antibiotics for epididymitis; if no improvement in 48–72h, re-examine or consider ultrasound. (5) If diagnosis uncertain after clinical assessment — perform urgent Doppler ultrasound. (6) If ultrasound shows absent or reduced testicular blood flow → exploration. (7) If increased or normal flow → likely epididymitis or appendix torsion → manage accordingly. (8) Reassess any patient whose symptoms worsen or do not improve within 24–48h. Trap: exploring all patients with acute scrotum is unnecessary — but if any doubt, explore. Part (e) — 8 marks: Doppler ultrasound: (1) Sensitivity for torsion: 85–95%, (2) Specificity: 90–98%, (3) false negatives: 5–15% — in early torsion (first 4–6 hours), a low-flow state may be misinterpreted as normal (especially in young boys with small testes), (4) false positives: 2–10% — epididymitis can sometimes show reduced flow (severe inflammation with oedema compressing vessels), (5) Limitations: operator-dependent; in children under 5, testicular vessels are small and difficult to interrogate; intermittent torsion with normal flow may be missed; it should not delay exploration if clinical suspicion is high. Role: confirm clinical suspicion but NOT a substitute for clinical judgment. The exploration threshold should be low — any clinical suspicion of torsion warrants exploration even with a normal ultrasound.

questions.title: Torsion of the Appendix Testis
questions.questionText:
A 10-year-old boy complains of a 2-day history of mild right groin and scrotal discomfort. The pain began gradually and has been constant but not severe. He has not vomited. He has no fever, no dysuria, and no urinary frequency. He is otherwise well. On examination, the right testis is normally positioned with a vertical lie. There is a small, exquisitely tender nodule at the upper pole of the testis. The testis itself is not swollen or tender. A tiny blue-black dot is visible through the scrotal skin at the upper pole. The left testis is normal.

(a) [IDENTIFY] the anatomical structure undergoing torsion. Describe its embryological origin. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the clinical presentation and the significance of the blue-dot sign. [4 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the management of torsion of the appendix testis, including indications for surgical intervention. [6 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the complications of torsion of the appendix testis and long-term outcomes. [5 marks]
(e) [LIST] four clinical scenarios where surgical exploration would be indicated despite a suspected diagnosis of appendix testis torsion. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks: The torted structure is the appendix testis (hydatid of Morgagni). 2 marks for description: a small pedunculated remnant of the Müllerian (paramesonephric) duct, located at the upper pole of the testis between the testis and the head of the epididymis. 2 marks for embryology: it is a vestigial remnant of the Müllerian duct — the upper end of the paramesonephric duct that regresses in male embryos under the influence of anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) produced by Sertoli cells. Other appendages: appendix epididymis (Wolffian remnant) and paradidymis (organ of Giraldes). Part (b) — 4 marks: Clinical presentation: (1) Gradual onset of mild-to-moderate scrotal pain over 12–48 hours, (2) No nausea/vomiting, (3) No urinary symptoms, (4) No fever. Blue-dot sign: (1) A small (2–5 mm) blue-black nodule visible through the scrotal skin at the upper pole of the testis, (2) Represents the torted, ischaemic appendix testis, (3) Pathognomonic when present, (4) Seen in 15–30% of cases — more easily seen in fair-skinned children. Significant because it confirms the diagnosis and allows conservative management. Common error: thinking the blue-dot is always present — it is not. Part (c) — 6 marks: Management: (1) Confirm diagnosis — clinical history, examination, blue-dot sign, (2) Conservative management is standard: NSAIDs (ibuprofen), scrotal support, rest, avoidance of vigorous activity for 1–2 weeks, (3) Analgesia as needed (paracetamol for breakthrough), (4) Review in 5–7 days — symptoms usually resolve within 5–10 days as the appendage auto-amputates and calcifies (may form a 'scrotal pearl'), (5) Indications for surgery: diagnostic uncertainty (cannot exclude testicular torsion), severe or worsening pain despite analgesia, failure of symptoms to resolve after 2 weeks, recurrent episodes, (6) Surgery involves excision of the torted appendage via a scrotal approach with testicular fixation if the testis is also mobilised. Trap: giving antibiotics or exploring all patients with appendix torsion. Part (d) — 5 marks: Complications: (1) Persistent pain requiring surgical excision (10–15%), (2) Reactive hydrocoele — self-limiting, (3) Inflammation of the adjacent testis (secondary epididymo-orchitis) — rare, (4) 'Scrotal pearl' — a calcified remnant of the auto-amputated appendage; benign, may be felt as a small nodule but no treatment needed, (5) Testicular atrophy — extremely rare, only if the inflammation is severe and causes vascular compromise (case reports only). Long-term outcomes: excellent — no impact on fertility or endocrine function. Virtually all patients recover fully without sequelae. Part (e) — 6 marks (1.5 each): (1) Inability to definitively rule out testicular torsion — no blue-dot sign, negative urine, atypical presentation, (2) Severe pain unresponsive to adequate analgesia, (3) Signs of testicular torsion (horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis), (4) Recurrent episodes suggesting ongoing torsion of an appendage that has not auto-amputated. Also accept: (5) Failure to improve after 2 weeks of conservative management, (6) Scrotal oedema obscuring testicular examination.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) The torted structure is the appendix testis (hydatid of Morgagni), an embryological remnant of the Müllerian (paramesonephric) duct. It is located at the upper pole of the testis between the testis and the epididymis. In normal male development, the Müllerian ducts regress under the influence of anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) from fetal Sertoli cells. The appendix testis is the small remnant that persists at the cranial pole of the testis. (b) The presentation is gradual onset of mild-to-moderate unilateral scrotal discomfort over 12–48 hours, without nausea, vomiting, fever, or urinary symptoms. The pathognomonic blue-dot sign is a small (2–5 mm) blue-black nodule visible through the scrotal skin at the upper pole, representing the ischaemic, torted appendage. It is present in 15–30% of cases. The testis itself is normally positioned with a vertical lie, and there is no generalised testicular tenderness. (c) Management: (1) Confirm the diagnosis clinically. (2) Conservative management is first-line: prescribe NSAIDs (ibuprofen 5–10 mg/kg TDS), scrotal support, rest, and avoiding sports for 1–2 weeks. (3) Provide a safety net — return if pain worsens or fails to improve. (4) Review in 5–7 days — symptoms typically resolve within 5–10 days. (5) Surgery (scrotal exploration and excision of the torted appendage) is indicated only if: diagnostic uncertainty (cannot exclude testicular torsion), severe pain despite analgesia, failure to resolve after 2 weeks, or recurrent episodes. (d) Complications: persistent pain requiring surgical excision (10–15%), reactive hydrocoele (self-limiting, no treatment needed), secondary epididymitis (rare, treat symptomatically), formation of a 'scrotal pearl' (calcified auto-amputated appendage — benign, no treatment). Testicular atrophy is extremely rare. Long-term outcomes are excellent — there is no impact on fertility, testicular growth, or endocrine function. (e) Four scenarios requiring surgical exploration: (1) Diagnostic uncertainty — cannot confidently exclude testicular torsion (no blue-dot, atypical features), (2) Severe pain despite adequate analgesia, (3) Features suspicious for testicular torsion (horizontal lie, absent cremasteric, high-riding testis), (4) Failure of symptoms to resolve after 2 weeks of conservative management.
discriminators: Identifies the embryological origin of the appendix testis (Müllerian remnant)
Confirms conservative management as standard, with clear surgical indications
Lists specific complications (scrotal pearl, reactive hydrocoele) and their benign nature
Provides specific indications for exploration despite suspected appendix torsion

Also accept: (5) Failure to improve after 2 weeks of conservative management, (6) Scrotal oedema obscuring testicular examination.

questions.title: Neonatal Testicular Torsion
questions.questionText:
A term male infant (38 weeks, 3.2 kg) born by spontaneous vaginal delivery is noted on day 1 of life to have a firm, non-tender, discoloured (bluish-black) right hemiscrotum. The left testis is normal in the scrotum. The right testis cannot be transilluminated. The scrotum does not transilluminate on the right side. The infant is otherwise well with normal observations and no signs of distress.

(a) [DEFINE] neonatal (perinatal) testicular torsion and explain the difference between extravaginal and intravaginal torsion. [5 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the typical clinical presentation of a neonatal testicular torsion and the findings on examination. [4 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] the evidence and controversy around emergency exploration versus conservative management for neonatal testicular torsion. [6 marks]
(d) [OUTLINE] the approach to contralateral orchidopexy in neonatal torsion — who should undergo fixation and when? [5 marks]
(e) [EXPLAIN] the long-term implications of neonatal testicular torsion for testicular function, fertility, and the risk of asynchronous contralateral torsion. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: Neonatal (perinatal) torsion: testicular torsion occurring in utero or in the first 30 days of life. Extravaginal torsion (1) the testis and the tunica vaginalis twist together on the spermatic cord, (2) occurs because the tunica vaginalis is not yet fixed to the scrotal wall — it is freely mobile, (3) typically occurs in utero (prenatal), (4) the testis is usually non-viable at birth. Intravaginal torsion (1) the testis twists within the tunica vaginalis (bell-clapper deformity), (2) typical of adolescent torsion, (3) rare in neonates. Trap: confusing extravaginal with intravaginal torsion — they have different aetiologies and management approaches. Part (b) — 4 marks: (1) Firm, painless (non-tender) scrotal mass noted at or shortly after birth, (2) Discoloration — bluish-black or erythematous scrotal skin, (3) The testis is enlarged and hard, (4) The scrotum does not transilluminate (distinguishes from hydrocoele), (5) The contralateral testis is normal. (6) Usually no systemic symptoms — infant feeds well, no fever. Trap: expecting pain or crying — the testis is typically insensate after intrauterine torsion, so the neonate is comfortable. Part (c) — 6 marks: Evidence and controversy: (1) Historically, some advocated emergency exploration to salvage the testis; however, the vast majority of neonatal torsions are prenatal and the testis is non-viable at birth, (2) Emergency exploration has a very low salvage rate for the torted testis (<5%), (3) The main argument for exploration is to perform contralateral orchidopexy to prevent asynchronous bilateral torsion (which would result in anorchia), (4) The counterargument: emergency surgery in a neonate carries anaesthetic risks; elective exploration at 2–3 months allows safer anaesthesia, (5) Modern consensus (BAPS/ESPU): if the torsion is clearly prenatal (firm, non-tender, discoloured at birth), urgent exploration is not required for the affected side; fix the contralateral testis electively at 2–3 months, (6) If the torsion occurs postnatally (normal scrotum at birth → acute scrotum in first 30 days), emergency exploration is indicated — salvage may be possible. Distinction: quoting data from the British Association of Paediatric Surgeons (BAPS) survey showing that most paediatric surgeons perform contralateral fixation but differ on timing. Part (d) — 5 marks: Contralateral fixation: (1) Strongly recommended because the risk of metachronous (asynchronous) contralateral torsion is estimated at 2–5% — bilateral anorchia would result, (2) Performed via a scrotal incision — place the contralateral testis in a subdartos pouch using non-absorbable suture, (3) Timing: elective — most surgeons fix the contralateral testis at 2–3 months of age under a general anaesthetic that is safer than a neonatal anaesthetic, (4) Some surgeons fix at the time of exploration of the torted side if surgery is undertaken, (5) The torted testis — most surgeons remove a non-viable testis (orchidectomy) to avoid the risk of the necrotic testis causing inflammation or infection. However, some parents may wish to leave it in situ; counsel re the risk of auto-amputation vs later removal. Common error: recommending no contralateral fixation — risk of anorchia is unacceptable. Part (e) — 5 marks: (1) Testicular function — the torted testis is almost always non-viable; if left in situ, it will atrophy and have no function, (2) Fertility — one normal testis is sufficient for normal fertility (paternity rates comparable to the general population), (3) Endocrine function — Leydig cells are more resistant to ischaemia; some endocrine function may persist in the torted testis if left in situ, but this is usually negligible, (4) Contralateral testis — may undergo compensatory hypertrophy; does not show reduced function, (5) Asynchronous contralateral torsion — risk is estimated at 2–5% (range 1–10%) — this is the primary reason for performing contralateral orchidopexy. The risk is present throughout life. Trap: suggesting the risk of asynchronous torsion is <1% — it merits fixation.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Neonatal testicular torsion is torsion occurring in utero or within the first 30 days of life. Extravaginal torsion: the entire testis, epididymis, and tunica vaginalis twist together on the spermatic cord because the tunica vaginalis is not yet attached to the scrotal wall. This is the typical form in neonates and is almost always prenatal, resulting in a non-viable testis at birth. Intravaginal torsion: torsion of the testis within the tunica vaginalis due to the bell-clapper deformity, typical of adolescent torsion but rarely seen in neonates. (b) The neonate presents at birth with a firm, non-tender, bluish-black hemiscrotum. The affected testis is enlarged, hard, and does not transilluminate. There is no erythema, warmth, or tenderness. The infant is otherwise well, feeds normally, and has normal vital signs. The contralateral testis is normal. (c) Controversy: Emergency exploration for neonatal torsion has a very low salvage rate (<5%) because the torsion is usually prenatal and the testis is non-viable at birth. The main reason for exploration is to perform contralateral orchidopexy to prevent bilateral anorchia from asynchronous torsion. However, neonatal anaesthesia carries higher risks. The current consensus (BAPS, ESPU) is: (1) If torsion is clearly prenatal (firm, non-tender, discoloured at birth), do NOT explore urgently — the affected testis is non-viable. (2) Remove the affected testis (or leave in situ if parents prefer) and perform contralateral orchidopexy electively at 2–3 months. (3) If torsion is suspected postnatal (normal at birth → acute scrotum in first 30 days), perform emergency exploration — salvage may be possible. (d) Contralateral orchidopexy is strongly recommended because the risk of asynchronous torsion is 2–5%, which would result in anorchia. The contralateral testis is fixed in a subdartos pouch via a scrotal approach using a non-absorbable suture (e.g., 4/0 Prolene). Timing: elective at 2–3 months of age under a general anaesthetic, which is safer than neonatal anaesthesia. Some surgeons perform it at the same time as exploration of the affected side if surgery is undertaken. The affected testis — if non-viable, orchidectomy is usually performed. If left in situ, it will atrophy. (e) Long-term implications: The torted testis is non-viable and provides no function. With a single normal testis, fertility is expected to be normal — paternity rates are comparable to the general population. Leydig cell function from the surviving testis is sufficient for normal endocrine function. The risk of asynchronous contralateral torsion is 2–5% — this is the driving reason for contralateral orchidopexy. Without fixation, the child could lose both testes. After fixation, the risk of torsion in the fixed testis is negligible. Parents should be counselled about these risks and the rationale for contralateral fixation.
discriminators: Clearly distinguishes extravaginal (neonatal) from intravaginal (adolescent) torsion
Explains the evidence-based shift from emergency exploration to elective contralateral fixation
Quantifies the risk of asynchronous contralateral torsion (2–5%) necessitating fixation
Differentiates prenatal (non-viable) from postnatal (potentially salvageable) neonatal torsion

questions.title: Missed Testicular Torsion — Medicolegal Aspects
questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a 36-hour history of right testicular pain. He initially attended his GP 24 hours ago who diagnosed 'epididymitis' and prescribed antibiotics. The pain has not improved. On examination, the right testis is swollen, tender, high-riding, and has a horizontal lie. The left testis is normal. scrotal Doppler ultrasound shows absent blood flow to the right testis. The patient is otherwise well. He is concerned that the GP misdiagnosed him and wants to know if his testicle will survive.

(a) [INTERPRET] the clinical findings and the significance of the 36-hour delay. Calculate the probability of testicular salvage. [6 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the intra-operative findings you expect. What criteria determine testicular viability at exploration, and how do you decide between orchidopexy and orchidectomy? [8 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] the medicolegal principles relevant to this case, including the concepts of breach of duty, causation, and the 'loss of a chance' doctrine. [8 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a management and counselling plan for this patient and his family. Include your operative plan, communication of bad news, and long-term follow-up. [8 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 6 marks: Interpretation: (1) Classic presentation of testicular torsion — horizontal lie, high-riding, absent flow on Doppler, (2) 36-hour delay significantly reduces salvage probability, (3) Salvage rates after 12–24h fall to <10–20%, (4) At 36 hours, salvage probability is <10% — most testes will be non-viable, (5) The GP missed the classic signs (horizontal lie, high-riding testis, sudden onset), (6) Antibiotics for epididymitis were inappropriate given the clinical picture — the absence of dysuria, fever, and the presence of acute severe pain should have raised suspicion. Trap: being overly optimistic about salvage at 36 hours — the testis is very likely non-viable. Part (b) — 8 marks: Intra-operative findings: (1) The testis will be dark, purple/black, swollen, (2) The cord is twisted (usually 360–720° medial rotation), (3) After detorsion, assess for signs of viability: (a) return of pink colour to the testis, (b) Doppler signal (or palpation of arterial pulsation in the cord), (c) bleeding from a small incision in the tunica albuginea (the most reliable sign — if bright red blood appears, the testis is viable; if dark, non-pulsatile blood or no blood — non-viable), (d) assessment of the degree and duration of torsion. Decision: (1) If the testis appears viable (pink, bleeds bright blood) → detorsion + orchiopexy, (2) If the testis is non-viable (dark, no bleeding after incision) → orchidectomy. (3) In borderline cases (some colour improvement but not fully pink) → some surgeons fix in situ and reassess with Doppler at 3 months — but the evidence for this is weak; most would remove if clearly non-viable. (4) Always perform contralateral orchiopexy (with non-absorbable suture). Common error: fixing a clearly necrotic testis — risk of infection, chronic pain, auto-amputation. Trap: using a Doppler probe intra-operatively as the only arbiter — a dark testis with a Doppler signal may still be non-viable. Part (c) — 8 marks: Medicolegal principles: (1) Duty of care — the GP owed a duty of care to the patient, (2) Breach of duty — a reasonably competent GP should recognise the signs of testicular torsion (acute severe pain, nausea, horizontal testis) and refer urgently; prescribing antibiotics without a urine culture for a boy with acute scrotal pain is likely a breach, (3) Causation — the delay of 24 hours (GP visit) + 12 hours (ED waiting) = 36 hours from onset. The key question: 'Would the outcome have been different if the GP had referred immediately?' If the testis could have been salvaged at 12 hours (50–70% chance) vs <10% at 36 hours, then the delay caused the loss, (4) 'Loss of a chance' — even if the salvage at 12h was not guaranteed (some would die at 12h), the loss of even a 50–70% chance of salvage may be compensable, (5) Bolam test (professional standard) — would a reasonable body of GPs have made the same error? Likely not — testicular torsion is a well-known 'can't miss' diagnosis, (6) The Bolitho modification — the court must be satisfied that the professional opinion has a logical basis, (7) Disclosure — the surgeon should be open and honest with the patient about the delay and its impact. Common error: minimising the role of delay in the outcome. Part (d) — 8 marks: Management plan: (1) Explain the situation to the patient and family — the testis has likely been torted for 36 hours and the chance of salvage is <10%, (2) Consent for emergency scrotal exploration, possible orchidectomy, and mandatory left orchiopexy, (3) In theatre: explore right scrotum, detorse, assess viability; if non-viable → orchidectomy; perform left orchiopexy (non-absorbable suture in subdartos pouch), (4) Send the testis for histology (to confirm torsion and exclude tumour — although the risk is low at this age, trauma is a differential), (5) Post-op: analgesia, scrotal support, wound care. Counselling: (6) Be honest about the likely outcome — the testis is very likely non-viable. (7) Discuss the GP's misdiagnosis — but do not blame the GP explicitly; focus on the current management. (8) Offer psychological support — loss of a testis can affect body image. (9) Discuss testicular prosthesis if desired (usually placed at a later date after scrotal oedema resolves). (10) Advise about fertility — with one normal testis, fertility is expected normal. (11) Arrange follow-up: 6 weeks for wound check, 3 months for scrotal ultrasound (to assess viability of the remaining testis), (12) Recommend lifelong self-examination of the remaining testis (increased risk of torsion was bilateral in this case). Long-term: if both testes were lost, discuss testosterone replacement and fertility options (sperm banking, if possible pre-operatively).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) This patient has a classic testicular torsion with a 36-hour history, horizontal lie, high-riding testis, and absent Doppler flow. At 36 hours, the salvage rate is <10% based on published evidence (0–20% at 12–24h, <10% after 24h). The testis is almost certainly non-viable. The GP's misdiagnosis as epididymitis is a significant error: the absence of fever, dysuria, and the presence of acute severe pain with vomiting should have prompted urgent referral. (b) Intra-operative findings: the right testis will be dark purple/black. The cord is twisted medially (inward). After detorsion, assess viability: (1) Return of pink colour — if the testis rapidly becomes pink, consider salvage. (2) Arterial pulsation in the cord. (3) Incision of the tunica albuginea — if bright red blood appears, the testis is viable; if dark non-pulsatile blood or no bleeding, it is non-viable. (4) Doppler signal assessment. If the testis is non-viable (dark, no bright bleeding after incision), perform orchidectomy. If viable, perform orchidopexy. In borderline cases, some surgeons perform orchiopexy but the risk of subsequent atrophy is high (up to 80–90% at 36h). Contralateral orchiopexy must always be performed with non-absorbable suture. (c) Medicolegal considerations: The GP owed a duty of care. A reasonably competent GP should recognise the acute scrotum as a potential torsion — the hallmark signs (sudden severe pain, vomiting, horizontal lie) are well-known. Prescribing antibiotics without a urine culture for an acute scrotum likely falls below the standard of care (Bolam test). Causation: at 12 hours (earlier possible referral), the salvage chance was 50–70%; at 36 hours it is <10%. The delay of 24 hours from GP visit likely caused the loss of a 50–70% chance of salvage — this is compensable under the 'loss of a chance' doctrine in medical negligence. The Bolitho modification requires that the professional opinion supporting the GP must have a logical basis — it is difficult to argue that misdiagnosing a torsion as epididymitis in a vomiting boy with an acute scrotum is logical. (d) Management plan: (1) See the patient and family together. Explain: 'The blood supply to your right testis has been cut off for 36 hours. The chance of saving it is very small — we need to operate urgently to assess it. You are likely to lose the testis.' (2) Consent for emergency exploration, orchidectomy (likely), and mandatory left orchiopexy. (3) Perform right orchidectomy and left orchiopexy. Send the right testis for histology. (4) After surgery, explain the findings honestly. 'The testis was not viable and had to be removed. The GP should have referred you earlier — I'm sorry this happened.' Do not apportion blame explicitly but document the sequence of events. (5) Offer psychological support — a testicular loss at 16 can affect body image and self-esteem. (6) Discuss testicular prosthesis (silicone implant) — offered at a later date (3–6 months) after inflammation has resolved. (7) Fertility advice: with one normal testis, fertility is expected to be normal. (8) Arrange follow-up at 6 weeks (wound check), 3 months (ultrasound of left testis). (9) Teach and recommend monthly self-examination of the remaining testis. (10) Document all discussions thoroughly in the medical records, including the timeline.
discriminators: Applies medicolegal principles (Bolam, Bolitho, loss of a chance) to a real clinical scenario
Provides realistic salvage rate at 36 hours (<10%) with intra-operative viability assessment
Manages the GP misdiagnosis honestly without explicit blame, focusing on patient care
Integrates psychological support, prosthesis counselling, and fertility advice after orchidectomy

questions.title: Surgical Exploration Protocol for Acute Scrotum
questions.questionText:
Theme: 

Options:


Scenarios:
questions.markingGuide:
Each part 3 marks. Part (a) — A (Detorsion + orchiopexy with non-absorbable suture). Testis is viable; standard orchiopexy with 3-point fixation using non-absorbable sutures. Contralateral orchiopexy also performed (80% bilateral bell-clapper). Common error: using absorbable sutures — risk of re-torsion. Part (b) — B (Orchidectomy + contralateral orchiopexy). Non-viable testis after 48 hours with no improvement after detorsion and warm soaks. Orchidectomy is indicated. Contralateral fixation is mandatory. Trap: leaving a clearly necrotic testis in situ. Part (c) — H (Detorsion + warm saline soaks for 10 minutes, then re-assess). A dusky testis with some improvement after initial detorsion should be given warm saline soaks for 10 minutes to maximise perfusion before final viability assessment. If fully pink after soaks → A; if still dusky → consider borderline viability → orchiopexy with close follow-up. Part (d) — E (Excision of torted appendix testis + orchiopexy of the same testis). Excision of the torted appendage is appropriate. Some also perform orchiopexy of the same testis if it was mobilised during exploration (to prevent future torsion). Contralateral orchiopexy is generally not required for isolated appendix torsion. Trap: performing a full contralateral orchiopexy for a simple appendix torsion is not necessary. Part (e) — A (Detorsion + orchiopexy with non-absorbable suture) for both sides. The contralateral bell-clapper deformity must be fixed to prevent future torsion. Non-absorbable sutures must be used. Part (f) — A (Detorsion + orchiopexy with non-absorbable suture), with correction of haemophilia (factor VIII concentrate) peri-operatively by haematology team. The haemophilia is not a contraindication to surgery; it requires multi-disciplinary planning. Part (g) — A (Detorsion + orchiopexy with non-absorbable suture) of the viable testis. With a solitary testis, this is even more critical. No contralateral fixation needed (no testis). Trap: forgetting to fix the solitary viable testis. Part (h) — E (Excision of torted appendix testis). If the testis was explored and mobilised, perform orchiopexy of the same testis (it has been lifted out of its dartos pouch and needs re-fixation). Part (i) — A (Detorsion + orchiopexy with non-absorbable suture) — for a borderline viable testis, most surgeons would fix it in situ and arrange close follow-up with Doppler ultrasound at 3 months. The testis may still atrophy, but orchiopexy gives it the best chance. Document the borderline appearance. Trap: performing orchidectomy for a borderline viable testis — always try to preserve if there is any hope. Part (j) — B (Orchidectomy + contralateral orchiopexy). Testicular prosthesis is NOT inserted at the same operation because of the high risk of infection in a recently ischaemic/inflamed field. It should be inserted 3–6 months later as a separate procedure. Trap: inserting a prosthesis at the acute exploration.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) A — Detorsion + orchiopexy with non-absorbable suture. Both testes should be fixed (the torted and the contralateral) using non-absorbable sutures (e.g., 3/0 or 4/0 Prolene) in a subdartos pouch, with at least 3-point fixation of the tunica albuginea to the dartos. (b) B — Orchidectomy + contralateral orchiopexy. After confirming non-viability with an incision into the tunica albuginea (no bright bleeding), the testis is removed and the vas is ligated. Contralateral orchiopexy is mandatory. (c) H — Apply warm saline-soaked packs to the testis for 10 minutes and reassess. The testis may recover after warm soaks. If it becomes pink, proceed with orchiopexy. If it remains dusky, consider it borderline viable — perform orchiopexy and schedule close ultrasound follow-up. (d) E — Excision of the torted appendix testis. Since the testis has been delivered and manipulated, perform ipsilateral orchiopexy to prevent future torsion. Contralateral orchiopexy is not required for appendix torsion unless the testis shows a bell-clapper deformity. (e) A — Fix both testes with non-absorbable sutures. The contralateral bell-clapper deformity is a strong risk factor for future torsion. (f) A — Proceed with detorsion and orchiopexy using non-absorbable sutures. Liaise with haematology for peri-operative factor VIII cover. The bleeding disorder is not a contraindication but requires careful management. (g) A — Detorsion and orchiopexy of the viable solitary testis with non-absorbable suture. This testis is 'precious' — fixation is critical. (h) E — Excision of the torted appendix. Since the testis has been delivered, fix it with an absorbable or non-absorbable suture in the scrotum. (i) A — Fix the testis with non-absorbable suture. A borderline viable testis should be preserved. Arrange follow-up with scrotal ultrasound at 3 months to assess for atrophy. If it atrophies, delayed orchidectomy can be performed. (j) B — Perform orchidectomy and contralateral orchiopexy. Do NOT insert a prosthesis at the same operation — the risk of infection is high due to the ischaemic/inflamed field. Discuss prosthesis at a later date (3–6 months post-op).
discriminators: Provides clear intra-operative decision-making algorithm based on viability assessment
Distinguishes appendix torsion from spermatic cord torsion — different management
Emphasises non-absorbable sutures for orchiopexy and warns against absorbable sutures
Clarifies that testicular prosthesis is not placed at the time of acute orchidectomy
