questions.title: DSD Classification and Initial Assessment
questions.questionText:
A newborn infant is transferred to the neonatal unit with ambiguous genitalia. The baby was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy. On examination, there is a phallic structure 2 cm in length, a single perineal opening, and no palpable gonads in the labioscrotal folds. The baby is otherwise well with normal vitals. The parents are distressed and asking if the baby is a boy or a girl.

(a) [CLASSIFY] the current classification of disorders of sex development as recommended by the 2006 Chicago Consensus. Define the three main categories with examples. [8 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] the initial assessment of a newborn with ambiguous genitalia. Include history, examination (with specific reference to Prader staging), and immediate investigations. [12 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] the role of the multidisciplinary team (MDT) in the initial management of a newborn with DSD. List the core team members and their contributions. [10 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the approach to gender assignment in a newborn with ambiguous genitalia based on the GHWP/Chicago consensus and current ethical framework. [10 marks]
(e) [OUTLINE] the key principles of communicating with parents of a newborn with DSD. Include language to use, what to avoid, and how to support shared decision-making. [10 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: 2006 Chicago/LWPES consensus. 46,XX DSD (CAH, 46,XX testicular, ovotesticular). 46,XY DSD (CAIS, PAIS, 5a-reductase deficiency, 17b-HSD deficiency, gonadal dysgenesis). Sex chromosome DSD (Turner, Klinefelter, MGD 45,X/46,XY, ovotesticular 46,XX/46,XY). Common error: using outdated terms like intersex. Part (b) 12 marks: History (maternal drugs, family history, consanguinity). Examination: Prader staging 1-5, phallic length, meatus position, gonadal palpation, labioscrotal fusion, anogenital ratio, rectal examination for uterus. Investigations: urgent karyotype (FISH X/Y), electrolytes, 17-OHP, T/DHT/androstenedione, pelvic ultrasound, ACTH stimulation test. Part (c) 10 marks: Core MDT: paediatric endocrinologist, paediatric urologist, psychologist, neonatologist, clinical geneticist, social worker, specialist nurse. Consensus-based decisions with family involvement. Part (d) 10 marks: Gender assignment based on karyotype, gonadal status, internal anatomy, hormonal profile, fertility potential. 46,XX CAH -> female. 46,XY CAIS -> female. 5a-reductase deficiency -> male. PAIS -> individualised. Surgery deferred. Part (e) 10 marks: Use your baby. Genitalia still developing. Avoid ambiguous, abnormal, disorder. Emphasise health. Consistent language. Support groups. Key worker.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) The 2006 Chicago Consensus classification: Sex chromosome DSD: Turner (45,X), Klinefelter (47,XXY), mixed gonadal dysgenesis (45,X/46,XY), ovotesticular DSD (46,XX/46,XY). 46,XX DSD: CAH 21-hydroxylase deficiency (most common), 46,XX testicular DSD, ovotesticular DSD. 46,XY DSD: CAIS, PAIS, 5a-reductase deficiency, 17b-HSD3 deficiency, complete/partial gonadal dysgenesis, Leydig cell hypoplasia, PMDS. (b) Initial assessment: History (maternal drug exposure, family history of DSD, consanguinity). Examination: Prader staging 1-5, phallic length, urethral meatus, gonadal palpation, labioscrotal fusion, rectal exam for uterus. Investigations: urgent karyotype FISH; electrolytes; 17-OHP; T, DHT, androstenedione; pelvic US; ACTH stimulation. (c) MDT: endocrinology (hormonal workup), urology (surgical assessment), psychology (counselling), neonatology (stabilisation), genetics (testing), social work (support), nursing (coordination). (d) Gender assigned after full workup. Key diagnoses: 46,XX CAH -> female; CAIS -> female; 5a-RD -> male; PAIS -> individualised. (e) Use your baby. Genitalia still developing. Consistent language. Support groups. Family key worker.
discriminators: Uses current Chicago 2006 classification
Integrates Prader staging with clinical assessment
Emphasises MDT consensus
Provides communication guidance
commonErrors: Part (a) 8 marks: 2006 Chicago/LWPES consensus. 46,XX DSD (CAH, 46,XX testicular, ovotesticular). 46,XY DSD (CAIS, PAIS, 5a-reductase deficiency, 17b-HSD deficiency, gonadal dysgenesis). Sex chromosome DSD (Turner, Klinefelter, MGD 45,X/46,XY, ovotesticular 46,XX/46,XY). Common error: using outdated terms like intersex. Part (b) 12 marks: History (maternal drugs, family history, consanguinity). Examination: Prader staging 1-5, phallic length, meatus position, gonadal palpation, labioscrotal fusion, anogenital ratio, rectal examination for uterus. Investigations: urgent karyotype (FISH X/Y), electrolytes, 17-OHP, T/DHT/androstenedione, pelvic ultrasound, ACTH stimulation test. Part (c) 10 marks: Core MDT: paediatric endocrinologist, paediatric urologist, psychologist, neonatologist, clinical geneticist, social worker, specialist nurse. Consensus-based decisions with family involvement. Part (d) 10 marks: Gender assignment based on karyotype, gonadal status, internal anatomy, hormonal profile, fertility potential. 46,XX CAH -> female. 46,XY CAIS -> female. 5a-reductase deficiency -> male. PAIS -> individualised. Surgery deferred. Part (e) 10 marks: Use your baby. Genitalia still developing. Avoid ambiguous, abnormal, disorder. Emphasise health. Consistent language. Support groups. Key worker.

questions.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency
questions.questionText:
A 2-week-old 46,XX female infant with Prader stage 3 ambiguous genitalia is diagnosed with salt-wasting CAH due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency. 17-OHP at day 5 was 800 nmol/L. She is stabilised on hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone. Parents ask about long-term management, genital surgery, fertility, and adrenal crisis risk.

(a) [EXPLAIN] the pathophysiology of salt-wasting 21-hydroxylase deficiency: the enzyme defect, hormonal consequences, and mechanism of salt-wasting. [10 marks]
(b) [CLASSIFY] the three clinical phenotypes of 21-hydroxylase deficiency: salt-wasting, simple virilising, non-classic. Compare presentations, hormonal profiles, and management. [10 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the surgical management of virilised female genitalia in 46,XX CAH: timing, techniques (clitoroplasty, vaginoplasty, urogenital sinus mobilisation), and controversies around early versus deferred surgery. [12 marks]
(d) [OUTLINE] the acute management of an adrenal crisis in a child with CAH presenting to ED with vomiting and hypotension. [8 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] the long-term outcomes for females with CAH including fertility, sexual function, and quality of life. Address the role of transition to adult services. [10 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 10 marks: CYP21A2 deficiency impairs cortisol and aldosterone synthesis. Cortisol deficiency -> disinhibited ACTH -> excess 17-OHP, androstenedione, testosterone. Aldosterone deficiency -> renal salt-wasting -> hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypovolaemia. Androgen excess virilises 46,XX external genitalia in utero. Part (b) 10 marks: Salt-wasting (>75%): complete deficiency, neonatal crisis in weeks 2-4, female ambiguous/male normal, very high 17-OHP, HC+FC+salt. Simple virilising (25%): partial deficiency, female virilised/male precocious puberty, high 17-OHP, HC only. Non-classic: mild, late-onset hirsutism/infertility, mildly elevated 17-OHP, symptomatic treatment. Part (c) 12 marks: Clitoroplasty: nerve-sparing reduction preserving dorsal neurovascular bundle and glans. Vaginoplasty: cutback flap for low confluence, total urogenital sinus mobilisation (Passerotti/Pena) for high confluence. Labioplasty. Timing controversy: traditional early single-stage (2-12 months) vs deferred until adolescence. Early: single anaesthetic, normal childhood appearance. Deferred: patient autonomy, better tissue, avoids unnecessary surgery. Current consensus favours deferring vaginoplasty unless obstructed. Part (d) 8 marks: ABC approach. IV 0.9% saline 10-20 mL/kg bolus. IV hydrocortisone 50-100 mg/m2 stat then 50-100 mg/m2/day divided q6h. Correct hypoglycaemia (glucose 10% if needed). Monitor electrolytes q4-6h. Identify and treat trigger (infection). Continue fludrocortisone. Stress-dosing until tolerating oral intake. Part (e) 10 marks: Fertility: reduced but achievable with good control (anovulation, endometriosis). Sexual function: may be affected by vaginal stenosis and clitoral sensation. QoL: generally good but psychosexual issues common. Transition to adult endocrinology/gynaecology/psychosexual services at 16-18 years. Lifelong steroid therapy, monitoring, emergency/GC cards.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) 21-hydroxylase deficiency impairs conversion of 17-OHP to 11-deoxycortisol and progesterone to deoxycorticosterone. Cortisol deficiency increases ACTH, driving excess androgens. Aldosterone deficiency causes salt-wasting. Intrauterine androgen excess virilises female genitalia. (b) Three phenotypes: salt-wasting (>75%, neonatal crisis, ambiguous female, very high 17-OHP, HC+FC+salt); simple virilising (25%, virilised female, high 17-OHP, HC only); non-classic (mild, late-onset, mildly elevated 17-OHP, symptomatic treatment). (c) Surgery: nerve-sparing clitoroplasty, vaginoplasty (cutback for low confluence, total urogenital sinus mobilisation for high). Debate: early surgery vs deferred to adolescence. Deferred now favoured unless obstructed. (d) Adrenal crisis: IV saline, IV HC, correct glucose, monitor electrolytes. (e) Fertility possible with good control. Transition to adult services essential.
discriminators: Differentiates three CAH phenotypes
Details nerve-sparing clitoroplasty
Addresses early vs deferred surgery debate
Provides adrenal crisis protocol
commonErrors: Part (a) 10 marks: CYP21A2 deficiency impairs cortisol and aldosterone synthesis. Cortisol deficiency -> disinhibited ACTH -> excess 17-OHP, androstenedione, testosterone. Aldosterone deficiency -> renal salt-wasting -> hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypovolaemia. Androgen excess virilises 46,XX external genitalia in utero. Part (b) 10 marks: Salt-wasting (>75%): complete deficiency, neonatal crisis in weeks 2-4, female ambiguous/male normal, very high 17-OHP, HC+FC+salt. Simple virilising (25%): partial deficiency, female virilised/male precocious puberty, high 17-OHP, HC only. Non-classic: mild, late-onset hirsutism/infertility, mildly elevated 17-OHP, symptomatic treatment. Part (c) 12 marks: Clitoroplasty: nerve-sparing reduction preserving dorsal neurovascular bundle and glans. Vaginoplasty: cutback flap for low confluence, total urogenital sinus mobilisation (Passerotti/Pena) for high confluence. Labioplasty. Timing controversy: traditional early single-stage (2-12 months) vs deferred until adolescence. Early: single anaesthetic, normal childhood appearance. Deferred: patient autonomy, better tissue, avoids unnecessary surgery. Current consensus favours deferring vaginoplasty unless obstructed. Part (d) 8 marks: ABC approach. IV 0.9% saline 10-20 mL/kg bolus. IV hydrocortisone 50-100 mg/m2 stat then 50-100 mg/m2/day divided q6h. Correct hypoglycaemia (glucose 10% if needed). Monitor electrolytes q4-6h. Identify and treat trigger (infection). Continue fludrocortisone. Stress-dosing until tolerating oral intake. Part (e) 10 marks: Fertility: reduced but achievable with good control (anovulation, endometriosis). Sexual function: may be affected by vaginal stenosis and clitoral sensation. QoL: generally good but psychosexual issues common. Transition to adult endocrinology/gynaecology/psychosexual services at 16-18 years. Lifelong steroid therapy, monitoring, emergency/GC cards.

questions.title: Gender Assignment Decision-Making in DSD
questions.questionText:
A newborn has ambiguous genitalia: 2.5 cm phallus, single perineal opening, right gonad palpable, left not. Karyotype 46,XY. Pelvic ultrasound shows a uterus and upper vagina. Testosterone low, DHT low-normal, androstenedione normal. Suspected PAIS or 5-alpha-reductase deficiency. Parents ask what gender to raise the baby.

(a) [LIST] the key factors determining gender assignment in 46,XY DSD per GHWP consensus. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the differences between PAIS and 5a-reductase deficiency in natural history, puberty, fertility, and implications for gender assignment. [6 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] the role of androgen sensitivity testing and hCG stimulation in distinguishing these two conditions. [5 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] the ethical considerations regarding early versus deferred gender assignment. [5 marks]
(e) [OUTLINE] the MDT approach to gender assignment. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 5 marks: (1) Karyotype, (2) Gonadal status, (3) Internal genital anatomy, (4) Hormonal profile and response to hCG, (5) Fertility potential. Also: degree of virilisation, diagnosis-specific outcomes, family values. Part (b) 6 marks: PAIS: AR mutation, variable virilisation, poor pubertal response, impaired fertility, assignment controversial. 5a-RD: SRD5A2 mutation, normal AR, virilises at puberty, spermatogenesis possible, strong evidence for male assignment (>60% change from female to male if raised female). Part (c) 5 marks: hCG stimulation test: measure T and DHT. Elevated T/DHT ratio (>10) suggests 5a-RD. Androgen-binding assay: genital skin fibroblast culture. PAIS shows reduced binding (low Bmax). Genetic testing (AR for PAIS, SRD5A2 for 5a-RD) is definitive. Part (d) 5 marks: Early assignment avoids social confusion. Deferred respects future autonomy. Consensus: assign gender early (weeks) based on best evidence but defer genital surgery. Part (e) 4 marks: MDT reviews all data -> consensus recommendation -> family discussion -> shared decision with family.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) Factors: karyotype, gonads, internal anatomy, hormonal profile, fertility potential. (b) PAIS: poor pubertal response, impaired fertility, assignment controversial. 5a-RD: virilises at puberty, spermatogenesis possible, >60% raised female change to male -> male assignment recommended. (c) hCG test: T/DHT >10 suggests 5a-RD. Genetic testing definitive. (d) Early assignment, deferred surgery. (e) MDT consensus with family.
discriminators: Distinguishes PAIS from 5a-RD by natural history and fertility
Explains hCG testing and T/DHT ratio
Stresses male assignment for 5a-RD
Emphasises MDT consensus with family
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Gonadectomy Indications in DSD
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old girl with Swyer syndrome (46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis) was diagnosed at age 16 after presenting with primary amenorrhoea. She has a uterus, bilateral streak gonads, and normal breast development on oestrogen replacement. You are discussing gonadectomy with her and her parents.

(a) [DEFINE] Swyer syndrome and explain why affected individuals have streak gonads and normal female external genitalia. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] gonadoblastoma and its relationship to Y chromosome material (GBY region, TSPY gene). [5 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] gonadal tumour risk in different DSD conditions: complete GD, partial GD, CAIS, PAIS, 5a-RD, ovotesticular DSD. [6 marks]
(d) [OUTLINE] indications for gonadectomy including recommended timing based on tumour risk. [5 marks]
(e) [DISCUSS] management options for this patient: surgery versus surveillance. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 4 marks: 46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis due to SRY mutation. Streak gonads produce no T or AMH -> Mullerian ducts (uterus, tubes) persist, Wolffian ducts regress, female external genitalia. Part (b) 5 marks: Gonadoblastoma = mixed germ cell/sex cord tumour. GBY region on Y chromosome contains TSPY gene. 30-50% contain invasive dysgerminoma/seminoma. Premalignant. Part (c) 6 marks: Swyer 25-35% (highest), partial GD 10-20%, PAIS 5-10%, CAIS 1-2%, 5a-RD <1%, ovotesticular variable. Part (d) 5 marks: High risk (Swyer, partial GD) -> gonadectomy at diagnosis. Intermediate (PAIS, MGD) -> after puberty. Low (CAIS, 5a-RD scrotal) -> defer with monitoring. Part (e) 5 marks: Swyer 25-35% risk. Strongly recommend laparoscopic gonadectomy. Surveillance not recommended (imaging low sensitivity, tumour markers unreliable). Preserve uterus for future fertility (egg donation). Continue oestrogen.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Swyer = 46,XY complete GD. Streak gonads -> no T/AMH -> Mullerian persist, female external. Gonadoblastoma risk from GBY/TSPY. Risk: Swyer 25-35%, CAIS 1-2%. High risk -> gonadectomy at diagnosis. This patient: strongly recommend gonadectomy, preserve uterus, continue oestrogen.
discriminators: Explains TSPY/GBY molecular basis
Provides condition-specific tumour risk
Recommends risk-adapted gonadectomy timing
Fertility preservation (uterine preservation)
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: 5-alpha-Reductase vs 17beta-HSD Deficiency
questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old phenotypically female adolescent has no breast development, recent voice deepening, and phallus now 6 cm. Raised female. Karyotype 46,XY. No uterus or ovaries. Bilateral inguinal testes. Testosterone 25 nmol/L (high), DHT 0.2 (low), androstenedione 12 (high). She is distressed and confused about gender identity.

(a) [DIFFERENTIATE] between 5-alpha-reductase type 2 deficiency and 17beta-HSD type 3 deficiency based on clinical presentation, hormonal profile, and genetic basis. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] the natural history of virilisation at puberty in both conditions and why the phenotype may change from female to male. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] management options: gender identity counselling, hormone therapy, gonadectomy, and genital reconstruction. [10 marks]
(d) [FORMULATE] a multidisciplinary management plan addressing immediate distress and long-term pathway. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) 8 marks: 5a-RD (SRD5A2): T high, DHT low, T/DHT >10, ANDRO normal. 17b-HSD3 (HSD17B3): ANDRO high, T low-normal, A/T >5, DHT low. Both: 46,XY, female/ambiguous at birth, Wolffian present, Mullerian absent. Part (b) 6 marks: Both have normal AR. At puberty LH surge increases T. 5a-RD: T acts directly -> virilisation (phallus, voice, muscle). 17b-HSD3: extragonadal ANDRO->T via other isozymes -> some virilisation but less. 5a-RD >60% change to male gender identity. Part (c) 10 marks: Urgent psychology: gender identity assessment. If female identity: gonadectomy + oestrogen + vaginoplasty. If male identity: T therapy + testicular descent + hypospadias repair. Sperm banking before gonadectomy (5a-RD has spermatogenic potential). Do not rush surgery. Part (d) 6 marks: Immediate: psychology crisis intervention. 1-4 weeks: genetic confirmation. 1-3 months: MDT discussion. 3-6 months: gender decision. Tailored management. Transition to adult services.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
5a-RD: T high, DHT low, T/DHT >10. 17b-HSD3: ANDRO high, T low, A/T >5. Both virilise at puberty via T action on normal AR. 5a-RD >60% gender change to male. Management: psychology-led exploration. Options for female or male identity. Sperm banking before gonadectomy. MDT plan.
discriminators: Distinguishes by hormonal profiles (T/DHT vs A/T ratios)
Explains pubertal virilisation mechanism
Psychology-led gender exploration before surgery
Fertility preservation considerations
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.questionText:
Theme:

questions.markingGuide:
Each part 3 marks. (a) B, (b) C, (c) E, (d) F, (e) G, (f) L, (g) K, (h) J, (i) F, (j) F. Key distinctions: Swyer requires early gonadectomy (E). PAIS underwirilised: defer surgery (C). 5a-RD considering gender change: sperm bank before gonadectomy (K).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
(a) B, (b) C, (c) E, (d) F, (e) G, (f) L, (g) K, (h) J, (i) F, (j) F. Defer surgery/assignment until appropriate. High-risk conditions need early gonadectomy. Psychological support throughout.

questions.title: Management of Wilms tumour: comparing SIOP and COG approaches
questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old girl is referred by her GP with a 2-week history of a right-sided abdominal mass noticed by her mother while bathing. The child is otherwise well, with no abdominal pain, hematuria, or constitutional symptoms. On examination, there is a smooth, firm, non-tender mass arising from the right flank that crosses the midline. Blood pressure is 112/76 mmHg (>95th centile for age). Abdominal ultrasound confirms a solid 10 × 9 × 8 cm mass arising from the right kidney, with no venous thrombus identified. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows no evidence of metastatic disease.

(a) [DESCRIBE] Describe the initial diagnostic workup for a child with a suspected Wilms tumour, including four key imaging features that help differentiate Wilms tumour from neuroblastoma. [5 marks]
(b) [COMPARE AND CONTRAST] Compare and contrast the SIOP and COG protocols regarding preoperative management, including the rationale for each approach, the evidence base, and how each protocol stages the tumour. [10 marks]
(c) [STAGE AND MANAGE] The patient undergoes nephrectomy. Histology shows triphasic Wilms tumour with anaplasia, confined to the kidney with intact capsule, negative margins, and negative lymph nodes. What is the NWTS/COG stage and what postoperative treatment is indicated? [8 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] Discuss the long-term surveillance protocol for this child and the potential late effects of the treatment you have recommended. [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 1 mark for abdominal ultrasound + Doppler (confirm renal origin, venous thrombus). 1 mark for CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging, contralateral kidney). 1 mark for FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation screen, urinary catecholamines. 2 marks for four key imaging features differentiating Wilms from neuroblastoma: (i) intrarenal origin vs suprarenal/crossing midline irregularly, (ii) claw sign — renal parenchyma draped around mass, (iii) tumour thrombus in renal vein/IVC, (iv) calcification is rare in Wilms (common in neuroblastoma). Part (b) — 10 marks: 2 marks for SIOP approach — preoperative chemotherapy (vincristine + actinomycin-D × 4 weeks), delayed nephrectomy, staging based on post-chemotherapy histology. 2 marks for COG approach — upfront nephrectomy, staging based on primary surgery histology. 2 marks for rationale of SIOP: tumour shrinkage reduces operative morbidity and rupture risk. 2 marks for rationale of COG: accurate initial staging without chemotherapy effect, avoidance of unnecessary chemotherapy in low-stage tumours. 2 marks for evidence: SIOP trials show reduced stage distribution and lower surgical complication rates; COG argues stage distribution shift may represent down-staging not true stage reduction; equivalent overall survival (~90%). Part (c) — 8 marks: 2 marks for stage II (tumour confined to kidney, completely excised, negative margins, no capsule penetration, negative nodes) — note presence of anaplasia. 2 marks for anaplasia as unfavourable histology. 4 marks for treatment: stage II favourable histology is vincristine + actinomycin-D (EE-4A regimen); stage II unfavourable histology (focal anaplasia) requires intensification with doxorubicin plus flank radiotherapy (DD-4A regimen plus RT). Part (d) — 7 marks: 2 marks for surveillance schedule: abdominal ultrasound and chest X-ray every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 2 years, then annually. 2 marks for doxorubicin-related cardiotoxicity (echocardiogram surveillance). 2 marks for radiotherapy-related effects: renal dysfunction, hypertension, scoliosis, second malignancy. 1 mark for nephrectomy-related: solitary kidney precautions, annual BP and renal function monitoring.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Initial workup begins with abdominal ultrasound with Doppler to confirm renal origin and assess for venous thrombus. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast is essential for staging, evaluating the contralateral kidney, and detecting metastases. Blood tests include FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation screen, and urinary catecholamines to exclude neuroblastoma. Four differentiating imaging features: (1) Wilms tumour arises within the renal parenchyma, producing a 'claw sign' of compressed renal tissue around the mass; (2) neuroblastoma typically arises from the suprarenal gland, displacing the kidney inferiorly and laterally; (3) calcification is common in neuroblastoma (stippled or amorphous) but rare in Wilms tumour; (4) Wilms tumour often invades the renal vein/IVC as tumour thrombus, whereas neuroblastoma encases vessels. Part (b): The SIOP protocol advocates 4 weeks of preoperative chemotherapy (vincristine + actinomycin-D) followed by delayed nephrectomy. Staging is based on post-chemotherapy histology. Rationale: chemotherapy shrinks the tumour, reduces the risk of intraoperative rupture (from ~20% to ~5%), and lowers surgical stage. The COG (formerly NWTS) protocol advocates primary nephrectomy without preoperative chemotherapy, with staging based on initial surgical pathology. Rationale: provides accurate initial staging, avoids unnecessary chemotherapy in stage I favourable histology tumours, and allows precise histological assessment without chemotherapy-induced changes. Evidence: SIOP trials demonstrate lower stage distribution and fewer surgical complications; COG trials show equivalent overall survival (~90% for all stages). The primary debate centres on whether SIOP's stage shift represents true down-staging or merely histological effect of chemotherapy. Both approaches yield excellent outcomes in protocol-driven care. Part (c): This is NWTS/COG stage II (tumour extends beyond kidney but is completely excised, negative margins, no capsule penetration, negative lymph nodes). However, the presence of anaplasia indicates unfavourable histology. For stage II favourable histology Wilms tumour, standard treatment is vincristine + actinomycin-D (18 weeks, regimen EE-4A). For unfavourable histology (anaplasia), treatment intensification with doxorubicin and flank radiotherapy (10.8 Gy) is recommended (regimen DD-4A). Renal function, cardiac function (due to doxorubicin), and growth must be closely monitored. Part (d): Surveillance: abdominal ultrasound and chest X-ray every 3 months for 2 years, every 6 months for years 2-4, then annually. Late effects: doxorubicin causes dose-dependent cardiomyopathy (cumulative risk >300 mg/m²), requiring lifelong echocardiogram surveillance. Flank radiotherapy increases risk of renal dysfunction, hypertension, scoliosis, and second malignancies (especially sarcomas within radiation field). Solitary kidney precautions: avoid contact sports, annual BP monitoring, and renal function assessment. Growth and development should be tracked. Fertility counselling may be needed if gonadal radiation exposure occurred.
discriminators: Detailed comparative knowledge of SIOP and COG protocols
Understanding of anaplasia as a histological marker of poor prognosis
Integration of staging system knowledge with treatment stratification
Awareness of the survival equivalence debate between the two approaches

Part (b) — 10 marks: 2 marks for SIOP approach — preoperative chemotherapy (vincristine + actinomycin-D × 4 weeks), delayed nephrectomy, staging based on post-chemotherapy histology. 2 marks for COG approach — upfront nephrectomy, staging based on primary surgery histology. 2 marks for rationale of SIOP: tumour shrinkage reduces operative morbidity and rupture risk. 2 marks for rationale of COG: accurate initial staging without chemotherapy effect, avoidance of unnecessary chemotherapy in low-stage tumours. 2 marks for evidence: SIOP trials show reduced stage distribution and lower surgical complication rates; COG argues stage distribution shift may represent down-staging not true stage reduction; equivalent overall survival (~90%). Part (c) — 8 marks: 2 marks for stage II (tumour confined to kidney, completely excised, negative margins, no capsule penetration, negative nodes) — note presence of anaplasia. 2 marks for anaplasia as unfavourable histology. 4 marks for treatment: stage II favourable histology is vincristine + actinomycin-D (EE-4A regimen); stage II unfavourable histology (focal anaplasia) requires intensification with doxorubicin plus flank radiotherapy (DD-4A regimen plus RT). Part (d) — 7 marks: 2 marks for surveillance schedule: abdominal ultrasound and chest X-ray every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 2 years, then annually. 2 marks for doxorubicin-related cardiotoxicity (echocardiogram surveillance). 2 marks for radiotherapy-related effects: renal dysfunction, hypertension, scoliosis, second malignancy. 1 mark for nephrectomy-related: solitary kidney precautions, annual BP and renal function monitoring.

questions.title: Nephron-sparing surgery and syndromic Wilms tumour: ethical and surgical challenges
questions.questionText:
A 2-year-old boy with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (BWS) is under routine abdominal ultrasound surveillance every 3 months. A screening scan reveals bilateral renal masses: a 5 cm mass in the lower pole of the left kidney and a 3 cm mass in the mid-zone of the right kidney. Both are solid on ultrasound and confirmed on MRI. He is normotensive with normal renal function (creatinine 30 µmol/L, eGFR ~90 mL/min/1.73 m²). His parents are extremely anxious and request 'the most aggressive treatment to cure him, including removal of both kidneys if necessary'.

(a) [DISCUSS] Discuss the genetic basis of Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and its association with Wilms tumour. Describe the rationale for screening, its timing, and its limitations. [7 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] Outline the multidisciplinary team approach to bilateral Wilms tumour, including the roles of preoperative chemotherapy and the principles of nephron-sparing surgery (NSS). [8 marks]
(c) [COMPARE] Compare NSS versus radical nephrectomy with dialysis in this context, including technical considerations, oncological outcomes, and long-term renal function. [8 marks]
(d) [ADDRESS] Address the ethical dimensions of the parents' request for 'aggressive treatment'. How would you counsel this family, and what is the role of the clinical ethics committee? [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 7 marks: 2 marks for BWS genetics — 11p15.5 imprinting centre abnormalities (ICR1 or ICR2), CDKN1C mutation, uniparental disomy. 2 marks for WT risk — ~7-10% lifetime risk, highest in first 7 years, hemihypertrophy further increases risk. 2 marks for screening rationale — ultrasound every 3 months until age 7 identifies tumours at earlier stage, improving NSS eligibility and survival. 1 mark for limitations — false positives, parental anxiety, cost-effectiveness debated. Part (b) — 8 marks: 1 mark for MDT composition — paediatric urology, oncology, radiology, pathology, clinical genetics, psychosocial support. 2 marks for role of preoperative chemotherapy — SIOP approach (vincristine + actinomycin-D ± doxorubicin for high-risk) for 4-12 weeks, re-imaging at 6 and 12 weeks to assess response, delayed surgery. 3 marks for NSS principles: (i) tumour resection with 0.5-1 cm margin, (ii) cold ischaemia with ice slush for renal protection, (iii) ureteric stenting, (iv) closure of collecting system, (v) use of intraoperative ultrasound to define margins, (vi) bench surgery with autotransplantation for complex hilar tumours. 2 marks for surgical strategy — tumour enucleation vs partial nephrectomy, approach based on tumour location, relationship to collecting system. Part (c) — 8 marks: 3 marks for NSS advantages: preserved renal function (eGFR stable vs decline to ~60%), avoids/long-delays dialysis, lower cardiovascular risk, better QoL. 2 marks for NSS disadvantages: higher local recurrence risk (5-10% vs <2%), positive margin rate, complication rate (urine leak, haemorrhage ~10%). 2 marks for bilateral nephrectomy/dialysis: anuric, dialysis dependence (vascular access issues in small child, growth restriction on PD), eventual transplant but with higher immunological risk, no survival advantage. 1 mark for outcomes: SIOP-2001 bilateral WT 5-year OS ~85%, NSS achievable in ~70% of renal units. Part (d) — 7 marks: 2 marks for identification of ethical principles: autonomy (parental request), beneficence (best interest of child — preserve renal function), non-maleficence (avoid overtreatment), justice (resource allocation). 2 marks for counselling approach: acknowledge parental anxiety, explain excellent prognosis of WT with modern therapy, clarify that survival with bilateral nephrectomy is not superior to NSS-based approach, involve child psychologist/parent support. 2 marks for role of clinical ethics committee: when parental request conflicts with medical team's assessment of best interests, document MDT discussion, consider second opinion. 1 mark for legal framework — Gillick competence does not apply (child is 2), parental responsibility is limited by child's best interests (Children Act 1989).

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): BWS results from dysregulation of imprinted genes on 11p15.5. The lifetime Wilms tumour risk is 7-10%, with the highest incidence in the first 7 years. Screening with abdominal ultrasound every 3 months until age 7 is recommended because it detects tumours at an earlier stage (typically stage I), allowing nephron-sparing surgery and avoiding chemotherapy in some cases. Limitations include false-positive findings causing unnecessary procedures, significant parental anxiety, and lack of randomised evidence proving survival benefit. Part (b): The MDT includes paediatric urology (surgical planning), paediatric oncology (chemotherapy), radiology (response assessment), pathology (histological response grading), clinical genetics (BWS confirmation, genetic counselling), and psychosocial support. Preoperative chemotherapy follows SIOP 2001/UMBRELLA protocol: vincristine + actinomycin-D for 4 weeks, reassess with MRI. If good response, continue to 12 weeks before definitive surgery. If poor response, escalate to high-risk regimen. NSS principles: achieve complete macroscopic resection with 0.5-1 cm margin, use cold ischaemia (ice slush, <30 minutes) to preserve nephrons, close collecting system meticulously, and use intraoperative ultrasound. Hilar tumours may require bench surgery with autotransplantation. Part (c): NSS preserves renal parenchyma, maintaining eGFR >80 mL/min in most cases, avoiding dialysis and its complications (vascular access, growth delay, infection). Long-term cardiovascular morbidity is lower. The trade-off is a higher local recurrence risk (5-10% vs <2% for radical nephrectomy) and increased perioperative complications (urine leak, haemorrhage). Bilateral nephrectomy commits the child to lifelong dialysis — challenging in small children (PD peritonitis, growth restriction) — followed by renal transplantation (immunosuppression, risk of rejection). Current evidence from SIOP-2001 shows 5-year OS ~85% for bilateral WT with NSS-based approaches. Part (d): The parents' request for 'aggressive treatment' reflects their anxiety and desire to protect their child. My counselling would: (1) acknowledge and validate their fear, (2) explain that Wilms tumour in BWS has excellent prognosis (>90% cure), (3) explain that bilateral nephrectomy does not improve survival over NSS but dramatically reduces quality of life, (4) present NSS as the standard of care balancing cure and renal preservation. If disagreement persists, the clinical ethics committee provides a formal forum for resolving conflicts between parental wishes and medical best interests. The legal principle is that parental autonomy is bounded by the child's best interests — the court would not support bilateral nephrectomy if a viable NSS option exists.
discriminators: Understanding of BWS genetics and Wilms tumour screening protocols
Detailed knowledge of nephron-sparing surgical technique and cold ischaemia principles
Ability to balance oncological radically with long-term renal function preservation
Sophisticated handling of ethical conflict between parental autonomy and child's best interests
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: NWTS/COG staging of Wilms tumour
questions.questionText:
A 4-year-old boy undergoes left nephrectomy for Wilms tumour. The histopathology report describes a tumour confined to the renal parenchyma with an intact capsule. All margins are negative. The renal sinus, renal vein, and lymph nodes are uninvolved. The tumour was removed completely without any intraoperative spillage.

(a) [STATE] What is the NWTS/COG stage of this tumour? [2 marks]
(b) [LIST] List four features that would upstage this tumour to stage III. [4 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] Outline the recommended postoperative treatment for stage I favourable histology Wilms tumour, including the chemotherapy regimen and duration. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 2 marks: Stage I (2 marks). Tumour confined to kidney, completely excised, intact capsule, negative margins, no nodal involvement, no spillage. Part (b) — 4 marks: Any four of: (1) positive surgical margins (microscopic residual), (2) tumour capsule penetration, (3) tumour spillage (preoperative or intraoperative, diffuse or local), (4) lymph node involvement (hilar, para-aortic, or more distant), (5) peritoneal tumour implants, (6) tumour thrombus at resection margin, (7) transected tumour thrombus, (8) renal sinus invasion. Part (c) — 4 marks: Regimen EE-4A: vincristine (1.5 mg/m² weekly × 10 doses, then days 1 and 28 of each 6-week cycle) and actinomycin-D (45 µg/kg on days 1-3 of each cycle) for 18 weeks (approximately 4 cycles). 2 marks for correct drugs and doses, 1 mark for duration (18 weeks/4 cycles), 1 mark for mentioning no radiotherapy required. Note: alternative regimen is regimen EE-4A without doxorubicin.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): NWTS/COG stage I. Part (b): (1) positive surgical resection margins, (2) tumour capsule penetration (beyond the renal capsule), (3) intraoperative tumour spillage (either local or diffuse), (4) involvement of regional lymph nodes. Part (c): Stage I favourable histology is treated with regimen EE-4A, consisting of vincristine (1.5 mg/m²) and actinomycin-D (45 µg/kg) for 18 weeks (approximately 4 cycles). No radiotherapy is required for stage I favourable histology. Long-term follow-up includes surveillance imaging and monitoring for chemotherapy-related effects.
discriminators: Accurate knowledge of the NWTS/COG staging definitions
Understanding of what constitutes 'unfavourable' surgical or pathological findings
Knowledge of stage-appropriate chemotherapy de-escalation
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Bladder/prostate rhabdomyosarcoma in children
questions.questionText:
A 4-year-old boy presents with a 24-hour history of acute urinary retention preceded by 2 weeks of straining to void and intermittent visible hematuria. On rectal examination, a firm mass is palpable anteriorly. A urethral catheter is passed with difficulty and 400 mL of urine is drained. Cystoscopy reveals a polypoid, grape-like mass arising from the bladder neck and extending into the proximal urethra.

(a) [IDENTIFY] What is the most likely histological diagnosis and subtype? Describe the characteristic macroscopic and microscopic appearances. [3 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] Outline the principles of chemotherapy for paediatric rhabdomyosarcoma, including the standard VAC regimen and its common toxicities. [4 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] How has the role of surgery evolved in the management of bladder/prostate rhabdomyosarcoma? Compare historical and contemporary approaches. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 3 marks: 1 mark for embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma. 1 mark for botryoid variant (sarcoma botryoides). 1 mark for macroscopic: grape-like/polypoid mass protruding into lumen. Microscopic: cambium layer (dense tumour cell zone beneath intact urothelium), rhabdomyoblasts with cross-striations, myogenin/desmin positive on IHC. Part (b) — 4 marks: 2 marks for VAC regimen: vincristine (1.5 mg/m², weekly), actinomycin-D (45 µg/kg), cyclophosphamide (1.2 g/m² per cycle). Given in 3-week cycles. 1 mark for common toxicities: myelosuppression (cyclophosphamide), peripheral neuropathy (vincristine — ileus, foot drop), hepatotoxicity (actinomycin — veno-occlusive disease), hemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide — prevent with MESNA and hyperhydration). 1 mark for durations: total 24-42 weeks depending on risk stratification. Part (c) — 3 marks: 1 mark for historical approach: anterior pelvic exenteration (cystoprostatectomy) with urinary diversion was standard — high morbidity, poor QoL. 1 mark for contemporary approach: primary chemotherapy (VAC ± other agents), followed by delayed local control with partial cystectomy / tumour resection preserving bladder if possible. Selective use of brachytherapy. 1 mark for paradigm shift: goal has moved from 'survival at any cost' to 'survival with functional bladder preservation' — bladder salvage rate now ~70% in selected series.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): The most likely diagnosis is embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma, botryoid subtype (sarcoma botryoides). Macroscopically, it appears as a polypoid, grape-like mass protruding into the bladder lumen. Microscopically, the key feature is the cambium layer — a dense zone of tumour cells beneath the intact urothelium. Rhabdomyoblasts showing cross-striations are seen, and immunohistochemistry is positive for desmin and myogenin. Part (b): The VAC regimen consists of vincristine (1.5 mg/m², weekly), actinomycin-D (45 µg/kg), and cyclophosphamide (1.2 g/m²), given in 3-week cycles. Common toxicities include: vincristine — peripheral neuropathy (ileus, foot drop, jaw pain); actinomycin-D — hepatotoxicity including veno-occlusive disease; cyclophosphamide — myelosuppression, haemorrhagic cystitis (prevented with MESNA and aggressive hydration). Part (c): Historically, the standard surgical approach was anterior pelvic exenteration with cystoprostatectomy and urinary diversion, associated with significant morbidity and poor quality of life. Contemporary management has shifted toward a bladder-preserving strategy: primary chemotherapy with VAC, response assessment at week 12-16, then delayed local control. Surgical options now include partial cystectomy or tumour resection with bladder reconstruction. Brachytherapy improves local control while preserving bladder function. Bladder salvage rates of ~70% are achievable without compromising survival.
discriminators: Recognition of botryoid rhabdomyosarcoma and its characteristic histology
Knowledge of VAC regimen composition and specific toxicity profile
Understanding of the paradigm shift from exenterative to bladder-preserving surgery
commonErrors: Part (a) — 3 marks: 1 mark for embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma. 1 mark for botryoid variant (sarcoma botryoides). 1 mark for macroscopic: grape-like/polypoid mass protruding into lumen. Microscopic: cambium layer (dense tumour cell zone beneath intact urothelium), rhabdomyoblasts with cross-striations, myogenin/desmin positive on IHC. Part (b) — 4 marks: 2 marks for VAC regimen: vincristine (1.5 mg/m², weekly), actinomycin-D (45 µg/kg), cyclophosphamide (1.2 g/m² per cycle). Given in 3-week cycles. 1 mark for common toxicities: myelosuppression (cyclophosphamide), peripheral neuropathy (vincristine — ileus, foot drop), hepatotoxicity (actinomycin — veno-occlusive disease), hemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide — prevent with MESNA and hyperhydration). 1 mark for durations: total 24-42 weeks depending on risk stratification. Part (c) — 3 marks: 1 mark for historical approach: anterior pelvic exenteration (cystoprostatectomy) with urinary diversion was standard — high morbidity, poor QoL. 1 mark for contemporary approach: primary chemotherapy (VAC ± other agents), followed by delayed local control with partial cystectomy / tumour resection preserving bladder if possible. Selective use of brachytherapy. 1 mark for paradigm shift: goal has moved from 'survival at any cost' to 'survival with functional bladder preservation' — bladder salvage rate now ~70% in selected series.

questions.title: Testicular germ cell tumour in an adolescent: diagnosis, MDT management, and fertility considerations
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of a painless right testicular swelling. He denies trauma or previous similar episodes. He is otherwise well with no significant medical history. On examination, there is a firm 3 × 2.5 cm intratesticular mass occupying the right testis. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. The left testis is normal. Ultrasound confirms a solid intratesticular mass with microcalcifications and increased vascularity. Tumour markers: AFP 450 ng/mL (elevated), β-hCG 2 IU/L (normal), LDH 180 U/L (normal). The family is present; his parents are visibly distressed, and the boy appears withdrawn and does not engage with questions easily.

(a) [DISCUSS] Discuss the differential diagnosis for an intratesticular mass in an adolescent. Interpret the tumour marker profile and suggest the most likely histological subtype. [5 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] Outline the principles of surgical management, including the role of radical inguinal orchidectomy and the indications for retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND). [5 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] Describe the multidisciplinary team (MDT) approach to managing this adolescent patient, including the specific roles of each core member. [5 marks]
(d) [EXPLAIN] The father asks: 'Will he be able to have children?' Explain how you would approach fertility counselling, including sperm banking options and sensitive communication with both the boy and his parents separately. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 1 mark for differential: germ cell tumour (most common in adolescents), epidermoid cyst, Leydig cell tumour, Sertoli cell tumour, lymphoma (rare at this age), trauma-related haematoma. 2 marks for tumour marker interpretation: elevated AFP indicates yolk sac tumour (endodermal sinus tumour) component — pure yolk sac vs mixed GCT. Normal β-hCG essentially excludes choriocarcinoma and most embryonal carcinomas. 1 mark for most likely: mixed GCT with yolk sac component, or pure yolk sac tumour depending on age (yolk sac more common in pre-pubertal boys, mixed GCT more common post-pubertal). 1 mark for confirming that post-pubertal GCTs are managed on adult protocols. Part (b) — 5 marks: 2 marks for radical inguinal orchidectomy: inguinal approach, high ligation of cord at internal ring, no scrotal violation, testicular prosthesis counselling, tumour markers followed postoperatively. 2 marks for RPLND indications: (i) presence of residual retroperitoneal mass after chemotherapy, (ii) metastatic disease with normalisation of markers, (iii) certain histologies (e.g. teratoma — chemoresistant), (iv) stage II disease. 1 mark for nerve-sparing technique and consideration of ejaculatory function. Part (c) — 5 marks: 1 mark for lead clinician co-ordination and role of each: paediatric urologist — surgery; paediatric oncologist — chemotherapy decision (e.g. BEP regimen: bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin); radiologist — response assessment on CT; pathologist — tumour classification, presence of LVI, histology of non-GCT elements; clinical psychologist/adolescent specialist — support for psychological needs. 2 marks for importance of discussion at MDT meeting: risk stratification (good/intermediate/poor prognosis IGCCCG), timing of surgery, need for RPLND. 2 marks for coordination between paediatric and adult services given this is an adolescent. Part (d) — 5 marks: 1 mark for acknowledging the question seriously and validating the concern. 2 marks for fertility risks: chemotherapy (especially cisplatin — dose-dependent gonadal toxicity), RPLND causes ejaculatory dysfunction (nerve damage), orchidectomy reduces sperm production. 1 mark for sperm banking: offer pre-chemotherapy, typically from age 12-13 onwards (British Fertility Society guidelines), involves discussion with boy directly (not just parents), consider low sperm count banking options. 1 mark for sensitive communication: see boy alone (Gillick competence), use age-appropriate language, provide written information, involve adolescent psychology team, offer separate consultation for parents.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): The differential diagnosis includes germ cell tumour (most common), epidermoid cyst, sex cord-stromal tumours (Leydig, Sertoli cell), and rarely lymphoma. The tumour marker profile shows isolated AFP elevation with normal β-hCG and LDH, indicating a yolk sac tumour component. This could be either a pure yolk sac tumour (more common pre-pubertally) or a mixed GCT containing yolk sac elements (more common post-pubertally). Given the age of 14, a mixed GCT is more likely. Post-pubertal GCTs are managed with adult testicular cancer protocols. Part (b): Management begins with radical inguinal orchidectomy — inguinal approach with high cord ligation at the internal ring, no scrotal violation. A testicular prosthesis should be discussed. Postoperative markers guide staging. RPLND is indicated for: (1) residual retroperitoneal mass >1 cm after first-line chemotherapy with normalised markers, (2) mature teratoma (chemoresistant, requires resection), (3) stage II disease at presentation. Nerve-sparing techniques preserve ejaculatory function. Part (c): The MDT includes: paediatric urologist (surgical planning and execution), paediatric oncologist (chemotherapy — typically BEP regimen), radiologist (CT response assessment, lung metastases evaluation), pathologist (histological subtyping, presence of LVI or teratoma elements), and clinical psychologist (adolescent-specific support). The MDT meeting determines risk stratification using IGCCCG criteria, decides on chemotherapy cycles (BEP × 3 for good prognosis, × 4 for intermediate), timing of post-chemotherapy RPLND, and coordinates transition to adult services due to the patient's age. Part (d): I would first acknowledge the father's concern seriously. I explain that: (1) removing one testis usually does not affect fertility if the remaining testis is normal; (2) chemotherapy with cisplatin carries a significant risk of temporary or permanent azoospermia; (3) RPLND may cause ejaculatory dysfunction. Sperm banking should be offered before chemotherapy begins. I would arrange a private consultation with the boy alone to discuss fertility preservation (Gillick competence — he is likely competent at 14). I would use age-appropriate language, explain that sperm banking is routine and not shameful, and involve the adolescent psychologist. A separate meeting with the parents addresses their concerns while maintaining the boy's confidentiality.
discriminators: Interpretation of tumour marker profiles specific to adolescent testicular GCTs
Knowledge of post-pubertal GCT management transitioning between paediatric and adult protocols
Integration of surgical, oncological, and fertility preservation considerations in a vulnerable age group
Demonstration of communication skills with adolescents and their families

Normal β-hCG essentially excludes choriocarcinoma and most embryonal carcinomas. 1 mark for most likely: mixed GCT with yolk sac component, or pure yolk sac tumour depending on age (yolk sac more common in pre-pubertal boys, mixed GCT more common post-pubertal). 1 mark for confirming that post-pubertal GCTs are managed on adult protocols. Part (d) — 5 marks: 1 mark for acknowledging the question seriously and validating the concern. 2 marks for fertility risks: chemotherapy (especially cisplatin — dose-dependent gonadal toxicity), RPLND causes ejaculatory dysfunction (nerve damage), orchidectomy reduces sperm production. 1 mark for sperm banking: offer pre-chemotherapy, typically from age 12-13 onwards (British Fertility Society guidelines), involves discussion with boy directly (not just parents), consider low sperm count banking options. 1 mark for sensitive communication: see boy alone (Gillick competence), use age-appropriate language, provide written information, involve adolescent psychology team, offer separate consultation for parents.

questions.title: Late effects surveillance in childhood genitourinary malignancy survivors
questions.questionText:
Theme: Late effects surveillance after childhood GU malignancy treatment

Options:
A. Echocardiogram
B. Audiometry
C. Pulmonary function tests
D. DMSA renal scan (dimercaptosuccinic acid)
E. Annual blood pressure measurement
F. Abdominal ultrasound of remaining kidney
G. Urinalysis for proteinuria
H. Growth assessment (height/weight centiles)
I. Fertility counselling and semen analysis
J. Bone density scan (DEXA)

questions.title: Non-operative management of paediatric renal trauma: AAST grading and decision-making
questions.questionText:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling from a tree (approximately 4 metres) onto his right flank. He has visible bruising over the right costovertebral angle and complains of right flank pain. His heart rate is 110 bpm, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg. He has received one fluid bolus of 20 mL/kg normal saline with improvement in heart rate to 95 bpm. There is macroscopic haematuria. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen reveals a 3.5 cm laceration of the right renal parenchyma extending into the medulla but not involving the collecting system. There is a perinephric haematoma contained within the Gerota fascia. The left kidney is normal.

(a) [CLASSIFY] Classify this renal injury according to the AAST renal injury grading system (grades I-V). Justify your answer with specific reference to the CT findings. [5 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] Discuss the criteria for non-operative management of paediatric renal trauma. Is this child suitable? What are the absolute indications for operative intervention? [8 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] Describe the role of follow-up imaging after renal trauma, including what modality, when, and why a DMSA scan at 3 months is recommended. [8 marks]
(d) [EXPLAIN] The child's father asks: 'Will his kidney work normally again?' Explain how you would counsel the family about expected outcomes, including the risk of hypertension and the need for long-term surveillance. [9 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 2 marks for grade III — laceration >1 cm into renal parenchyma extending to medulla, no collecting system involvement, contained perinephric haematoma. 1 mark for grade defined by depth of laceration and collecting system involvement. 1 mark for distinguishing grade III from grade IV (collecting system involvement/urinoma). 1 mark for noting contained haematoma within Gerota fascia is consistent with grade III. Part (b) — 8 marks: 2 marks for NOM criteria: haemodynamically stable (or transiently responsive to initial fluid bolus), no contrast extravasation, contained haematoma, no associated visceral injuries requiring laparotomy. 2 marks for suitability: this child is suitable — responded to single bolus, no active extravasation, contained haematoma. 2 marks for absolute indications for intervention: haemodynamic instability despite adequate resuscitation (≥40 mL/kg blood products), pulsatile/expanding haematoma at exploration for other injuries, grade V vascular injury (renal hilum avulsion). 2 marks for relative indications: ongoing transfusion requirement, urinoma not resolving with conservative measures, infected urinoma. Part (c) — 8 marks: 2 marks for acute follow-up: repeat CT or US at 48-72 hours if clinical concern (ongoing bleeding, fever, ileus). 2 marks for DMSA at 3 months: assesses differential renal function, detects parenchymal loss, predicts risk of complications (hypertension). 2 marks for DMSA interpretation: function <25% of total is significant loss, <10% may indicate non-function. 2 marks for late follow-up: annual BP monitoring for minimum 5 years (hypertension risk ~5% after grade III injury). Part (d) — 9 marks: 2 marks for expected outcome: >95% renal salvage rate with NOM in children, most regain normal or near-normal differential function on DMSA. 2 marks for quantifying likely functional outcome: grade III injuries typically retain 35-45% differential function, risk of long-term function loss ~10%. 2 marks for hypertension risk: ~5% after grade III, usually develops within first 1-2 years, managed with medical therapy. 2 marks for surveillance: annual BP checks for at least 5 years, repeat DMSA if hypertension develops or growth concerns. 1 mark for activity restrictions: avoid contact sports for 6-8 weeks until radiographic healing confirmed.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): This is an AAST grade III renal injury. The laceration is 3.5 cm (>1 cm threshold for grade III) extending into the renal medulla but not involving the collecting system. The perinephric haematoma is contained within Gerota fascia. Grade IV would require collecting system involvement (urinary extravasation/urinoma). Grade V would involve shattered kidney or renal hilum avulsion. Part (b): This child is suitable for non-operative management. He was transiently tachycardic but responded to a single 20 mL/kg fluid bolus. The CT shows no active contrast extravasation, no urinoma, and a contained haematoma. Absolute indications for intervention are: (1) haemodynamic instability despite adequate resuscitation (≥40 mL/kg packed red cells), (2) Grade V renal pedicle injury, (3) associated visceral injuries requiring laparotomy with an expanding retroperitoneal haematoma. Part (c): Follow-up includes a DMSA renal scan at 3 months post-injury. DMSA is the gold standard for quantifying differential renal function and detecting parenchymal scarring. A differential function <25% indicates significant loss. If the child remains asymptomatic, annual blood pressure monitoring for 5 years is sufficient. Repeat imaging is indicated if hypertension, proteinuria, or reduced renal growth is detected on surveillance ultrasound. Part (d): I would counsel the father that children have excellent renal recovery after grade III trauma, with >95% renal salvage rates using NOM. The majority of children retain normal or near-normal differential function (35-45% on DMSA for the injured kidney). Hypertension is a risk in approximately 5% of grade III injuries, usually developing within 1-2 years, but is medically manageable. I would recommend annual blood pressure checks for at least 5 years and a follow-up DMSA scan at 3 months. Contact sports should be avoided for 6-8 weeks.
discriminators: Precise application of AAST grading criteria to clinical and radiological findings
Clear understanding of haemodynamic stability as the cornerstone of NOM decision-making
Knowledge of DMSA role in quantifying long-term functional outcome
Appropriate risk communication about hypertension and long-term surveillance
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Urethral distraction defect after pelvic fracture: delayed repair versus primary realignment
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old boy is involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He sustains an unstable pelvic fracture (Tile type C, APC III pattern) and a crush injury to the perineum. On arrival in the emergency department, he is haemodynamically unstable (BP 70/40 mmHg, HR 140 bpm) and has blood at the urethral meatus. A retrograde urethrogram shows complete disruption of the membranous urethra with contrast extravasation at the level of the urogenital diaphragm. The bladder is displaced superiorly ('pie-in-the-sky' appearance). After initial resuscitation and pelvic fixation in the orthopaedic theatre, he is referred to urology for definitive management of the urethral injury.

(a) [ANALYSE] Analyse the anatomy of the bulbomembranous urethral injury in the context of pelvic fracture. Explain the mechanism of injury and why the prostate is displaced. [7 marks]
(b) [COMPARE AND CONTRAST] Compare and contrast delayed urethroplasty (3-6 months) versus primary endoscopic realignment for the management of complete posterior urethral distraction defects in children. Include outcomes for stricture rate, continence, and erectile function. [10 marks]
(c) [RECOMMEND] Recommend a management strategy for this boy. Justify your choice with reference to his age, injury pattern, and available evidence. [8 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] Discuss the long-term complications these patients face, including erectile dysfunction and incontinence, and how you would counsel the family at the time of injury. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 7 marks: 2 marks for mechanism: pelvic fracture causes shearing force at the junction of the fixed membranous urethra (urogenital diaphragm) and mobile prostatic urethra. The puboprostatic ligaments are disrupted. 2 marks for anatomy: the membranous urethra (2 cm, external sphincter) is the segment most vulnerable. The bulbar urethra is attached to the perineal membrane. 2 marks for 'pie-in-the-sky' bladder: the bladder and prostate are displaced superiorly by the expanding pelvic haematoma, and the urethra is completely transected at the apex of prostate. 1 mark for relevance: complete distraction defect means the two urethral ends are separated by haematoma and scar tissue. Part (b) — 10 marks: 3 marks for delayed urethroplasty: suprapubic cystostomy at time of injury, definitive perineal anastomotic urethroplasty at 3-6 months after haematoma resolution and scar maturation. 3 marks for primary realignment: endoscopic or catheter-guided realignment within 24-48 hours, often using a rendezvous technique. 2 marks for outcomes: stricture rate is lower with delayed urethroplasty (~10% vs ~50% for realignment), but erectile dysfunction is higher (~30-40% vs ~15-20%). Continence rates are similar (~5% incontinence in both). 2 marks for paediatric-specific considerations: children have smaller urethras, higher risk of iatrogenic injury during realignment, and longer life expectancy requiring durable result — delayed repair may offer better long-term patency. Part (c) — 8 marks: 2 marks for recommendation: delayed urethroplasty (suprapubic cystostomy, wait 3-6 months). 2 marks for justification — boy is 14 with long life ahead, needs durable stricture-free outcome, delayed repair offers lowest stricture rate. 2 marks for age consideration: preserves erectile function better than historical approaches, no immediate instrumentation of the injured pelvis. 2 marks for noting that primary realignment in children has poorer outcomes than in adults due to small calibre and technical difficulty. Part (d) — 5 marks: 2 marks for erectile dysfunction: occurs in 30-40% of pelvic fracture urethral injuries due to cavernous nerve injury at time of fracture, not due to surgery itself — counsel that this risk exists regardless of approach. 2 marks for incontinence: uncommon (~5%), related to external sphincter damage. 1 mark for ongoing surveillance for stricture recurrence, need for further procedures, and psychosexual counselling for the adolescent.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): The injury occurs at the junction of the membranous urethra (fixed within the urogenital diaphragm) and the prostatic apex. Pelvic fracture, particularly APC III (Tile C) patterns with pubic diastasis and sacroiliac disruption, causes shearing forces that avulse the puboprostatic ligaments. The bladder and prostate are displaced superiorly by the pelvic haematoma, creating the classic 'pie-in-the-sky' appearance on cystography. The membranous urethra is completely transected, leaving a defect filled with haematoma that will organise into scar tissue. Part (b): Delayed urethroplasty involves initial suprapubic cystostomy, allowing the haematoma to resolve and the prostate to descend. At 3-6 months, a perineal anastomotic urethroplasty is performed, excising the scarred segment and performing a spatulated, tension-free anastomosis. Primary endoscopic realignment attempts to pass a catheter across the defect in the acute setting, often using a combined antegrade/retrograde rendezvous technique. Outcomes: stricture rate is significantly lower with delayed urethroplasty (~10% vs ~50% for primary realignment). Erectile dysfunction is paradoxically lower with primary realignment (~15-20% vs ~30-40%) — this likely reflects selection bias (more severe injuries undergo delayed repair), not a causal effect. Incontinence rates are low in both (~5%). In children, delayed urethroplasty is preferred because the smaller urethral calibre makes realignment technically difficult, and the need for lifelong patency favours the more durable repair. Part (c): I recommend delayed perineal anastomotic urethroplasty. This boy is 14 years old with a complete distraction defect after severe pelvic fracture. His long life expectancy demands a durable, stricture-free outcome. Suprapubic cystostomy will be placed during the acute orthopaedic procedure. Definitive repair at 3-6 months allows the pelvic haematoma to resolve, the prostate to descend toward the perineum, and the scar to mature, facilitating a tension-free anastomosis. Primary realignment in children has higher failure rates and risks further urethral trauma. Part (d): Erectile dysfunction is a recognised complication, occurring in 30-40% of cases, primarily due to cavernous nerve injury at the moment of pelvic fracture. I would counsel the family that this risk exists regardless of surgical approach and is a consequence of the injury itself, not the treatment. Incontinence is uncommon (~5%) and relates to external sphincter damage. Long-term follow-up includes monitoring for stricture recurrence and psychosexual support for the adolescent as he approaches sexual maturity.
discriminators: Detailed anatomical understanding of the pelvic fracture-urethral injury mechanism
Evidence-based comparison of delayed repair versus primary realignment with paediatric-specific data
Recognition that erectile dysfunction is primarily from the injury itself, not surgical approach
Age-appropriate counselling about long-term sexual function in an adolescent

The puboprostatic ligaments are disrupted. 2 marks for anatomy: the membranous urethra (2 cm, external sphincter) is the segment most vulnerable. The bulbar urethra is attached to the perineal membrane. 2 marks for 'pie-in-the-sky' bladder: the bladder and prostate are displaced superiorly by the expanding pelvic haematoma, and the urethra is completely transected at the apex of prostate. 1 mark for relevance: complete distraction defect means the two urethral ends are separated by haematoma and scar tissue.
