questions.title: Classification and management of paediatric bladder injuries
questions.questionText:
A 6-year-old girl is a rear-seat passenger involved in a road traffic collision. She arrives in the emergency department with a seatbelt sign across the lower abdomen and suprapubic tenderness. There is macroscopic haematuria. Her pelvis is stable on examination. A CT cystogram is performed.

(a) [CLASSIFY] Classify bladder injuries as intraperitoneal versus extraperitoneal rupture. Describe the mechanism, characteristic CT cystogram findings for each type, and the management approach. [5 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] Explain why a retrograde CT cystogram (not a standard CT with intravenous contrast) is the imaging modality of choice for suspected bladder injury. [2 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] Describe the surgical principles of repairing an intraperitoneal bladder rupture in a child, including postoperative bladder drainage. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 2 marks for classification: intraperitoneal (dome rupture, often from rapid deceleration with distended bladder, urine spills into peritoneal cavity) vs extraperitoneal (anterolateral wall, often associated with pelvic fracture, urine contained within perivesical space). 2 marks for CT cystogram findings: intraperitoneal — contrast outlines bowel loops, surrounds intra-abdominal organs; extraperitoneal — flame-shaped contrast collection in perivesical space, tracking into prevesical space of Retzius, limited by peritoneal reflection. 1 mark for management: intraperitoneal requires surgical repair; extraperitoneal is managed conservatively with catheter drainage (10-14 days). Part (b) — 2 marks: CT cystogram requires distension of bladder with dilute contrast via urethral catheter (passive filling to capacity, then 200-300 mL or until leak seen). Standard venous phase CT may miss bladder injury because bladder may not be adequately distended (false negative). Sensitivity of CT cystogram >95% vs 60-80% for standard CT. Part (c) — 3 marks: 1 mark for principles: exploratory laparotomy, identify dome laceration, repair in two layers (mucosa and muscularis) with absorbable sutures (3-0 or 4-0 Vicryl), check watertight closure. 1 mark for drainage: urethral catheter (not suprapubic unless necessary) for 7-10 days, cystogram before removal to confirm healing. 1 mark for paediatric considerations: smaller bladder capacity, use fine sutures, ensure no distal obstruction.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Intraperitoneal bladder rupture occurs at the dome, usually from a blow to a distended bladder, causing peritoneum to tear. CT cystogram shows contrast outlining bowel loops and pooling in the paracolic gutters. Management is surgical repair. Extraperitoneal rupture involves the anterolateral wall, typically from pelvic fracture bone spicules. CT cystogram shows flame-shaped contrast in the perivesical space, limited by the peritoneal reflection. Management is conservative with urethral catheter drainage for 10-14 days. Part (b): CT cystogram requires active retrograde bladder filling with dilute contrast via a urethral catheter to achieve adequate distension. Standard venous CT may miss a bladder injury because the bladder may not be distended enough to demonstrate a leak, giving a false-negative rate of 20-40%. Part (c): Repair is via a lower midline laparotomy. The bladder is opened anteriorly (cystotomy) to inspect the dome laceration from within, then closed in two layers (mucosa and muscularis) with 3-0 or 4-0 absorbable sutures. A watertight test confirms closure. A urethral catheter is left for 7-10 days; a cystogram is performed before removal to confirm healing.
discriminators: Clear differentiation between intraperitoneal and extraperitoneal rupture
Understanding of why CT cystogram is superior to standard CT for bladder injury diagnosis
Knowledge of basic surgical repair principles adapted to paediatric anatomy
commonErrors: Part (a) — 5 marks: 2 marks for classification: intraperitoneal (dome rupture, often from rapid deceleration with distended bladder, urine spills into peritoneal cavity) vs extraperitoneal (anterolateral wall, often associated with pelvic fracture, urine contained within perivesical space). 2 marks for CT cystogram findings: intraperitoneal — contrast outlines bowel loops, surrounds intra-abdominal organs; extraperitoneal — flame-shaped contrast collection in perivesical space, tracking into prevesical space of Retzius, limited by peritoneal reflection. 1 mark for management: intraperitoneal requires surgical repair; extraperitoneal is managed conservatively with catheter drainage (10-14 days). Part (b) — 2 marks: CT cystogram requires distension of bladder with dilute contrast via urethral catheter (passive filling to capacity, then 200-300 mL or until leak seen). Standard venous phase CT may miss bladder injury because bladder may not be adequately distended (false negative). Sensitivity of CT cystogram >95% vs 60-80% for standard CT. Part (c) — 3 marks: 1 mark for principles: exploratory laparotomy, identify dome laceration, repair in two layers (mucosa and muscularis) with absorbable sutures (3-0 or 4-0 Vicryl), check watertight closure. 1 mark for drainage: urethral catheter (not suprapubic unless necessary) for 7-10 days, cystogram before removal to confirm healing. 1 mark for paediatric considerations: smaller bladder capacity, use fine sutures, ensure no distal obstruction.

questions.title: Testicular rupture: ultrasound findings and surgical management
questions.questionText:
A 15-year-old boy presents to the emergency department 6 hours after sustaining a direct blow to the scrotum during a football match. He has severe scrotal pain, nausea, and mild swelling. On examination, the right scrotum is swollen, tender, and the testis cannot be clearly palpated. The left testis is normal. There is a visible purple discolouration of the overlying scrotal skin.

(a) [DESCRIBE] Describe the key ultrasound findings that would confirm a diagnosis of testicular rupture, including the role of colour Doppler. [4 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] Explain the indications for urgent surgical exploration in testicular trauma. What is the rationale for early surgery? [3 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] Outline the surgical steps for repair of a ruptured testis, including principles of debridement, closure, and postoperative care. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks: 1 mark for loss of normal homogeneous echotexture of testicular parenchyma. 1 mark for discontinuity/breach in tunica albuginea (the hallmark finding). 1 mark for irregularity of testicular contour with extrusion of seminiferous tubules. 1 mark for colour Doppler: assesses testicular vascularity (blood flow may be reduced but should be present — absent flow suggests torsion or infarction). Haematocoele (echogenic fluid within tunica vaginalis) is an associated finding. Part (b) — 3 marks: 1 mark for indications for exploration: US evidence of tunica albuginea rupture, large haematocele, non-palpable testis, suspicion of torsion, or penetrating injury. 1 mark for rationale for early exploration (<72 hours): salvage rates >80-90% when explored early vs <30-50% if delayed >72 hours due to ischaemia, infection, and necrosis. 1 mark for timing: urgent exploration within 24 hours of injury is standard. Part (c) — 3 marks: 1 mark for approach: midline scrotal raphe incision, open tunica vaginalis, evacuate haematocoele, deliver testis. 1 mark for steps: debride non-viable seminiferous tubules (extruded necrotic tissue), irrigate with warm saline, close tunica albuginea with 4-0 absorbable suture (Vicryl/PDS) in a watertight fashion. 1 mark for closure: place testis back in scrotum, close dartos and skin, drain (optional). Postoperative: scrotal support, analgesia, antibiotics, avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Ultrasound findings of testicular rupture include: (1) interruption or focal loss of the normal echogenic line of the tunica albuginea, (2) heterogeneous testicular parenchyma with loss of normal homogeneous echotexture, (3) irregular contour with protrusion of seminiferous tubules through the tunica defect (the 'extrusion' sign), and (4) haematocoele (echogenic fluid within the tunica vaginalis). Colour Doppler should demonstrate preserved or reduced intratesticular blood flow; absent flow raises concern for testicular torsion or infarction. Part (b): Urgent surgical exploration is indicated when ultrasound clearly demonstrates tunica albuginea rupture, when the testis is non-palpable due to a large haematocele, or when there is concern for testicular torsion. Early exploration (within 72 hours, ideally within 24 hours) is associated with testicular salvage rates of 80-90%, compared to <50% with delayed exploration due to progressive ischaemia, necrosis, and infection. Part (c): Surgical repair is performed via a midline scrotal raphe incision. The haematocoele is evacuated. Non-viable, extruded seminiferous tubules are debrided. Viable tubules are replaced into the tunica. The tunica albuginea defect is closed with interrupted 4-0 absorbable sutures in a watertight fashion. The testis is returned to the scrotum, the dartos is closed, and skin is closed with absorbable sutures. Postoperative care includes scrotal support, analgesia, antibiotics, and activity restriction for 4-6 weeks.
discriminators: Recognition of the key diagnostic ultrasound sign (tunica albuginea disruption)
Understanding of the critical time window for maximal testicular salvage
Knowledge of specific surgical technique for testicular salvage
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Polytrauma with genitourinary involvement: MDT decision-making and consent challenges
questions.questionText:
An 8-year-old boy is struck by a car while crossing the road. On arrival in the emergency department, he has a GCS of 14, heart rate 130 bpm, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg. He has a seatbelt sign across the lower abdomen, pelvic instability, and scrotal swelling. After two 20 mL/kg fluid boluses, his BP improves to 95/60 mmHg. A trauma CT scan reveals: (1) AAST grade IV right renal injury (deep laceration with urinary extravasation), (2) Tile B pelvic fracture, (3) an intraperitoneal bladder rupture, (4) small volume free intraperitoneal fluid (possible bowel injury). The orthopaedic and general surgical teams are discussing emergency laparotomy for pelvic packing and bowel assessment. His parents are in the waiting room and have been informed their son is seriously injured but stable.

(a) [PRIORITISE] Prioritise the injuries in terms of immediate life-threatening versus organ-threatening. What is the sequence of surgical intervention in a damage control setting? [6 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] Describe the MDT approach to this child's care, specifying the role of the paediatric urologist during the acute phase and the decision-making process for each GU injury. [6 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] The grade IV renal injury has urinary extravasation but the patient is now haemodynamically stable. Discuss the management of renal collecting system injuries in children and the indications for ureteric stenting. [4 marks]
(d) [ADDRESS] The parents are approached for consent for emergency laparotomy. The father asks if his son will lose his kidney or be infertile from the injuries. Explain how you would address these questions and obtain valid consent in an emergency setting. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 6 marks: 2 marks for life-threatening priorities: hypovolaemic shock from pelvic fracture (retroperitoneal bleeding), possible bowel perforation/sepsis. These take precedence — damage control laparotomy with pelvic packing, external fixation. 2 marks for organ-threatening: renal injury (grade IV, extravasation), bladder rupture (intraperitoneal), scrotal injury. 2 marks for sequence: (1) pelvic external fixation and packing by orthopaedics, (2) exploratory laparotomy by general surgeons for bowel evaluation, (3) concurrent repair of intraperitoneal bladder rupture by urology, (4) assessment of retroperitoneum — if expanding haematoma, explore; otherwise NOM for renal injury, (5) scrotal exploration delayed until stable. Part (b) — 6 marks: 2 marks for MDT composition: paediatric urology, orthopaedics, general surgery, paediatric intensive care, radiology, emergency medicine. 2 marks for urology role: (i) assess renal injury on CT — no active extravasation → NOM candidate; (ii) repair intraperitoneal bladder rupture during laparotomy; (iii) decide on retroperitoneal exploration if haematoma expanding; (iv) scrotal exploration if testicular rupture suspected after stabilisation. 2 marks for decision-making: grade IV kidney with extravasation but stable → NOM; bladder rupture → repair during laparotomy; pelvic fracture → no urethral catheter until GU injury assessed. Part (c) — 4 marks: 2 marks for management of collecting system injury: most paediatric grade IV injuries with extravasation can be managed conservatively with catheter drainage and broad-spectrum antibiotics. 1 mark for indications for stenting: persistent urinary extravasation >48-72 hours, enlarging urinoma, infected urinoma, severe extravasation with ureteropelvic junction disruption. 1 mark for technique: retrograde ureteric stent placement (double-J) for 4-6 weeks, or percutaneous nephrostomy if retrograde fails. Part (d) — 4 marks: 1 mark for acknowledging the father's fear and anxiety. 1 mark for renal injury: explain that grade IV kidney with extravasation is managed conservatively in stable children, >90% salvage rate, only removed if uncontrollable bleeding. 1 mark for fertility: explain testicular injury is assessable and repairable; isolated trauma to one testis does not affect overall fertility; pelvic fracture may carry a small risk of erectile dysfunction (cavernous nerve injury). 1 mark for consent in emergency: Mental Capacity Act — emergency treatment in best interests, document discussion, parents can provide consent on child's behalf. Provide honest, clear information without unnecessary detail in the emergency setting.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): The life-threatening priorities are haemorrhagic shock from the pelvic fracture and possible bowel perforation. Damage control sequence: (1) orthopaedic external fixation and pelvic packing, (2) exploratory laparotomy by general surgery for bowel assessment, (3) repair of intraperitoneal bladder rupture by urology during the same laparotomy, (4) renal injury — retroperitoneum assessed; if haematoma is non-expanding, the kidney is managed non-operatively; (5) scrotal exploration deferred until the patient is haemodynamically stable, ideally within 24-72 hours if testicular rupture is confirmed. Part (b): The MDT includes paediatric urology, orthopaedics, general surgery, PICU, and radiology. The urologist's role: (1) reviews CT to classify renal injury — grade IV deep laceration with extravasation but no active bleeding, suitable for NOM; (2) during laparotomy, inspects and repairs the intraperitoneal bladder rupture in two layers; (3) evaluates the retroperitoneum — if the haematoma is contained, leave the kidney untouched; (4) post-stabilisation, arranges scrotal ultrasound to assess the testis. Part (c): Most paediatric grade IV renal injuries with urinary extravasation can be managed non-operatively with a urethral catheter (to ensure low-pressure urinary drainage) and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Ureteric stenting is indicated for: (1) persistent extravasation beyond 48-72 hours, (2) enlarging or symptomatic urinoma, (3) infected urinoma, (4) ureteropelvic junction disruption. Stenting can be performed retrograde cystoscopically or via percutaneous nephrostomy. Part (d): I would reassure the father that his son's kidney is likely salvageable — grade IV injuries in stable children are managed without surgery in >90% of cases, with excellent functional recovery. Regarding fertility: (1) testicular trauma affecting one testis does not impair overall fertility; (2) if the testis is ruptured, it can be surgically repaired; (3) pelvic fracture carries a small risk of erectile dysfunction (cavernous nerve injury at time of fracture, not from surgery). Consent in this emergency is obtained under the Mental Capacity Act — treatment is in the child's best interests, and parental agreement is sought but not strictly required if delay would cause harm.
discriminators: Ability to prioritise injuries in a damage-control polytrauma setting
Understanding of the urologist's role as part of a larger trauma MDT
Knowledge of modern NOM principles for even high-grade renal injuries
Emergency consent communication skills with distressed parents

These take precedence — damage control laparotomy with pelvic packing, external fixation. 2 marks for organ-threatening: renal injury (grade IV, extravasation), bladder rupture (intraperitoneal), scrotal injury. 2 marks for sequence: (1) pelvic external fixation and packing by orthopaedics, (2) exploratory laparotomy by general surgeons for bowel evaluation, (3) concurrent repair of intraperitoneal bladder rupture by urology, (4) assessment of retroperitoneum — if expanding haematoma, explore; otherwise NOM for renal injury, (5) scrotal exploration delayed until stable. questions.title: AAST renal injury grading
questions.questionText:
Theme: AAST renal injury grading and management

Options:
A. Grade I: renal contusion or subcapsular haematoma, no laceration
B. Grade II: superficial laceration <1 cm, no collecting system involvement
C. Grade III: laceration >1 cm, medulla but not collecting system, contained haematoma
D. Grade IV: laceration involving collecting system (urinoma), or segmental vascular injury
E. Grade V: shattered kidney or renal hilum avulsion
F. Grade II: perinephric haematoma without laceration
G. Grade III: subcapsular haematoma >50% surface area

Scenarios:
undefined. undefined
undefined. undefined
undefined. undefined

questions.title: Laparoscopic nephrectomy in children: indications, technique, and consent
questions.questionText:
A 3-year-old girl is referred from the nephrology clinic with a history of recurrent urinary tract infections. She was diagnosed antenatally with a left multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK). Serial postnatal ultrasound scans have shown involution of the MCDK, but she has had three documented febrile UTIs in the past 12 months, all requiring hospital admission for intravenous antibiotics. A DMSA renal scan shows no function in the left kidney and normal differential function (51%) in the right kidney. A micturating cystourethrogram (MCUG) shows grade III vesicoureteric reflux into the left MCDK ureter. Her parents are concerned about ongoing infections and request definitive management.

(a) [DISCUSS] Discuss the indications for nephrectomy in children, including the specific indications for non-functioning kidneys, MCDK, and reflux nephropathy. Is nephrectomy indicated in this child? [8 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] Compare the transperitoneal and retroperitoneal laparoscopic approaches for nephrectomy in children. Include advantages, disadvantages, and patient positioning for each. [8 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] Describe the key steps of a transperitoneal laparoscopic nephrectomy in a 3-year-old, including port placement, colonic mobilisation, and hilar control. [8 marks]
(d) [EXPLAIN] The parents ask about the alternatives including continued observation versus surgical management. Explain how you would obtain informed consent for laparoscopic nephrectomy, including discussion of risks, benefits, and alternatives. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: 2 marks for general indications: non-functioning kidney (<10% differential function on DMSA), recurrent febrile UTIs despite antibiotic prophylaxis, hypertension, pain, associated nephrolithiasis, suspicious mass in a non-functioning unit. 2 marks for MCDK-specific: most involute spontaneously, ~50% by 5 years. Nephrectomy indicated for: hypertension, failure to involute after 5 years, very large size causing mass effect, associated VUR with breakthrough UTIs. 2 marks for reflux nephropathy: nephrectomy indicated for non-functioning kidney with reflux causing recurrent pyelonephritis. 2 marks for this child: nephrectomy is indicated — non-functioning left kidney (<10% function), recurrent breakthrough febrile UTIs despite prophylaxis, associated VUR into the MCDK ureter. Part (b) — 8 marks: 4 marks for transperitoneal: advantages — larger working space, familiar anatomy, easier to access both upper and lower poles, can address associated pathology (e.g., refluxing ureter can be resected at the bladder). Disadvantages — risk of bowel injury, need for bowel mobilisation, potential for ileus, intraperitoneal spillage if infected. Position: semi-decubitus (30-45°), table flexed. 4 marks for retroperitoneal: advantages — direct access to retroperitoneum, no bowel mobilisation, lower risk of intraperitoneal injury, quicker recovery (less ileus), ideal for small infants. Disadvantages — smaller working space, steeper learning curve, less familiar landmarks, more difficult for large kidneys or upper pole access. Position: full lateral decubitus with flank elevated, table flexed. Part (c) — 8 marks: 1 mark for positioning: supine with ipsilateral flank elevated 30-45°, table flexed at costal margin, all pressure points padded. 2 marks for port placement: 5 mm umbilical camera port (Hassan open technique), two 3-5 mm working ports — one subcostal in midclavicular line, one in iliac fossa. Optional 5th port for retraction if needed. 2 marks for steps: (1) incise peritoneum along Toldt's line to mobilise colon medially, (2) identify ureter (dissect distally and divide), (3) trace ureter proximally to lower pole, (4) identify renal hilum, (5) gonadal vessels and ureter divided, (6) hilar dissection — identify renal vein and artery, ligate and divide individually (hem-o-lok clips or LigaSure), (7) divide remaining attachments (upper pole, adrenal if necessary). 2 marks for specimen extraction: place in retrieval bag, morcellate or extend port site for intact removal. 1 mark for CO2 insufflation: 8-12 mmHg, lower flow for small children. Part (d) — 6 marks: 2 marks for discussion of alternatives: (1) continued observation with prophylactic antibiotics — risk of recurrent UTI, potential renal scarring of contralateral kidney, need for ongoing prophylaxis; (2) surgical management — laparoscopic nephrectomy offers definitive cure. 2 marks for risks: bleeding (<1% requiring transfusion), infection, bowel injury, port-site hernia, need for conversion to open (<5%), ureteric stump syndrome if refluxing ureter not excised. 2 marks for consent process: age-appropriate explanation to child (simplified), detailed discussion with parents, written information, opportunity for questions, documentation of shared decision-making.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Indications for nephrectomy in children include a non-functioning kidney (<10% differential function on DMSA) with recurrent febrile UTIs, hypertension, or pain. For MCDK specifically, most undergo spontaneous involution (~50% by 5 years) and can be observed. Surgery is indicated for: breakthrough febrile UTIs despite prophylaxis, hypertension, failure to involute after 5 years, or very large size causing symptoms. In this child, nephrectomy is clearly indicated: the left kidney has no function on DMSA, she has had three febrile UTIs requiring hospitalisation, and MCUG shows grade III VUR into the non-functioning moiety, creating a reservoir for ongoing infection that risks the contralateral kidney. Part (b): Transperitoneal approach: patient in semi-decubitus position, ports placed along the pararectal line. Advantages — spacious working area, familiar anatomy, easy to address the lower ureter. Disadvantages — bowel mobilisation required, risk of bowel injury and postoperative ileus. Retroperitoneal approach: patient in full lateral flank position, balloon dissection creates the retroperitoneal space. Advantages — direct access to kidney, no bowel handling, lower risk of ileus and intraperitoneal injury. Disadvantages — smaller working space, steeper learning curve, limited access to the distal ureter. In this 3-year-old with a refluxing ureter, transperitoneal allows both nephrectomy and distal ureterectomy. Part (c): The patient is positioned in semi-decubitus (30-45° flank elevation) with table flexion. Port placement: 5 mm umbilical camera port (Hassan technique), 3 mm working port in the subcostal midclavicular line, and 3-5 mm working port in the iliac fossa. Steps: (1) mobilise the colon medially along the white line of Toldt; (2) identify and dissect the ureter, divide distal ureter with bipolar cautery; (3) trace ureter proximally to the lower pole; (4) identify renal hilum — divide gonadal vessels; (5) skeletonise and individually ligate the renal vein (posterior to the colon) and renal artery (posterior to vein) using Hem-o-Lok clips; (6) divide the ureteropelvic junction and upper pole attachments; (7) place the specimen in a retrieval bag and extract via the umbilical port site. CO2 insufflation pressure 10-12 mmHg. Part (d): I would explain to the parents that there are two options: continued observation with prophylactic antibiotics (which has failed with three breakthrough UTIs) versus laparoscopic nephrectomy (which offers definitive cure). I would explain that the risk of contralateral renal scarring from ongoing infection outweighs the risks of surgery. Specific risks include: bleeding (<1%), infection, bowel injury, need for open conversion (<5%), and port-site hernia. The benefits are cessation of infections, no further antibiotic requirement, and protection of the normal right kidney. I would provide written information and allow time for questions. The child would be given a simplified, age-appropriate explanation with play therapy preparation.
discriminators: Clear understanding of the indications for nephrectomy in paediatric non-functioning kidneys
Detailed comparative knowledge of transperitoneal vs retroperitoneal approaches
Stepwise description of laparoscopic nephrectomy technique with port placement adapted to age
Comprehensive consent discussion including alternatives and shared decision-making

questions.title: Robotic pyeloplasty versus open pyeloplasty: evidence-based comparison
questions.questionText:
A 6-month-old boy is found to have a right antenatal hydronephrosis (APD 18 mm on third-trimester ultrasound). Postnatal ultrasound at 6 weeks shows SFU grade 4 hydronephrosis with calyceal dilatation and a cortical thickness of 3 mm. A MAG3 renogram shows a T½ of 160 minutes (obstructive drainage curve) with differential renal function of 38% on the right side. A diuretic was given at 20 minutes. The parents are informed that their child has a right pelvi-ureteric junction (PUJ) obstruction and requires pyeloplasty. The father, a software engineer, has read online that robotic surgery is superior and specifically requests a robotic procedure.

(a) [INTERPRET] Interpret the renogram findings. What defines obstruction on a MAG3 scan, and why is differential renal function important in surgical decision-making? [7 marks]
(b) [COMPARE AND CONTRAST] Compare and contrast open dismembered pyeloplasty (Anderson-Hynes) versus robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty in infants. Discuss operative time, length of stay, success rates, and cost. [10 marks]
(c) [ANALYSE] Analyse the specific challenges of robotic pyeloplasty in infants under 1 year of age, including port placement, working space constraints, and the role of the da Vinci X/Xi systems. [7 marks]
(d) [RECOMMEND] How would you respond to the father's request for robotic surgery? Discuss the evidence base, your local practice, and how you would counsel the family to make a shared decision. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 7 marks: 2 marks for MAG3 interpretation: prolonged T½ >20 minutes after furosemide (diuretic renogram), obstructive drainage curve (type 1 or 2 depending on washout). T½ of 160 minutes is clearly obstructive. 2 marks for definition of obstruction: impaired urinary drainage that, if untreated, will cause progressive renal damage. 2 marks for differential function: 38% indicates significant but not critical loss — threshold for surgery is typically <40% or declining function on serial scans. 1 mark for decision-making: differential function <40% combined with clear obstruction on washout curve is an indication for pyeloplasty. Part (b) — 10 marks: 2 marks for open pyeloplasty: gold standard, >95% success rate. Incision — flank/dorsal lumbotomy, shorter operative time (90-120 min). Length of stay: 2-3 days. 2 marks for robotic pyeloplasty: success rate 94-98% (equivalent to open). Operative time longer (150-200 min including docking). Length of stay shorter (24-48 hours). 2 marks for advantages of robotic: superior 3D magnification, wristed instruments, easier intracorporeal suturing, shorter hospital stay, smaller scars, less postoperative analgesia. 2 marks for disadvantages of robotic: higher cost (equipment, consumables ~$2000-4000 more per case), lack of haptic feedback, longer operative time, limited availability. 2 marks for evidence in infants: systematic reviews show equivalent success rates, robotic has longer OR time but shorter LOS; no differences in complication rates. Part (c) — 7 marks: 2 marks for port placement: 8.5-12 mm camera port (umbilicus), two 8 mm working ports (subcostal and iliac fossa), 5 mm assistant port. Spacing is critical in infants — ports must be >4 cm apart to avoid external arm collision. 2 marks for working space constraints: infant abdominal cavity is small (15-20 cm width), CO2 at 8-10 mmHg, limited space for instrument manoeuvring. 1 mark for da Vinci systems: Si has large arms that collide easily; X/Xi have smaller, more manoeuvrable arms with better range in confined spaces. Xi allows easier multi-quadrant access. 2 marks for technical modifications: need for smaller instruments (5 mm vs 8 mm), careful port placement (slightly more caudal), use of hitch stitch to elevate the PUJ, reducing working space requirements. Part (d) — 6 marks: 2 marks for acknowledging the father's research and discussing evidence: robotic and open pyeloplasty have equivalent success rates (>95%). 2 marks for discussing local availability: if robotic is available and the surgeon has appropriate paediatric robotic experience, it is a valid option. If not, open pyeloplasty is the gold standard with excellent outcomes. 2 marks for shared decision-making: explain that both approaches offer excellent outcomes, the key determinant of success is surgeon experience rather than approach. Describe what each entails (scars, hospital stay, recovery) and support the parents' choice if robotic is available and appropriate for the child's size.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): The MAG3 renogram shows an obstructive drainage curve with T½ of 160 minutes (normal <20 minutes after furosemide). The right differential function is 38% — below the typical threshold of 40% that triggers surgical intervention. Obstruction on MAG3 is defined as delayed washout of tracer after diuretic administration (T½ >20 minutes) with preserved or declining function. The differential function of 38% indicates that the right kidney has already sustained measurable functional loss, and delays further may cause irreversible damage, making pyeloplasty clearly indicated. Part (b): Open dismembered pyeloplasty (Anderson-Hynes) remains the gold standard with a success rate of >95%. Operative time is shorter (90-120 minutes) and cost is lower. Hospital stay is typically 2-3 days. Robotic-assisted pyeloplasty offers equivalent success rates (94-98%) with the advantages of 3D magnification, wristed instruments facilitating precise suturing, smaller scars, less postoperative pain, and shorter hospital stay (24-48 hours). Disadvantages include longer operative time (150-200 minutes), higher cost (equipment and consumables), lack of haptic feedback, and limited da Vinci system availability. In infants, comparative studies show no significant difference in success or complication rates. Part (c): Robotic pyeloplasty in 6-month-old infants is technically challenging. Port placement must accommodate the small abdominal cavity (15-20 cm wide) — a 8.5-12 mm umbilical camera port and two 8 mm working ports placed >4 cm apart to avoid external arm collision. The da Vinci Xi system has advantages over Si: smaller arm profile, lighter booms, and better range of motion in tight spaces. Endoscopic 30° lens helps visualise the PUJ. A hitch stitch through the renal pelvis can suspend the PUJ, improving access for suturing. CO2 insufflation is limited to 8-10 mmHg. Despite these challenges, robotic pyeloplasty in infants under 6 months is feasible in experienced centres, with outcomes equivalent to open surgery. Part (d): I would first thank the father for his research. I would explain that both open and robotic pyeloplasty have >95% success rates in published series. I would discuss what is available at our centre: if we have a robotic platform and my experience with infant robotic pyeloplasty, I would offer it as an option. I would explain that the most important predictor of success is surgeon experience, not the approach. If robotic is not available or the child's size makes it technically unfavourable, I would explain that open pyeloplasty via a dorsal lumbotomy incision offers excellent outcomes with a small cosmetic scar. I would support the family's choice within the constraints of available expertise and equipment.
discriminators: Detailed interpretation of MAG3 renogram findings and threshold values for surgical decision-making
Evidence-based comparison of open vs robotic pyeloplasty with infant-specific data
Technical understanding of robotic platform limitations and adaptations required in small infants
Patient-centered counselling that respects informed preferences while providing balanced evidence
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Retroperitoneal versus transperitoneal access in paediatric laparoscopy
questions.questionText:
You are planning a left laparoscopic nephrectomy for a 2-year-old girl with a non-functioning left kidney due to reflux nephropathy. She has normal bowel habit and no previous abdominal surgery.

(a) [COMPARE] Compare the advantages and disadvantages of the retroperitoneal and transperitoneal approaches for this specific case. [5 marks]
(b) [STATE] State the typical CO2 insufflation pressures used in children for laparoscopic procedures. How do these differ from adult pressures and why? [2 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] Describe the technique for establishing the retroperitoneal space using a balloon dilator in a child. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 2 marks for transperitoneal advantages: larger working space, familiar anatomy, easier access to lower ureter for concurrent distal ureterectomy (useful in reflux nephropathy). 1 mark for transperitoneal disadvantages: risk of bowel injury, need for bowel mobilisation, postoperative ileus. 1 mark for retroperitoneal advantages: direct access to kidney, no bowel handling, no peritoneal contamination (useful if infected urine), less postoperative pain and ileus. 1 mark for retroperitoneal disadvantages: smaller working space, limited access to distal ureter, more difficult hilar dissection in small space. Part (b) — 2 marks: Children: 8-12 mmHg (typically 10 mmHg). Adults: 12-15 mmHg. The lower pressure in children is because: (1) children have more compliant abdominal walls, so lower pressures achieve adequate working space, (2) higher pressures in children can cause more significant cardiorespiratory compromise (reduced venous return, decreased cardiac output, diaphragmatic splinting), (3) lower body surface area means less CO2 absorption. Part (c) — 3 marks: 1 mark for incision: 1-1.5 cm incision in the posterior axillary line at the tip of 12th rib, dissect through lumbodorsal fascia. 1 mark for balloon creation: use a dissecting balloon (e.g., PPB balloon) or a glove finger on a catheter, advance into retroperitoneal space, inflate with 200-400 mL of air, creating a working space between Gerota fascia and psoas muscle. 1 mark for confirming correct plane: visualisation of psoas muscle and ureter confirms correct dissection plane. Insert camera port, maintain space with CO2 insufflation, place working ports.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): For this case, the transperitoneal approach offers the advantage of larger working space and allows concomitant distal ureterectomy because the refluxing ureter should be excised at the bladder level to prevent ureteric stump syndrome. The retroperitoneal approach avoids bowel handling but provides limited distal ureteral access. Given the reflux nephropathy indication requiring distal ureterectomy, transperitoneal is preferred in this case. Part (b): CO2 insufflation pressures in children are typically 8-12 mmHg (commonly 10 mmHg), compared to 12-15 mmHg in adults. Lower pressures are used because children have more compliant abdominal walls (adequate working space at lower pressures), and higher pressures cause greater cardiorespiratory compromise (reduced venous return, diaphragmatic splinting, impaired ventilation). Part (c): A 1-1.5 cm skin incision is made in the posterior axillary line at the tip of the 12th rib. The lumbodorsal fascia is incised. A dissecting balloon (commercial PPB balloon or a glove finger secured over a red rubber catheter) is advanced into the retroperitoneal space. The balloon is inflated with 200-400 mL of air under direct vision, creating a working space by bluntly displacing the peritoneum medially and Gerota fascia laterally. The correct plane is confirmed when the psoas muscle and ureter are visualised. The balloon is removed, a port is inserted, and the space is maintained with CO2 insufflation.
discriminators: Practical comparative knowledge of approach selection based on indication
Understanding of age-specific differences in CO2 insufflation physiology
Knowledge of retroperitoneal balloon dissection technique
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Laparoscopic varicocelectomy: Palomo versus artery-sparing approach
questions.questionText:
A 14-year-old adolescent presents with a 6-month history of a dull ache in the left scrotum that is worse after standing for prolonged periods. On examination, there is a visible and palpable left varicocele that is easily felt with the patient standing but not visible from a distance. There is no testicular size discrepancy. Ultrasound confirms a left varicocele with maximum venous diameter of 3.2 mm on standing with prominent reflux on Valsalva. Both testes are equal in volume (15 mL).

(a) [CLASSIFY] Classify this varicocele using the Dubin and Amelar grading system. [1 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] Compare the Palomo (en masse ligation of the entire pedicle) and the artery-sparing (Laparoscopic) approach for varicocelectomy. Discuss recurrence rates, postoperative hydrocele formation, and testicular atrophy risk. [5 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] What are the current indications for varicocele treatment in adolescents? Is this boy a candidate for treatment based on current guidelines? [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 1 mark: Dubin and Amelar grade II — varicocele easily palpable, visible from a distance with standing, normal lying down. Part (b) — 5 marks: 2 marks for Palomo technique: ligation of the entire spermatic cord pedicle (artery, veins, lymphatics) en masse at a site above the vas deferens. 2 marks for comparison: Palomo has lower recurrence rates (1-3% vs 5-15% for artery-sparing) because it ligates all collateral veins, but higher hydrocele rates (5-15% vs <1%) because lymphatic interruption compromises drainage. Testicular atrophy is rare with both if testicular artery is preserved (risk ~1-2% with Palomo if aberrant artery is sole supply). 1 mark for artery-sparing: preserves artery and lymphatics, dissecting out the veins, clipping them individually. Higher recurrence but lower hydrocele rate. Part (c) — 4 marks: 2 marks for indications: (1) varicocele associated with testicular size discrepancy (≥2 mL or ≥20% volume difference), (2) bilateral palpable varicoceles, (3) grade III varicocele with abnormal semen analysis (in older adolescents), (4) persistent pain. 2 marks for this boy: he has no testicular size discrepancy (equal volumes 15 mL) — this is the strongest predictor of future infertility. Pain alone (dull ache) is a relative indication — if it affects quality of life, treatment can be considered. Current AUA/ESPU guidelines recommend treatment primarily for size discrepancy or abnormal semen parameters. This boy is a borderline candidate — treatment is reasonable if pain is significant.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Dubin and Amelar grade II varicocele. Part (b): The Palomo technique involves en masse ligation of the entire spermatic cord pedicle (including the testicular artery, veins, and lymphatics) proximal to the vas. It has a lower recurrence rate (1-3%) because all venous collaterals are interrupted, but a higher hydrocele rate (5-15%) due to lymphatic obstruction. The artery-sparing laparoscopic approach isolates and clips individual veins while preserving the testicular artery and lymphatics, resulting in higher recurrence (5-15%) but lower hydrocele (<1%) and better lymphatic drainage. Testicular atrophy risk is similar (~1-2%) with both techniques if the testicular artery is preserved. Part (c): Indications for varicocele treatment in adolescents include: (1) significant testicular size discrepancy (≥20% volume difference or ≥2 mL), (2) bilateral palpable varicoceles, (3) grade III varicocele with abnormal semen analysis in older adolescents, (4) persistent pain affecting daily activities. This boy has no size discrepancy, which is the strongest predictor of future fertility impairment. His pain is a relative indication. Current AUA/ESPU guidelines suggest observation and annual examination if no size discrepancy. However, if the pain is significantly affecting his quality of life, treatment is a reasonable option after discussion of risks and benefits.
discriminators: Precise classification of varicocele and understanding of the two laparoscopic approaches
Knowledge of the inverse relationship between recurrence and hydrocele rates
Application of evidence-based guidelines to adolescent varicocele management
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Laparoscopic management of the intra-abdominal testis: one-stage versus two-stage Fowler-Stephens
questions.questionText:
A 9-month-old boy is referred by the neonatal screening team with a diagnosis of bilateral undescended testes. He was born at 38 weeks of gestation with a birth weight of 3.2 kg. On examination, neither testis is palpable in the scrotum or inguinal region. There is no associated hernia. The scrotum is well developed but empty. Under anaesthesia, examination confirms bilateral non-palpable testes. A diagnostic laparoscopy is performed and reveals: (a) both testes are located at the level of the internal inguinal ring, (b) the vas and gonadal vessels enter a closed internal ring on the right side, (c) on the left side, the vas and gonadal vessels are seen to enter an open internal ring with a small, atrophic-appearing testis just inside the ring. The distance from the testis to the contralateral internal ring is 4 cm on the right and 3 cm on the left.

(a) [INTERPRET] Interpret the laparoscopic findings. What does each finding imply about testicular position and viability? Classify each side using the standard laparoscopic classification. [5 marks]
(b) [COMPARE] Compare the one-stage versus two-stage Fowler-Stephens laparoscopic orchidopexy. Include the anatomical basis, success rates, and indications for each technique. [6 marks]
(c) [CHOOSE] Recommend a surgical strategy for each testis, justifying your choices with reference to the laparoscopic findings and evidence base. [5 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] Discuss the long-term follow-up for intra-abdominal testes after orchidopexy, including surveillance for testicular atrophy, malignancy risk, and the role of testicular prostheses. [4 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 1 mark for right side: intra-abdominal testis at the level of the internal ring with closed ring implies a 'peeping' testis or low intra-abdominal testis. This is favourable — adequate vessel length to reach scrotum is likely. 1 mark for left side: atrophic-appearing testis with open internal ring and vessels entering the ring — this suggests a 'vanishing' testis or very short vessels. 2 marks for classification: Right — type 2a (low intra-abdominal, closed ring). Left — type 2b (open ring with processus vaginalis, likely a testis within a hernia sac or low abdominal with short vessels). 1 mark for additional note: distance >3 cm from testis to contralateral internal ring suggests vessels may be too short for single-stage. Part (b) — 6 marks: 2 marks for one-stage FSO: single laparoscopic procedure — divide gonadal vessels and mobilise testis on deferential artery (collateral blood supply via the vas). Success rate 50-80% — variable. Indicated for high intra-abdominal testis where adequate vessel length cannot be achieved. 2 marks for two-stage FSO: stage 1 — laparoscopic clipping/division of gonadal vessels (induces collateral circulation via vasal artery); stage 2 (6 months later) — mobilise testis on deferential artery and bring to scrotum. Success rate 85-95%. 1 mark for rationale: two-stage approach allows time for collateral vessels to develop and enlarge, reducing ischaemic atrophy risk. 1 mark for evidence: the Shehata meta-analysis (2014) showed two-stage FSO has higher success (90% vs 70% for one-stage). Part (c) — 5 marks: 2 marks for right testis: the testis is low intra-abdominal at the closed ring. Distance 4 cm — borderline for single-stage, but with closed ring and low position, standard laparoscopic orchidopexy (without vessel division) should be attempted first. Only proceed to FSO if vessels are too short. 2 marks for left testis: the atrophic-appearing testis with open ring suggests a low abdominal testis. Attempt standard orchidopexy first. If the testis appears viable and vessels reach, do single-stage without division. If vessels are short, two-stage FSO is preferred over one-stage due to higher success rates. 1 mark for noting that the atrophic appearance may reflect dysgenesis — if testis is very small and dysmorphic, consider orchidectomy and prosthetic placement given malignancy risk. Part (d) — 4 marks: 1 mark for follow-up: annual scrotal examination until puberty. 1 mark for malignancy risk: intra-abdominal testes have 5-10× higher risk of germ cell tumours (especially seminoma) even after orchidopexy — the testis remains dysgenetic. Self-examination education from adolescence. 1 mark for atrophy surveillance: ultrasound for testicular volume if clinically indicated. 1 mark for prostheses: consider testicular prosthesis placement when scrotal development is adequate (usually after 12-14 years) if testis is atrophic or was removed.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Right side: low intra-abdominal testis at the closed internal ring (type 2a). The closed ring and low position suggest adequate vessel length is likely. Left side: open internal ring with atrophic-appearing testis (type 2b) — the testis is likely in a hernia sac with short pedicle. The distance measurements (>3 cm bilaterally) raise concern for adequate vessel length for single-stage orchidopexy. Part (b): The two-stage Fowler-Stephens orchidopexy has superior outcomes compared to one-stage. Stage 1 laparoscopically clips the gonadal vessels, allowing collateral supply via the deferential artery to hypertrophy over 6 months. Stage 2 mobilises the testis to the scrotum. Success rates: two-stage 85-95%, one-stage 50-80%. The anatomical basis is the collateral blood supply through the vasal artery (branch of the superior vesical artery, from the internal iliac), which provides the primary blood supply after vessel division. Part (c): For the right testis: I would attempt a standard (non-Fowler-Stephens) laparoscopic orchidopexy because the testis is at the closed ring — vessel length is usually adequate for single-stage mobilization without division. If the vessels are too short despite maximal dissection, I would convert to a two-stage Fowler-Stephens rather than attempting one-stage, given the higher success rate. For the left testis: I would first assess the testicular viability laparoscopically. If the testis is very atrophic and dysmorphic, I would perform orchidectomy (with later prosthesis). If viable, I would attempt standard orchidopexy. If vessels are short, I would perform two-stage Fowler-Stephens (clip gonadal vessels now, second stage in 6 months). Part (d): Follow-up requires annual scrotal examination to assess testicular volume and position. The malignancy risk remains elevated even after successful orchidopexy (5-10× higher relative risk, primarily seminoma), and patients should be educated about testicular self-examination from adolescence. Ultrasound can assist in assessing testicular volume. If the testis is atrophic or removed, testicular prosthesis placement can be discussed after puberty (typically from age 12-14) for cosmetic and psychological benefit.
discriminators: Ability to interpret laparoscopic findings and classify intra-abdominal testis position
Evidence-based comparison of one-stage vs two-stage Fowler-Stephens with success rates
Individualised surgical strategy based on intraoperative findings
Long-term follow-up awareness including malignancy risk counselling
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes
