questions.title: Transition of care in paediatric urology: from paediatric to adult services
questions.questionText:
A 17-year-old young man with a history of posterior urethral valves (PUV) diagnosed antenatally and managed since birth is attending his final paediatric urology clinic. He had bilateral high-grade VUR, underwent valve ablation in the neonatal period, and has been on clean intermittent self-catheterisation (CISC) since age 7 for poor bladder emptying. His current eGFR is 55 mL/min/1.73 m² (stage 3b CKD). He has episodic urinary incontinence between catheterisations. He is about to finish school and is applying for university. He has not met the adult urology team. His mother answers most of the questions during the consultation while he looks at his phone.

(a) [DEFINE] Define the key components of a structured transition programme for adolescents with complex congenital urological conditions. What are the recognised barriers to successful transition? [7 marks]
(b) [ASSESS] This young man has not yet transitioned. Use a validated transition readiness assessment tool (e.g., Transition Readiness Assessment Questionnaire — TRAQ) to identify the domains in which he may need support. [8 marks]
(c) [OUTLINE] Outline the key differences between paediatric and adult urology services that this young man will encounter, and how these differences can be bridged by a transition clinic. [8 marks]
(d) [DISCUSS] The mother asks: 'Why can't he just stay with you forever?' Discuss the ethical and practical arguments for and against transition, including the concept of healthcare autonomy. [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 7 marks: 2 marks for definition: a purposeful, planned process that addresses the medical, psychosocial, educational, and vocational needs of adolescents with chronic conditions as they move from child-centred to adult-oriented healthcare. 3 marks for key components: (1) early planning (start at age 12-14), (2) transition readiness assessment, (3) a joint paediatric-adult transition clinic, (4) a named transition coordinator/keyworker, (5) a written transition plan, (6) gradual transfer of responsibility from parent to patient, (7) comprehensive medical summary shared with adult team. 2 marks for barriers: patient factors (lack of engagement, cognitive impairment, mental health), family factors (parental overprotectiveness, reluctance to let go), provider factors (paediatrician reluctance to transfer, lack of adult specialists with congenital expertise), systemic factors (different funding models, loss to follow-up). Part (b) — 8 marks: 2 marks for TRAQ domains: (1) Managing medications, (2) Appointment keeping, (3) Tracking health issues, (4) Talking with providers, (5) Managing daily activities. 3 marks for identifying gaps: his mother answering questions suggests deficits in 'Talking with providers' and 'Managing health issues' — he is not taking ownership. Phone distraction suggests low engagement. Need to assess his knowledge of: medications, catheterisation technique, symptoms of UTI, CKD monitoring. 2 marks for assessment tools: TRAQ, or the STARx questionnaire, or individualised assessment during consultation with the young person seen alone. 1 mark for action plan: based on gaps, create a skills checklist to achieve before transition. Part (c) — 8 marks: 3 marks for differences: (i) Paediatric care is family-centred, multidisciplinary, and often sees the patient with parents; adult care is patient-centred, expects self-management, and excludes parents unless consented. (ii) Paediatric clinics have more time, integrated psychology; adult clinics are faster-paced. (iii) Adult urologists may be less familiar with congenital conditions like PUV. 3 marks for bridging: transition clinic — joint paediatric-adult clinic where the patient meets both teams, a keyworker coordinates, and there is a phased approach (e.g., see paediatrician alone first, then adult alone, then joint). 2 marks for practical steps: a comprehensive transition plan including a medical passport/summary, emergency care plan, and named adult consultant. Part (d) — 7 marks: 2 marks for arguments for staying in paediatric: continuity of care, trusted relationships, team knows his history, anxiety about new team for the patient and parent. 2 marks for arguments for transitioning: (i) ethical duty to foster autonomy (Gillick competence, Children Act 1989), (ii) paediatric services are not designed for adult-sized patients (different equipment, protocols), (iii) paediatric teams lack experience with adult-onset complications. 2 marks for practical compromise: transition should be a graduated process, not an abrupt transfer; however, staying indefinitely in a paediatric service is not sustainable or appropriate. 1 mark for legal/ethical framework: the young person has the right to make decisions about his healthcare as he approaches 18 (Mental Capacity Act), and continuation in paediatric services beyond the age limit is not standard practice.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Transition is a planned, structured process of moving from paediatric to adult healthcare. Key components include: starting early (age 12-14), using readiness assessment tools, a joint transition clinic with both teams present, a named keyworker, a written transition plan, and gradual transfer of health responsibility from parent to patient. Barriers include: patient disengagement and cognitive impairment, parental overprotectiveness, paediatrician reluctance to transfer, and lack of adult specialists experienced in congenital conditions. This young man presents several barriers — his mother speaks for him and he is disengaged, putting him at high risk of being lost to follow-up. Part (b): Using the TRAQ domains, this young man has deficits in: (1) Talking with providers — his mother answers for him, suggesting he cannot describe his condition; (2) Managing health issues — he may not know his diagnosis, medications, or CKD stage; (3) Appointment keeping — he may rely on his mother to schedule. I need to assess his ability to: perform CISC independently, recognise UTI symptoms, know his medications and doses, describe his condition to a new doctor. I would see him alone, without his mother, to start building his autonomy. Part (c): Paediatric urology is family-centred, multidisciplinary (nephrology, psychology, nursing), and has longer appointment times. Adult urology is patient-centred, expects independent self-management, and has shorter, more focused appointments. Adult urologists may have limited experience with PUV. The transition clinic bridges this: joint paediatric-adult clinics allow a phased handover, a keyworker ensures continuity, and a comprehensive medical summary (e-portfolio or 'health passport') is shared. The patient gradually takes responsibility: starting with being seen alone for part of the consultation, then managing his own appointments. Part (d): The mother's request reflects natural concern. Ethically, transition is necessary to respect the young person's developing autonomy (the Gillick-competent adolescent should be gradually taking on healthcare decisions) and prepares him for adult life. Staying indefinitely in paediatric care is inappropriate — paediatric services are not equipped for adult problems (e.g., CKD progression requiring dialysis access, sexual health, fertility). The transition should be gradual and supported, not abrupt. I would reassure the mother that we will accompany them through the process, that she will remain involved as he wishes, and that transition is about expanding his support network, not abandoning her.
discriminators: Comprehensive understanding of transition programme components and validated readiness assessment tools
Recognition of specific patient behaviours (mother speaking for him, phone distraction) as clinical warning signs
Detailed knowledge of differences between paediatric and adult service cultures
Ethical balance between fostering adolescent autonomy and respecting parental involvement
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Sexual function and fertility after bladder exstrophy reconstruction
questions.questionText:
A 19-year-old university student born with classic bladder exstrophy presents to the adolescent urology clinic. He underwent a modern staged reconstruction (bladder closure at birth, epispadias repair at 18 months, and bladder neck reconstruction at age 4). He has been dry for >4 hours during the day with timed voiding and uses a pad at night. He is now in a relationship and is concerned about erectile function and his ability to father children. He is otherwise well and has normal renal function.

(a) [DESCRIBE] Describe the anatomical basis of erectile dysfunction in males born with bladder exstrophy, including the role of corporal shortening, divergence, and chordee. [8 marks]
(b) [EXPLAIN] Explain the potential mechanisms of infertility in male bladder exstrophy patients, distinguishing between ejaculatory dysfunction and spermatogenic failure. [8 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] Discuss the options for managing erectile dysfunction and infertility in this patient, including surgical options (penile lengthening, prosthesis) and assisted reproductive technologies (ART) such as ICSI. [8 marks]
(d) [ADDRESS] The patient asks: 'Should I tell my girlfriend about my condition before we become sexually active?' Discuss the psychosocial dimensions of disclosure, intimacy, and body image in exstrophy patients. [6 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 8 marks: 2 marks for anatomical abnormalities in exstrophy: the corpora cavernosa are shorter than normal (short penis), they are attached to the widely separated pubic rami (splayed divergent corpora), resulting in a short, broad penis with dorsal chordee. 2 marks for erectile dysfunction causes: (i) corporal shortening — absolute loss of length, (ii) corporal divergence — the two corpora are separated at their crural attachments, reducing effective rigidity, (iii) dorsal chordee from epispadias repair, (iv) fibrosis from prior surgeries. 2 marks for erectile function in exstrophy: most patients can achieve erections adequate for penetrative intercourse, but the penis appears shorter and may have an upward curvature. 2 marks for pudendal nerve or vascular injury from pelvic osteotomy or reconstruction — rare but possible contributor. Part (b) — 8 marks: 2 marks for ejaculatory dysfunction: most exstrophy men have absent or retrograde ejaculation because of absent bladder neck (cannot close to allow antegrade ejaculation) and surgical distortion of the posterior urethra and verumontanum. 2 marks for spermatogenesis: usually normal because testes are normal (they descend normally, unlike in some other GU anomalies). However, some series show reduced sperm quality due to reflux of urine into ejaculatory ducts (urinary toxic effect) or ascending infection. 2 marks for anejaculation: some patients cannot ejaculate at all due to surgical damage to the sympathetic chain during pelvic reconstruction. 2 marks for fertility options: sperm can be retrieved from post-ejaculatory urine (retrograde ejaculation) or by penile vibratory stimulation / electroejaculation, then used for IVF/ICSI. Part (c) — 8 marks: 2 marks for ED management: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) are first line — effective in ~60-70%. 2 marks for surgical options: if insufficient, penile lengthening procedures (V-Y plasty, suspensory ligament release, or complete penile disassembly with or without a bone-anchored prosthesis). 2 marks for infertility management: (i) collect sperm from post-ejaculatory urine after alkalisation (retrograde ejaculation), (ii) penile vibratory stimulation if neurological, (iii) testicular sperm extraction (TESE) as last resort, (iv) ICSI for fertilisation. 2 marks for multidisciplinary approach: urologist, fertility specialist, psychologist, and sexual health counsellor. Part (d) — 6 marks: 2 marks for disclosure: this is a personal decision — no right or wrong answer. Honest communication is generally recommended for healthy relationships and to manage expectations. 2 marks for body image: exstrophy men often have significant anxiety about their genital appearance, which can affect timing of sexual debut, ability to initiate intimacy, and fear of rejection. 2 marks for support: offer couple counselling, peer support groups (e.g., Exstrophy-Epispadias Support Group), and psychologist input. Role of the urologist is to provide factual information and refer for psychosocial support.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): In bladder exstrophy, the pubic symphysis is diastatic and the corpora cavernosa are attached to the separated pubic rami, creating a short, broad phallus with dorsal chordee. The corporal bodies are divergent (increased inter-crural distance), which compromises the effective rigidity of the erect penis. Despite these anatomical differences, most exstrophy men can achieve erections adequate for penetrative intercourse. However, perceived penile length is a major source of dissatisfaction. Part (b): Infertility in male exstrophy is primarily due to ejaculatory dysfunction rather than spermatogenic failure. The absent or incompetent bladder neck prevents antegrade ejaculation, causing retrograde ejaculation into the bladder. Additionally, surgical reconstruction can damage the bladder neck and posterior urethra, further impairing ejaculation. Spermatogenesis is usually normal because the testes are intrinsically normal. However, some patients have reduced sperm quality from: (1) urinary reflux into the ejaculatory ducts (toxic environment), (2) chronic prostatitis from recurrent UTIs, (3) obstruction at the verumontanum. Part (c): For erectile dysfunction, PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil 50-100 mg, tadalafil 10-20 mg) are first-line therapy and effective in approximately 60-70%. If insufficient, surgical options include penile lengthening (suspensory ligament release, V-Y plasty, or complete penile disassembly with osteotomy) and placement of a penile prosthesis if necessary. For infertility: retrograde ejaculation is managed by alkalising the urine and collecting sperm from post-ejaculatory urine for intrauterine insemination or ICSI. If anejaculation is present, penile vibratory stimulation or electroejaculation can retrieve sperm. TESE is reserved for cases of azoospermia. IVF/ICSI achieves good pregnancy rates with surgically retrieved sperm from these patients. Part (d): Disclosure to a partner is a personal decision, but I would support openness in a trusted relationship. Many exstrophy patients have significant body image concerns, fear of rejection, and delayed sexual debut. I would offer to meet with the couple together to provide accurate information about his condition, function, and fertility. Referral to a psychologist specialising in sexual health and to peer support groups can be invaluable. I would also provide written resources and normalise the conversation about disclosure.
discriminators: Detailed understanding of the anatomical basis of sexual dysfunction specific to bladder exstrophy
Clear distinction between ejaculatory dysfunction and spermatogenic failure when discussing fertility
Knowledge of advanced ART options including ICSI with surgically retrieved sperm
Psychosocial sensitivity to body image, disclosure, and relationship dynamics in young adults
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Fertility counselling in adolescent urology
questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old boy is about to start BEP chemotherapy (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) for a metastatic testicular germ cell tumour. His father asks: 'Will the chemotherapy make him sterile?'

(a) [EXPLAIN] Explain the effect of cisplatin-based chemotherapy on spermatogenesis, including the risk of azoospermia and potential for recovery. [4 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] Describe the process of sperm banking in an adolescent, including the ethical and practical considerations of obtaining consent from a 16-year-old. [4 marks]
(c) [LIST] List three alternative options for fertility preservation if the patient is unable to produce a semen sample. [2 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks: 2 marks for effect: cisplatin causes dose-dependent germ cell depletion, with the most sensitive cells being spermatogonia. Cumulative cisplatin dose >400 mg/m² carries >80% risk of prolonged azoospermia. 1 mark for recovery: spermatogenesis may recover in 50-80% within 2-5 years of completing chemotherapy, depending on cumulative dose and pre-treatment fertility. 1 mark for BEP-specific: 3 cycles of BEP carries ~50-70% risk of azoospermia at 2 years, with recovery in ~50% by 5 years. 4 cycles carries higher risk. Part (b) — 4 marks: 1 mark for process: masturbation to produce a semen sample. 1-3 samples collected before starting chemotherapy. Cryopreservation in liquid nitrogen. 1 mark for consent: the 16-year-old is presumed Gillick competent and should consent personally, not just parental consent. The Mental Capacity Act 2005 states that 16-17 year olds are presumed to have capacity. 1 mark for practical: some adolescents find masturbation in a clinical setting difficult or embarrassing — offer a private room, allow partner help, consider home collection with transport within 1 hour. 1 mark for counselling: see the adolescent alone and with parents separately; discuss confidentiality; provide written information; destigmatise the process. Part (c) — 2 marks: Any two of: (1) electroejaculation under anaesthesia (if anejaculation or severe distress), (2) penile vibratory stimulation, (3) testicular sperm extraction (TESE, though this is more invasive and typically reserved for azoospermic patients who cannot produce a sample), (4) micro-TESE.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Cisplatin, the cornerstone of BEP chemotherapy, is gonadotoxic to spermatogonia. The risk of azoospermia is dose-dependent: 3 cycles of BEP carries a 50-70% risk at 2 years, with approximately 50% of patients recovering spermatogenesis by 5 years. Higher cumulative doses (≥4 cycles or >400 mg/m² cisplatin) increase the risk of permanent azoospermia. Pre-treatment sperm quality and baseline fertility also influence recovery. Part (b): Sperm banking requires the adolescent to produce a semen sample via masturbation. At 16, he is legally presumed competent to consent (Mental Capacity Act 2005, Section 2). He should be seen alone to discuss the process privately and consent should be obtained from him directly, not just from his parents. The practical setting must be private and comfortable — a dedicated room near the clinic, with appropriate materials (sterile pot, DVD/tablet if desired). At least one sample should be collected before chemotherapy begins; ideally 2-3 samples with 48-hour abstinence intervals. If he cannot produce a sample in the clinic, home collection with transport within 1 hour is an option. Part (c): Alternative fertility preservation options include: (1) electroejaculation under general anaesthesia, (2) penile vibratory stimulation, (3) testicular sperm extraction (TESE) with cryopreservation of testicular tissue. In a 16-year-old unable to produce a semen sample, electroejaculation under anaesthesia is the next practical step; TESE is reserved for those who cannot provide a sample by any other means.
discriminators: Knowledge of cisplatin dose-dependent gonadotoxicity and recovery rates
Understanding of adolescent consent law and ethical obligations
Practical knowledge of fertility preservation options beyond routine sperm banking
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Neurogenic bladder in adolescence: independence and surveillance
questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old girl with spina bifida (lumbosacral myelomeningocoele) is transitioning to the adolescent urology clinic. She performs CISC 4-5 times daily via her native urethra and takes oxybutynin 15 mg daily. She has been dry between catheterisations. Recent renal tract ultrasound shows mild left hydronephrosis (APD 8 mm) and stable right kidney. Her eGFR is 80 mL/min/1.73 m². She has a new boyfriend and is concerned about her bladder management affecting intimacy. Her mother has always accompanied her to appointments.

(a) [DISCUSS] Discuss the key urological issues that need to be addressed in adolescents with neurogenic bladder, including renal surveillance, CISC independence, and bladder cancer risk. [4 marks]
(b) [ADDRESS] How would you address the patient's concerns about bladder management affecting intimacy and sexual relationships? [3 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] Explain the importance of transitioning to independent CISC and self-management, and how you would wean maternal involvement. [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks: 1 mark for renal surveillance: annual ultrasound and eGFR monitoring — the new mild hydronephrosis requires further evaluation (possibly urodynamics to assess detrusor leak point pressure and compliance). 1 mark for CISC independence: the goal is independent CISC by adolescence — assess if she performs CISC herself or needs assistance. 1 mark for bladder cancer risk: patients with neurogenic bladder, especially those with indwelling catheters or recurrent UTIs, have a 2-4× increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder. Surveillance with imaging and urinalysis, and prompt evaluation of hematuria. 1 mark for sexual health: discuss contraception, UTIs after intercourse, and the interaction of her bladder management with sexual activity. Part (b) — 3 marks: 1 mark for practical advice: time catheterisation before sexual activity to ensure an empty bladder; consider a Mitrofanoff channel if urethral catheterisation is uncomfortable or difficult. 1 mark for open discussion: normalise the conversation, ask about her specific concerns privately (without mother present), provide information about positions, and discuss the role of intravesical Botox or augmentation if leakage is an issue. 1 mark for multidisciplinary: offer referral to psychologist, sexual health counsellor, and continence nurse. Part (c) — 3 marks: 1 mark for importance: independent CISC is essential for autonomy, body image, and ability to manage at university/work. If she relies on her mother, she will not be able to leave home. 1 mark for weaning: start by seeing the adolescent alone for part (then all) of the consultation; use a transition readiness checklist; encourage her to take ownership of catheterisation; provide a named keyworker. 1 mark for practical: ask mother to wait outside for part of the consultation, give the adolescent responsibility for ordering supplies, managing the catheterisation schedule, and recording bladder diaries.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Key issues include: (1) renal surveillance — annual ultrasound and eGFR; the new hydronephrosis requires urodynamics to check detrusor leak point pressure and bladder compliance; untreated high-pressure bladders cause upper tract deterioration. (2) CISC independence — essential for autonomy; if she relies on her mother, an urgent transition plan is needed. (3) Bladder cancer risk — neurogenic bladder patients have a 2-4× increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma, especially with chronic inflammation from recurrent UTIs or long-term catheterisation; hematuria requires prompt cystoscopy. (4) Bladder management must integrate with her social and sexual life. Part (b): I would ask her mother to step out and speak to the patient privately. I would normalise her concerns and validate that many young women with neurogenic bladder worry about this. Practical advice: (1) empty the bladder by CISC immediately before sexual activity, (2) consider a Mitrofanoff catheterisable channel if urethral access is difficult, (3) discuss the role of Botox injections or augmentation cystoplasty if leakage is unpredictable, (4) advise on UTI prevention (voiding after intercourse, antibiotic prophylaxis if recurrent infections). Offer referral to a psychologist or sexual health counsellor with experience in disability. Part (c): Independent CISC is critical for her ability to live independently, attend university, and form adult relationships. I would start by seeing her alone for the entire consultation, gradually shifting responsibility for ordering supplies, recording bladder diaries, and scheduling appointments. I would use a transition readiness checklist to track her progress and involve a keyworker. I would reassure the mother that this gradual process does not mean excluding her, but rather empowering her daughter to manage her own health as an adult.
discriminators: Awareness of the specific bladder cancer risk in neurogenic bladder patients
Provision of practical sexual health advice for young women with neurogenic bladder
Structured approach to weaning parental involvement and building adolescent independence
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: GU malignancy survivorship in adolescence: surveillance, late effects, and transition
questions.questionText:
A 19-year-old female is referred to the adolescent urology clinic for 'transition of care' after being treated for a left Wilms tumour at age 3. She underwent left nephrectomy and received chemotherapy with vincristine and actinomycin-D (stage II, favourable histology, no radiotherapy). She has been followed up in the paediatric oncology late-effects clinic and has been discharge from paediatric follow-up. She is now at university and presents to her GP with fatigue. Blood tests reveal eGFR 65 mL/min/1.73 m², BP 135/85 mmHg, and urinalysis shows 1+ proteinuria. She has not had any surveillance imaging since discharge.

(a) [IDENTIFY] Identify the key long-term health risks for this patient based on her Wilms tumour treatment history. Explain why her current findings (hypertension, reduced eGFR, proteinuria) are significant. [5 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] Outline the recommended surveillance protocol for a unilateral Wilms tumour survivor (no radiotherapy) in adulthood, including frequency and modalities. [5 marks]
(c) [DISCUSS] Discuss the importance of a structured transition programme for childhood cancer survivors, and why this patient 'fell through the gap' between paediatric and adult services. [5 marks]
(d) [PLAN] Develop a management plan for her current findings, including further investigations, specialist referrals, and lifestyle modifications. [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 2 marks for long-term risks: (i) solitary kidney — hyperfiltration injury, (ii) hypertension (10-15% long-term), (iii) proteinuria and progressive CKD, (iv) cardiac toxicity (vincristine has minimal, but actinomycin-D can cause) — though no doxorubicin, risk is low. 2 marks for significance of her findings: eGFR 65 = stage 3 CKD — hyperfiltration injury to solitary kidney. BP 135/85 = pre-hypertension (95th centile for 19 yo female adds to risk). Proteinuria = early glomerular injury. All three together signal hyperfiltration nephropathy. 1 mark for risk: untreated, this may progress to end-stage renal disease over decades. Part (b) — 5 marks: 2 marks for recommended surveillance: (i) annual BP measurement, (ii) annual serum creatinine/eGFR and urinalysis (proteinuria), (iii) annual renal ultrasound of remaining kidney (screen for tumours, compensatory hypertrophy). 2 marks for frequency: for low-risk (no radiotherapy, no bilateral), long-term surveillance is annual for life. 1 mark for additional: consider DMSA or MAG3 if differential function suspected; echocardiogram only if cardiotoxic chemotherapy was given (not in this case). Part (c) — 5 marks: 2 marks for the gap: paediatric services discharge after 5-10 years follow-up (age 15-18). Adult late-effects services are often separate from routine urology. The patient does not know who to see and cannot navigate the system. 2 marks for structured transition: paediatric oncology should refer to an adult late-effects / survivorship clinic with a named coordinator. A written transition plan and medical summary should be shared. 1 mark for responsibility: the GP is not trained to manage Wilms tumour survivorship protocols — specialist follow-up in an adult late-effects clinic is essential. Part (d) — 5 marks: 2 marks for further investigations: (i) 24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring to confirm hypertension, (ii) spot urine protein:creatinine ratio, (iii) renal ultrasound, (iv) echocardiogram (given fatigue and possible cardiotoxicity), (v) fasting glucose and lipids (CKD + hypertension). 1 mark for referrals: nephrology (CKD management), cardiology (echo), dietitian (renal-protective diet). 1 mark for management: (i) antihypertensives — ACE inhibitor (e.g., ramipril) first line for renoprotection (proteinuria reduction), (ii) low-salt diet, (iii) avoid NSAIDs and nephrotoxic medications, (iv) maintain adequate hydration. 1 mark for lifestyle: regular exercise, avoid smoking/alcohol, monitor for UTI symptoms, annual flu vaccination.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Her primary long-term risk is hyperfiltration injury to the solitary kidney, leading to progressive CKD, hypertension, and proteinuria. Her current eGFR of 65 (stage 3 CKD), hypertension (135/85 suggests pre-hypertension for a 19-year-old female), and proteinuria all point to early hyperfiltration nephropathy. Without intervention, this can progress over decades to end-stage kidney disease. She also has a small risk of cardiac effects from actinomycin-D (though minimal compared to doxorubicin). Part (b): Recommended annual surveillance includes: (1) blood pressure measurement, (2) serum creatinine and eGFR, (3) urinalysis for proteinuria, (4) renal ultrasound of the remaining kidney. These should continue for life, even in low-risk patients. For this patient with no radiotherapy and no cardiotoxic chemotherapy, no additional cardiac surveillance is needed, but given her current hypertension and CKD, an echocardiogram is reasonable to establish baseline. Part (c): This patient fell through the gap because paediatric oncology discharged her after 15 years of follow-up without a formal transition to an adult survivorship clinic. No named adult specialist was identified, no transition clinic was arranged, and the GP did not know the surveillance protocol. A structured transition programme would include: (1) joint paediatric-adult transfer clinic, (2) a comprehensive survivorship care plan given to the patient and her GP, (3) a named keyworker/coordinator, (4) clear guidelines on which adult services to follow (nephrology vs. urology vs. oncology late-effects). Part (d): I would: (1) Arrange 24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring to confirm pre-hypertension/hypertension. (2) Check urine protein:creatinine ratio. (3) Arrange renal ultrasound to assess renal size and exclude mass. (4) Refer to nephrology for CKD management. (5) Start an ACE inhibitor (ramipril 2.5 mg once daily) for renoprotection and proteinuria reduction. (6) Advise low-salt diet, adequate hydration, avoid NSAIDs. (7) Check fasting glucose, lipids, and arrange echocardiogram for baseline cardiac assessment. (8) Provide written information on solitary kidney precautions. (9) Refer to a dietitian for renal-protective dietary advice. (10) Ensure a transition plan is in place with a named consultant in adult urology/nephrology for ongoing annual surveillance.
discriminators: Recognition of hyperfiltration injury as the primary long-term risk in solitary kidney survivors
Knowledge of appropriate surveillance protocols for low-risk Wilms tumour survivors
Understanding of transition failures that lead to loss to follow-up
Comprehensive management plan integrating nephrology, diet, and lifestyle interventions
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Erectile function concerns after hypospadias and epispadias repair
questions.questionText:
Theme: Erectile and cosmetic concerns after hypospadias/epispadias surgery

Options:
A. Chordee — ventral penile curvature due to fibrous urethral plate
B. Urethrocutaneous fistula — persistent leak after failed repair
C. Meatal stenosis — narrowing at the neomeatus
D. Corporal shortening and divergence (exstrophy-epispadias complex)
E. Dorsal chordee from asymmetrical corporal bodies (epispadias)
F. Glanular dehiscence — breakdown of glans wings after repair
G. Urethral stricture — narrowing in proximal neourethra
H. Ejaculatory dysfunction — anejaculation or retrograde ejaculation
I. Penile torsion from asymmetrical skin closure
J. Poor glans contour / cobra deformity after glanuloplasty

Scenarios:
undefined. undefined
undefined. undefined
undefined. undefined

questions.title: Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome: diagnosis, management, and psychological support
questions.questionText:
A 16-year-old girl presents to the paediatric gynaecology clinic with a history of primary amenorrhoea. She has never had a menstrual period. She has normal breast development (Tanner stage 4) and normal pubic hair (Tanner stage 4). She has not yet been sexually active. She reports cyclical lower abdominal pain for the past 6 months that lasts 2-3 days each month. She is accompanied by her mother, who is visibly anxious. An abdominal examination reveals no masses. On inspection, the external genitalia are normal with a normal introitus but the vaginal introitus appears blind-ending. An ultrasound demonstrates the presence of a normal uterus but no identifiable vagina. The ovaries are normal bilaterally. There is a small pelvic collection suggestive of endometriosis.

(a) [DISCUSS] Discuss the differential diagnosis for primary amenorrhoea with normal secondary sexual characteristics. How do the clinical features and imaging findings lead to a diagnosis of MRKH syndrome? [7 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] Describe the different surgical and non-surgical options for neovagina creation in MRKH syndrome, including the Vecchietti procedure, McIndoe vaginoplasty, and the Frank/Dilator method. Compare success rates and complication profiles. [10 marks]
(c) [EXPLAIN] Explain the associated anomalies in MRKH syndrome (renal, skeletal, auditory) and the recommended screening investigations. [6 marks]
(d) [ADDRESS] The mother asks: 'Will she ever be able to have a normal sex life or have children?' Explain how you would counsel both the patient and her mother, including fertility options (surrogacy, uterus transplantation, adoption) and psychological support. [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 7 marks: 2 marks for differential diagnosis: MRKH syndrome, androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS), imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum, and congenital absence of the uterus/vagina. 2 marks for distinguishing features: normal secondary sexual characteristics (breasts, pubic hair) rule out hypogonadism and indicate normal ovarian function. The blind-ending vaginal introitus with normal external genitalia is classic for MRKH. 2 marks for imaging: ultrasound demonstrates a normal or absent uterus with normal ovaries — Type I MRKH (isolated) vs Type II (MURCS association — Müllerian, Renal, Cervicothoracic Somite). 1 mark for the pelvic fluid collection: suggests retrograde menstruation via rudimentary uterine horn with functional endometrium, confirming the presence of a uterus. Part (b) — 10 marks: 3 marks for non-surgical: Frank method — progressive self-dilatation using vaginal dilators of increasing size over 3-6 months. Success rate 80-90% with motivated patient. No anaesthesia or hospitalisation required. 3 marks for Vecchietti procedure: laparoscopic-assisted traction device applied to a vaginal olive, creating a neovagina over 5-10 days by continuous traction. Success rate >95%, shorter dilation time, but requires surgical expertise. 2 marks for McIndoe vaginoplasty: split-thickness skin graft over a mould placed in a dissected rectovesical space. Historically common but higher complication rate (graft contracture, stenosis, fistulae). 2 marks for comparison: Frank method is first-line in most centres — non-invasive, excellent outcomes, but requires patient compliance. Vecchietti is an effective surgical alternative. McIndoe is reserved for failed conservative approaches due to higher morbidity. Part (c) — 6 marks: 2 marks for renal anomalies: 30-40% have renal agenesis (usually unilateral, left side more common), horseshoe kidney, pelvic kidney. 2 marks for skeletal: 10-15% have vertebral anomalies (hemivertebrae, scoliosis, Klippel-Feil syndrome). 2 marks for auditory: 10% have hearing impairment (sensorineural). Recommended screening: renal ultrasound, MRI spine if skeletal abnormality suspected, audiometry. Part (d) — 7 marks: 2 marks for sexual function: reassure that neovagina creation (dilators or surgical) allows satisfactory penetrative intercourse in most patients. 2 marks for fertility: she has normal ovaries → own genetic children possible via surrogacy (IVF with her eggs and partner's sperm). Uterus transplantation is experimental but emerging. 2 marks for adoption as an alternative. 1 mark for psychological support: offer specialist counselling, peer support groups, and involve a clinical psychologist experienced in congenital gynaecological anomalies. Address the mother separately and together, acknowledging her anxiety while supporting the daughter's autonomy.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): The key differential diagnoses for primary amenorrhoea with normal secondary sexual characteristics include MRKH syndrome, complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS), and distal outflow obstruction (imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum). MRKH is distinguished by its triad: (1) vaginal agenesis with a blind-ending vaginal dimple, (2) absent or rudimentary uterus, (3) normal ovaries and normal female karyotype (46,XX). This contrasts with CAIS, where the karyotype is 46,XY and there are no ovaries. The presence of a normal uterus on ultrasound with a blind-ending vagina is consistent with MRKH Type I (isolated) — the rudimentary uterine horns explain her cyclical pain due to retrograde menstruation and associated endometriosis. Part (b): First-line management is the Frank method — progressive self-dilatation using graduated vaginal dilators applied for 20-30 minutes daily for 3-6 months. Success rates of 80-90% for functional neovagina (adequate length ≥6 cm for intercourse). The Vecchietti procedure uses a laparoscopic-guided traction device that applies tension to a vaginal olive, creating a neovagina over 5-10 days — success >95% with shorter duration of dilation. The McIndoe vaginoplasty involves creating a space between bladder and rectum and lining it with a split-thickness skin graft on a mould; success rates are high (85-95%) but complications include graft contracture (10-20%), stenosis, and rectovesical fistula. Current consensus favours Frank method as first-line due to its non-invasive nature and excellent outcomes in motivated patients. Part (c): MRKH syndrome (especially Type II/MURCS association) is associated with: (1) Renal anomalies (30-40%): unilateral renal agenesis (most common), horseshoe kidney, pelvic kidney. (2) Skeletal anomalies (10-15%): vertebral abnormalities (hemivertebrae, scoliosis, Klippel-Feil syndrome — fused cervical vertebrae). (3) Hearing impairment (10%): sensorineural. Recommended screening includes: renal ultrasound (or MRI urogram), spinal X-ray or MRI if clinical signs of scoliosis, and audiometry. Part (d): I would counsel them together initially, then see the daughter separately to allow private discussion. For sexual function: neovagina creation is highly successful, and most patients achieve satisfactory intercourse. For fertility: she has normal ovaries producing healthy eggs — her genetic children are possible through IVF with surrogacy. Uterus transplantation is an emerging option but remains experimental with significant risks. Adoption is another path to parenthood. I would address the mother's anxiety by acknowledging her concern and explaining that MRKH does not define her daughter's future. Psychological support is essential — I would refer to a clinical psychologist with experience in congenital gynaecological anomalies and suggest peer support groups (e.g., MRKH UK, Beautiful You MRKH Foundation).
discriminators: Clear differentiation of MRKH from other causes of primary amenorrhoea with normal secondary sexual characteristics
Detailed comparative knowledge of neovagina creation techniques and their evidence base
Awareness of associated anomalies and mandatory screening in MRKH
Sensitive and comprehensive counselling about sexual function and fertility options
Recognition of the need for psychological support and peer networks

The blind-ending vaginal introitus with normal external genitalia is classic for MRKH. 2 marks for imaging: ultrasound demonstrates a normal or absent uterus with normal ovaries — Type I MRKH (isolated) vs Type II (MURCS association — Müllerian, Renal, Cervicothoracic Somite). 1 mark for the pelvic fluid collection: suggests retrograde menstruation via rudimentary uterine horn with functional endometrium, confirming the presence of a uterus. Recommended screening: renal ultrasound, MRI spine if skeletal abnormality suspected, audiometry. questions.title: Ovarian torsion in adolescence: diagnosis, surgical management, and fertility preservation
questions.questionText:
A 13-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of acute-onset severe left lower quadrant abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She is afebrile. Heart rate is 110 bpm. There is tenderness in the left iliac fossa with involuntary guarding. Urine β-hCG is negative. A pelvic ultrasound shows a 6 cm left ovarian cyst with internal echoes and debris. On colour Doppler, there is no detectable blood flow in the left ovary. The right ovary is normal. She is previously well and her mother is at the bedside, tearful and insisting that 'everything must be saved'.

(a) [INTERPRET] Interpret the clinical presentation and ultrasound findings. What is the diagnosis and how urgent is the need for surgical intervention? [6 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] Discuss the surgical management options for ovarian torsion, including the arguments for ovarian salvage (detorsion) versus oophorectomy. What factors influence the decision to salvage or remove? [10 marks]
(c) [EVALUATE] Evaluate the role of oophoropexy in preventing recurrence of ovarian torsion. What techniques are available and when should they be performed? [7 marks]
(d) [RESPOND] The mother insists on oophorectomy because she 'cannot bear the thought of this happening again'. Discuss the ethical conflict between the mother's request and the principle of ovarian conservation. How would you approach this conversation? [7 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 6 marks: 2 marks for diagnosis: left ovarian torsion — acute onset severe lower abdominal pain, associated nausea/vomiting, peritonism, tachycardia. 2 marks for ultrasound: enlarged ovary (6 cm), heterogeneous internal echoes (oedema/haemorrhage), absent Doppler flow — diagnostic of torsion. 2 marks for urgency: surgical emergency — the 'golden window' for ovarian salvage is 6-8 hours from onset of pain; 4 hours into symptoms, she is within the window but every hour counts. Part (b) — 10 marks: 3 marks for detorsion: untwist the pedicle, observe for reperfusion (pink colour return). Even a black/blue ovary can recover — do not remove based on appearance alone. Success rates: 80-90% ovarian function preserved on follow-up ultrasound. 3 marks for oophorectomy: historically common because of fear of thromboembolism from untwisting, but this risk is now known to be extremely low (case reports only). Oophorectomy is indicated only for a frankly necrotic, non-viable ovary after observation, or suspected malignancy. 2 marks for factors favouring salvage: (i) short symptom duration (<24 hours), (ii) presence of any blood flow on Doppler, (iii) return of colour after detorsion, (iv) adolescent age (peak reproductive potential ahead). 2 marks for factors for oophorectomy: (i) clear evidence of necrosis after detorsion and 10-15 minutes observation, (ii) suspicion of malignancy (complex cyst with solid components), (iii) grossly infarcted, non-viable tissue. Part (c) — 7 marks: 2 marks for rationale: ovarian torsion recurrence rate 5-20% after detorsion alone, particularly in patients with hypermobile ovaries or known pathology (cysts). 3 marks for techniques: (i) utero-ovarian ligament plication (shorten the ligament by suturing it to the posterior uterus), (ii) oophoropexy to the pelvic sidewall or posterior broad ligament using non-absorbable sutures, (iii) plication of the ovarian ligament to the round ligament. 2 marks for indications: (i) recurrent torsion, (ii) isolated ovarian torsion with hypermobile ovary, (iii) after detorsion of a normal-appearing ovary (to prevent recurrence). Part (d) — 7 marks: 2 marks for ethical conflict: mother's emotional response ('cannot bear recurrence') vs. the principle of ovarian conservation in an adolescent with future fertility potential. The mother is prioritising avoiding future emergency over preserving reproductive function. 3 marks for counselling approach: (i) acknowledge the mother's fear and distress, (ii) explain that oophorectomy carries no advantage over detorsion + oophoropexy for preventing recurrence, (iii) detorsion + oophoropexy has a recurrence risk of only 1-3%, (iv) removing a healthy ovary now removes half her daughter's reproductive potential, (v) the ovary is likely to recover fully with detorsion. 2 marks for legal/ethical: the best interest of the child is to preserve reproductive function — oophorectomy without clear indication of non-viability would be difficult to defend. Emphasise shared decision-making with the clinical team recommendation for salvage.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): This is a classic presentation of ovarian torsion with acute-onset severe unilateral lower abdominal pain, vomiting, tachycardia, and peritonism. The ultrasound confirms the diagnosis: an enlarged ovary (6 cm) with internal echoes suggesting haemorrhagic infarction, and absent Doppler flow indicating vascular compromise. Surgical exploration is an emergency — the window for ovarian salvage is approximately 6-8 hours from symptom onset. At 4 hours, she has a good chance of salvage, but urgent intervention is critical. Part (b): The standard of care is detorsion (untwisting the adnexal pedicle) and observation for reperfusion. Even a black/blue appearance can recover fully. Oophorectomy should be reserved for: (1) frankly necrotic ovary that does not reperfuse after detorsion and 10-15 minutes of observation, (2) suspicion of malignancy (complex cyst with solid mural nodules, papillary projections), (3) grossly infarcted, unsalvageable tissue. The historic concern about thromboembolism from untwisting is not supported by evidence — the risk is negligible. In an adolescent, every effort should be made to preserve the ovary. Part (c): After detorsion, the recurrence risk is 5-20%. Oophoropexy significantly reduces this to 1-3%. Techniques include: (1) plication of the utero-ovarian ligament by suturing it to the posterior uterine wall, (2) suturing of the ovary to the pelvic sidewall or posterior broad ligament with non-absorbable sutures, (3) plication to the ipsilateral round ligament. I would perform oophoropexy at the time of detorsion because the ovary is likely normal and at risk of recurrent torsion. The contralateral ovary is also at risk and should be pexied if there is evidence of hypermobility or a long utero-ovarian ligament. Part (d): The mother's request for oophorectomy stems from fear and a desire to protect her daughter from a recurrence. However, my ethical duty is to act in the patient's best interests, which means preserving ovarian function. I would explain: (1) detorsion with oophoropexy is the evidence-based standard — recurrence after oophoropexy is <3%; (2) removing a healthy ovary would unnecessarily reduce her daughter's fertility by 50%; (3) the ovary is likely to recover fully and function normally. If the mother remains insistent, I would involve the clinical ethics committee and explain that oophorectomy without clear evidence of non-viability would be difficult to justify clinically or legally.
discriminators: Understanding of the critical time window and signs of ovarian torsion
Evidence-based argument for detorsion over oophorectomy even in the presence of a black/blue ovary
Knowledge of oophoropexy techniques and their indications
Ethical reasoning in balancing parental anxiety against best-interest standard for the adolescent

Part (b) — 10 marks: 3 marks for detorsion: untwist the pedicle, observe for reperfusion (pink colour return). Even a black/blue ovary can recover — do not remove based on appearance alone. The mother is prioritising avoiding future emergency over preserving reproductive function. 3 marks for counselling approach: (i) acknowledge the mother's fear and distress, (ii) explain that oophorectomy carries no advantage over detorsion + oophoropexy for preventing recurrence, (iii) detorsion + oophoropexy has a recurrence risk of only 1-3%, (iv) removing a healthy ovary now removes half her daughter's reproductive potential, (v) the ovary is likely to recover fully with detorsion. 2 marks for legal/ethical: the best interest of the child is to preserve reproductive function — oophorectomy without clear indication of non-viability would be difficult to defend. questions.title: Labial adhesions and imperforate hymen
questions.questionText:
A 6-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric clinic by her mother who has noticed that the child 'looks different down there' and seems to have difficulty directing her urinary stream. The mother is concerned that 'something might be fused shut'. On examination, the labia minora are fused in the midline with a thin, translucent membrane covering the vaginal introitus. A small opening is visible at the anterior aspect near the clitoris. A separate case involves a 13-year-old girl with primary amenorrhoea and cyclical lower abdominal pain who has a bulging, bluish membrane at the introitus.

(a) [IDENTIFY AND MANAGE] Identify the diagnosis in the 6-year-old. Outline the management options including conservative measures, topical oestrogen cream, and indications for surgical separation. [5 marks]
(b) [DESCRIBE] Describe the presentation, diagnosis, and definitive management of imperforate hymen in the 13-year-old. What surgical technique is used? [3 marks]
(c) [DISTINGUISH] Distinguish between labial adhesions and imperforate hymen in terms of embryology, age at presentation, and clinical findings. [2 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 1 mark for diagnosis: labial adhesions (labial agglutination). 2 marks for management: (i) reassurance and observation — 80% resolve spontaneously by puberty due to oestrogenisation, (ii) topical oestrogen cream (conjugated oestrogen 0.625 mg/g) applied twice daily to the fusion line for 2-4 weeks — success rate 50-90% — instruct mother on application technique and warn about transient breast budding, (iii) if failed or recurrent: betamethasone 0.05% cream as second-line. 2 marks for indications for surgical separation: failure of medical therapy after 3 months, significant urinary obstruction, recurrent UTIs, or symptomatic. Manual separation in clinic (with EMLA cream) or surgical division under anaesthesia. Common error: trying to forcibly separate without adequate oestrogen preparation. Part (b) — 3 marks: 1 mark for presentation: primary amenorrhoea, cyclical abdominal pain, bulging blue/black membrane at introitus (haematocolpos from retained menstrual blood). 1 mark for diagnosis: inspection reveals imperforate hymen with haematocolpos. Ultrasound confirms blood in the vagina. 1 mark for treatment: cruciate incision of the hymen under general anaesthesia, excise the hymenal 'petals', and drain haematocolpos. Closely examine for concomitant vaginal septum. Part (c) — 2 marks: 1 mark for embryology: labial adhesions are acquired (inflammatory/fusion of labial minora in low-oestrogen state) vs imperforate hymen is congenital (failure of hymenal canalisation). 1 mark for presentation: labial adhesions present in pre-pubertal girls (2-8 years) with asymptomatic fusion or urinary stream issues; imperforate hymen presents at menarche (11-14) with amenorrhoea and cyclical pain.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): The 6-year-old has labial adhesions. Management begins with reassurance — most resolve spontaneously with puberty. First-line treatment is topical conjugated oestrogen cream (0.625 mg/g) applied to the fusion line twice daily for 2-4 weeks with a gloved finger, gentle pressure to encourage separation. Success rate is 50-90%. Parents should be warned about transient breast budding and pigmentation. Surgical separation is reserved for medical treatment failure, recurrent UTIs, or significant urinary obstruction — performed under anaesthesia with sharp division and post-separation oestrogen cream to prevent recurrence. Part (b): The 13-year-old has imperforate hymen with haematocolpos. Diagnosis is confirmed by the classic bluish, bulging membrane at the introitus in a patient with primary amenorrhoea and cyclical pain. Treatment is a cruciate incision of the hymen under general anaesthesia, with excision of the hymenal flaps to create a patent introitus, and drainage of haematocolpos. The hymenal 'petals' are excised with absorbable sutures. Postoperative dilation or intercourse prevents restenosis. Part (c): Labial adhesions are an acquired condition in pre-pubertal girls due to inflammation and fusion of the labia minora in a low-oestrogen environment. Imperforate hymen is a congenital anomaly from failure of the urogenital sinus membrane to perforate, presenting at menarche with acute pain and amenorrhoea.
discriminators: Practical management of labial adhesions with oestrogen cream, including side effect counselling
Clear differentiation between acquired and congenital conditions
Knowledge of cruciate hymenal incision technique

questions.title: Ovarian cysts in children: neonatal versus pubertal
questions.questionText:
A 2-day-old female neonate is found to have a 4 cm simple ovarian cyst on a routine postnatal ultrasound performed for antenatal hydronephrosis. The cyst is anechoic, thin-walled, and unilocular with no internal echoes or septations. No solid components are seen. The contralateral ovary is normal. A second case involves a 14-year-old girl presenting with acute right iliac fossa pain. Ultrasound shows a 7 cm ovarian cyst with internal echoes, debris, and a fluid level. There is no Doppler flow within the cyst wall.

(a) [COMPARE] Compare the aetiology and natural history of neonatal (fetal) ovarian cysts versus pubertal ovarian cysts. [4 marks]
(b) [OUTLINE] Outline the management of a 4 cm simple neonatal ovarian cyst. What size threshold would trigger surgical intervention? [3 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] Describe the management of the 14-year-old's cyst. How would you differentiate between a haemorrhagic cyst and ovarian torsion? [3 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 4 marks: 2 marks for neonatal cysts: arise from maternal hormonal stimulation (oestrogen and hCG) causing fetal ovarian follicular development. They are almost always benign (follicular cysts). Most regress spontaneously within the first 6-12 months as maternal hormones wane. 2 marks for pubertal cysts: arise from endogenous hormonal stimulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. Can be functional (follicular or corpus luteum cysts) or pathological (dermoid/teratoma, cystadenoma). Simple cysts <5 cm are usually functional and resolve with observation over 2-3 menstrual cycles. Part (b) — 3 marks: 2 marks for management of 4 cm simple neonatal cyst: observation with serial ultrasound (every 4-6 weeks). Most resolve by 6 months. 1 mark for size threshold: surgery indicated for cysts >5 cm (risk of torsion, haemorrhage, or compression) or those that persist beyond 12 months. Also, complex features (septations, solid components, internal echoes) trigger intervention. The 4 cm simple cyst can be observed. Part (c) — 3 marks: 2 marks for the 14-year-old: ultrasound shows a 7 cm cyst with internal echoes and debris but no flow within the cyst wall. This may represent a haemorrhagic corpus luteum cyst (spider-web appearance, retracting clot) or ovarian torsion (absent flow in the ovarian parenchyma, not just cyst wall). Differentiating features: (i) torsion — enlarged oedematous ovary with peripheral follicles, absent or diminished parenchymal Doppler flow, (ii) haemorrhagic cyst — retracting clot, fibrin strands, lace-like appearance, intact parenchymal flow. 1 mark for management: if torsion is suspected, urgent laparoscopy. If haemorrhagic cyst, symptomatic treatment (analgesia, NSAIDs, serial ultrasound). If the cyst persists >8 weeks or is complex (dermoid), consider cystectomy.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Neonatal ovarian cysts develop from maternal hormone stimulation (hCG, oestrogen) crossing the placenta and stimulating fetal ovaries. These are almost exclusively functional follicular cysts. With declining maternal hormone levels after birth, 90% regress spontaneously within 6-12 months. Pubertal cysts arise from endogenous gonadotropin stimulation after menarche. They can be functional (follicular — mid-cycle, anechoic; corpus luteum — post-ovulatory, thicker wall, may be haemorrhagic) or pathological (mature cystic teratoma/dermoid, serous cystadenoma). Functional cysts typically resolve over 2-3 menstrual cycles. Part (b): A 4 cm simple neonatal cyst is managed conservatively with serial ultrasound scans every 4-6 weeks to document resolution. The threshold for surgical intervention is: (1) cyst >5 cm (increased risk of torsion and complications), (2) complex features (septations, solid components, internal echoes), (3) failure to resolve by 12 months, (4) symptomatic (pain, vomiting). This cyst is <5 cm and simple, so observation is appropriate. Part (c): The 14-year-old's cyst with internal echoes and debris needs differentiation: a haemorrhagic corpus luteum cyst shows fibrin strands, retracting clot, and lace-like pattern on ultrasound with preserved ovarian parenchymal flow; it is managed symptomatically with NSAIDs. Ovarian torsion shows an enlarged, oedematous ovary with peripheralised follicles and absent/diminished Doppler flow in the ovarian parenchyma; it requires urgent laparoscopy. The absence of Doppler flow specifically within the cyst wall (vs ovarian parenchyma) is more consistent with a haemorrhagic cyst than torsion. If there is any doubt, diagnostic laparoscopy is warranted.
discriminators: Clear differentiation in aetiology between neonatal and pubertal cysts
Knowledge of size thresholds and complex features triggering surgical intervention in neonatal cysts
Ability to differentiate haemorrhagic cyst from torsion on ultrasound
commonErrors: See marking guide for examiner notes

questions.title: Vulvovaginitis in prepubertal girls: non-specific versus specific causes
questions.questionText:
A 5-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric gynaecology clinic by her mother with a 3-month history of recurrent vaginal discharge and vulval irritation. The mother describes a yellowish-green discharge with an offensive odour that stains her underwear. She has been treated twice with oral antibiotics for 'urinary tract infections' without improvement. She is otherwise well, afebrile, and has no dysuria. On examination, there is erythema of the introitus and a small amount of purulent discharge at the vaginal orifice. There are no foreign bodies palpable on rectal examination. The hymen is intact and normal in appearance. There are no skin lesions, excoriations, or signs of trauma. Urine dipstick is negative for leucocytes and nitrites.

(a) [DIFFERENTIATE] Differentiate between non-specific (non-infective) vulvovaginitis and specific (infective) vulvovaginitis in prepubertal girls, including the typical organisms involved. [5 marks]
(b) [DISCUSS] Discuss the role of hygiene measures and topical treatments in managing non-specific vulvovaginitis. [5 marks]
(c) [DESCRIBE] Describe the investigation of this child, including when a vulvovaginal swab is indicated and what organisms should be specifically sought. [5 marks]
(d) [ADDRESS] The mother is worried that this might be related to sexual abuse. How would you approach this concern and what is your responsibility in terms of safeguarding? [5 marks]
questions.markingGuide:
Part (a) — 5 marks: 2 marks for non-specific: most common (70-80%). Due to: (i) thin, atrophic vaginal epithelium (pre-pubertal, low oestrogen), (ii) neutral-alkaline pH (low Lactobacillus), (iii) poor perineal hygiene, (iv) mechanical irritation (bubble baths, tight clothing, wet swimwear). 2 marks for specific: infective causes — Group A Streptococcus (most common bacterial cause, bright red erythema, 'strawberry' vulva), Haemophilus influenzae, Shigella, pinworm (Enterobius vermicularis — nocturnal perianal itching). 1 mark for Candida: uncommon in healthy prepubertal girls (requires risk factors like recent antibiotics, immunosuppression, diabetes). Part (b) — 5 marks: 2 marks for hygiene measures: sitz baths, gentle cleansing with water only (no soap/perfumed products), front-to-back wiping, loose cotton underwear, avoidance of bubble baths, changing out of wet swimwear promptly. 2 marks for topical treatments: barrier creams (zinc oxide, Vaseline), emollients (E45 cream), or a short course of 1% hydrocortisone cream for inflammation (max 7 days). 1 mark for reassurance: non-specific vulvovaginitis is self-limiting and resolves with hygiene optimisation. Part (c) — 5 marks: 2 marks for vulvovaginal swab: indicated when symptoms are persistent (>4-6 weeks despite hygiene measures), purulent/blood-stained discharge, or suspected specific infection. Technique: use a small, moistened swab inserted just past the hymenal ring — painless in most children. 2 marks for organisms to seek: (i) Group A Streptococcus (culture), (ii) Haemophilus influenzae, (iii) Shigella (stool culture also), (iv) Enterobius (tape test for ova), (v) sexually transmitted infections (Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, Trichomonas vaginalis) if abuse is suspected. 1 mark for noting that Gardnerella vaginalis and Candida are uncommon and may be contaminants. Part (d) — 5 marks: 2 marks for approaching the concern: listen to the mother's concern without being dismissive. Explain that vulvovaginitis in prepubertal girls is extremely common and most cases are non-specific. However, any concern about abuse must be taken seriously and explored. 2 marks for safeguarding: if there is any clinical suspicion (hymenal injury, abnormal discharge for STI, perianal bruising, behavioural changes), follow local safeguarding protocol — discuss with the named safeguarding lead and make a referral to children's social care if indicated. 1 mark for documentation: carefully document the conversation, examination findings, and any actions taken. Avoid making any definitive statements about abuse without evidence.

─── MODEL ANSWER ───
Part (a): Non-specific vulvovaginitis accounts for 70-80% of cases and results from the hypo-oestrogenic state of the prepubertal vagina — thin, atrophic epithelium with neutral pH and low Lactobacillus, making it susceptible to irritation. Triggers include poor hygiene, tight clothing, bubble baths, and chemical irritants. Specific vulvovaginitis is caused by identifiable pathogens: Group A Streptococcus (most common — causes bright red 'strawberry' vulva), Haemophilus influenzae, Shigella, and Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm — nocturnal perianal itching). Candida is rare without specific risk factors. Part (b): First-line management is hygiene optimisation: twice-daily sitz baths with plain water, front-to-back wiping, loose cotton underwear, avoidance of bubble baths and perfumed products, and changing out of wet swimwear immediately. Topical barrier creams (zinc oxide, petroleum jelly) protect the skin. A short course of 1% hydrocortisone cream can be used for significant inflammation. Most cases resolve with these measures, and antibiotics are not indicated. Part (c): A vulvovaginal swab is indicated given the 3-month duration and purulent discharge. The swab should be taken using a small, moistened swab inserted past the hymenal ring and sent for: (1) bacterial culture including Group A Streptococcus, Haemophilus influenzae, and Shigella species, (2) fungal culture if risk factors present, (3) pinworm tape test (perianal). Given the offensive, purulent discharge, Group A Streptococcus is the most likely pathogen. If there is any concern about sexual abuse, swabs for Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, and Trichomonas should be taken with appropriate chain-of-evidence protocols. Part (d): I would thank the mother for raising her concern. I would explain that vulvovaginitis is very common in prepubertal girls and most cases are non-specific, related to hygiene and the immature vaginal epithelium. However, any concern about abuse must be taken seriously. I would carefully document the examination findings (normal intact hymen, no signs of trauma) and ask about any other concerning signs (behavioural changes, other children affected). If there are any red flags (hymenal injury, anal injury, STI diagnosis, disclosure from the child), I would follow safeguarding procedures: discuss with the named safeguarding lead and make a referral to children's social care if appropriate. In this case, with a normal examination and no other concerns, the presentation is consistent with a specific bacterial vulvovaginitis that can be treated with targeted antibiotics after the swab result.
discriminators: Clear differentiation between non-specific and specific causes of prepubertal vulvovaginitis
Knowledge of the unique microbiology of prepubertal vaginal infections
Appropriate use and technique of vulvovaginal swabs in children
Sensitive and professional handling of safeguarding concerns

questions.title: Ethical and psychosocial considerations in paediatric and adolescent gynaecology
questions.questionText:
Theme: Ethical and psychosocial considerations in paediatric/adolescent gynaecology

Options:
A. Informed consent and Gillick competence in an adolescent
B. Parental request for treatment against medical advice
C. Safeguarding and child protection referral
D. Fertility preservation counselling
E. Psychological support for congenital anomaly diagnosis
F. Confidentiality and disclosure to parents
G. MDT decision-making for complex surgical cases
H. Cultural considerations in genital surgery
I. Consent for examination under anaesthesia (EUA) in a child
J. Decision-making about prophylactic oophorectomy
